# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter** ($\chi$). What does a positive value of $\chi$ indicate about polymer–polymer miscibility?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter** ($\chi$) is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the **enthalpic interaction energy** between segments of two different polymers per mole of lattice sites, normalized by $RT$. It is defined as:

$$\chi = \frac{z \cdot \Delta w_{AB}}{k_B T} = \frac{z \left[w_{AB} - \frac{1}{2}(w_{AA} + w_{BB})\right]}{k_B T}$$

A **positive value** of $\chi$ indicates **unfavorable segmental interactions** between the two polymers ($w_{AB}$ is greater than the average of $w_{AA}$ and $w_{BB}$). Positive $\chi$ values **promote phase separation** and reduce miscibility. (3 Marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **nucleation and growth (NG)** and **spinodal decomposition (SD)** mechanisms of phase separation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Nucleation and Growth | Spinodal Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermodynamic region** | Metastable (between binodal and spinodal) | Unstable (inside spinodal) |
| **Energy barrier** | Yes — activation barrier exists | No barrier — spontaneous |
| **Initial morphology** | Discrete spherical nuclei | Continuous composition fluctuations |
| **Final morphology** | Droplet-in-matrix | Bicontinuous, interpenetrating |
| **Domain size** | Larger, micrometer scale | Smaller, nanometer scale |

(3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** 

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Flory–Huggins free energy of mixing** curve as a function of composition for a partially miscible polymer blend. Mark the binodal, spinodal, and critical point on the curve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for the **critical composition** ($\phi_{1,c}$) and the **critical Flory–Huggins parameter** ($\chi_c$) for a binary polymer blend. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part A(a):

The Flory–Huggins free energy of mixing per mole of lattice sites is:

$$\frac{\Delta G_{mix}}{RT} = \frac{\phi_1}{m_1} \ln \phi_1 + \frac{\phi_2}{m_2} \ln \phi_2 + \chi \phi_1 \phi_2$$

For a partially miscible blend, when $\chi$ is large enough, the curve exhibits **two minima** separated by a **local maximum** in the middle. 

- The **binodal points** are obtained by drawing a **common tangent** to the two minima. They give the **equilibrium compositions** of the co-existing phases (say $\phi_1^\alpha$ and $\phi_1^\beta$).
- The **spinodal points** are the locations of the **inflection points** of the free energy curve, where $\frac{\partial^2 \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi_1^2} = 0$.
- The **critical point** is where the binodal and spinodal coincide.

```
        ΔGmix/RT
            |
            |              ___
            |           /     \      ← Binodal envelope
            |         /         \
            |        /   ____    \
            |       /   /    \    \   ← Spinodal (inflection points)
            |______/__/______\____\__  ___
            |     /  /        \    \/   \___
            |    /  /          \   /        \
            |___/__/____________\_/__________\____
              0  α    sp1    sp2  β           1
                  →——————————→ φ1
                  
              α, β = Binodal (coexistence) points
              sp1, sp2 = Spinodal points
              Middle peak = Local maximum
```

(7 Marks for neat sketch with proper labeling of all features)

#### Model Solution to Part A(b):

**Derivation of $\phi_{1,c}$:**

We use the two critical-point conditions:

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_1^2}\right)_{T,P} = 0 \quad (1)$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial^3 (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_1^3}\right)_{T,P} = 0 \quad (2)$$

The second derivative of the Flory–Huggins expression is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_1^2} = \frac{1}{m_1 \phi_1} + \frac{1}{m_2 (1 - \phi_1)} - 2\chi = 0$$

The third derivative is:

$$\frac{\partial^3 (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_1^3} = -\frac{1}{m_1 \phi_1^2} + \frac{1}{m_2 (1 - \phi_1)^2} = 0$$

Solving the third-derivative equation:

$$\frac{1}{m_1 \phi_{1,c}^2} = \frac{1}{m_2 (1 - \phi_{1,c})^2}$$

Taking the positive square root and rearranging:

$$\phi_{1,c} = \frac{\sqrt{m_2}}{\sqrt{m_1} + \sqrt{m_2}}$$

**Derivation of $\chi_c$:**

Substituting $\phi_{1,c}$ into the second-derivative equation:

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{m_1 \phi_{1,c}} + \frac{1}{m_2 (1 - \phi_{1,c})}\right]$$

After algebraic simplification (as shown in Section 3.2):

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{m_1}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{m_2}}\right)^2$$

For a symmetric blend ($m_1 = m_2 = m$): $\phi_{1,c} = 0.5$ and $\chi_c = \frac{2}{m}$.

(7 Marks — full derivation with all intermediate steps)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** What is the **binodal curve**? Explain the **common-tangent construction** used to determine the equilibrium compositions of co-existing phases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A binary polymer blend of polymer A ($m_1 = 800$) and polymer B ($m_2 = 1200$) has a Flory–Huggins parameter $\chi = 0.025$ at the processing temperature. Calculate the critical $\chi_c$ and the critical composition. Determine whether a blend with $\phi_1 = 0.30$ is in the stable, metastable, or unstable region. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part B(a):

**Definition of Binodal Curve (3 Marks):**

The **binodal curve** (also called the **coexistence curve** or **cloud-point curve**) is the locus of compositions and temperatures at which two co-existing equilibrium phases of a polymer blend have **equal chemical potentials**. Below the binodal, the blend separates into two phases; above it, the blend exists as a single homogeneous phase.

**Common-Tangent Construction (4 Marks):**

On a plot of $\Delta G_{mix}$ versus composition $\phi_1$, the equilibrium compositions of the co-existing phases ($\phi_1^\alpha$ and $\phi_1^\beta$) are determined by drawing a **common tangent** line that touches the free energy curve at two points.

Mathematically, the construction requires two conditions:

1. **Equal Chemical Potentials (slope equality):**
$$\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_1}\right)_{\phi_1 = \phi_1^\alpha} = \left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_1}\right)_{\phi_1 = \phi_1^\beta}$$

2. **Equal Chemical Potentials (intercept equality):**
$$\Delta G_{mix}(\phi_1^\alpha) - \phi_1^\alpha \cdot \mu = \Delta G_{mix}(\phi_1^\beta) - \phi_1^\beta \cdot \mu$$

Where $\mu$ is the slope of the common tangent. The points $\phi_1^\alpha$ and $\phi_1^\beta$ where the tangent touches the free energy curve define the **binodal compositions** of the two co-existing equilibrium phases.

(7 Marks total)

#### Model Solution to Part B(b):

**Step 1 — Calculate $\chi_c$:**

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{m_1}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{m_2}}\right)^2$$

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{800}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1200}}\right)^2$$

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{28.284} + \frac{1}{34.641}\right)^2$$

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(0.03536 + 0.02887\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.06423)^2$$

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}(0.004126) = 0.002063$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\phi_{1,c}$:**

$$\phi_{1,c} = \frac{\sqrt{m_2}}{\sqrt{m_1} + \sqrt{m_2}} = \frac{\sqrt{1200}}{\sqrt{800} + \sqrt{1200}}$$

$$\phi_{1,c} = \frac{34.641}{28.284 + 34.641} = \frac{34.641}{62.925} = 0.5505$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Spinodal $\chi$ at $\phi_1 = 0.30$:**

$$\chi_{spinodal} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{m_1 \phi_1} + \frac{1}{m_2 (1 - \phi_1)}\right]$$

$$\chi_{spinodal} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{800 \times 0.30} + \frac{1}{1200 \times 0.70}\right]$$

$$\chi_{spinodal} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{240} + \frac{1}{840}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\left[0.004167 + 0.001190\right]$$

$$\chi_{spinodal} = \frac{1}{2}(0.005357) = 0.002679$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

- Given: $\chi = 0.025$
- $\chi_c = 0.002063$
- $\chi_{spinodal} = 0.002679$

Since $\chi = 0.025 > \chi_{spinodal}$, the blend at $\phi_1 = 0.30$ lies **inside the spinodal region** — the blend is **thermodynamically unstable** and will undergo **spinodal decomposition** spontaneously, leading to a bicontinuous morphology.

(7 Marks — full numerical working with explicit units and final classification)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint $\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 1$** in derivations: This is a 1-mark deduction per occurrence. Always substitute $\phi_2 = 1 - \phi_1$ *before* differentiating.
> 
> 2. **Confusing spinodal with binodal:** Many students write "spinodal" when they mean "binodal" or vice versa. Remember: *Binodal* = coexistence (first derivative condition); *Spinodal* = stability limit (second derivative condition).
> 
> 3. **Omitting the entropy term:** For high-molecular-weight polymers, students sometimes drop the $\frac{\phi_i}{m_i} \ln \phi_i$ term entirely. This is **only valid in the limit $m \to \infty$** — for finite $m$, always include it.
> 
> 4. **Wrong sign of $\chi$ in diagrams:** Phase separation is driven by **positive** $\chi$ (unfavorable interactions). A negative $\chi$ implies exothermic mixing and stability.
> 
> 5. **Not converting units:** $\chi$ is dimensionless, but $T$ must be in **Kelvin** and $R$ in **$J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$**. Mixing units (e.g., using $T$ in °C) is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the physical interpretation:** KTU examiners award up to **1–2 marks** for *physical insight* in derivation questions. Always conclude with a sentence like "Thus, higher molecular weight reduces miscibility because $\chi_c \propto 1/m$."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Phase Separation**

- **Phase separation** = spontaneous demixing of a homogeneous polymer blend into two co-existing phases when $\Delta G_{mix} > 0$.

- **Flory–Huggins free energy of mixing:**
$$\frac{\Delta G_{mix}}{RT} = \frac{\phi_1}{m_1} \ln \phi_1 + \frac{\phi_2}{m_2} \ln \phi_2 + \chi \phi_1 \phi_2$$

- **Flory–Huggins parameter** $\chi$: dimensionless; positive $\chi$ = phase separation; negative $\chi$ = miscibility promoted.

- **Binodal condition:** equality of chemical potentials (double-tangent rule).

- **Spinodal condition:** $\frac{\partial^2 \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi_1^2} = 0$.

- **Critical point:** second *and* third derivatives vanish; yields $\phi_{1,c} = \frac{\sqrt{m_2}}{\sqrt{m_1} + \sqrt{m_2}}$ and $\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{m_1}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{m_2}}\right)^2$.

- **Two mechanisms of phase separation:**
  - **Nucleation and Growth (NG):** metastable region, discrete droplets, energy barrier present.
  - **Spinodal Decomposition (SD):** unstable region, bicontinuous morphology, no energy barrier.

- **Molecular-weight rule:** $\chi_c \propto 1/m \Rightarrow$ high-MW polymers are *less miscible*.

- **Temperature dependence:** $\chi = A + B/T$; LCST common in polymer blends (problem during melt processing); UCST common in polymer solutions.

- **Engineering applications:** impact-modified plastics (HIPS, ABS), membranes (phase inversion), microcellular foams, barrier films, compatibilization with block copolymers.

- **Common student trap:** A *miscible* blend is *not the same* as a *compatible* blend. Compatibilization ≠ true miscibility.

- **Key dimensionless groups to remember:** $\chi$, $\phi_i$, $m_i$; the **only** physical constant you need is $R = 8.314 \, J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spinodal Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spinodal Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spinodal Decomposition (SD)** is a *spontaneous, continuous, and diffusion-controlled phase separation mechanism* that occurs when a homogeneous polymer blend or solution is quenched (rapidly cooled) into the *unstable region* of its phase diagram — the region located **inside** the **binodal curve** but bounded by the **spinodal curve**.

Unlike **Nucleation and Growth (NG)** which requires a finite activation energy barrier to form discrete nuclei, spinodal decomposition proceeds via the *amplification of infinitesimal composition fluctuations*. The system is intrinsically unstable: any tiny local concentration deviation from the mean grows exponentially with time, leading to a **bicontinuous (interpenetrating) morphology** without any nucleation step.

Mathematically, the condition for spinodal decomposition to occur in a binary blend is given by:

$$\left( \frac{\partial^{2} \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi^{2}} \right)_{T,P} \; < \; 0$$

where $\Delta G_{mix}$ is the **Gibbs free energy of mixing** and $\phi$ is the volume fraction of one component. When this second derivative is negative, the homogeneous phase is *unstable* and spontaneously demixes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Spinodal decomposition is the *signature mechanism* in critical quench deep into the immiscibility region of a polymer blend. It is fundamental to understanding morphology development in **Polymer Blends and Composites (PBPOT604)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you have a perfectly still pond of muddy water where the mud is uniformly suspended. If the water chemistry suddenly changes (e.g., the pH drops, or you add salt), the mud no longer "wants" to stay dissolved. Instead of small clumps forming at random spots (that would be **nucleation**), *every single point* in the pond becomes slightly more muddy than its neighbor, and these tiny differences start growing — everywhere, all at once — until the pond transforms into a marbled pattern of mud-rich and water-rich regions.

> **Plain English Translation:** Spinodal decomposition = "Everywhere, simultaneously, by itself." The phase separation does not need a "seed" to begin — the whole system becomes its own seed.

### The Two Routes to Phase Separation (Side-by-Side Intuition)

| Mechanism | Initiation Point | Analogy | Energy Barrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nucleation & Growth (NG)** | Discrete nuclei at random sites | Raindrops forming on dust particles | **High** (Activation energy required) |
| **Spinodal Decomposition (SD)** | Everywhere — uniform amplification | A flat sand surface spontaneously rippling into waves | **Zero** (No barrier) |

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Characteristic Diffusion Length:** Typically **1 nm to 100 nm** in polymer blends
- **Domain Size Growth Law:** $L(t) \sim t^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha \approx 1/3$ (late stage, Lifshitz–Slyozov)
- **Quench Depth Parameter:** $\chi N$ (Flory–Huggins interaction parameter times degree of polymerization)
- **Critical Point:** $\phi_c = 0.5$ (symmetric blend), $\chi_c N = 2$ (at critical temperature)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board:** The **binodal** (or *coexistence*) curve marks thermodynamic *equilibrium*; the **spinodal** curve marks the *limit of metastability*. Phase separation *inside* the spinodal is always spinodal decomposition. Phase separation *between* binodal and spinodal must occur by nucleation.

## 1.4 Historical & Engineering Context

Spinodal decomposition was first mathematically formalized by **Josiah Willard Gibbs (1876–1878)** in his work on heterogeneous equilibrium. The modern kinetic theory for polymer blends was developed by **John W. Cahn and John E. Hilliard (1958, 1959)**, leading to the celebrated **Cahn–Hilliard equation**. This framework is widely used today in:
- Designing **microporous membranes** for filtration
- Producing **bicontinuous blends** with high electrical conductivity
- Manufacturing **phase-separated coatings**
- Tailoring **optical materials** with controlled refractive index gradients

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Free Energy Landscape

The thermodynamic origin of spinodal decomposition lies in the **Flory–Huggins free energy of mixing**. For a binary polymer blend of components A and B:

$$\Delta G_{mix} \; = \; RT \left[ \frac{\phi_{A}}{N_{A}} \ln \phi_{A} \; + \; \frac{\phi_{B}}{N_{B}} \ln \phi_{B} \; + \; \chi \phi_{A} \phi_{B} \right]$$

where:
- $R$ = Universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (**K**)
- $\phi_A$, $\phi_B$ = Volume fractions of components A and B
- $N_A$, $N_B$ = Degrees of polymerization
- $\chi$ = **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter** (dimensionless, temperature-dependent)

The **first derivative** of $\Delta G_{mix}$ with respect to composition gives the chemical potential difference; the **second derivative** governs stability:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi^{2}} \; = \; \frac{1}{N_{A} \phi_{A}} \; + \; \frac{1}{N_{B} \phi_{B}} \; - \; 2\chi$$

## 2.2 The Three Stability Regimes

A polymer blend exists in one of three thermodynamic regimes:

### Regime 1: Stable (Homogeneous)
$$\frac{\partial^{2} \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi^{2}} \; > \; 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \chi N \; < \; 2$$
A single phase is stable. **No phase separation occurs.** This region lies *outside* the binodal curve.

### Regime 2: Metastable (Nucleation & Growth)
$$\frac{\partial^{2} \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi^{2}} \; > \; 0 \quad \text{but system is below binodal}$$
Phase separation requires a *finite activation energy* to overcome the nucleation barrier. Discrete spherical domains of the minority phase grow.

### Regime 3: Unstable (Spinodal Decomposition)
$$\frac{\partial^{2} \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi^{2}} \; < \; 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \chi N \; > \; 2$$
The system is **absolutely unstable**. Composition fluctuations of *any wavelength* above a critical value will spontaneously grow. **No nucleation barrier exists.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Memory Trick:** Think of the three regimes as **SMU** — **S**table, **M**etastable, **U**nstable — going from outside the binodal toward the spinodal.

## 2.3 The Spinodal Condition (Derivation Logic)

The **spinodal curve** in the $(\phi, T)$ plane is defined by the locus of points where:

$$\left( \frac{\partial^{2} \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi^{2}} \right)_{T,P} \; = \; 0$$

This yields the **critical value of the interaction parameter**:

$$\chi_{s} \; = \; \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{N_{A} \phi_{A}} \; + \; \frac{1}{N_{B} \phi_{B}} \right)$$

At the **critical point** (symmetric blend, $N_A = N_B = N$, $\phi_A = \phi_B = 0.5$):

$$\chi_{c} N \; = \; 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \chi_{c} \; = \; \frac{2}{N}$$

This is the **threshold** below which the blend is one phase at the critical composition.

## 2.4 The Cahn–Hilliard Linearized Theory

The cornerstone of spinodal decomposition kinetics is the **Cahn–Hilliard diffusion equation**. The key idea is that the *chemical potential gradient* drives a flux, and includes a **gradient energy term** accounting for the energy cost of sharp interfaces.

The free energy functional used is:

$$F[\phi] \; = \; \int \left[ f(\phi) \; + \; \frac{K}{2} \left( \nabla \phi \right)^{2} \right] dV$$

where:
- $f(\phi)$ = Bulk free energy density
- $K$ = **Gradient energy coefficient** (positive; related to chain statistics)
- $(\nabla \phi)^{2}$ = Composition gradient penalty

The chemical potential becomes:

$$\mu \; = \; \frac{\delta F}{\delta \phi} \; = \; \frac{\partial f}{\partial \phi} \; - \; K \nabla^{2} \phi$$

The **Cahn–Hilliard equation of motion** is then:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \; = \; M \, \nabla^{2} \mu \; = \; M \, \nabla^{2} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial \phi} \; - \; K \nabla^{2} \phi \right)$$

where $M$ is the **Onsager mobility coefficient** (positive).

## 2.5 Amplification of Fluctuations

Assuming an initial composition fluctuation of the form:

$$\phi(\vec{r}, t) \; - \; \phi_{0} \; = \; A(\vec{q}, t) \, e^{i \vec{q} \cdot \vec{r}}$$

substituting into the linearized Cahn–Hilliard equation gives the **amplification rate**:

$$R(q) \; = \; -M q^{2} \left( \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} \; + \; K q^{2} \right)$$

where $q = \vert \vec{q} \vert$ is the **wave number** of the fluctuation. The fluctuation grows exponentially as:

$$A(q, t) \; = \; A(q, 0) \, e^{R(q) t}$$

The maximum growth rate occurs at the **dominant wave number** $q_m$:

$$q_{m} \; = \; \left( -\frac{1}{2K} \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} \right)^{1/2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a dominant wave number?** Short wavelengths are penalized by the gradient energy $Kq^2$ (cost of sharp interfaces), while long wavelengths are penalized by the diffusion term $-Mq^2$ (slow transport). The competition selects a *characteristic length scale* $\lambda_m = 2\pi / q_m$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flory–Huggins Free Energy | $\Delta G_{mix} = RT \left[ \dfrac{\phi_A}{N_A} \ln \phi_A + \dfrac{\phi_B}{N_B} \ln \phi_B + \chi \phi_A \phi_B \right]$ | Thermodynamic driving force for mixing/demixing |
| 2 | Second Derivative (Stability) | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{N_A \phi_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_B \phi_B} - 2\chi$ | Sign determines stability regime |
| 3 | Spinodal Condition | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi^{2}} = 0$ | Boundary between metastable and unstable |
| 4 | Critical Value | $\chi_c = \dfrac{2}{N}$ (symmetric blend) | Threshold for phase separation |
| 5 | Free Energy Functional | $F[\phi] = \int \left[ f(\phi) + \dfrac{K}{2} (\nabla \phi)^2 \right] dV$ | Cahn–Hilliard energy including gradient penalty |
| 6 | Chemical Potential | $\mu = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \phi} - K \nabla^2 \phi$ | Generalized chemical potential |
| 7 | Cahn–Hilliard Equation | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = M \nabla^2 \mu$ | Governing PDE for SD kinetics |
| 8 | Amplification Rate | $R(q) = -M q^2 \left( \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} + K q^2 \right)$ | Exponential growth rate of fluctuation of wave number $q$ |
| 9 | Dominant Wave Number | $q_m = \left( -\dfrac{1}{2K} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} \right)^{1/2}$ | Fastest-growing fluctuation |
| 10 | Characteristic Wavelength | $\lambda_m = \dfrac{2\pi}{q_m}$ | Initial periodic length scale of morphology |
| 11 | Domain Growth (Late Stage) | $L(t) \sim t^{1/3}$ | Lifshitz–Slyozov coarsening law |
| 12 | Coarsening Exponent | $\alpha = 1/3$ | For diffusive transport |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Spinodal decomposition is not just an academic curiosity — it is a **manufacturing tool**:

- **Microporous Membranes:** Celgard, ULTRAFILM — produced by spinodal decomposition followed by extraction of one phase.
- **Toughened Plastics:** HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene) uses spinodal-like structures to absorb impact energy.
- **Optically Clear Composites:** Controlled SD yields materials with sub-wavelength refractive index variation, useful for gradient-index (GRIN) optics.
- **Biomimetic Scaffolds:** Bicontinuous morphologies from SD are ideal for tissue engineering scaffolds due to interconnected porosity.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Flory–Huggins Second Derivative and Spinodal Condition

### Step 1 — Write the dimensionless free energy of mixing per lattice site:

$$\frac{\Delta G_{mix}}{RT} \; = \; \frac{\phi_A}{N_A} \ln \phi_A \; + \; \frac{\phi_B}{N_B} \ln \phi_B \; + \; \chi \phi_A \phi_B$$

### Step 2 — First derivative with respect to $\phi_A$ (using $\phi_B = 1 - \phi_A$):

$$\frac{\partial (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_A} \; = \; \frac{1}{N_A} (\ln \phi_A + 1) \; - \; \frac{1}{N_B} (\ln(1-\phi_A) + 1) \; + \; \chi (1 - 2\phi_A)$$

**Explanation:** The derivative of $\phi_A \ln \phi_A$ is $\ln \phi_A + 1$; the derivative of $\phi_B \ln \phi_B$ is $-(\ln \phi_B + 1)$ via chain rule since $\phi_B = 1 - \phi_A$; and the derivative of $\chi \phi_A (1-\phi_A)$ is $\chi(1 - 2\phi_A)$.

### Step 3 — Second derivative (this is the stability determinant):

$$\frac{\partial^{2} (\Delta G_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_A^{2}} \; = \; \frac{1}{N_A \phi_A} \; + \; \frac{1}{N_B \phi_B} \; - \; 2\chi$$

**Explanation:** $\frac{d}{d\phi_A} \ln \phi_A = 1/\phi_A$; $\frac{d}{d\phi_A} \ln(1-\phi_A) = -1/(1-\phi_A)$ → flipping the sign from the first derivative step gives the positive second term; and $\frac{d}{d\phi_A} \chi(1-2\phi_A) = -2\chi$.

### Step 4 — Apply the spinodal condition (set the second derivative to zero):

$$\frac{1}{N_A \phi_A} \; + \; \frac{1}{N_B \phi_B} \; - \; 2\chi_{s} \; = \; 0$$

Solving for $\chi_s$:

$$\chi_{s} \; = \; \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{N_A \phi_A} \; + \; \frac{1}{N_B \phi_B} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Specialize to the symmetric critical point: $N_A = N_B = N$, $\phi_A = \phi_B = 0.5$:

$$\chi_{c} \; = \; \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{N \cdot 0.5} \; + \; \frac{1}{N \cdot 0.5} \right) \; = \; \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{2}{N} \; + \; \frac{2}{N} \right) \; = \; \frac{2}{N}$$

Therefore, the dimensionless threshold is:

$$\chi_c N \; = \; 2$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Linearized Cahn–Hilliard Amplification Rate

### Step 1 — Start with the Cahn–Hilliard equation:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \; = \; M \, \nabla^{2} \mu \; = \; M \, \nabla^{2} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial \phi} \; - \; K \nabla^{2} \phi \right)$$

### Step 2 — Linearize around the mean composition $\phi_0$. Let $\phi = \phi_0 + u(\vec{r}, t)$ with $u$ small. Then:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \phi} \;\approx\; \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial \phi} \right|_{\phi_0} \; + \; \left. \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} \right|_{\phi_0} u$$

The constant first-derivative term vanishes upon taking the Laplacian, leaving:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} \; = \; M \, \nabla^{2} \left( \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} u \; - \; K \nabla^{2} u \right)$$

### Step 3 — Because $\partial^2 f / \partial \phi^2$ is constant in the linearized regime, it factors out:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} \; = \; M \left[ \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} \nabla^{2} u \; - \; K \nabla^{4} u \right]$$

### Step 4 — Fourier-decompose: $u(\vec{r}, t) = \sum_q A(q, t) e^{i \vec{q} \cdot \vec{r}}$. Apply $\nabla^2 \to -q^2$, $\nabla^4 \to q^4$:

$$\frac{d A(q, t)}{d t} \; = \; M \left[ -\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} q^{2} \; - \; K q^{4} \right] A(q, t)$$

### Step 5 — Identify the amplification rate:

$$R(q) \; = \; -M q^{2} \left( \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} \; + \; K q^{2} \right)$$

### Step 6 — To find the dominant wave number, maximize $R(q)$ with respect to $q$. Compute:

$$\frac{dR}{dq} \; = \; -2Mq \left( \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} + 2Kq^{2} \right) \; = \; 0$$

For a nontrivial solution:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} \; + \; 2K q_m^{2} \; = \; 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_{m} \; = \; \left( -\frac{1}{2K} \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial \phi^{2}} \right)^{1/2}$$

### Step 7 — Characteristic initial wavelength:

$$\lambda_{m} \; = \; \frac{2\pi}{q_{m}} \; = \; 2\pi \left( -\frac{2K}{\partial^{2} f / \partial \phi^{2}} \right)^{1/2}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Numerical Example — Critical Temperature for PS/PMMA Blend

A symmetric blend of **Polystyrene (PS)** and **Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)** with $N = 10^4$ has Flory–Huggins parameter approximated by:

$$\chi(T) \; = \; 0.028 \; + \; \frac{3.9}{T}$$

Determine the **critical temperature of phase separation**.

### Step 1 — Apply the critical condition $\chi_c N = 2$:

$$\chi_c \; = \; \frac{2}{N} \; = \; \frac{2}{10^{4}} \; = \; 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the critical temperature:

$$2 \times 10^{-4} \; = \; 0.028 \; + \; \frac{3.9}{T_c}$$

$$\frac{3.9}{T_c} \; = \; 2 \times 10^{-4} \; - \; 0.028 \; = \; -0.0278$$

$$T_c \; = \; \frac{3.9}{-0.0278} \; \approx \; -140.3 \text{ K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A negative critical temperature indicates that PS/PMMA is **immiscible at all positive temperatures** for $N = 10^4$. This is why PS/PMMA blends are classically immiscible and require compatibilizers (e.g., PS-PMMA block copolymers) to stabilize the interface.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SD vs NG

| Feature | Spinodal Decomposition (SD) | Nucleation & Growth (NG) |
|---|---|---|
| Thermodynamic region | **Inside** spinodal | **Between** binodal and spinodal |
| Driving force sign | $\partial^2 \Delta G / \partial \phi^2 < 0$ | $\partial^2 \Delta G / \partial \phi^2 > 0$ |
| Energy barrier | **None** | Finite activation energy required |
| Initial morphology | Continuous, bicontinuous, periodic | Discrete spherical domains |
| Length scale | Characteristic $\lambda_m$ selected | No preferred length scale |
| Time evolution | Exponential then coarsening | Steady growth from nuclei |
| Late-stage coarsening | $L(t) \sim t^{1/3}$ (Lifshitz–Slyozov) | $L(t) \sim t^{1/2}$ (diffusion-limited) |
| Typical blend examples | Critical quenches, UCST/LCST near center | Off-critical compositions, shallow quenches |
