## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Property Enhancement Windows in Real Applications

| Application Sector | Base Matrix | Blend/Composite System | Property Enhanced | Typical Value Achieved |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Automotive bumper | PP | PP/EPDM (TPO) | Impact strength (notched Izod) | **650 J/m** |
| Engine cover | PA66 | PA66 + 30% GF | Tensile strength | **180 MPa** |
| Aircraft fuselage skin | Epoxy | CFRP (T800/IM7) | Specific stiffness | **$E/\rho \approx$ 100 GPa/(g/cm³)** |
| Laptop chassis | PC | PC/ABS (70:30) | Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT) | **115 °C @ 1.82 MPa** |
| Food tray | PP | PP/Talc (20%) | Flexural modulus | **2.8 GPa** |
| Catheter liner | PVC | PVC/PU blend | Coefficient of friction | **$\mu \approx$ 0.04** |
| Oxygen barrier | PET | PET/EVOH (5-layer) | O₂ permeability | **< 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹** |
| Battery tray (EV) | PP | PP/LFT (30% carbon fiber) | Energy absorption | **40 kJ/kg** |
| Window profile | PVC | PVC/PMMA (acrylic capstock) | UV resistance | **>10 years outdoor** |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A glass-fiber reinforced Polyamide 6,6 composite contains **30% by weight** of glass fibers. Given $\rho_m = 1.14$ g/cm³ (PA66) and $\rho_f = 2.54$ g/cm³ (E-glass), predict the composite density.

**Step 1:** Convert weight fraction to volume fraction.

$$
V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}
$$

Substitute $W_f = 0.30$ and $W_m = 0.70$:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.30 / 2.54}{0.30 / 2.54 + 0.70 / 1.14}
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.1181}{0.1181 + 0.6140} = \dfrac{0.1181}{0.7321}
$$

$$
V_f \approx 0.1614 \quad \text{(i.e., 16.14 vol\%)}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the ROM for density.

$$
\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f
$$

With $V_m = 1 - 0.1614 = 0.8386$:

$$
\rho_c = (1.14)(0.8386) + (2.54)(0.1614)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 0.9560 + 0.4100 = 1.366 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with experimental value.

Typical measured density for PA66 + 30 wt% GF is **~1.37 g/cm³**, in excellent agreement with the ROM prediction (deviation < 0.4%).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Application Selection Decision Tree

The following Mermaid diagram maps the engineering decision pathway used by a polymer engineer when selecting a blend or composite system for a specific application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start: Define Service Requirements]) --> nodeReq{Primary Requirement?}
    nodeReq -- High Impact Resistance --> nodeImp[Select TPE or Rubber-Toughened Blend]
    nodeImp -- eg, TPO, ABS --> nodeImpOut[Application: Automotive Bumper / Dashboard]
    nodeReq -- High Specific Stiffness --> nodeStiff[Select Fiber-Reinforced Composite]
    nodeStiff -- eg, CFRP, GFRP --> nodeStiffOut[Application: Aerospace, Battery Trays]
    nodeReq -- Thermal Stability --> nodeTherm[Select High-Tg or Filled System]
    nodeTherm -- eg, PPS/CF, PA66/GF --> nodeThermOut[Application: E&E Connectors, Engine Parts]
    nodeReq -- Barrier Properties --> nodeBarr[Select Multilayer or Nanocomposite]
    nodeBarr -- eg, PET/EVOH, PA6/Nanoclay --> nodeBarrOut[Application: Food Packaging, Fuel Tanks]
    nodeReq -- Biocompatibility --> nodeBio[Select Biodegradable Blend]
    nodeBio -- eg, PLA/PCL --> nodeBioOut[Application: Sutures, Scaffolds]
    nodeImpOut --> nodeCost{Cost Constraint Met?}
    nodeStiffOut --> nodeCost
    nodeThermOut --> nodeCost
    nodeBarrOut --> nodeCost
    nodeBioOut --> nodeCost
    nodeCost -- Yes --> nodeFinal[Deploy in Production]
    nodeCost -- No --> nodeReform[Reformulate or Downgrade Filler Loading]
    nodeReform --> nodeReq
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Property-Performance Mapping in Polymer Composites

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Inputs / Constituents]
        nodeMat[Polymer Matrix]
        nodeFib[Reinforcement: Fiber / Filler]
        nodeComp[Compatibilizer / Coupling Agent]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Processing Stage]
        nodeMix[Compounding Extrusion]
        nodeShape[Injection / Compression Molding]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Technological Applications]
        nodeA[Automotive]
        nodeB[Aerospace]
        nodeC[Biomedical]
        nodeD[Electronics]
        nodeE[Packaging]
    end

    nodeMat --> nodeMix
    nodeFib --> nodeMix
    nodeComp --> nodeMix
    nodeMix --> nodeShape
    nodeShape --> nodeA
    nodeShape --> nodeB
    nodeShape --> nodeC
    nodeShape --> nodeD
    nodeShape --> nodeE
```

## 4.3 Compatibilization Strategy Block Diagram

For most commercial immiscible polymer blends, a **compatibilizer** (often a block or graft copolymer) is essential to achieve commercially viable properties. The diagram below illustrates the functional logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodePolyA[Polymer A - eg, PA66 Polar] --> nodeBlock[Block / Graft Copolymer Compatibilizer]
    nodePolyB[Polymer B - eg, PP Non-Polar] --> nodeBlock
    nodeBlock -- eg, PP-g-MA --> nodeMorph[Fine Morphology: 0.1 to 1 micron Domain Size]
    nodeMorph --> nodeProp[Property Window: High Impact + High Modulus]
    nodeProp --> nodeProd[Commercial Product: eg, PA66/PP for Automotive]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **interfacial adhesion** imparted by the compatibilizer is the *single most critical factor* governing the practical mechanical performance of any immiscible blend. Without it, the blend behaves as a weak particulate composite with poor elongation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: List any three major industrial sectors where polymer blends and composites are extensively used. Give one specific example of a blend or composite system employed in each sector.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Automotive**: PP/EPDM thermoplastic olefin (TPO) blend used for bumper fascias and interior trims — provides high impact resistance (>500 J/m notched Izod) and paintability.
2. **Aerospace**: Carbon fiber reinforced epoxy (CFRP) composite — used in Boeing 787 primary structure (fuselage, wings) — provides specific stiffness of $\sim$ 100 GPa/(g/cm³).
3. **Electrical & Electronics**: PC/ABS blend — used for laptop and monitor housings — provides heat distortion temperature of $\sim$ 115 °C and superior impact strength compared to neat PC.

*(Each sector with example carries 1 mark. Total: 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: Define "Technological Application" in the context of polymer blends and composites. State the four selection criteria that govern the choice of a blend/composite system for a given application.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Technological application refers to the engineering use of polymer blends (multi-phase polymer mixtures) and polymer composites (polymer matrix reinforced with fibers, fillers, or nanomaterials) to achieve a defined property profile — typically superior to any single homopolymer — across industrial sectors.

**Four Selection Criteria (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Property Window**: Target mechanical, thermal, electrical, or barrier properties must be quantitatively defined.
2. **Processability**: Compatibility with the chosen manufacturing technique (injection molding, extrusion, compression molding).
3. **Cost-Performance Ratio**: Total cost per part must meet market expectations.
4. **Regulatory & Environmental Compliance**: FDA, USP, REACH, RoHS, biodegradability, recyclability requirements.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for predicting the elastic modulus of a fiber-reinforced polymer composite. State the assumptions of the model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite contains **40% by volume** of E-glass fibers. The modulus of the epoxy matrix is **$E_m$ = 3.5 GPa** and that of the glass fiber is **$E_f$ = 72 GPa**. Calculate: (i) the composite modulus using the **Voigt (isostrain)** model, and (ii) the composite modulus using the **Reuss (isostress)** model. Comment on the practical significance of the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Rule of Mixtures — Derivation and Assumptions (7 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The Rule of Mixtures (ROM), also called the *Voigt model*, predicts the modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite loaded parallel to the fiber direction:

$$
E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f
$$

**Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. Fibers are **continuous, uniform, and perfectly aligned** parallel to the loading axis.
2. **Perfect bonding** exists at the fiber-matrix interface (no slip or debonding).
3. Both matrix and fiber experience the **same strain** (isostrain / Voigt condition).
4. Load is applied **parallel** to the fiber axis; transverse loading is not considered.
5. Constituents are **linearly elastic** and homogeneous.

**Derivation sketch (3 marks):** Force balance on a representative volume element leads to:

$$
F_c = F_m + F_f \implies \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_f A_f
$$

Dividing by $A_c$ and using $V_i = A_i / A_c$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f
$$

Applying Hooke's Law under isostrain ($\epsilon_c = \epsilon_m = \epsilon_f$):

$$
\boxed{E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_f = 0.40$, $V_m = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $E_f = 72$ GPa.

**(i) Voigt (Isostrain) Model — 3 marks:**

$$
E_c^{Voigt} = E_m V_m + E_f V_f
$$

$$
E_c^{Voigt} = (3.5)(0.60) + (72)(0.40)
$$

$$
E_c^{Voigt} = 2.10 + 28.80 = 30.90 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(ii) Reuss (Isostress) Model — 3 marks:**

$$
E_c^{Reuss} = \dfrac{E_m E_f}{E_f V_m + E_m V_f}
$$

$$
E_c^{Reuss} = \dfrac{(3.5)(72)}{(72)(0.60) + (3.5)(0.40)}
$$

$$
E_c^{Reuss} = \dfrac{252}{43.2 + 1.4} = \dfrac{252}{44.6}
$$

$$
E_c^{Reuss} \approx 5.65 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Practical Significance (1 mark):** Voigt gives the **upper bound** (best-case loading along fibers); Reuss gives the **lower bound** (worst-case loading perpendicular to fibers). Experimental values for well-bonded unidirectional composites typically fall between these two bounds. For transverse loading or short fibers, the Reuss model is more realistic. The wide spread (5.65 vs. 30.9 GPa) underscores the importance of **fiber orientation control** in real engineering designs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss, with suitable examples, the application of polymer blends in the **automotive** and **packaging** industries. Highlight the specific property advantages achieved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **critical fiber volume fraction**? Derive an expression for $V_f^{crit}$ for a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite. A glass/epoxy composite has matrix ultimate strength $\sigma_{mu}$ = 45 MPa, fiber ultimate strength $\sigma_{fu}$ = 2.5 GPa, matrix failure strain $\epsilon_{mu}$ = 0.025, and fiber modulus $E_f$ = 72 GPa. Compute $V_f^{crit}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Polymer Blends in Automotive and Packaging (7 marks):**

**Automotive — 3.5 marks:**

- **PP/EPDM (TPO) blends**: Used for bumper fascias, dashboard skins, and door panels. Property advantages: high notched Izod impact strength (>500 J/m at room temperature, retained even at $-30$ °C), excellent UV stability, paintability without primer, and recyclability.
- **PA/PPO blends** (e.g., Noryl® GTX): Used for automotive exterior body panels (fenders, mirror housings). Advantages: high heat distortion temperature ($\sim$ 190 °C), low coefficient of thermal expansion, and online paint compatibility.
- **PC/ABS blends** (e.g., Bayblend®): Used for instrument panel retainers and center consoles. Advantages: superior impact strength, high HDT ($\sim$ 115 °C), good aesthetics and surface finish.

**Packaging — 3.5 marks:**

- **LDPE/LLDPE blends**: Used for stretch/cling films. Advantages: high tear and puncture resistance, balanced optical clarity, and tunable cling strength.
- **EVOH-containing multilayer structures** (e.g., PET/EVOH/PE): Used for vacuum-packed meat, cheese, and ketchup pouches. Advantages: oxygen transmission rate < 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ extends shelf-life by 2–5x.
- **PLA/PHB blends**: Used for compostable single-use cutlery and trays. Advantages: biodegradability under industrial composting (EN 13432), renewable feedstock origin.
- **PP/Talc composites** (filled, not strictly blend, but related): Used for thermoformed trays. Advantages: enhanced stiffness ($\sim$ 2.8 GPa flexural modulus) at low cost.

---

**(b) Critical Fiber Volume Fraction (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The *critical fiber volume fraction* $V_f^{crit}$ is the **minimum fiber loading** required for the composite to match the tensile strength of the unreinforced matrix. For $V_f < V_f^{crit}$, the composite is *weaker* than the matrix alone and serves no engineering purpose.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
At the critical condition, $\sigma_c = \sigma_{mu}$. The composite fails when the matrix reaches its ultimate strain $\epsilon_{mu}$; at this strain, the fiber stress is $\sigma_f^* = E_f \cdot \epsilon_{mu}$ (assuming fiber remains elastic).

Using ROM under the assumption that matrix fails at composite failure:

$$
\sigma_{mu} = \sigma_{mu}(1 - V_f^{crit}) + E_f \epsilon_{mu} V_f^{crit}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sigma_{mu} V_f^{crit} = E_f \epsilon_{mu} V_f^{crit} \implies V_f^{crit} \cdot (\sigma_{mu} - E_f \epsilon_{mu}) + \sigma_{mu} V_f^{crit} - \sigma_{mu} = 0
$$

Solving yields:

$$
\boxed{V_f^{crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu}}{\sigma_{fu} - E_f \epsilon_{mu} + \sigma_{mu}}}
$$

A more common simplified form is:

$$
V_f^{crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu}}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{mu}^{'}} \quad \text{where } \sigma_{mu}^{'} = E_f \epsilon_{mu}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $\sigma_{mu} = 45$ MPa $= 45 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\sigma_{fu} = 2.5$ GPa $= 2.5 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\epsilon_{mu} = 0.025$, $E_f = 72$ GPa $= 72 \times 10^9$ Pa.

Step 1: Compute the fiber stress at matrix failure strain.

$$
\sigma_{mu}^{'} = E_f \cdot \epsilon_{mu} = (72 \times 10^9)(0.025) = 1.8 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 1.8 \text{ GPa}
$$

Step 2: Substitute into the formula.

$$
V_f^{crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu}}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{mu}^{'}} = \dfrac{45 \text{ MPa}}{2500 \text{ MPa} - 1800 \text{ MPa}}
$$

$$
V_f^{crit} = \dfrac{45}{700} = 0.0643 = 6.43\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A minimum of **~6.4 vol%** of glass fiber is required for the composite to be as strong as the unreinforced epoxy matrix. Real designs use $V_f = 30-60\%$ to provide substantial reinforcement margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical for 14-mark answers):**
> 
> 1. **Units mismatch**: Always convert MPa ↔ GPa before substituting into a formula. Mixing units is the single most common reason students lose 2–3 marks silently.
> 2. **In ROM derivations**, students often forget to state the **isostrain assumption explicitly**. Examiners allocate 1 mark for this; missing it costs you a band.
> 3. **In $V_f^{crit}$ problems**, do *not* use the trivial form $V_f^{crit} = \sigma_{mu}/\sigma_{fu}$ unless the problem explicitly states fiber-matrix strain compatibility. The general form $\sigma_f^* = E_f \epsilon_{mu}$ is almost always required.
> 4. **Naming real commercial products** (e.g., Noryl®, Bayblend®, Toray T800) fetches higher marks in descriptive sub-parts than generic descriptions — examiners interpret it as evidence of wider reading.
> 5. **Always comment** on the practical significance of the numerical result. A bare number without context loses at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for "Technological Application"**

- **Definition**: Technological application = engineering deployment of polymer blends and composites to achieve property windows unattainable by homopolymers.
- **Six Major Sectors**: Automotive, Aerospace, Electrical & Electronics, Biomedical, Packaging, Construction.
- **Four Selection Criteria**: Property Window, Processability, Cost-Performance Ratio, Regulatory Compliance.
- **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)**: $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ — valid for **unidirectional, continuous fibers under isostrain** along fiber axis. Represents the **upper bound**.
- **Inverse ROM (Reuss)**: $E_c = (E_m E_f) / (E_f V_m + E_m V_f)$ — represents the **lower bound**, applicable to **isostress (transverse loading)**.
- **Critical Fiber Volume Fraction**: $V_f^{crit} = \sigma_{mu} / (\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{mu}^{'})$ — minimum fiber loading for composite to outperform the matrix.
- **Density ROM**: $\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$ — used for weight optimization in aerospace and EV battery trays.
- **$T_g$ of Miscible Blends (Fox Eq.)**: $T_g^{blend} = w_1 T_{g1} + w_2 T_{g2}$.
- **Viscosity of Miscible Blends (Arrhenius)**: $\eta_{blend} = \eta_1^{\phi_1} \cdot \eta_2^{\phi_2}$.
- **Key Compatibilizer Examples**: PP-g-MA (for PA/PP blends), SEBS-g-MA (for PA/PBT blends), EPR-g-MA (for PA/PE blends).
- **Commercial Product Mnemonics**:
  - *Bayblend* = PC/ABS
  - *Noryl* = PPO/PS or PPO/PA
  - *Vestamid* = PA12 compounds
  - *Torlon* = PAI (high-temp aerospace thermoplastic)
- **Must-remember ratios**:
  - Boeing 787 = **50%** composite by mass
  - Automotive engineering plastics = **~30%** of total polymer demand
  - Carbon fiber density = **1.8 g/cm³** vs. steel = **7.85 g/cm³**
  - Typical GF loading in PA66 = **30 wt%** = ~16 vol%
- **Weight-to-Volume Conversion**: $V_f = \dfrac{W_f/\rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$.
- **Synergy Principle**: The performance of a well-formulated blend/composite **exceeds** the arithmetic weighted average — this is the *raison d'être* of the entire field.
- **Two Common Exam Traps**:
  1. Confusing *isostrain* (Voigt) with *isostress* (Reuss) assumptions.
  2. Forgetting to convert MPa to GPa in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoplastic Styrene Block Copolymers: Polyester Thermoplastic Elastomers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermoplastic Styrene Block Copolymers: Polyester Thermoplastic Elastomers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Thermoplastic Styrene Block Copolymers (TPS or SBC)

**Formal Definition:**
Thermoplastic Styrene Block Copolymers (SBCs) are a unique class of **A-B-A type triblock copolymers** (or sometimes star-shaped/branched variants) in which the end blocks are rigid **polystyrene (PS)** segments and the mid-block is an elastomeric **polydiene** (polybutadiene, polyisoprene) or its hydrogenated version (polyethylene-co-butylene, poly-ethylene-co-propylene). They behave as **thermoplastic elastomers (TPEs)** because the glassy PS microdomains act as physical crosslinks below the glass transition of polystyrene (~**100 °C**), while the rubbery mid-block provides elastic recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Sub-Types (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> - **SBS** — Styrene–Butadiene–Styrene (unsaturated, original Kraton-type)
> - **SIS** — Styrene–Isoprene–Styrene (used in hot-melt pressure-sensitive adhesives)
> - **SEBS** — Styrene–Ethylene–Butylene–Styrene (saturated, weather/UV resistant)
> - **SEPS** — Styrene–Ethylene–Propylene–Styrene (saturated, premium)

### 1.2 Polyester Thermoplastic Elastomers

**Formal Definition:**
Polyester Thermoplastic Elastomers (TPC-ET in ISO 18064 nomenclature, often called **copolyester elastomers**) are segmented block copolymers composed of alternating **hard crystalline polyester segments** (typically **Polybutylene Terephthalate – PBT**, or PTT/PTN) and **soft amorphous segments** (typically aliphatic polyethers such as **PTMG – Polytetramethylene Glycol**, PEG, or PEO, or sometimes aliphatic polyesters like polycaprolactone). The hard segments crystallize into reinforcing domains, while the soft segments provide elastomeric flexibility, yielding a material that processes like a thermoplastic yet performs like a rubber.

> [!NOTE]
> **Commercial Examples:** DuPont™ **Hytrel®**, DSM **Arnitel®**, Ticona **Riteflex®**, Eastman **Ecdel®**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy for TPS (SBS / SEBS):**
Imagine a chain of **marshmallows (polystyrene)** connected by **rubber bands (polybutadiene)**. Below 100 °C, the marshmallows harden and pin the rubber band at both ends, preventing it from flowing — like a load-bearing "pin." Pull it: the rubber band stretches, the marshmallows hold it back. Heat it above 100 °C: the marshmallows melt, the chain slides and flows — you can remould it. Cool it: the marshmallows re-freeze. **The whole material is recyclable like a plastic but stretches like rubber.**

**Analogy for Polyester TPEs:**
Think of a **brick-and-mortar wall**. The **bricks** are the highly ordered, crystalline PBT hard segments — strong, rigid, and they hold the structure together. The **mortar** is the soft polyether chain — flexible, rubbery, absorbing shocks. When you pull the wall, the bricks stay locked but can rotate slightly, while the mortar stretches. Heat it past the PBT melting point (~**225 °C**): the bricks melt, and you can reshape the wall like clay. Cool it: the bricks re-crystallize. **That is why polyester TPEs feel "leathery" and yet are melt-processable.**

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The unifying theme of *all* thermoplastic elastomers is **hard domains + soft matrix + physical crosslink (no covalent sulfur bridges)**. TPS use **glassy** hard domains; polyester TPEs use **crystalline** hard domains.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why They Behave as Elastomers Without Vulcanization

Traditional rubber is crosslinked by **covalent sulfur bridges (–S–S–)**. These are permanent — the scrap cannot be remelted. In TPS and polyester TPEs, crosslinking is **physical**, not chemical:

1. **Microphase Separation:** The chemically dissimilar blocks (polar vs non-polar in TPS; aromatic vs aliphatic in polyesters) are **immiscible** at the segmental level and tend to phase-separate.
2. **Domain Formation:** Blocks of the same type cluster into discrete **microdomains** (typically 10–50 nm in size).
3. **Domain Locking:** Below a critical temperature (Tg of PS for TPS, Tm of PBT for polyesters), the domains are rigid/glassy/crystalline and act as **anchors** at both ends of every soft chain.
4. **Elastic Recovery:** On deformation, the soft chains stretch, but cannot flow past the rigid anchors. When the load is removed, **entropic recoil** restores the chain — exactly like a covalently crosslinked rubber.
5. **Processability:** On heating past the soft-block/anchor transition temperature, the domains soften/melt, viscosity drops, and the material can be injection-moulded, extruded, blow-moulded like a regular thermoplastic.

### 2.2 Morphology of Styrene Block Copolymers (SBS / SEBS / SIS)

| Feature | Value | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Polystyrene content (by mass) | **20–40 %** (typical) | Below 20 %: weak; above 40 %: stiff plastic |
| Polystyrene domain shape | **Spheres, cylinders, or lamellae** | Depends on volume fraction $\phi_{PS}$ |
| Polystyrene domain size (radius) | **10–30 nm** | Determined by chain length & temperature |
| PS Glass transition Tg | **~100 °C** | Sets upper use temperature |
| Mid-block Tg (PB / PI / EB) | **–90 °C / –60 °C / –50 °C** | Sets lower flexibility limit |
| Molecular weight per block | **10,000–30,000 g/mol** | Controls domain size & entanglement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Domain Geometry Rule:** When $\phi_{PS} < 0.20$ → PS spheres in rubber matrix; $0.20 < \phi_{PS} < 0.40$ → PS cylinders; $\phi_{PS} > 0.40$ → alternating lamellae (and the material is no longer a TPE but a tough plastic).

### 2.3 Morphology of Polyester TPEs (TPC-ET / Copolyester Elastomers)

| Feature | Value | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Hard segment (PBT) crystallinity | **30–60 %** | Reinforcing filler effect |
| PBT melting point (Tm) | **~225 °C** | Upper service temperature limit |
| Soft segment (PTMG) Tg | **–70 to –50 °C** | Low temperature flexibility |
| Hard/Soft ratio (by mass) | **30:70 to 80:20** | Tunable from soft to rigid |
| Shore Hardness range | **D 30 – D 82** | Wide engineering range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Role of Hydrogen Bonding:** The carbonyl (C=O) of the ester linkage on the PBT segment can form **inter-chain hydrogen bonds** with the ether oxygen (–O–) of the PTMG soft segment. This *partial* compatibility prevents macroscopic phase separation and gives copolyester elastomers their characteristic **high strength, high modulus, and excellent fatigue resistance** — far superior to pure SBS systems.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Value | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $M_n$ (block) | Number-average molecular weight of a block | $1 \times 10^4$ to $3 \times 10^4$ | g/mol | Domain size control |
| $D$ (PS domain) | Domain diameter from SAXS | $10$ – $30$ | nm | Mechanical strength |
| $\phi_{PS}$ | Volume fraction of PS block | $0.20$ – $0.40$ | – | Determines morphology |
| $L$ (contour) | Contour length $\approx n \cdot l$ | – | nm | Maximum stretch |
| $E$ (rubbery plateau) | Rubber plateau modulus $\approx \nu_{e} RT$ | $0.1$ – $1.0$ | MPa | Elastic stiffness |
| $\nu_{e}$ | Network chain density (active strands/volume) | – | mol/m³ | Crosslink proxy |
| $T_g$ (Fox eqn) | $1/T_g = w_1/T_{g1} + w_2/T_{g2}$ | – | K | Tg of copolymer |
| $X_c$ (PBT) | Crystallinity of PBT segment | $30$ – $60$ | % | Hard domain strength |
| $T_{m,H}$ | Melting of hard segment (PBT) | $\sim 498$ | K | Process temp |
| $T_{g,S}$ | Glass transition of soft segment (PTMG) | $\sim 200$ – $220$ | K | Low-temp limit |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | $8.314$ | J/(mol·K) | Rubber elasticity |
| $G_N^0$ | Plateau modulus of mid-block | $\sim 0.3$ – $1.0$ | MPa | Modulus prediction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Convention:** The vertical bar `$\vert$` is written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table — the **Fox equation** is written without any pipe.

### 2.5 Why Are They Used in Industry?

- **Footwear soles** (SBS / SEBS) — light, comfortable, recyclable.
- **Hot-melt adhesives** (SIS) — tacky mid-block + stiff anchors.
- **Asphalt modification** (SBS) — improves rutting resistance and low-temp cracking resistance of roads.
- **Automotive interior skins** (polyester TPEs) — leather-like feel, UV stable.
- **Hoses, belts, gears, cable jackets** (Hytrel®) — high strength, oil resistance, fatigue life.
- **Medical tubing** (SEBS, polyester TPEs) — sterilizable, biocompatible, no sulfur residues.
- **3D printing flexible filaments** (TPC-ET) — durable, chemically resistant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating the Polystyrene Domain Size in SBS

The radius of a spherical PS microdomain in a styrene–butadiene–styrene block copolymer can be estimated by balancing the chain-stretching energy of the PS block with the interfacial energy.

**Step 1: Define the geometric variables.**

Let:
- $R$ = radius of the PS spherical domain (m)
- $N_{PS}$ = degree of polymerization of one PS block
- $l$ = bond length $\approx 0.154$ nm (C–C)
- $\gamma$ = interfacial tension between PS and PB domains $\approx 0.003$ N/m
- $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins interaction parameter (PS–PB) $\approx 0.07$

**Step 2: Volume occupied by one PS block.**

$$
V_{block} = \frac{M_{PS}}{\rho_{PS} \cdot N_A}
$$

**Step 3: Number of PS chains per domain of radius R.**

$$
n = \frac{\tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \cdot \rho_{PS} \cdot N_A}{M_{PS}}
$$

**Step 4: Energy balance (Meier–Helfand approach).**

The free energy per unit area of the interface is balanced by the elastic free energy of the PS block stretched to fill the domain:

$$
\frac{3 \gamma R}{N_{PS}} \approx k_B T \left( \frac{R^2}{N_{PS} \, l^2} \right)
$$

**Step 5: Solve for R.**

Rearranging:

$$
R \approx \left( \frac{3 \gamma \, N_{PS} \, l^2}{k_B T} \right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute numerical values.**

Let $N_{PS} = 200$, $l = 1.54 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $\gamma = 0.003$ N/m, $T = 300$ K, $k_B T = 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J.

First, compute the numerator:

$$
3 \times 0.003 \times 200 \times (1.54 \times 10^{-10})^2
$$

$$
= 0.6 \times 200 \times 2.3716 \times 10^{-20}
$$

$$
= 120 \times 2.3716 \times 10^{-20} = 2.846 \times 10^{-18} \; \text{J·m}
$$

Now divide by $k_B T$:

$$
\frac{2.846 \times 10^{-18}}{4.14 \times 10^{-21}} = 687.4 \; \text{m}
$$

Take the cube root:

$$
R = (687.4)^{1/3} \approx 8.83 \; \text{nm}
$$

So **diameter $D \approx 17.7$ nm** — which is in excellent agreement with SAXS experimental values of 15–25 nm for typical SBS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of derivation:** Increasing block length ($N_{PS}$), stronger interfacial tension ($\gamma$), or higher temperature initially swells the domains. But $\gamma$ decreases with $T$ (critical point near 150 °C for PS–PB), so domain size typically *increases* slowly with temperature until the order–disorder transition (ODT).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Glass Transition of a Copolyester TPE using the Fox Equation

For a segmented block copolymer of hard segment PBT (Tg1 = 50 °C = 323 K) and soft segment PTMG (Tg2 = –75 °C = 198 K), find the effective Tg of the bulk material at hard:soft mass ratio 40:60.

**Step 1: Write the Fox equation.**

$$
\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute weight fractions.**

$w_1 = 0.40$ (PBT), $w_2 = 0.60$ (PTMG).

$$
\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.40}{323} + \frac{0.60}{198}
$$

**Step 3: Compute each term.**

$$
\frac{0.40}{323} = 1.238 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{0.60}{198} = 3.030 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Add.**

$$
\frac{1}{T_g} = (1.238 + 3.030) \times 10^{-3} = 4.268 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5: Invert.**

$$
T_g = \frac{1}{4.268 \times 10^{-3}} = 234.3 \; \text{K} = -38.7 \; \text{°C}
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation.**

The effective soft-segment-dominated Tg is **–38.7 °C**, which means the *amorphous rubbery phase* remains flexible down to nearly –40 °C, explaining the excellent low-temperature performance of TPC-ET elastomers.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering use:** The Fox Tg correlates with the **brittle point** of the elastomer. KTU examiners frequently ask this type of "calculate the effective Tg" problem.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Plateau Modulus from Active Network Strand Density

In a styrene block copolymer, every mid-block is anchored at both ends by a PS domain, so the **number of elastically active strands per unit volume** equals the number density of mid-blocks.

**Step 1: Mid-block molecular weight** $M_{PB} = 5 \times 10^4$ g/mol.

**Step 2: Density** $\rho = 0.94$ g/cm³ $= 940$ kg/m³.

**Step 3: Number density of mid-block strands.**

$$
\nu_{e} = \frac{\rho \, N_A}{M_{PB}}
$$

$$
\nu_{e} = \frac{940 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{50000}
$$

$$
\nu_{e} = 1.132 \times 10^{22} \; \text{strands/m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Convert to mol/m³.**

$$
\nu_{e} = \frac{1.132 \times 10^{22}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 0.0188 \; \text{mol/m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Apply rubber elasticity theory (small-strain shear modulus).**

$$
G = \nu_{e} R T
$$

At $T = 298$ K:

$$
G = 0.0188 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 46.6 \; \text{kPa} \approx 0.047 \; \text{MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the rubbery plateau:** The shear modulus is independent of strain for small deformations because every mid-block contributes one network strand. The plateau extends from above the mid-block Tg up to ~100 °C (PS Tg), at which point the PS domains soften and modulus drops 2–3 decades — this is the **thermoplastic window**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Property | SBS | SIS | SEBS | TPC-ET (Polyester TPE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hard segment type | Polystyrene (glassy) | Polystyrene (glassy) | Polystyrene (glassy) | PBT (crystalline) |
| Soft segment type | Polybutadiene | Polyisoprene | Poly(ethylene-co-butylene) | PTMG (polyether) |
| Mid-block Tg (°C) | –90 | –60 | –50 | –70 to –50 |
| Hard Tg / Tm (°C) | 100 (Tg) | 100 (Tg) | 100 (Tg) | 225 (Tm) |
| UV / ozone resistance | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Service temp (°C) | –50 to 75 | –50 to 70 | –50 to 130 | –40 to 150 |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 5 – 30 | 5 – 25 | 10 – 40 | 25 – 50 |
| Elongation at break (%) | 600 – 800 | 600 – 1200 | 500 – 700 | 350 – 700 |
| Hardness (Shore) | A 30 – A 90 | A 25 – A 80 | A 30 – A 95 | D 30 – D 82 |
| Recyclable | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Key applications | Footwear, asphalt | Adhesives, sealants | Medical, automotive | Hoses, belts, gears |
| Hydrogen bonding present? | No | No | No | **Yes** (key advantage) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: TPS Triblock Copolymer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polystyrene End-Block PS1 - Tg 100C] --> B[Elastomeric Mid-Block - PB, PI, or EB - Tg -90 to -50C]
    B --> C[Polystyrene End-Block PS2 - Tg 100C]
    A --> A1[Glassy Domain - acts as physical crosslink anchor]
    C --> C1[Glassy Domain - acts as physical crosslink anchor]
    B --> B1[Soft Rubber Matrix - provides entropic elasticity]
    A1 --> D[Microphase-Separated Morphology]
    C1 --> D
    B1 --> D
    D --> E[Macroscopic TPE Properties - Processable, Recyclable, Elastic]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Polyester TPE (TPC-ET)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hard Segment - PBT - Tm 225C] --> S1[Soft Segment - PTMG - Tg -70 to -50C]
    S1 --> H2[Hard Segment - PBT - Tm 225C]
    S2[Soft Segment - PTMG] --> H3[Hard Segment - PBT]
    H --> H4[Crystalline Domain - acts as physical crosslink and reinforcement]
    H2 --> H4
    H3 --> H4
    S1 --> S1A[Amorphous Flexible Region - entropic recoil]
    S2 --> S1A
    H4 --> F[Partial Miscibility due to Hydrogen Bonding between C=O of PBT and O of PTMG]
    S1A --> F
    F --> G[Macroscopic High Strength - High Modulus - Chemical Resistance]
```

### 4.3 Phase-Separation & Temperature Window

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Below Soft-Block Tg] --> P1[Both phases rigid - hard glassy material]
    T2[Above Soft-Block Tg - Below Hard Domain Transition] --> P2[Elastomeric window - physical crosslinks intact - TPE behavior]
    T3[Above PS Tg for TPS OR PBT Tm for Polyester TPE] --> P3[All domains soft - viscous melt - processable by injection moulding and extrusion]
    T4[On Cooling] --> P4[Hard domains re-form - physical crosslinks re-established - properties restored]
    P2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> P2
```

### 4.4 Domain Geometry as a Function of PS Volume Fraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[phi PS less than 0.20] --> A1[PS Spheres in Rubber Matrix - soft elastomer]
    B[0.20 less than phi PS less than 0.40] --> B1[PS Cylinders in Rubber Matrix - tough TPE]
    C[0.40 less than phi PS less than 0.62] --> C1[Alternating Lamellae - toughened plastic]
    D[phi PS greater than 0.62] --> D1[Rubber Spheres in PS Matrix - high impact polystyrene HIPS]
```

### 4.5 Functional Flow: Why a TPE Recovers Elastically

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Apply Tensile Strain] --> S2[Soft Mid-Block Chains Uncoil and Align]
    S2 --> S3[Glassy or Crystalline Hard Domains Hold the Chain Ends]
    S3 --> S4[No Chain Slippage - Permanent Crosslink Effect]
    S4 --> S5[Remove Load - Entropic Recoil Restores Coiled Conformation]
    S5 --> S6[Original Shape Recovered - No Permanent Set]
    S1 --> S7[Heat Above PS Tg or PBT Tm]
    S7 --> S8[Hard Domains Soften or Melt]
    S8 --> S9[Material Flows - Injection Moulding Possible]
    S9 --> S10[Cool - Hard Domains Reform - Recycled Product Ready]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**(3 Marks)**
Define a thermoplastic elastomer. How does a styrene–butadiene–styrene (SBS) block copolymer qualify as a thermoplastic elastomer without being chemically crosslinked?

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner Scoring Key):**
A thermoplastic elastomer (TPE) is a polymeric material that exhibits elastomeric behaviour at room temperature but can be processed like a thermoplastic above a certain transition temperature. (1 mark)
In SBS, the two end polystyrene (PS) blocks are immiscible with the central polybutadiene (PB) block and microphase-separate into discrete glassy PS domains that act as **physical crosslinks** anchoring the rubbery mid-block at both ends. (1 mark)
On heating above the PS glass transition (~100 °C), these physical crosslinks soften, the material flows and can be remoulded; on cooling, the PS domains re-harden, restoring the elastic network. Thus SBS behaves as a crosslinked rubber at use temperature and as a melt-processable plastic at processing temperature. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**(3 Marks)**
Differentiate between the hard segments and soft segments in a polyester thermoplastic elastomer (TPC-ET). State the typical Tm of the hard segment.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner Scoring Key):**
In a polyester thermoplastic elastomer (TPC-ET), the **hard segment** is typically polybutylene terephthalate (PBT), which is semi-crystalline with a melting point of approximately **225 °C** and provides rigidity, strength, and a high upper service temperature. (1 mark)
The **soft segment** is typically an aliphatic polyether such as polytetramethylene glycol (PTMG) or poly(ethylene oxide), which is amorphous and rubbery at room temperature with a Tg in the range of **–70 °C to –50 °C**, providing low-temperature flexibility and elastic recovery. (1 mark)
The two segments are linked by ester linkages formed via transesterification during synthesis. The hard PBT segments crystallize into reinforcing domains that act as physical crosslinks, while the soft PTMG segments form the continuous flexible matrix. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with neat sketches the morphology of styrene–butadiene–styrene (SBS) block copolymer. Discuss how the morphology varies with polystyrene content.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the preparation, structure, properties and applications of polyester thermoplastic elastomers (TPC-ET).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Morphology of SBS Block Copolymer**

SBS is a linear triblock copolymer with the architecture **PS–PB–PS**. Because polystyrene and polybutadiene are chemically dissimilar, they microphase-separate into discrete domains of the order of 10–30 nm.

**Drawing description (3 marks):**
```
       [PS sphere domain]   ← glassy anchor
    ~~~ PB chain ~~~        ← soft mid-block
              [PS sphere domain]   ← glassy anchor
   ↕  10-30 nm  ↕
```

**Morphology variation with PS content (4 marks):**

| PS content (wt %) | Morphology | Resulting material behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **< 20 %** | PS **spheres** dispersed in continuous PB matrix | Soft elastomer; low modulus |
| **20 – 40 %** | PS **cylinders** in PB matrix (hexagonal) | Tough, balanced TPE — most commercial SBS |
| **40 – 60 %** | **Alternating lamellae** of PS and PB | Toughened plastic, not a TPE |
| **> 60 %** | PB **spheres** in PS matrix | High-impact polystyrene (HIPS-type) |

The PS microdomains act as **multifunctional physical crosslinks**, and the molecular bridges of PB chains connecting them give rise to a 3-D rubbery network. The whole material is therefore a true thermoplastic elastomer with no chemical crosslinks.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — Polyester TPEs (TPC-ET)**

**Preparation (2 marks):**
TPC-ET is synthesized by a **two-step transesterification polycondensation**:
1. **Transesterification:** Dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) reacts with an excess of 1,4-butanediol in the presence of a titanium catalyst (e.g., Ti(OBu)₄) at ~160–180 °C to form bis(hydroxybutyl) terephthalate and liberate methanol.
2. **Polycondensation:** PTMG (or PEG) soft segment is added, and the temperature is raised to 240–260 °C under high vacuum to drive off 1,4-butanediol and build high molecular weight segmented block copolymer.

**Structure (2 marks):**
The resulting polymer has the segmented structure:
–[–(PBT)–(PTMG)–]ₙ–
with PBT as the hard, crystalline segment (Tm ≈ 225 °C) and PTMG as the soft, amorphous segment (Tg ≈ –70 °C). **Hydrogen bonds** between the C=O of PBT and the ether O of PTMG enhance inter-phase adhesion.

**Properties (1.5 marks):**
- Tensile strength 25–50 MPa; elongation 350–700 %.
- Service temperature –40 °C to +150 °C.
- Excellent resistance to oils, fuels, and chemicals.
- High flex fatigue life; good creep resistance.
- Density 1.1–1.25 g/cm³.

**Applications (1.5 marks):**
- Hydraulic and pneumatic **hoses** (Hytrel® cores).
- **Belts**, **gears**, **cable jackets**.
- Automotive CVJ boots, airbag covers.
- Sports shoe soles, ski-boots.
- Medical tubing (biocompatible, sterilizable by gamma radiation).
- 3D printing flexible filaments.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the structure, properties and applications of SEBS and SBS block copolymers. Why is SEBS preferred for outdoor applications?

**(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of the Fox equation, calculate the effective glass transition temperature of a polyester TPE having 50 % by mass PBT (Tg1 = 323 K) and 50 % by mass PTMG (Tg2 = 198 K). State one engineering significance of the result.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — SBS vs SEBS Comparison**

| Property | SBS | SEBS |
|---|---|---|
| Mid-block | Polybutadiene (C=C unsaturation) | Poly(ethylene-co-butylene) – fully saturated |
| Tg of mid-block | –90 °C | –50 °C |
| UV / ozone resistance | Poor — C=C bonds attacked | Excellent — no unsaturation |
| Oil resistance | Moderate | Better (saturated backbone) |
| Service temperature | –50 °C to +75 °C | –50 °C to +130 °C |
| Recyclability | Yes | Yes |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (extra hydrogenation step) |
| Applications | Footwear, asphalt, toys | Medical, automotive weather-strips, food contact |

**Why SEBS for outdoor use (3 marks):**
The polybutadiene mid-block of SBS contains aliphatic C=C double bonds in the backbone, which are highly susceptible to **ozone attack, UV-induced chain scission and oxidative degradation**. Hydrogenation converts these double bonds to saturated C–C bonds in the ethylene–butylene mid-block, giving SEBS. The saturated mid-block cannot undergo oxidative chain cleavage as readily, providing 5–10× longer outdoor service life. SEBS is therefore the standard for **automotive exterior trims, weather seals, roofing membranes, and cable insulation**.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Fox Equation Calculation**

Given: $w_1 = 0.50$ (PBT, Tg1 = 323 K); $w_2 = 0.50$ (PTMG, Tg2 = 198 K).

**Step 1: Apply the Fox equation** (2 marks)

$$
\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.50}{323} + \frac{0.50}{198}
$$

**Step 2: Compute** (2 marks)

$$
\frac{0.50}{323} = 1.548 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{0.50}{198} = 2.525 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Add and invert** (1 mark)

$$
\frac{1}{T_g} = 4.073 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
T_g = \frac{1}{4.073 \times 10^{-3}} = 245.5 \; \text{K} = -27.5 \; \text{°C}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering significance** (2 marks)
The effective Tg of –27.5 °C indicates that the **soft PTMG-dominated amorphous phase** of the polyester TPE remains flexible and rubbery down to about –27 °C, which is well below normal winter service temperatures. This is the **theoretical low-temperature flexibility limit**; below –27.5 °C the soft segment will vitrify and the material will become brittle. The result justifies the use of TPC-ET in **outdoor hydraulic hoses and arctic-grade cable jacketing**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks (Top 5 KTU Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Drawing a 3-block ladder instead of triblock architecture** — Always draw SBS as a linear chain (PS-PB-PS), not as a crosslinked network.
> 2. **Confusing physical crosslink with chemical crosslink** — Physical crosslinks are **reversible** (heat softens them), chemical crosslinks (sulfur vulcanization) are **permanent**. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for saying "SBS is chemically crosslinked."
> 3. **Forgetting the role of hydrogen bonding in TPC-ET** — This is the single most important reason polyester TPEs outperform SBS in strength and chemical resistance. Omitting it costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong units in Fox equation** — Tg must be in **Kelvin**, not Celsius. A common error is to write $w_1/50 + w_2/(-75)$ and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing domain transitions** — For TPS the relevant transition is **Tg of PS (100 °C)**; for TPC-ET it is **Tm of PBT (225 °C)**. Do not interchange them.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **TPE = Hard Domains + Soft Matrix + Physical Crosslinks.** No chemical crosslinking.
- [ ] **TPS family = SBS, SIS, SEBS, SEPS.** All are A-B-A triblocks with PS end blocks.
- [ ] **Mid-block determines flexibility; PS content determines modulus and morphology.**
- [ ] **Domain geometry rule:** $\phi_{PS} < 0.20$ → spheres; $0.20$–$0.40$ → cylinders; $0.40$–$0.62$ → lamellae; $> 0.62$ → inverted.
- [ ] **SEBS is the saturated, UV-resistant grade** used for medical, automotive exterior, and food-contact applications.
- [ ] **Polyester TPE (TPC-ET) = PBT (hard, crystalline) + PTMG (soft, amorphous).** Hard segment Tm ≈ **225 °C**.
- [ ] **Hydrogen bonding between C=O (PBT) and O (PTMG)** is the key reason for TPC-ET's superior strength and chemical resistance.
- [ ] **Trade names to remember:** **Hytrel®**, **Arnitel®**, **Riteflex®** (polyester TPEs); **Kraton®** (SBS/SEBS).
- [ ] **Fox equation** $1/T_g = w_1/T_{g1} + w_2/T_{g2}$ is a high-yield tool — always use **Kelvin**.
- [ ] **Rubber plateau modulus** $G = \nu_e R T$ — calculate $\nu_e$ from the density and mid-block molecular weight.
- [ ] **Recyclability** is the major engineering advantage of all thermoplastic elastomers over vulcanized rubber.
- [ ] **Hard domains reform on cooling** — this is what enables reprocessing and why scrap is reusable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers (TPU)** are a unique class of linear, segmented block copolymers that combine the high-performance elastomeric properties of thermoset rubbers with the melt-processability and recyclability of engineering thermoplastics. Formally, TPU is defined as a **(AB)n type segmented block copolymer** formed by the step-growth addition polymerization of three primary monomers: 1. A **diisocyanate** (the hard segment precursor, e.g., MDI, TDI, HDI, IPDI) 2. A **long-chain macromolecular diol** (the soft segment, with molecular weight typically between **1000–6000 g/mol**, e.g., PTMEG, PCL, PBA) 3. A **short-chain diol or diamine** chain extender (e.g., 1,4-butanediol, ethylene diamine) The key structural feature is the thermodynamic incompatibility between the urethane-rich **hard segments (HS)** and the polyol-derived **soft segments (SS)**, which drives **microphase separation** and gives TPU its characteristic elastomeric behavior without requiring chemical crosslinking (vulcanization). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TPU belongs to the family of "thermoplastic elastomers" (TPEs). Unlike thermoset rubbers, TPU can be repeatedly melt-processed (injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, 3D printing) and recycled, while still exhibiting rubber-like elasticity up to a service temperature of approximately **80–90 °C**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **brick wall built using two alternating types of bricks**: - **Soft bricks (soft segments):** Long, flexible, springy polymer chains that act like tiny rubber bands — they stretch easily. - **Hard bricks (hard segments):** Short, rigid, polar urethane clusters that act like "hooks" or "anchors" — they hold the structure together by physical association (like Lego studs clicking together). When you pull the wall, the soft bricks stretch while the hard bricks stay locked. When you release, the soft bricks snap back and the hard bricks re-associate. The wall behaves like rubber, but you can also **heat it up to melt the hard bricks apart** and reshape the wall — just like melting and reforming a thermoplastic. **No permanent chemical bonds are broken or formed.** This is exactly how TPU behaves: - **At room temperature:** hard segments act as physical crosslinks (like vulcanized rubber) - **At elevated temperature (above melt temperature):** hard segments disassociate, allowing flow (like a thermoplastic) --- ## 1.3 Block Copolymer Architecture TPU is synthesized as a multiblock copolymer of the **(AB)n** type, where: - **A block** = hard segment (diisocyanate + chain extender) - **B block** = soft segment (macrodiol) The general structural formula is: $$ [-(HS)-(SS)-]_n $$ where **HS** = hard segment (typically MW = 200–1000 g/mol) and **SS** = soft segment (typically MW = 1000–6000 g/mol). > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in TPU:** > - Hard segment content (HSC): typically **30–65% by weight** > - Soft segment glass transition (T_g,SS): approximately **−60 °C to −30 °C** > - Hard segment melting/dissociation temperature: approximately **180–230 °C** > - Shore hardness range: **60 Shore A to 75 Shore D** > - Service temperature range: **−40 °C to +90 °C** --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Three-Component Reaction Chemistry The synthesis of TPU proceeds via a **two-step (or one-shot) addition polymerization** involving three reactive components: ### Step 1: Prepolymer Formation (Macrodiol + Excess Diisocyanate) The long-chain diol (HO–R–OH) reacts with an **excess of diisocyanate (OCN–R'–NCO)** to form an **isocyanate-terminated prepolymer**: $$ HO-R-OH + 2\,OCN-R'-NCO \longrightarrow OCN-R'-NH-CO-O-R-O-CO-NH-R'-NCO $$ ### Step 2: Chain Extension (Prepolymer + Chain Extender) The prepolymer is then reacted with a short-chain diol chain extender (HO–R''–OH) to build the high-molecular-weight TPU: $$ 2\,(Prepolymer-NCO) + HO-R''-OH \longrightarrow HS-SS-HS-SS-HS $$ The complete polymer chain therefore alternates between rigid **urethane-rich hard segments** and flexible **polyol-based soft segments**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The NCO:OH Stoichiometric Ratio (R-Index):** A critical KTU-relevant parameter controlling molecular weight, hard segment content, and final properties. The R-index is defined as: > > $$ > R = \frac{[\text{NCO}]}{[\text{OH}]} > $$ > > - **R = 1.00 → exact stoichiometry** (theoretical maximum MW, allophanate-free) > - **R > 1.00 (typically 1.00–1.05) → slight excess of NCO** (industry standard; promotes chain extension and improves mechanical strength) > - **R >> 1 → branching / crosslinking via allophanate or biuret formation** (yields thermoset-like behavior — undesirable for TPU) --- ## 2.2 Microphase Separation: The Heart of TPU Behavior The single most important concept in TPU physics is **microphase separation (MPS)**. Because the hard segments contain numerous **N–H, C=O, and aromatic groups**, they participate in strong **hydrogen bonding** and **dipole–dipole interactions**, while the soft segments are relatively nonpolar and flexible. This thermodynamic incompatibility drives the system to self-organize into **nanodomains** (typically 5–50 nm in size). **Driving force for MPS:** $$ \chi_{HS-SS} = \frac{V_{ref}}{k_B T}\,\left(\delta_{HS} - \delta_{SS}\right)^2 $$ where: - $\chi_{HS-SS}$ = Flory–Huggins interaction parameter between HS and SS - $V_{ref}$ = reference molar volume - $\delta_{HS}, \delta_{SS}$ = Hildebrand solubility parameters of hard and soft segments - $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant - $T$ = absolute temperature For typical TPU systems, $\delta_{HS} \approx 22\text{–}24\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$ and $\delta_{SS} \approx 17\text{–}19\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$, giving a **large positive $\chi$** that drives strong phase separation. However, complete separation is kinetically trapped, and **partial mixing at the interface** (interphase) is always present. > [!NOTE] > **The Flory–Huggins criterion for microphase separation in block copolymers:** > > $$ > \chi N > 10.5 > $$ > > where $N$ is the total degree of polymerization. For TPU, this condition is easily satisfied due to the strong polarity of urethane linkages. --- ## 2.3 Hard Segment Domain: The Physical Crosslink The hard segment domains are **semi-crystalline or glassy** at room temperature, and they are held together by a three-dimensional network of **hydrogen bonds** between N–H (donor) and C=O (acceptor) groups. The geometry is shown below: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{N–H} \cdots \text{O=C} \\ \downarrow \text{hydrogen bond} \\ \text{N–H} \cdots \text{O=C} \end{aligned} $$ These hydrogen-bonded hard domains act as: - **Multifunctional physical crosslinks** (replacing sulfur crosslinks in conventional rubber) - **Filler-like reinforcing particles** (similar to carbon black in natural rubber) - **Load-bearing junctions** that transfer stress between soft segments When heated above the hard segment dissociation temperature (~180 °C), the hydrogen bonds break, the domains "melt," and the polymer flows. Upon cooling, hydrogen bonds reform, restoring elasticity. This reversibility is the basis for thermoplastic processability. --- ## 2.4 Soft Segment Matrix: The Elastic Spring The soft segment domains form a **continuous, rubbery matrix** with a low T_g (typically −60 °C to −30 °C). The soft segment dictates: - **Low-temperature flexibility** - **Elongation at break** (typically 300–700%) - **Elastic recovery** - **Chemical resistance** (depending on polyol type) Common soft segment types and their trade-offs: | Soft Segment | Chemistry | T_g (°C) | Strengths | Weaknesses | |---|---|---|---|---| | **PTMEG** (Polytetramethylene ether glycol) | Polyether | −75 | Excellent hydrolytic stability, low T_g, dynamic properties | Cost, UV sensitivity | | **PCL** (Polycaprolactone) | Polyester | −60 | High tensile strength, oil/solvent resistance | Poor hydrolytic stability | | **PBA** (Polybutylene adipate) | Polyester | −55 | Good balance, low cost | Moderate hydrolysis resistance | | **PHC** (Polyhexylene carbonate) | Polycarbonate | −50 | Excellent UV/hydrolytic resistance, low yellowing | Higher cost, lower elasticity | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | R-index (stoichiometry) | $R = \dfrac{[\text{NCO}]}{[\text{OH}]}$ | Controls MW, hard segment content, branching | | 2 | Hard Segment Content (wt%) | $\text{HSC} = \dfrac{W_{HS}}{W_{HS} + W_{SS}} \times 100$ | Stiffness, hardness, modulus tuning | | 3 | Flory–Huggins interaction parameter | $\chi_{HS-SS} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{k_B T}\left(\delta_{HS} - \delta_{SS}\right)^2$ | Predicts microphase separation | | 4 | MPS criterion | $\chi N > 10.5$ | Threshold for domain formation | | 5 | Average hard segment length (units) | $\overline{n}_{HS} = \dfrac{M_{n,HS}}{M_{0,HS}}$ | Domain crystallinity & T_m | | 6 | Average soft segment length (units) | $\overline{n}_{SS} = \dfrac{M_{n,SS}}{M_{0,SS}}$ | Matrix T_g, flexibility | | 7 | Theoretical Carothers MW | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1 + R}{1 + R - 2R\,p}$ | Predicts achievable MW from conversion p | | 8 | Density (amorphous vs. semi-crystalline) | $\rho_{TPU} = 1.10\text{–}1.25\ \text{g/cm}^3$ | Influences melt viscosity & cost | | 9 | Service temperature range | $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $+90\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Application window | | 10 | Hardness range | $60\ \text{Shore A} \to 75\ \text{Shore D}$ | Product grade selection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Numerical Memory Aid for KTU exams:** > - HS density of H-bonds ≈ **1 H-bond per urethane unit (N–H···O=C)** > - Soft segment MW must be **>>** hard segment MW for elastomeric behavior > - If **HSC > 65%** → material behaves as rigid thermoplastic (not elastomer) > - If **HSC < 25%** → material behaves as soft gel (poor mechanical strength) --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Processing Methods | Method | Temperature Window | Typical Products | |---|---|---| | **Injection Molding** | 190–220 °C | Footwear soles, automotive parts, mobile phone cases | | **Extrusion (film/sheet)** | 180–210 °C | Films, tubes, hoses, wire & cable jacketing | | **Blow Molding** | 200–225 °C | Hollow parts, inflatable products | | **Calendering** | 170–200 °C | Conveyor belts, fabric coatings | | **Solution Casting** | RT (DMF/THF solvent) | Medical films, specialty coatings | | **3D Printing (FDM)** | 210–240 °C | Prototypes, custom orthopedic devices | | **Melt Coating** | 180–200 °C | Textile laminates, adhesive layers | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculation of Hard Segment Content (HSC) from Monomer Formulation A TPU is formulated by reacting **1.00 mol of PTMEG (M_n = 2000 g/mol)**, **3.50 mol of MDI (M = 250 g/mol)**, and **2.50 mol of 1,4-butanediol (BDO, M = 90 g/mol)**. **Step 1: Identify the boundary of the hard segment.** The hard segment consists of **(MDI + BDO)** repeating units, while the soft segment is **PTMEG**. **Step 2: Determine moles of each component contributing to the hard segment.** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{MDI} &= 3.50\ \text{mol} \\ n_{BDO} &= 2.50\ \text{mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Calculate the mass of hard segment (MDI + BDO).** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{MDI} &= n_{MDI} \times M_{MDI} = 3.50 \times 250 = 875\ \text{g} \\ W_{BDO} &= n_{BDO} \times M_{BDO} = 2.50 \times 90 = 225\ \text{g} \\ W_{HS} &= W_{MDI} + W_{BDO} = 875 + 225 = 1100\ \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Calculate the mass of soft segment (PTMEG).** $$ W_{SS} = n_{PTMEG} \times M_{n,PTMEG} = 1.00 \times 2000 = 2000\ \text{g} $$ **Step 5: Calculate the total mass and HSC.** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{total} &= W_{HS} + W_{SS} = 1100 + 2000 = 3100\ \text{g} \\ \text{HSC} &= \frac{W_{HS}}{W_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{1100}{3100} \times 100 = 35.48\ \% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** With HSC ≈ 35.5%, this TPU falls within the standard elastomeric range (25–50%) and is expected to behave as a flexible, soft-grade elastomer (approximately 80–85 Shore A). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stoichiometric R-Index from a Given Formulation For the same formulation: 1.00 mol PTMEG, 3.50 mol MDI, 2.50 mol BDO. **Step 1: Identify OH groups and NCO groups.** PTMEG has 2 OH groups per molecule (diol): $$ [\text{OH}]_{total} = (1.00 \times 2) + (2.50 \times 2) = 2.00 + 5.00 = 7.00\ \text{mol} $$ MDI has 2 NCO groups per molecule: $$ [\text{NCO}]_{total} = 3.50 \times 2 = 7.00\ \text{mol} $$ **Step 2: Calculate R-index.** $$ R = \frac{[\text{NCO}]}{[\text{OH}]} = \frac{7.00}{7.00} = 1.00 $$ **Step 3: Interpretation.** With R = 1.00, the formulation is at **exact stoichiometric equivalence**, leading to the theoretical maximum molecular weight. In industrial practice, R is usually pushed slightly above 1.00 (e.g., 1.01–1.03) to compensate for trace moisture and to suppress side reactions. > [!NOTE] > **Note on Carothers Equation:** The number-average degree of polymerization at any conversion $p$ is: > > $$ > \overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + R}{1 + R - 2Rp} > $$ > > At $R = 1$ and $p \to 1$, $\overline{X}_n \to \infty$ (gelation at exact stoichiometry). Industrial TPUs are made with R slightly < 1 (e.g., 0.995–0.999) to **avoid gelation while still achieving high MW** (>100,000 g/mol). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Theoretical Microphase Separation Criterion For a TPU with total degree of polymerization $N = 800$ and Flory–Huggins parameter $\chi = 0.06$ at 298 K: **Step 1: Apply the MPS criterion $\chi N > 10.5$.** $$ \chi N = 0.06 \times 800 = 48 $$ **Step 2: Compare with threshold.** $$ 48 >> 10.5 $$ **Step 3: Conclusion.** The product $\chi N = 48$ is approximately **4.6× the threshold**, confirming strong thermodynamic driving force for microphase separation. Nanodomain morphology is therefore guaranteed in this TPU. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Estimation of Domain Spacing (d) from Block Lengths The domain spacing in a strongly segregated block copolymer can be approximated by the strong-segregation limit (SSL) scaling: $$ d \propto a \cdot N^{2/3} \cdot \chi^{1/6} $$ where $a$ is the statistical segment length (~0.5–0.7 nm for polyurethanes). For $N = 800$, $a = 0.6\ \text{nm}$, $\chi = 0.06$: $$ \begin{aligned} d &\approx 0.6 \times 800^{2/3} \times 0.06^{1/6} \\ &\approx 0.6 \times 86.2 \times 0.652 \\ &\approx 33.7\ \text{nm} \end{aligned} $$ This 30–35 nm domain size is typical of TPU and is consistent with SAXS (small-angle X-ray scattering) experimental values reported in the literature. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Structure–Property Relationships | Design Variable | Effect on Hardness | Effect on Tensile Strength | Effect on Elongation | Effect on Melt Viscosity | |---|---|---|---|---| | ↑ HSC (hard segment ↑) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | | ↑ Soft segment MW | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (entanglements) | | ↑ R-index (>1) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases (branching) | | Switching SS from polyether to polyester | ↑ Slight increase | ↑ Increases | ↓ Slight decrease | ↑ Increases | | Switching CE from diol to diamine | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases (urea more ordered) | | Use of aliphatic MDI vs. aromatic MDI | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | | Adding chain extender (BDO) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | --- ## 3.6 Comparative Engineering Analysis: TPU vs. Other TPEs | Property | TPU | TPS (Styrenic) | TPO (Olefinic) | TPV (Vulcanizate) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Hardness range (Shore A) | 60–95 | 20–95 | 60–95 | 40–95 | | Tensile strength (MPa) | 25–60 | 5–30 | 5–25 | 5–25 | | Elongation (%) | 300–700 | 500–800 | 200–500 | 250–600 | | Service temperature (°C) | −40 to +90 | −50 to +70 | −40 to +100 | −40 to +135 | | Oil/solvent resistance | **Excellent** | Poor | Poor | Good | | Abrasion resistance | **Excellent** | Fair | Good | Good | | Recyclability | Yes | Yes | Yes | Limited | | Cost (relative) | High | Low | Low | Medium | | Key application | Footwear, hoses, films | Adhesives, grips | Automotive bumpers | Seals, gaskets | **Key insight:** TPU is the **only TPE that combines high mechanical strength, excellent abrasion resistance, and good oil resistance** — making it irreplaceable in high-performance engineering applications. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TPU Synthesis & Morphology ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Macrodiol

of MDI] B -.Optional pi-pi stacking.-> E style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow: Industrial TPU Production ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Raw Material Dosing] --> A2[Step 1: Prepolymer Reactor

They are formed by step-growth addition polymerization of the following three monomers: 1. **Diisocyanate** (e.g., MDI, TDI) — hard segment precursor 2. **Long-chain macrodiol** (e.g., PTMEG, PCL, MW = 1000–6000 g/mol) — soft segment precursor 3. **Short-chain diol or diamine chain extender** (e.g., 1,4-butanediol, ethylene diamine) — completes the hard segment The hard and soft segments are thermodynamically incompatible and undergo **microphase separation** to form nanodomains, where the hard segment domains act as physical crosslinks and the soft segment matrix provides elasticity. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Q: Explain the phenomenon of microphase separation in TPU. Why is it essential for elastomeric behavior?** **Model Answer:** Microphase separation (MPS) is the self-organization of the segmented TPU block copolymer into nanometer-scale domains due to the thermodynamic incompatibility between the polar, rigid **hard segments (HS)** and the nonpolar, flexible **soft segments (SS)**. The driving force is quantified by the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi_{HS-SS}$, which is large and positive for TPU systems. **Why MPS is essential for elastomeric behavior:** 1. **Hard segment domains act as multifunctional physical crosslinks**, holding the soft matrix together — replacing the chemical sulfur crosslinks of conventional rubber. 2. **Soft segment matrix provides rubber-like elasticity** with low T_g (−60 °C to −30 °C), allowing large reversible deformation. 3. **Physical crosslinks are thermally reversible** — they break on heating (above ~180 °C) and reform on cooling, giving TPU its thermoplastic processability. 4. **Without MPS**, TPU would behave as a viscous, weak, non-elastic material (e.g., a homopolymer blend or random copolymer). --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the synthesis of TPU by the two-step (prepolymer) process. Explain the role of each monomer. (7 Marks) **(b)** A TPU is formulated using 1.50 mol of PTMEG (M_n = 1000 g/mol), 4.00 mol of MDI (M = 250 g/mol), and 2.50 mol of 1,4-butanediol (M = 90 g/mol). Calculate (i) the hard segment content (HSC), (ii) the R-index, and (iii) comment on the expected hardness grade and elastomeric character. (7 Marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) — Synthesis of TPU by Two-Step Process **Step 1: Prepolymer Formation** A long-chain macrodiol (e.g., PTMEG) is reacted with an **excess of diisocyanate (e.g., MDI)** at 80–110 °C under nitrogen atmosphere. This produces an **isocyanate-terminated prepolymer**: $$ HO-R-OH + 2\,OCN-R'-NCO \rightarrow OCN-R'-NH-CO-O-R-O-CO-NH-R'-NCO $$ **Step 2: Chain Extension** The prepolymer is then reacted with a short-chain diol (e.g., 1,4-butanediol) at 100–130 °C to build the high-molecular-weight segmented TPU: $$ 2\,(OCN\text{-}HS\text{-}NCO) + HO\text{-}R''\text{-}OH \rightarrow \text{TPU segmented block copolymer} $$ **Roles of each monomer:** | Monomer | Role | Chemistry | |---|---|---| | Macrodiol (PTMEG) | Provides flexible soft segment | Polyether/polyester diol of MW 1000–6000 | | Diisocyanate (MDI) | Provides rigid, polar hard segment core | Aromatic/aliphatic NCO groups | | Chain extender (BDO) | Extends hard segment length, controls HSC | Short diol or diamine | **Sketch of the prepolymer process:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Macrodiol HO-R-OH
MW 1000-6000] --> B[Step 1
Excess MDI
80-110 C, N2] C[Excess Diisocyanate OCN-R-NCO] --> B B --> D[Isocyanate-Terminated Prepolymer
OCN-HS-NCO] D --> E[Step 2
Chain Extender
100-130 C] F[Chain Extender HO-R'-OH] --> E E --> G[Segmented TPU
ABABAB alternating structure] style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` #### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation **Given:** - 1.50 mol PTMEG (M_n = 1000 g/mol) - 4.00 mol MDI (M = 250 g/mol) - 2.50 mol BDO (M = 90 g/mol) **(i) Hard Segment Content (HSC):** The hard segment consists of MDI + BDO. $$ \begin{aligned} W_{MDI} &= 4.00 \times 250 = 1000\ \text{g} \\ W_{BDO} &= 2.50 \times 90 = 225\ \text{g} \\ W_{HS} &= 1000 + 225 = 1225\ \text{g} \\ W_{SS} &= 1.50 \times 1000 = 1500\ \text{g} \\ W_{total} &= 1225 + 1500 = 2725\ \text{g} \\ \text{HSC} &= \frac{1225}{2725} \times 100 = 44.95\% \approx 45.0\% \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) R-Index:** $$ \begin{aligned} [\text{OH}]_{total} &= (1.50 \times 2) + (2.50 \times 2) = 3.00 + 5.00 = 8.00\ \text{mol} \\ [\text{NCO}]_{total} &= 4.00 \times 2 = 8.00\ \text{mol} \\ R &= \frac{[\text{NCO}]}{[\text{OH}]} = \frac{8.00}{8.00} = 1.00 \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Comment on hardness and elastomeric character:** With HSC ≈ 45% and R = 1.00: - The TPU falls **squarely in the elastomeric range** (HSC between 30–50% is optimal for elastomers). - Expected **Shore A hardness**: approximately **85–90 Shore A** (medium-soft grade). - The polymer will have **good elastic recovery**, **moderate to high tensile strength (~40–50 MPa)**, and **elongation at break of 400–600%**. - R = 1.00 is the theoretical stoichiometric optimum, but in industrial practice R is slightly < 1.00 to prevent gelation and side reactions. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Discuss the phenomenon of microphase separation in TPU. With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the role of hard and soft segment domains in determining mechanical and thermal properties. (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare TPU with thermoset rubber and other thermoplastic elastomers (TPS, TPO) with respect to: (i) processability, (ii) mechanical properties, (iii) oil and abrasion resistance, and (iv) recyclability. (7 Marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) — Microphase Separation in TPU **Phenomenon:** Microphase separation (MPS) in TPU is the thermodynamic self-organization of the segmented block copolymer into nanometer-scale domains, driven by the strong incompatibility (large positive $\chi_{HS-SS}$) between the polar hard segments and the nonpolar soft segments. **Labeled Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HSD[Hard Segment Domain - Reinforcing Phase] H1[Urethane Group 1
H-bonded] H2[Urethane Group 2
H-bonded] H3[Urethane Group 3
H-bonded] end subgraph SSM[Soft Segment Matrix - Continuous Phase] S1[Soft Segment Chain
T_g = -60 C] S2[Soft Segment Chain
T_g = -60 C] end HSD --- SSM style HSD fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style SSM fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` **Roles in properties:** - **Hard segment domains** (5–50 nm, semi-crystalline or glassy, H-bonded): - Act as **multifunctional physical crosslinks** - Provide **modulus, hardness, and tensile strength** - Determine the **upper service temperature** (~90 °C for typical TPU) - Reinforce the soft matrix similar to filler particles - Responsible for **melt processability** (dissociate on heating) - **Soft segment matrix** (continuous rubbery phase): - Provides **low-temperature flexibility** (T_g < −30 °C) - Determines **elongation at break** and **elastic recovery** - Contributes to **chemical resistance** (depending on polyol type) - Lowers the material's density and improves impact resistance The interplay between these two phases is what gives TPU its **unique combination of rubber-like elasticity, plastic-like processability, and engineering-grade mechanical performance**. #### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis | Property | TPU | Thermoset Rubber (EPDM, NBR) | TPS (SBS/SEBS) | TPO (TPE-O) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **(i) Processability** | Melt-processable (injection, extrusion, blow, 3D print) — **Excellent** | Cannot be remelted; requires vulcanization — **Poor** | Easily melt-processed — **Excellent** | Easily melt-processed — **Excellent** | | **(ii) Mechanical Strength** | **Very high** (25–60 MPa tensile) | High (10–30 MPa) | Low to medium (5–30 MPa) | Medium (5–25 MPa) | | **(iii) Oil/Abrasion Resistance** | **Outstanding** (best among TPEs) | Good (depends on type) | Poor (swell in oil) | Poor to fair | | **(iv) Recyclability** | **Yes** — full thermoplastic recyclability | No — thermoset (crosslinked) | Yes | Yes | **Conclusion:** - TPU occupies a **unique niche** as the **only melt-processable elastomer with rubber-like strength AND oil/abrasion resistance**. - It is preferred over thermoset rubber when **design flexibility, recyclability, and rapid processing** are required. - Compared to TPS and TPO, TPU has **superior mechanical properties and chemical resistance** but at a higher cost. --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in TPU Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing hard segment with chain extender alone.** The hard segment = diisocyanate + chain extender. Marks will be deducted if the student only counts the chain extender. > 2. **Wrong units in HSC calculation.** HSC must be in **wt%** (not mol%) and should be expressed to **two decimal places** for full credit. > 3. **Forgetting the role of microphase separation.** Many students describe TPU structure but fail to mention **microphase separation** as the source of elastomeric behavior — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. > 4. **Using R-index incorrectly.** R = [NCO]/[OH] is on a **functional group (molar) basis**, not a molecular basis. Multiply by functionality first. > 5. **Drawing the synthesis scheme as a single step.** The two-step (prepolymer) process must show both the prepolymer formation and the chain extension as **two distinct reaction steps** for full credit. > 6. **Forgetting to mention H-bonding** in the hard segment — this is the molecular origin of physical crosslinking and a KTU favorite question. > 7. **Not commenting on numerical answers.** After calculating HSC and R, the examiner expects a **qualitative interpretation** (e.g., "HSC = 45% indicates medium-soft grade elastomer"). --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — TPU** > > - **TPU = linear, segmented (AB)n block copolymer** with alternating hard and soft segments. > - **Three monomers:** diisocyanate + macrodiol + chain extender. > - **Hard segment (HS):** diisocyanate + chain extender (e.g., MDI + BDO). Polar, H-bonded, semi-crystalline/glassy. Forms 5–50 nm domains. > - **Soft segment (SS):** long-chain diol (e.g., PTMEG, PCL). Nonpolar, flexible, low T_g. Forms continuous rubbery matrix. > - **Microphase separation (MPS):** driven by $\chi_{HS-SS}$; required for elastomeric behavior; physical crosslinks from HS domains. > - **R-index:** $R = [NCO]/[OH]$; ideal ≈ 1.00; industrial ≈ 0.995–1.03. > - **HSC range for elastomers:** 25–50 wt% (sweet spot ~30–45%). > - **HSC > 65%:** rigid thermoplastic; **HSC < 25%:** weak gel. > - **Synthesis:** two-step prepolymer method (80–110 °C prepolymer, 100–130 °C chain extension). > - **Processability:** melt-processable above 180 °C (HS dissociation); recyclable. > - **T_g of soft segment:** −60 °C to −30 °C. > - **Hardness range:** 60 Shore A to 75 Shore D. > - **Tensile strength:** 25–60 MPa; **Elongation:** 300–700%. > - **Service temperature:** −40 °C to +90 °C. > - **Unique advantages:** best oil/abrasion resistance among TPEs; thermoplastic recyclability; transparent grades possible. > - **Key applications:** footwear soles, automotive hoses/tubing, wire & cable jacketing, films, medical devices, mobile phone cases, conveyor belts, sports equipment. > - **Hydrogen bonding:** N–H···O=C between urethane groups; the origin of physical crosslinking. > - **Common diisocyanates:** MDI (most common, aromatic), TDI, HDI (aliphatic, UV-stable), IPDI. > - **Common soft segments:** PTMEG (best hydrolysis), PCL, PBA, PHC. > - **Common chain extenders:** 1,4-butanediol (most common), ethylene glycol, ethylene diamine (gives urea, stiffer). > - **Carothers equation:** $\overline{X}_n = (1+R)/(1+R-2Rp)$; at R = 1 and p → 1, gelation occurs. > - **MPS criterion:** $\chi N > 10.5$ for block copolymer phase separation. ---
