## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**: *List any three industrial applications of polymer blends and justify the choice of the blend system for each application.*

**Model Answer**:

| Application | Blend System | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive bumpers | **PP/EPDM (80:20)** | EPDM provides rubber-toughened impact resistance (Izod ≈ 50 kJ/m²); PP provides rigidity and moldability at low cost. |
| Laptop housings | **PC/ABS (70:30)** | PC contributes heat resistance (HDT ≈ 115 °C); ABS contributes processability and impact strength (≈ 55 kJ/m²). |
| Fuel tanks | **HDPE/PA (multi-layer)** | HDPE gives moisture barrier and toughness; PA provides hydrocarbon barrier — co-continuous morphology reduces permeation by **>10×** vs. neat HDPE. |
| Cable insulation | **PVC/NBR (70:30)** | PVC provides flame retardancy; NBR adds flexibility and oil resistance. |

(Any 3 with proper justification — **3 marks**.)

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**: *Explain the role of compatibilizers in polymer blends with two examples.*

**Model Answer**:

A **compatibilizer** is typically a **block or graft copolymer** whose segments are chemically identical (or miscible) with each of the blend components. It localizes at the polymer–polymer interface and:
1. **Reduces interfacial tension**, allowing finer phase dispersion.
2. **Improves interfacial adhesion**, enhancing stress transfer between phases.
3. **Stabilizes morphology** against coalescence during processing.

**Examples**:
- **SEBS (Styrene–Ethylene–Butylene–Styrene)** as compatibilizer in **PP/PS blends** — PS block anchors into PS phase; EB block anchors into PP phase.
- **PE-g-MA (maleic anhydride grafted PE)** as compatibilizer in **PA/PE blends** — MA groups react with PA amine end-groups, forming in-situ graft copolymers at the interface.

(**3 marks**: 1 mark for definition + role, 1 mark for first example, 1 mark for second example.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**:

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe with examples the applications of polymer blends in the **automotive industry**. Discuss the morphology-property relationship in at least two blend systems.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A short glass fiber-reinforced polypropylene composite has the following data:
- $V_f = 0.30$, $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 1.7 \text{ GPa}$, $\sigma_f = 2000 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_i = 30 \text{ MPa}$, fiber diameter $d = 12 \, \mu\text{m}$.

Calculate:
1. Longitudinal modulus using the rule of mixtures.
2. Transverse modulus using the inverse rule of mixtures.
3. Critical fiber length.

Comment on whether the fibers used are effective for reinforcement.

---

**Model Answer for Question A**:

**(a) Polymer Blends in Automotive Industry** *(7 marks)*:

Automotive applications exploit **lightweighting, cost reduction, safety, and durability** — all simultaneously achievable via blends.

1. **PP/EPDM (Polypropylene / Ethylene-Propylene-Diene-Monomer)**:
   - **Application**: Bumpers, side trims, dashboards, fascia.
   - **Morphology**: EPDM forms dispersed rubber droplets (typically 0.5–3 µm) in continuous PP matrix.
   - **Mechanism**: Rubber droplets initiate crazes and shear yielding in PP, dissipating impact energy → impact strength increases from ~5 kJ/m² (neat PP) to **>40 kJ/m²**.
   - **Advantage**: Density 0.9 g/cm³, recyclable, low cost.

2. **PC/ABS (Polycarbonate / Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene)**:
   - **Application**: Instrument panels, consoles, headlamp housings.
   - **Morphology**: PC and SAN (from ABS) are partially miscible; SAN forms droplets in PC, while butadiene rubber domains act as sub-inclusions.
   - **Property Synergy**: PC contributes HDT ≈ 115 °C; SAN contributes processability; rubber domains contribute impact resistance.
   - **Resulting Property**: HDT ≈ 105 °C, notched Izod ≈ 55 kJ/m², surface finish excellent.

3. **PBT/PC (Polybutylene Terephthalate / Polycarbonate)**:
   - **Application**: Exterior body panels, connectors, lamp housings.
   - **Morphology**: Partially miscible; co-continuous at certain compositions.
   - **Advantages**: Excellent chemical resistance (PBT) + high heat resistance (PC).

**Justification for using blends in automotive**:
- Weight reduction (15–25%) improves fuel efficiency.
- Part consolidation via injection molding reduces assembly cost.
- Recycling capability supports circular economy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Students often *just list* applications without explaining the *morphology-property link*. **2 marks are reserved specifically for the morphology-property discussion**. Always mention the dispersed phase, matrix phase, and the property each contributes.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*:

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus** (rule of mixtures, **2 marks**):

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

$$E_c = (72 \times 0.30) + (1.7 \times 0.70)$$

$$E_c = 21.6 + 1.19 = \boxed{22.79 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Transverse Modulus** (inverse rule, **2 marks**):

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{(1 - V_f)}{E_m}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{0.30}{72} + \frac{0.70}{1.7}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = 0.004167 + 0.4118 = 0.4159 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_{c,\perp} = \boxed{2.40 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Critical Fiber Length** (**2 marks**):

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$$

$$l_c = \frac{2000 \times 10^6 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 30 \times 10^6}$$

$$l_c = \frac{24000 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^6} \text{ ... careful unit handling}$$

Let me recompute carefully:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i} = \frac{(2000 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa})(12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m})}{2 (30 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa})}$$

$$l_c = \frac{24 \, \text{N}}{60 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = \boxed{0.4 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 4 — Effectiveness Comment** (**1 mark**):

For effective reinforcement, fiber length should be at least $10 l_c$ to $15 l_c$:

$$l_{\text{required}} \approx 10 \times 0.4 \, \mu\text{m} = 4 \, \mu\text{m}$$

Commercial short glass fibers for PP composites typically range from **3 mm to 12 mm** ($3000$ to $12000 \, \mu\text{m}$), which is **>10,000×** the critical length. Hence, the fibers are **highly effective** for reinforcement, and the rule of mixtures applies confidently.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]:

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss in detail the applications of **polymer composites in aerospace and wind-energy sectors**. Include the role of matrix and fiber selection, fiber volume fractions used, and at least two specific material systems.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With a neat sketch, explain the **morphology types in polymer blends** (matrix-droplet, co-continuous, and laminar). For each morphology, give one example application.

---

**Model Answer for Question B (a)**:

**Polymer Composites in Aerospace & Wind Energy** *(7 marks)*:

**Aerospace Applications**:

The aerospace industry demands materials with **high specific strength (strength-to-density ratio)**, **fatigue resistance**, and **temperature stability**.

| Component | Material System | $V_f$ | Why this system? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aircraft fuselage skin | Carbon-fiber/epoxy (CFRP) | **0.55–0.65** | Density 1.55 g/cm³ vs. 2.7 for Al; strength 1500 MPa vs. 500 MPa for Al alloy 2024 |
| Wing leading edges | Glass-fiber/epoxy (GFRP) | **0.40–0.50** | Lower cost than CFRP; impact resistance against bird strikes |
| Interior panels | Phenolic/glass (FST-rated) | **0.30–0.45** | Meets **FAR 25.853** flame/smoke/toxicity regulations |
| Engine cowlings | Carbon-fiber/PEEK (thermoplastic) | **0.50–0.60** | Withstands 180 °C continuous service; weldable, recyclable |

**Wind Energy Applications**:

Modern wind turbine blades (e.g., GE Haliade-X, 107 m long) use composites almost exclusively.

| Component | Material System | $V_f$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blade spar cap | **E-glass/epoxy** (sometimes carbon-glass hybrid) | **0.55–0.65** | Maximum longitudinal stiffness to resist flapwise bending |
| Blade shell | E-glass/polyester or vinyl ester | **0.35–0.45** | Lower cost; good fatigue resistance |
| Hub and nacelle | Cast iron / steel (still metals) | — | Mass preferred over light-weighting for stability |

**Role of Fiber Selection**:
- **E-glass**: Cheapest, good tensile strength (3500 MPa), density 2.6 g/cm³ — used where cost dominates.
- **Carbon fiber**: Strength 4000–7000 MPa, modulus 230–700 GPa, density 1.8 g/cm³ — used where stiffness dominates.
- **Aramid (Kevlar®)**: Density 1.44 g/cm³, excellent impact resistance — used in leading edges.

**Role of Matrix Selection**:
- **Epoxy**: Excellent adhesion, low shrinkage, high T_g (~120–180 °C) — aerospace-grade.
- **Vinyl ester**: Better chemical resistance, lower cost — wind blades.
- **Polyester (unsaturated)**: Cheapest — non-critical applications.
- **PEEK / PPS**: High-temperature thermoplastic matrices — emerging thermoplastic composites.

**Synergy Principle**:
Using the rule of mixtures at $V_f = 0.6$, $E_f$ (carbon) = 230 GPa, $E_m$ (epoxy) = 3.5 GPa:

$$E_c = 230 \times 0.6 + 3.5 \times 0.4 = 138 + 1.4 = 139.4 \text{ GPa}$$

This is **>40× the modulus of unreinforced epoxy**, confirming the extraordinary property boost achievable.

---

**Model Answer for Question B (b)**:

**Morphology Types in Polymer Blends** *(7 marks)*:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Immiscible Blend] --> B[Matrix Droplet Sea Island]
    A --> C[Co Continuous]
    A --> D[Laminar Stacked]
    B --> B1[Minor Phase 5 to 30 percent]
    B --> B2[Used for Impact Toughening]
    C --> C1[Both Phases 30 to 70 percent]
    C --> C2[Used for Synergistic Properties and Conductivity]
    D --> D1[Platelet Like Sheets Stacked]
    D --> D2[Used for Barrier and Damping]
```

**1. Matrix-Droplet (Sea-Island) Morphology** *(2 marks)*:
- **Structure**: Continuous matrix of polymer A with dispersed spherical droplets of polymer B.
- **Composition Range**: Typically when minor phase is **<30 vol%**.
- **Application Example**: **HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene)** — polybutadiene rubber droplets (1–5 µm) in continuous PS matrix. Rubber particles initiate crazes that absorb impact energy; impact strength rises from ~15 kJ/m² (PS) to **>80 kJ/m²** (HIPS).

**2. Co-Continuous Morphology** *(2 marks)*:
- **Structure**: Both phases form continuous, interpenetrating 3D networks.
- **Composition Range**: Typically 30–70 vol% of each phase (often near phase-inversion point).
- **Application Example**: **PE/EVOH blend** for fuel tanks. Co-continuous EVOH phase creates tortuous paths for hydrocarbon permeation, reducing fuel loss by **>90%** vs. neat HDPE.

**3. Laminar Morphology** *(2 marks)*:
- **Structure**: Plate-like layers of one phase stacked in matrix (or alternating layers in co-extrusion).
- **Application Example**: **Multilayer PET/PA films** for food packaging — PA layers provide oxygen barrier, PET provides mechanical strength.

**1 mark for the sketch** (sketch can be schematic — circles-in-matrix for sea-island, two interlocked networks for co-continuous, stacked rectangles for laminar).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Don't confuse HIPS with ABS.** HIPS uses *polybutadiene rubber*; ABS uses a *graft copolymer of butadiene rubber with SAN*. Examiners mark this distinction strictly.
> 2. **In numerical problems**, do NOT mix up **weight fraction** and **volume fraction**. Rule of mixtures uses **volume fraction**. If given weight fraction, convert: $V_i = \dfrac{w_i / \rho_i}{\sum_j (w_j / \rho_j)}$.
> 3. **In the critical fiber length derivation**, units MUST cancel. Many students write inconsistent units (e.g., MPa and mm without conversion). Always express in **SI base units** (Pa, m) for the calculation, then convert the answer to a convenient unit.
> 4. **Don't omit "isostrain" and "isostress" assumptions** when deriving the rule of mixtures — they are mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **For application-based questions**, never just say "PC is used in automotive." Always quantify: "PC/ABS (70:30) provides HDT of ~115 °C and Izod impact of ~55 kJ/m², making it suitable for instrument panels."

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications of Polymer Blends & Composites**

- **Core Idea**: Blends/composites are used because *combined properties exceed any single component*.
- **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal)**: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ — applies when fibers are continuous and aligned with load.
- **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Transverse)**: $\dfrac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{E_m}$ — applies when fibers are perpendicular to load.
- **Critical Fiber Length**: $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$ — fibers longer than ~$10 l_c$ are effective.
- **Blend Morphology–Property Link**:
  - Sea-island → impact toughening (HIPS, ABS)
  - Co-continuous → barrier, conductivity (PE/EVOH, conductive polymer blends)
  - Laminar → barrier packaging, damping
- **Compatibilizers** (block/graft copolymers) reduce interfacial tension and stabilize morphology. Examples: SEBS for PP/PS, PE-g-MA for PA/PE.
- **Key Commercial Blends**:
  - PP/EPDM — automotive bumpers
  - PC/ABS — instrument panels, laptops
  - PBT/PC — connectors, lamp housings
  - PE/EVOH — fuel tanks
  - PVC/NBR — cable insulation
  - PLA/PHB — biodegradable packaging
- **Key Composites**:
  - CFRP (carbon-fiber/epoxy) — aerospace primary structures ($V_f$ ≈ 0.6)
  - GFRP (glass-fiber/epoxy) — wind turbine blades, marine
  - Phenolic/glass — aerospace interior (FST-rated)
  - CNT/epoxy — EMI shielding, structural health monitoring
  - PLA/HA — bone scaffolds (biomedical)
- **High-Yield Constants**:
  - Glass transition of PC ≈ **150 °C**
  - LOI of pure PP ≈ **18%** (limbs as combustible); FR-modified PP can reach **28–32%**
  - Density of CFRP ≈ **1.55 g/cm³**; Aluminum = **2.70 g/cm³**
  - Tensile strength of carbon fiber ≈ **4000–7000 MPa**
- **Examiner's Eye**: Always link *morphology → property → application*; always mention the **rule of mixtures** for composite problems; always state the **critical fiber length condition**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoplastic 1,2-Polybutadiene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermoplastic 1,2-Polybutadiene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermoplastic 1,2-Polybutadiene (1,2-PB or TPB)** is a structurally unique synthetic polymer of 1,3-butadiene in which the addition of monomer units occurs preferentially through the **1,2-addition mechanism**, placing a pendant **vinyl group (–CH=CH₂)** on every other backbone carbon. Its idealized repeat unit is:

$$\text{–[CH}_2\text{–CH(CH=CH}_2\text{)–]}_n$$

It belongs to the family of **vinyl pendant polybutadienes** and is technically classified as a *semicrystalline thermoplastic elastomer precursor* because the pendant vinyl bonds can be thermally processed (thermoplastic) and later selectively crosslinked (elastomeric) through the pendant unsaturation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> 1,2-PB is the *only commercially significant polybutadiene* that is simultaneously a **thermoplastic** and a **reactive (crosslinkable) polymer**. This dual nature is the foundation for its use in *reactive polymer blending* and *toughening engineering thermoplastics* — a core topic under Module 2 of PBPOT604.

---

## 💡 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sticky Notepad Polymer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Spiral Notebook with Reusable Glue Sticks"**

Imagine a long **spiral-bound notebook** (the polymer backbone). On every alternate page, there is a **small adhesive tag** sticking out (the pendant vinyl group).

- The notebook pages can slide past each other when heated → **thermoplastic processability** (extrusion, injection moulding).
- When you apply heat + a chemical activator (peroxide / sulfur), the adhesive tags **bond with tags on neighbouring notebooks** → **vulcanization / crosslinking** to form a thermoset elastomeric network.
- The adhesive tag is also **reactive** — you can write on it, paint it, or attach small molecules (epoxidation, maleation, hydroxylation) to change the surface properties.

This duality is precisely why 1,2-PB is the *Swiss Army knife* of polybutadienes in polymer blends and composites.

---

## 🧪 Why Isomeric Microstructure Matters

1,3-Butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂) is a **conjugated diene** and can polymerize via three distinct microstructural pathways:

| Addition Mode | Resulting Pendant Group | Backbone Character |
|---|---|---|
| **1,4-cis** | –CH=CH– (in-chain) | Cis-double bond in main chain → rubbery |
| **1,4-trans** | –CH=CH– (in-chain) | Trans-double bond in main chain → crystalline |
| **1,2-addition** | **–CH=CH₂ (pendant)** | Saturated main chain + pendant vinyl |

The **vinyl content (1,2-content)** is the **single most important quality parameter** for any polybutadiene, since it controls Tg, crystallinity, reactivity, and end-use.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Quality Metric (Bold):** Commercial 1,2-PB grades are specified by **vinyl content (mol %, 50 – 99 %)**, **Mooney viscosity**, **Tacticity (syndio / iso / atactic)**, and **Crystallinity (% by DSC)**.

---

## 🏗️ Tacticity — The Hidden Variable

Because the repeating unit –CH₂–C*H(CH=CH₂)– contains a **stereogenic (chiral) carbon**, 1,2-PB exhibits **tacticity**:

- **Syndiotactic (s-1,2-PB):** Alternating R / S configuration → highly **crystalline** (Tm ≈ 200 °C, crystallinity up to ~50 %).
- **Isotactic (i-1,2-PB):** All same configuration → also **crystalline** but with a different unit cell.
- **Atactic (a-1,2-PB):** Random configuration → fully **amorphous**, transparent, rubbery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Syndiotactic 1,2-polybutadiene (s-1,2-PB) is the **commercially dominant grade** used in reactive blending. It behaves as a *crystalline thermoplastic* below its Tm and as a *reactive toughener* when its pendant vinyl bonds are activated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## ⚙️ 2.1 Polymerization Mechanisms of 1,2-Polybutadiene

The **1,2-selectivity** of butadiene polymerization is governed by the **catalyst system, solvent polarity, and temperature**.

### A. Anionic / Coordination Catalysis (Industrial)

| Catalyst System | Typical 1,2-Content | Tacticity Control |
|---|---|---|
| **CoCl₂ / AlEt₂Cl** (cobalt-based) | 80 – 99 % | Predominantly **syndiotactic** |
| **Co(acac)₃ / Al(i-Bu)₃ / CS₂** | > 90 % | High syndiotactic |
| **MoO₂Cl₂ / AlR₃** (molybdenum) | 60 – 85 % | Mixed |
| **Ni(OCOCF₃)₂ / AlEt₃ / BF₃·OEt₂** | 70 – 95 % | Mixed / atactic |
| **Nd-based (Ziegler)** | < 30 % | Predominantly 1,4-cis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Catalyst:** Cobalt-based systems (e.g., Co-bis(2-ethylhexanoate) / AlEt₃ / H₂O) developed by *UBE Industries* and *JSR Corporation* are the workhorse catalysts for producing high-vinyl syndiotactic 1,2-PB at industrial scale.

### B. Influence of Process Variables

1. **Solvent Polarity ↑ → 1,2-content ↑** (polar solvents stabilize the η³-allyl complex that favors 1,2-insertion).
2. **Temperature ↓ → 1,2-content ↑** (lower thermal energy suppresses the 1,4-π-allyl rearrangement).
3. **Co-catalyst / Lewis base addition (e.g., PPh₃, ethers, CS₂)** can shift selectivity and tacticity dramatically.

---

## 🔬 2.2 Structure-Property Relationships

### A. Effect of Vinyl Content on Glass Transition

The Fox-type relationship for polybutadiene Tg as a function of 1,2-content (mass fraction w₁,₂):

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_{1,2}}{T_{g,1,2}} + \frac{w_{1,4}}{T_{g,1,4}}$$

where $T_{g,1,2} \approx -15\,^\circ\text{C}$ (syndiotactic) and $T_{g,1,4} \approx -90\,^\circ\text{C}$ (1,4-cis-rich PB).

> Higher vinyl content → higher Tg → stiffer, less rubbery at room temperature.

### B. Crystallinity from DSC

$$X_c \,(\%) = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100$$

where $\Delta H_f$ is the measured heat of fusion (J/g) and $\Delta H_f^\circ$ is the heat of fusion of 100 % crystalline s-1,2-PB (literature value ≈ **60.5 J/g**).

### C. Degree of Crystallinity from Density (Flory-type)

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \frac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}$$

where $\rho_c$ = density of crystalline region, $\rho_a$ = density of amorphous region, $\rho$ = measured bulk density.

### D. Crosslink Density (Pendant Vinyl Curing with Peroxide)

For peroxide vulcanization, the **number of crosslinks per unit volume** (ν) is given by:

$$\nu = \frac{\rho \cdot N_A}{2 \cdot M_c}$$

where $M_c$ is the **number-average molecular weight between crosslinks** (kg/mol), $\rho$ is polymer density, $N_A$ is **Avogadro's number (6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹)**.

The elastic shear modulus in the rubbery plateau is:

$$G = \nu \cdot R \cdot T = \frac{\rho \cdot R \cdot T}{2 \cdot M_c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **KTU high-yield formula**: peroxide loading (phr) is directly converted to $M_c$ and hence to design modulus and hardness of crosslinked 1,2-PB.

---

## 📊 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_{1,2}}{T_{g,1,2}} + \frac{w_{1,4}}{T_{g,1,4}}$ | Fox blend Tg | K | Predict Tg from microstructure |
| 2 | $X_c = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100$ | Crystallinity by DSC | % | DSC quantification |
| 3 | $\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \frac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}$ | Crystallinity by density | — | Quick QC |
| 4 | $G = \nu R T = \frac{\rho R T}{2 M_c}$ | Rubber shear modulus | Pa | Modulus of crosslinked TPB |
| 5 | $\nu = \frac{\rho N_A}{2 M_c}$ | Crosslink density | mol/m³ | Network design |
| 6 | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Intrinsic viscosity–MW | dL/g | MW characterization |
| 7 | $\%\,\text{vinyl} = \frac{I_{910}}{I_{910} + I_{967}} \times 100$ | FTIR vinyl content | % | Microstructure by IR |

> The last equation is a standard **IR spectroscopic method** for vinyl content using the pendant vinyl band at **910 cm⁻¹** and the 1,4-trans band at **967 cm⁻¹**.

---

## 🛠️ 2.4 Reactions of the Pendant Vinyl Group (Reactive Toolbox)

The pendant vinyl group is the **reactivity engine** of 1,2-PB. Common functionalization reactions:

| Reaction | Reagent | Functional Group Introduced | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxidation** | m-CPBA / H₂O₂/AcOH | Epoxide (oxirane) | Reactive toughener for polyester, vinyl ester |
| **Hydroxylation** | KMnO₄ / OsO₄ | –CH(OH)–CH₂OH | Polyol for PU, epoxy curing |
| **Maleic anhydride grafting** | MAH + peroxide initiator | Succinic anhydride grafts | Compatibilizer for PA/PP/PE blends |
| **Sulfonation** | SO₃ / AcSO₃ | –SO₃H | Ionomer membranes |
| **Silane grafting** | Vinyl silane + Pt catalyst | Alkoxysilane | Moisture-curable coatings |
| **Peroxide crosslinking** | DCP / BPO | C–C crosslinks | Thermoplastic → thermoset conversion |
| **Sulfur vulcanization** | S + accelerators | Polysulfide bridges | Classical rubber technology |

> [!TIP]
> **Reactive Blending (KTU Module 2 Core Concept):** When 1,2-PB is melt-blended with a thermoplastic (PP, PE, PA, PS) in the presence of a peroxide, the **pendant vinyl groups of 1,2-PB react with the macroradicals of the matrix polymer**, forming in-situ graft or block copolymers at the interface. These act as **compatibilizers**, dramatically improving impact strength and morphology stability of the blend.

---

## 🏭 2.5 Engineering Utility Summary

| Application Sector | Role of 1,2-PB | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Toughened PP / Nylon** | Impact modifier | Reactive grafting + rubber cavitation |
| **Shoe soles & footwear** | Thermoplastic elastomer | Selective crosslink of pendant vinyl |
| **Wire & cable insulation** | Crosslinkable insulation | Peroxide crosslinking after extrusion |
| **Adhesives & hot-melt tackifiers** | Base polymer | Pendant vinyl acts as crosslink site |
| **Epoxy resin toughener** | Reactive liquid rubber | Epoxidation → epoxy-reactive oligomer |
| **Blends with PVC, PS, ABS** | Compatibilizer | In-situ graft formation during melt mixing |

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 📐 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fox Equation for Tg of Vinyl / 1,4-Polybutadiene Blends

The Fox equation relates the Tg of a random copolymer (or a polymer blend treated as a copolymer microstructurally) to the weight fractions of its components.

**Starting assumption (Fox, 1956):** Free volume contributions of each component are additive in their reciprocal Tg.

**Step 1:** Define the free-volume fraction $f_g$ at Tg as the sum of the contributions from each segment type:

$$f_g = w_1 \cdot f_{g,1} + w_2 \cdot f_{g,2}$$

**Step 2:** Relate $f_g$ to Tg through the standard thermal expansion model:

$$f_g = f_g^\circ + \alpha_f (T_g - T_g^\circ)$$

Substituting into Step 1 and solving for $1/T_g$ (with the assumption that all components share the same $T_g^\circ$ and $f_g^\circ$):

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

**Final, working form:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_{1,2}}{T_{g,1,2}} + \frac{w_{1,4}}{T_{g,1,4}}\;}$$

where all temperatures are in **Kelvin**.

**Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**

A polybutadiene sample has a vinyl content of 80 wt % and 1,4-cis content of 20 wt %. Given $T_{g,1,2} = -15\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_{g,1,4} = -90\,^\circ\text{C}$, calculate Tg.

Convert to Kelvin:
- $T_{g,1,2} = -15 + 273.15 = 258.15\,\text{K}$
- $T_{g,1,4} = -90 + 273.15 = 183.15\,\text{K}$

Apply Fox equation:

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.80}{258.15} + \frac{0.20}{183.15}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 3.099 \times 10^{-3} + 1.092 \times 10^{-3} = 4.191 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_g = \frac{1}{4.191 \times 10^{-3}} = 238.6\,\text{K} = -34.5\,^\circ\text{C}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Increasing the vinyl content from 20 % to 80 % raised Tg by **~55 °C**, shifting the polymer from a low-temperature rubber to a room-temperature elastomer.

---

## 📐 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crystallinity of 1,2-Polybutadiene from DSC

**Objective:** A DSC scan of a syndiotactic 1,2-PB sample shows a melting endotherm with enthalpy $\Delta H_f = 18.2\,\text{J/g}$. Determine the crystallinity.

**Step 1:** Identify the literature value of heat of fusion for 100 % crystalline s-1,2-PB:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ = 60.5\,\text{J/g}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the standard crystallinity equation:

$$X_c = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$X_c = \frac{18.2}{60.5} \times 100 = 30.1\%$$

**Result:** The sample is **30.1 % crystalline** (typical for commercial s-1,2-PB grades).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Always state the reference $\Delta H_f^\circ$ value explicitly in your answer; failure to do so costs marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 📐 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crosslink Density from Peroxide Loading

A 1,2-PB sample ($\rho = 910\,\text{kg/m}^3$) is crosslinked with **2.0 phr dicumyl peroxide (DCP)**. The molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$ is determined by solvent swelling (Flory-Rehner) to be **4.8 kg/mol**. Calculate the crosslink density and the rubbery shear modulus at 25 °C.

**Step 1:** Convert phr to mass fraction (per 100 g polymer, 2 g DCP):

$$w_{DCP} = \frac{2}{102} = 0.0196$$

(not needed for modulus calculation since $M_c$ is given).

**Step 2:** Apply the crosslink density formula:

$$\nu = \frac{\rho \cdot N_A}{2 \cdot M_c}$$

Insert values (in SI units: $\rho = 910\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $M_c = 4.8\,\text{kg/mol}$, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$):

$$\nu = \frac{910 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 4.8}$$

$$\nu = \frac{5.480 \times 10^{26}}{9.6} = 5.708 \times 10^{25}\,\text{crosslinks/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Compute shear modulus at T = 25 °C = 298.15 K, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K):

$$G = \nu \cdot R \cdot T = \frac{\rho \cdot R \cdot T}{2 M_c}$$

$$G = \frac{910 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{2 \times 4.8}$$

$$G = \frac{2.255 \times 10^{6}}{9.6} = 2.349 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} = 0.235\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to Young's modulus (assuming incompressibility, Poisson's ratio $\nu_p = 0.5$):

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu_p) = 3G = 0.705\,\text{MPa}$$

> **Result:** The crosslinked 1,2-PB has a rubbery Young's modulus of approximately **0.7 MPa**, consistent with a soft elastomeric network — ideal for shoe-sole or gasket applications.

---

## 📐 3.4 Derivation 4 — IR-Based Vinyl Content Determination

**Principle:** The Beer-Lambert law in IR spectroscopy gives absorbance $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$. For a copolymer, the concentration of each type of C=C can be related to its absorbance.

**Equation:**

$$\%\;\text{vinyl} = \frac{A_{910}}{A_{910} + k \cdot A_{967}} \times 100$$

where $A_{910}$ = absorbance of pendant vinyl at **910 cm⁻¹**, $A_{967}$ = absorbance of 1,4-trans at **967 cm⁻¹**, and $k$ = ratio of extinction coefficients ($\varepsilon_{967}/\varepsilon_{910} \approx 2.3$ as per ASTM D3677).

**Numerical example:**
- $A_{910} = 0.62$, $A_{967} = 0.18$, $k = 2.3$

$$\%\;\text{vinyl} = \frac{0.62}{0.62 + 2.3 \times 0.18} \times 100 = \frac{0.62}{0.62 + 0.414} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{0.62}{1.034} \times 100 = 60.0\%$$

> **Result:** This sample is a **60 % vinyl polybutadiene**, in the mid-range for general-purpose applications.

---

## 📋 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Microstructure

| Property | Low Vinyl (< 50 %) | Medium Vinyl (50–80 %) | High Vinyl (> 80 %) | Syndiotactic, High Vinyl |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tg | ≈ –85 °C | –50 to –20 °C | –20 to 0 °C | ≈ –15 °C |
| Crystallinity | Amorphous | Semi-crystalline | Semi-crystalline | Highly crystalline (Tm ≈ 200 °C) |
| Processability | Excellent (rubbery) | Good | Fair (stiff melt) | Good above Tm |
| Crosslink reactivity | Low | Moderate | High | Very high |
| Typical use | Tyre rubber | Impact modifier | Adhesives, TPE | Engineering blend compatibilizer |

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*Define thermoplastic 1,2-polybutadiene. Highlight two features that distinguish it from conventional 1,4-polybutadienes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermoplastic 1,2-polybutadiene (1,2-PB) is a polymer of 1,3-butadiene in which monomer addition occurs predominantly through the 1,2-position, yielding a saturated backbone with a pendant vinyl group (–CH=CH₂) on every alternate carbon.
**Distinguishing features (2 marks):**
1. The pendant vinyl group is **reactive** — it can undergo epoxidation, maleation, peroxide crosslinking, etc., unlike the in-chain unsaturation of 1,4-PB.
2. The saturated main chain gives it **thermoplastic processability** and, in syndiotactic form, **crystallinity (Tm ≈ 200 °C)**, which 1,4-cis-PB lacks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*Explain the role of cobalt-based catalysts in controlling the microstructure of 1,2-polybutadiene.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cobalt-based coordination catalysts (e.g., CoCl₂/AlEt₂Cl, Co(2-ethylhexanoate)₃/AlEt₃/H₂O) form an **η³-allyl cobalt complex** as the active species. The geometry of this complex enforces 1,2-insertion of the incoming butadiene monomer, yielding **vinyl contents > 90 %** with predominantly **syndiotactic placement** of the pendant vinyl group. The high syndiotacticity is responsible for the semicrystalline character of the resulting 1,2-PB. (3 marks)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Two sub-parts, internal choice between Question A and Question B.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the various methods of controlling the vinyl content and tacticity in 1,2-polybutadiene. Compare the properties of syndiotactic, isotactic, and atactic 1,2-PB.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A syndiotactic 1,2-polybutadiene sample shows a melting enthalpy of 21.4 J/g in DSC. The density of the sample is 0.913 g/cm³, while the densities of 100 % crystalline and 100 % amorphous regions are 0.965 and 0.890 g/cm³, respectively. Calculate the **degree of crystallinity by both DSC and density methods** and comment on the agreement.

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

1. **Catalyst control (3 marks):** Cobalt-based systems give high 1,2-content (> 90 %) and high syndiotacticity; Mo- and Ni-based give medium vinyl content; Ziegler Nd systems give mainly 1,4-cis. Catalyst geometry dictates the stereochemistry of insertion — chiral Co sites favor syndio placement.
2. **Process variable control (2 marks):**
   - Lower polymerization temperature → higher 1,2-content.
   - Polar solvents (CH₂Cl₂, THF) → favor η³-allyl complex → higher 1,2-addition.
   - Lewis bases (PPh₃, CS₂) → shift tacticity.
3. **Tacticity comparison (2 marks):**
   - **Syndiotactic:** Crystalline, Tm ≈ 200 °C, Tg ≈ –15 °C; rigid, used in reactive blending.
   - **Isotactic:** Also crystalline, different unit cell, less common industrially.
   - **Atactic:** Amorphous, soft, transparent; used in adhesives and coatings.

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — DSC method (3 marks):**
$\Delta H_f^\circ = 60.5\,\text{J/g}$, $\Delta H_f = 21.4\,\text{J/g}$

$$X_c^{DSC} = \frac{21.4}{60.5} \times 100 = 35.4\%$$

**Step 2 — Density method (3 marks):**
$\rho = 0.913\,\text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_c = 0.965$, $\rho_a = 0.890$

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \frac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}$$

$$\frac{1}{0.913} = \frac{X_c}{0.965} + \frac{1 - X_c}{0.890}$$

$$1.0953 = 1.0363\,X_c + 1.1236 - 1.1236\,X_c$$

$$1.0953 - 1.1236 = (1.0363 - 1.1236)X_c$$

$$-0.0283 = -0.0873\,X_c$$

$$X_c^{density} = \frac{0.0283}{0.0873} \times 100 = 32.4\%$$

**Step 3 — Comment (1 mark):**
The two values (35.4 % vs 32.4 %) agree within ~3 %, which is within experimental uncertainty for crystallinity measurements. Slight discrepancy arises from the choice of $\Delta H_f^\circ$ reference and the assumption of two-phase morphology.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the role of thermoplastic 1,2-polybutadiene as a **reactive compatibilizer** in polypropylene (PP) / polyamide (PA) blends. Discuss the chemistry of in-situ graft formation.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 1,2-PB sample ($\rho = 905\,\text{kg/m}^3$) is crosslinked with dicumyl peroxide. After curing, the molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$ is found to be 5.5 kg/mol. Calculate (i) the **crosslink density** (in mol/m³) and (ii) the **shear modulus** at 80 °C. Comment on whether the material behaves as a rubber at that temperature, given that the melting point of the syndiotactic crystalline domains is 200 °C and the Tg of the amorphous regions is –15 °C.

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

1. **Problem in PP/PA blends (2 marks):** PP and PA are thermodynamically immiscible; high interfacial tension leads to coarse morphology and poor impact strength.
2. **Role of 1,2-PB (2 marks):** The pendant vinyl group of 1,2-PB can react with macroradicals of PP (generated by peroxide initiators such as DCP) to form an **in-situ graft copolymer (PP-g-1,2-PB)** at the blend interface. This graft copolymer acts as a **surfactant-like compatibilizer**, lowering interfacial tension and stabilising the dispersed phase.
3. **Chemistry of in-situ graft (3 marks):**
   - Peroxide → RO• radicals.
   - RO• abstracts H from PP backbone → PP• macroradical.
   - PP• attacks pendant vinyl of 1,2-PB → covalent PP–1,2-PB graft bond.
   - Also, 1,2-PB radicals can react with PA's –NH– group (via H-abstraction) → secondary grafting.

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Crosslink density (3 marks):**

$$\nu = \frac{\rho \cdot N_A}{2 M_c} = \frac{905 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 5.5}$$

$$\nu = \frac{5.450 \times 10^{26}}{11.0} = 4.954 \times 10^{25}\,\text{crosslinks/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Shear modulus at T = 80 °C = 353.15 K (3 marks):**

$$G = \nu R T = 4.954 \times 10^{25} \times 8.314 \times 353.15$$

$$G = 4.954 \times 10^{25} \times 2937.6 = 1.455 \times 10^{29}\,\text{(units inconsistency check)}$$

> **Unit correction:** $\nu$ is in crosslinks/m³; $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) expects mol/m³. Convert:

$$\nu = \frac{4.954 \times 10^{25}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 82.3\,\text{mol/m}^3$$

$$G = 82.3 \times 8.314 \times 353.15 = 2.416 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} = 0.242\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Rubber vs plastic behaviour (1 mark):**
At 80 °C, the temperature is:
- **Above Tg** (–15 °C) → amorphous regions are rubbery.
- **Below Tm** (200 °C) → crystalline domains are intact and act as physical crosslinks.

Therefore the material behaves as a **thermoplastic elastomer (TPE)**: physically crosslinked by crystallites, with rubbery amorphous matrix. The calculated G ≈ 0.24 MPa is characteristic of a soft TPE.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** in the Fox equation → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Not stating the reference $\Delta H_f^\circ$** when using the DSC method → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up crosslink density units** (crosslinks/m³ vs mol/m³) → huge mark loss; always check whether you need to divide by $N_A$ before multiplying by R.
> 4. **Not drawing/identifying the chemical equation** for the epoxidation or MAH-grafting reaction → KTU examiners specifically award 2 marks for the structure of the functionalized repeat unit.
> 5. **Confusing 1,2-PB with high-cis 1,4-PB** — many students write "polybutadiene rubber" instead of highlighting the *pendant vinyl* structure. Always draw the repeat unit in your answer.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Thermoplastic 1,2-Polybutadiene**

- 🔹 **Repeat unit:** –[CH₂–CH(CH=CH₂)]ₙ– (saturated backbone + pendant vinyl)
- 🔹 **Vinyl content** = mol fraction of 1,2-added units (typical 60–99 %)
- 🔹 **Industrial catalyst for s-1,2-PB:** Cobalt-based (Co/AlR₃/CS₂ or H₂O)
- 🔹 **Syndiotactic 1,2-PB:** Tm ≈ 200 °C, Tg ≈ –15 °C, semi-crystalline
- 🔹 **Atactic 1,2-PB:** Amorphous, soft, transparent
- 🔹 **Tg increases** with increasing vinyl content (Fox equation)
- 🔹 **Key DSC crystallinity formula:** $X_c = \Delta H_f / \Delta H_f^\circ \times 100$, with $\Delta H_f^\circ \approx 60.5\,\text{J/g}$ for s-1,2-PB
- 🔹 **Crosslink modulus:** $G = \rho R T / 2 M_c$
- 🔹 **IR vinyl content** uses absorbance at 910 cm⁻¹ (vinyl) vs 967 cm⁻¹ (1,4-trans)
- 🔹 **Reactive pendant vinyl** can be functionalized via epoxidation, hydroxylation, MAH grafting, silane grafting, sulfonation
- 🔹 **Crosslinking options:** Peroxide (DCP/BPO) → C–C; Sulfur → polysulfide
- 🔹 **Primary use in blends:** Reactive compatibilizer in PP/PA/PS via in-situ grafting during melt mixing
- 🔹 **Applications:** Toughened thermoplastics, TPEs, footwear, wire & cable, adhesives, epoxy tougheners
- 🔹 **Always convert Tg/Tm to Kelvin** for Fox equation calculations
- 🔹 **Always state reference $\Delta H_f^\circ$** explicitly in DSC crystallinity problems

# MODULE 2: TRANS 1,4-POLYISOPRENE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Trans 1,4-Polyisoprene (TPI)** is a stereoregular addition polymer of isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene) in which the addition across the conjugated diene system occurs predominantly (typically $> 90\%$) through the **C1 and C4 positions**, and the residual **C2=C3 double bond in each repeat unit is locked in the *trans* geometric configuration**.

The chemical structure of the TPI repeat unit can be precisely written as:

$$-\left[\text{CH}_2-\text{C}\bigl(\text{CH}_3\bigr)=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2\right]_n-$$

where the substituents ($\text{CH}_3$ and $\text{H}$) on the **C2=C3** internal double bond lie on **opposite sides** of the double bond axis. This is the key geometric attribute that distinguishes TPI from its cis-stereoisomer, **Cis 1,4-Polyisoprene (CPI / Natural Rubber / NR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 (PBPOT604, Module 2) classifies TPI as a *stereoisomer of polyisoprene* and emphasises the structure–property–application triad. Natural sources include **Gutta-percha** (from *Palaquium gutta*) and **Balata** (from *Manilkara bidentata*).

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a railway track made of identical wagons. If the connecting hooks are bent **in the same direction** (cis), the train cannot be pushed into a narrow tunnel — it keeps buckling. If the hooks are **straight and alternating (trans)**, the wagons form a long, straight, rigid train that stacks neatly into parallel rows.

| Stereoisomer | Hook Shape | Real Material | Packing Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cis 1,4-PI** | Bent hooks (kinked chain) | Natural Rubber (Hevea brasiliensis) | Loose, amorphous, soft, elastic |
| **Trans 1,4-PI** | Straight, zig-zag chain | Gutta-percha / Balata | Tightly packed, crystalline, hard, rigid |

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line intuition:** TPI is to Natural Rubber what dry spaghetti is to cooked spaghetti — the *same chemistry*, but **trans geometry → straight chain → crystalline → hard**, whereas *cis geometry → kinked chain → amorphous → rubbery*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Repeat unit chemical formula:** $\text{C}_5\text{H}_8$ (molecular weight = **68.12 g/mol**)
- **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$):** approximately **$-68^\circ\text{C}$** ($-63^\circ\text{C}$ to $-73^\circ\text{C}$ reported in literature)
- **Melting temperature ($T_m$):** approximately **$+65^\circ\text{C}$** (range $60$–$74^\circ\text{C}$ depending on % trans content and crystallinity)
- **Density (crystalline):** approximately **$1.00\ \text{g/cm}^3$**
- **Density (amorphous):** approximately **$0.91\ \text{g/cm}^3$**
- **Degree of crystallinity (as-polymerised):** **$30$ – $40\%$**, can rise to $> 60\%$ on annealing
- **Refractive index ($n_D$):** approximately **$1.523$**
- **Solubility:** soluble in hot benzene, chloroform, carbon tetrachloride; **insoluble in acetone and alcohols**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stereochemistry of Isoprene Polymerisation

Isoprene ($\text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}=\text{CH}_2$) is a **1,3-conjugated diene** with **two polymerisable double bonds** and **2-methyl substitution**. The four possible structural outcomes of chain-growth polymerisation are:

1. **1,2-addition** – vinyl pendant group on the backbone
2. **3,4-addition** – pendant $\text{-CH}_2(\text{CH}_3)\text{=CH}_2$ group (isopropenyl)
3. **1,4-cis addition** – natural rubber configuration
4. **1,4-trans addition** – gutta-percha configuration (TPI)

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU exam, students are expected to draw all four microstructures and identify the *trans* 1,4-unit on either side of the **C2=C3** double bond with the methyl and the backbone $\text{H}$ on opposite sides.

### 2.2 Why Trans Geometry Yields a Hard, Crystalline Polymer

The crystallisation behaviour of polyisoprene is governed by the **chain conformation in the crystalline unit cell**:

- **Trans 1,4-PI** adopts an **all-trans, zig-zag planar conformation**. The chain is essentially a straight ribbon. This allows adjacent chains to **register** (lattice match) and pack into a **pseudo-hexagonal / orthorhombic unit cell** with strong dispersion and dipole interactions.
- **Cis 1,4-PI** has a naturally **kinked chain** (because the *cis* double bond forces a $\sim 140^\circ$ bend in the backbone). The chain cannot lie flat, and packing is frustrated → **amorphous at room temperature** in the unstressed state.

### 2.3 Unit Cell Parameters of TPI (crystal form $\beta$)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Crystal system | Orthorhombic |
| $a$ | $7.98\ \text{Å}$ |
| $b$ | $6.29\ \text{Å}$ |
| $c$ (fibre axis) | $8.77\ \text{Å}$ |
| Repeat units per cell ($Z$) | $4$ |
| Helical sense | Antichiral, planar zig-zag |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the ones that most frequently surface in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 problems on TPI. **All mark-bearing variables are defined alongside.**

| Equation No. | Formula (LaTeX) | Description & Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| F-1 | $\% \text{ Crystallinity} = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^{\,0}} \times 100$ | DSC-based crystallinity; $\Delta H_m$ = measured melting enthalpy, $\Delta H_m^{\,0}$ = enthalpy of fusion of $100\%$ crystalline TPI ($\approx 105\ \text{J/g}$). |
| F-2 | $X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)}$ | Density-based crystallinity; $\rho_c$ = crystalline density, $\rho_a$ = amorphous density, $\rho_s$ = sample density. |
| F-3 | $\dfrac{1}{T_m} - \dfrac{1}{T_m^{\,0}} = -\dfrac{R}{\Delta H_u}\ln X_A$ | **Flory equation** for melting-point depression by a diluent (e.g., plasticiser or non-crystallisable comonomer). $X_A$ = mole fraction crystallisable repeat unit, $\Delta H_u$ = enthalpy per mole repeat unit. |
| F-4 | $\ln\bigl(1 - X_c(t)\bigr) = -K\,t^{\,n}$ | **Avrami equation** for isothermal crystallisation kinetics; $K$ = crystallisation rate constant, $n$ = Avrami index (typically $2$–$3$ for TPI spherulites). |
| F-5 | $T_g(\text{blend}) = w_1\,T_{g1} + w_2\,T_{g2}$ | **Fox equation** (weight-fraction form) — useful when TPI is blended with another elastomer to form a TPE. |
| F-6 | $T_g(\text{blend}) = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}}$ | **Fox equation** (inverse form), more widely used; $w_i$ = weight fraction, $T_{gi}$ in Kelvin. |
| F-7 | $\Delta G_{\text{cryst}} = \Delta H_{\text{cryst}} - T\,\Delta S_{\text{cryst}}$ | Thermodynamic driving force for crystallisation; at $T < T_m$, $\Delta G_{\text{cryst}} < 0$ and crystallisation is spontaneous. |
| F-8 | $E_{\text{chain}} = \dfrac{3}{2}k_B T \cdot N_{\text{segments}}$ | Entropic elastic energy of a Gaussian chain (relevant when TPI is dynamically vulcanised into a TPE). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In your KTU answer scripts, **always state the units of $\Delta H$ (J/g or J/mol) and convert $T$ to Kelvin** before substituting into Flory or Avrami equations — this is a common 1-mark deduction site.

## 2.5 Comparative Analysis: Cis vs Trans 1,4-Polyisoprene

| Property | Cis 1,4-PI (Natural Rubber) | Trans 1,4-PI (Gutta-percha / TPI) |
|---|---|---|
| Natural source | *Hevea brasiliensis* latex | *Palaquium gutta*, *Manilkara bidentata* |
| Chain conformation | Kinked (cis bend at C=C) | Linear zig-zag (trans) |
| Crystallinity (at RT, unstressed) | Amorphous (low) | **Semi-crystalline ($>$30%)** |
| $T_m$ | $\sim 35^\circ\text{C}$ (on stretching) | $\sim 65^\circ\text{C}$ |
| $T_g$ | $\sim -73^\circ\text{C}$ | $\sim -68^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Hardness (Shore A) | $30$ – $50$ (soft) | $90$ – $100$ (hard, leathery) |
| Mechanical state at RT | Elastomer | **Thermoplastic / rigid plastic** |
| Tensile strength (unfilled) | $20$ – $30\ \text{MPa}$ | $30$ – $40\ \text{MPa}$ |
| Elongation at break | $700$ – $1000\%$ | $5$ – $50\%$ (brittle when highly crystalline) |
| Resilience | High (rebound resilience $> 80\%$) | Low (resilience $< 30\%$) |
| Stress crystallisation | Yes (on stretching) | No (already crystalline) |
| Typical application | Tyres, gloves, condoms | Dental gutta-percha points, golf-ball cores, splints, TPI-TPE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does NR crystallise on stretching but TPI does not need to?** Because NR is amorphous at rest, and the *cis* chains align under strain into a *trans-like* ordered phase. TPI chains are *already* in the favoured trans conformation and pack without any mechanical prompting.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of the Trans Geometry

The fact that TPI is a **semi-crystalline thermoplastic elastomer (TPE) precursor** with a moderate $T_m$ makes it a strategic blending component in:

- **TPI / PP thermoplastic vulcanisates (TPV):** dynamically vulcanised blends yield a hard-elastic material for cable jacketing and automotive weather-strip seals.
- **TPI / NR / SBR blends:** small TPI additions (5–20 phr) increase green strength and modulus of rubber compounds.
- **TPI / SBR / silica tyre-tread prototypes:** improves wet grip and reduces rolling resistance via micro-crystalline domains.
- **Medical applications:** endodontic (root-canal) filling points, surgical splints, prosthetic devices.
- **Sports goods:** golf-ball core (combined with NR and zinc salts), thermoplastic footwear components.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Flory Melting-Point Depression Equation for a TPI Plasticiser System

A common KTU-style 14-mark derivation asks the student to show how a non-crystallisable diluent (e.g., low-MW ester plasticiser or an atactic CPI fraction) depresses the equilibrium melting point of TPI.

**Starting point (thermodynamic equilibrium):** at the melting point $T_m$, the chemical potential of the crystalline repeat unit in the crystal equals the chemical potential of the same unit in the amorphous (diluted) phase.

$$
\mu_u^{crystal} = \mu_u^{amorphous, dilute}
$$

The depression of $\mu_u$ in the amorphous phase is given by the **partial molar free energy of mixing** (assuming Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi$ and treating the diluent as a small-molecule solvent):

$$
\Delta \mu_u^{mix} = RT \left[ \ln \phi_u + \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{x}\right)(1 - \phi_u) + \chi (1 - \phi_u)^2 \right]
$$

For a low-MW diluent, $x \rightarrow 0$ (or large solvent molecules of degree of polymerisation $x$ are approximated as $x \gg 1$), and the term $\bigl(1 - \tfrac{1}{x}\bigr) \approx 1$. Setting $\phi_u = X_A$ (mole fraction of the crystallisable unit) and requiring equilibrium:

$$
\Delta H_u \left(1 - \dfrac{T_m}{T_m^{\,0}}\right) = -RT_m \ln X_A
$$

Rearranging step by step:

$$
\Delta H_u - \Delta H_u \dfrac{T_m}{T_m^{\,0}} = -RT_m \ln X_A
$$

$$
\dfrac{\Delta H_u}{T_m^{\,0}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_u}{T_m} - R \ln X_A
$$

Multiplying both sides by $1 / \Delta H_u$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_m^{\,0}} = \dfrac{1}{T_m} - \dfrac{R \ln X_A}{\Delta H_u}
$$

Therefore the **Flory equation (F-3 in the cheat sheet)**:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_m} - \dfrac{1}{T_m^{\,0}} = -\dfrac{R}{\Delta H_u}\ln X_A
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As $X_A$ (crystallisable fraction) decreases (more diluent or comonomer), $\ln X_A$ becomes more negative, $-(R/\Delta H_u)\ln X_A$ becomes more positive, and $1/T_m$ rises → $T_m$ **falls**. This is exactly why a plasticised TPI flows at a lower processing temperature.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Crystallinity of an Annealed TPI Sample

**Problem (KTU 3-mark style):** A TPI sample is analysed by DSC. The melting endotherm integrates to $\Delta H_m = 47.3\ \text{J/g}$. The literature enthalpy of fusion of $100\%$ crystalline TPI is $\Delta H_m^{\,0} = 105.0\ \text{J/g}$. Calculate the **% crystallinity** of the sample.

**Solution:**

$$
\% X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^{\,0}} \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\% X_c = \dfrac{47.3}{105.0} \times 100
$$

Evaluate the division step by step:

$$
\dfrac{47.3}{105.0} = 0.4504\ldots
$$

Multiply by $100$:

$$
\% X_c = 45.04\ \%
$$

**Answer:** The TPI sample is **$\approx 45\%$ crystalline** — a typical value for an annealed, high-trans synthetic TPI. *(Unannealed, as-polymerised TPI is typically $30$–$35\%$ crystalline.)*

## 3.3 Worked Example: $T_g$ of a TPI / NR Blend (Fox Equation)

**Problem (KTU 3-mark style):** A TPE blend is formulated with $40\ \text{wt}\%$ TPI ($T_{g1} = -68^\circ\text{C}$) and $60\ \text{wt}\%$ NR ($T_{g2} = -73^\circ\text{C}$). Predict the $T_g$ of the miscible blend using the Fox equation.

**Step 1 — Convert $T_g$ to Kelvin (Fox equation requires absolute temperature):**

$$
T_{g1} = -68 + 273 = 205\ \text{K}
$$

$$
T_{g2} = -73 + 273 = 200\ \text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Fox equation (F-6):**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{0.40}{205} + \dfrac{0.60}{200}
$$

Evaluate each term:

$$
\dfrac{0.40}{205} = 1.9512 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.60}{200} = 3.0000 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

Add them:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_g} = 4.9512 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

Take the reciprocal:

$$
T_g = \dfrac{1}{4.9512 \times 10^{-3}} = 201.97\ \text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to Celsius:**

$$
T_g = 201.97 - 273 = -71.03^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Answer:** The blend's $T_g \approx -71^\circ\text{C}$, lying between the two component $T_g$'s and closer to NR (the higher-weight-fraction component). This confirms that the blend behaves as a **miscible elastomeric TPE** at room temperature.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Stereoregularity Controls Property Spectrum

| Stereoregularity Parameter | Effect on TPI Property | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Increase % *trans* 1,4 content ($90\% \to 99\%$) | $T_m \uparrow$ by $5$–$10^\circ\text{C}$ | Higher heat-deflection temperature, better dimensional stability |
| Increase % *cis* 1,4 content (impurity) | Crystallinity $\downarrow$, $T_m \downarrow$ | Softer material, more rubbery behaviour |
| Increase 3,4-addition units (branch points) | Crystallinity $\downarrow\downarrow$ | Loss of mechanical strength; useful for adhesive formulations |
| Increase molecular weight ($M_n$) | Melt viscosity $\uparrow$, modulus $\uparrow$ | Better film-forming and mechanical integrity |
| Add $5$–$20\ \text{phr}$ TPI to NR compound | Green strength $\uparrow$, modulus $\uparrow$ | Easier handling in tyre building, less cold flow |
| Dynamic vulcanisation of TPI with PP | Forms cross-linked TPI domains in PP matrix | True thermoplastic vulcanisate (TPV) with elastic recovery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important learning outcome for KTU Module 2 is the ability to **link stereochemistry → chain conformation → crystallinity → bulk mechanical state (rigid vs elastomeric)**. Every property you write must be traceable back to the *trans* geometry of the C2=C3 double bond.
