## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**: *List any three industrial applications of polymer blends and justify the choice of the blend system for each application.*

**Model Answer**:

| Application | Blend System | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive bumpers | **PP/EPDM (80:20)** | EPDM provides rubber-toughened impact resistance (Izod ≈ 50 kJ/m²); PP provides rigidity and moldability at low cost. |
| Laptop housings | **PC/ABS (70:30)** | PC contributes heat resistance (HDT ≈ 115 °C); ABS contributes processability and impact strength (≈ 55 kJ/m²). |
| Fuel tanks | **HDPE/PA (multi-layer)** | HDPE gives moisture barrier and toughness; PA provides hydrocarbon barrier — co-continuous morphology reduces permeation by **>10×** vs. neat HDPE. |
| Cable insulation | **PVC/NBR (70:30)** | PVC provides flame retardancy; NBR adds flexibility and oil resistance. |

(Any 3 with proper justification — **3 marks**.)

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**: *Explain the role of compatibilizers in polymer blends with two examples.*

**Model Answer**:

A **compatibilizer** is typically a **block or graft copolymer** whose segments are chemically identical (or miscible) with each of the blend components. It localizes at the polymer–polymer interface and:
1. **Reduces interfacial tension**, allowing finer phase dispersion.
2. **Improves interfacial adhesion**, enhancing stress transfer between phases.
3. **Stabilizes morphology** against coalescence during processing.

**Examples**:
- **SEBS (Styrene–Ethylene–Butylene–Styrene)** as compatibilizer in **PP/PS blends** — PS block anchors into PS phase; EB block anchors into PP phase.
- **PE-g-MA (maleic anhydride grafted PE)** as compatibilizer in **PA/PE blends** — MA groups react with PA amine end-groups, forming in-situ graft copolymers at the interface.

(**3 marks**: 1 mark for definition + role, 1 mark for first example, 1 mark for second example.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**:

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe with examples the applications of polymer blends in the **automotive industry**. Discuss the morphology-property relationship in at least two blend systems.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A short glass fiber-reinforced polypropylene composite has the following data:
- $V_f = 0.30$, $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 1.7 \text{ GPa}$, $\sigma_f = 2000 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_i = 30 \text{ MPa}$, fiber diameter $d = 12 \, \mu\text{m}$.

Calculate:
1. Longitudinal modulus using the rule of mixtures.
2. Transverse modulus using the inverse rule of mixtures.
3. Critical fiber length.

Comment on whether the fibers used are effective for reinforcement.

---

**Model Answer for Question A**:

**(a) Polymer Blends in Automotive Industry** *(7 marks)*:

Automotive applications exploit **lightweighting, cost reduction, safety, and durability** — all simultaneously achievable via blends.

1. **PP/EPDM (Polypropylene / Ethylene-Propylene-Diene-Monomer)**:
   - **Application**: Bumpers, side trims, dashboards, fascia.
   - **Morphology**: EPDM forms dispersed rubber droplets (typically 0.5–3 µm) in continuous PP matrix.
   - **Mechanism**: Rubber droplets initiate crazes and shear yielding in PP, dissipating impact energy → impact strength increases from ~5 kJ/m² (neat PP) to **>40 kJ/m²**.
   - **Advantage**: Density 0.9 g/cm³, recyclable, low cost.

2. **PC/ABS (Polycarbonate / Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene)**:
   - **Application**: Instrument panels, consoles, headlamp housings.
   - **Morphology**: PC and SAN (from ABS) are partially miscible; SAN forms droplets in PC, while butadiene rubber domains act as sub-inclusions.
   - **Property Synergy**: PC contributes HDT ≈ 115 °C; SAN contributes processability; rubber domains contribute impact resistance.
   - **Resulting Property**: HDT ≈ 105 °C, notched Izod ≈ 55 kJ/m², surface finish excellent.

3. **PBT/PC (Polybutylene Terephthalate / Polycarbonate)**:
   - **Application**: Exterior body panels, connectors, lamp housings.
   - **Morphology**: Partially miscible; co-continuous at certain compositions.
   - **Advantages**: Excellent chemical resistance (PBT) + high heat resistance (PC).

**Justification for using blends in automotive**:
- Weight reduction (15–25%) improves fuel efficiency.
- Part consolidation via injection molding reduces assembly cost.
- Recycling capability supports circular economy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Students often *just list* applications without explaining the *morphology-property link*. **2 marks are reserved specifically for the morphology-property discussion**. Always mention the dispersed phase, matrix phase, and the property each contributes.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*:

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus** (rule of mixtures, **2 marks**):

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

$$E_c = (72 \times 0.30) + (1.7 \times 0.70)$$

$$E_c = 21.6 + 1.19 = \boxed{22.79 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Transverse Modulus** (inverse rule, **2 marks**):

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{(1 - V_f)}{E_m}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{0.30}{72} + \frac{0.70}{1.7}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = 0.004167 + 0.4118 = 0.4159 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_{c,\perp} = \boxed{2.40 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Critical Fiber Length** (**2 marks**):

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$$

$$l_c = \frac{2000 \times 10^6 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 30 \times 10^6}$$

$$l_c = \frac{24000 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^6} \text{ ... careful unit handling}$$

Let me recompute carefully:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i} = \frac{(2000 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa})(12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m})}{2 (30 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa})}$$

$$l_c = \frac{24 \, \text{N}}{60 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = \boxed{0.4 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 4 — Effectiveness Comment** (**1 mark**):

For effective reinforcement, fiber length should be at least $10 l_c$ to $15 l_c$:

$$l_{\text{required}} \approx 10 \times 0.4 \, \mu\text{m} = 4 \, \mu\text{m}$$

Commercial short glass fibers for PP composites typically range from **3 mm to 12 mm** ($3000$ to $12000 \, \mu\text{m}$), which is **>10,000×** the critical length. Hence, the fibers are **highly effective** for reinforcement, and the rule of mixtures applies confidently.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]:

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss in detail the applications of **polymer composites in aerospace and wind-energy sectors**. Include the role of matrix and fiber selection, fiber volume fractions used, and at least two specific material systems.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With a neat sketch, explain the **morphology types in polymer blends** (matrix-droplet, co-continuous, and laminar). For each morphology, give one example application.

---

**Model Answer for Question B (a)**:

**Polymer Composites in Aerospace & Wind Energy** *(7 marks)*:

**Aerospace Applications**:

The aerospace industry demands materials with **high specific strength (strength-to-density ratio)**, **fatigue resistance**, and **temperature stability**.

| Component | Material System | $V_f$ | Why this system? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aircraft fuselage skin | Carbon-fiber/epoxy (CFRP) | **0.55–0.65** | Density 1.55 g/cm³ vs. 2.7 for Al; strength 1500 MPa vs. 500 MPa for Al alloy 2024 |
| Wing leading edges | Glass-fiber/epoxy (GFRP) | **0.40–0.50** | Lower cost than CFRP; impact resistance against bird strikes |
| Interior panels | Phenolic/glass (FST-rated) | **0.30–0.45** | Meets **FAR 25.853** flame/smoke/toxicity regulations |
| Engine cowlings | Carbon-fiber/PEEK (thermoplastic) | **0.50–0.60** | Withstands 180 °C continuous service; weldable, recyclable |

**Wind Energy Applications**:

Modern wind turbine blades (e.g., GE Haliade-X, 107 m long) use composites almost exclusively.

| Component | Material System | $V_f$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blade spar cap | **E-glass/epoxy** (sometimes carbon-glass hybrid) | **0.55–0.65** | Maximum longitudinal stiffness to resist flapwise bending |
| Blade shell | E-glass/polyester or vinyl ester | **0.35–0.45** | Lower cost; good fatigue resistance |
| Hub and nacelle | Cast iron / steel (still metals) | — | Mass preferred over light-weighting for stability |

**Role of Fiber Selection**:
- **E-glass**: Cheapest, good tensile strength (3500 MPa), density 2.6 g/cm³ — used where cost dominates.
- **Carbon fiber**: Strength 4000–7000 MPa, modulus 230–700 GPa, density 1.8 g/cm³ — used where stiffness dominates.
- **Aramid (Kevlar®)**: Density 1.44 g/cm³, excellent impact resistance — used in leading edges.

**Role of Matrix Selection**:
- **Epoxy**: Excellent adhesion, low shrinkage, high T_g (~120–180 °C) — aerospace-grade.
- **Vinyl ester**: Better chemical resistance, lower cost — wind blades.
- **Polyester (unsaturated)**: Cheapest — non-critical applications.
- **PEEK / PPS**: High-temperature thermoplastic matrices — emerging thermoplastic composites.

**Synergy Principle**:
Using the rule of mixtures at $V_f = 0.6$, $E_f$ (carbon) = 230 GPa, $E_m$ (epoxy) = 3.5 GPa:

$$E_c = 230 \times 0.6 + 3.5 \times 0.4 = 138 + 1.4 = 139.4 \text{ GPa}$$

This is **>40× the modulus of unreinforced epoxy**, confirming the extraordinary property boost achievable.

---

**Model Answer for Question B (b)**:

**Morphology Types in Polymer Blends** *(7 marks)*:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Immiscible Blend] --> B[Matrix Droplet Sea Island]
    A --> C[Co Continuous]
    A --> D[Laminar Stacked]
    B --> B1[Minor Phase 5 to 30 percent]
    B --> B2[Used for Impact Toughening]
    C --> C1[Both Phases 30 to 70 percent]
    C --> C2[Used for Synergistic Properties and Conductivity]
    D --> D1[Platelet Like Sheets Stacked]
    D --> D2[Used for Barrier and Damping]
```

**1. Matrix-Droplet (Sea-Island) Morphology** *(2 marks)*:
- **Structure**: Continuous matrix of polymer A with dispersed spherical droplets of polymer B.
- **Composition Range**: Typically when minor phase is **<30 vol%**.
- **Application Example**: **HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene)** — polybutadiene rubber droplets (1–5 µm) in continuous PS matrix. Rubber particles initiate crazes that absorb impact energy; impact strength rises from ~15 kJ/m² (PS) to **>80 kJ/m²** (HIPS).

**2. Co-Continuous Morphology** *(2 marks)*:
- **Structure**: Both phases form continuous, interpenetrating 3D networks.
- **Composition Range**: Typically 30–70 vol% of each phase (often near phase-inversion point).
- **Application Example**: **PE/EVOH blend** for fuel tanks. Co-continuous EVOH phase creates tortuous paths for hydrocarbon permeation, reducing fuel loss by **>90%** vs. neat HDPE.

**3. Laminar Morphology** *(2 marks)*:
- **Structure**: Plate-like layers of one phase stacked in matrix (or alternating layers in co-extrusion).
- **Application Example**: **Multilayer PET/PA films** for food packaging — PA layers provide oxygen barrier, PET provides mechanical strength.

**1 mark for the sketch** (sketch can be schematic — circles-in-matrix for sea-island, two interlocked networks for co-continuous, stacked rectangles for laminar).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Don't confuse HIPS with ABS.** HIPS uses *polybutadiene rubber*; ABS uses a *graft copolymer of butadiene rubber with SAN*. Examiners mark this distinction strictly.
> 2. **In numerical problems**, do NOT mix up **weight fraction** and **volume fraction**. Rule of mixtures uses **volume fraction**. If given weight fraction, convert: $V_i = \dfrac{w_i / \rho_i}{\sum_j (w_j / \rho_j)}$.
> 3. **In the critical fiber length derivation**, units MUST cancel. Many students write inconsistent units (e.g., MPa and mm without conversion). Always express in **SI base units** (Pa, m) for the calculation, then convert the answer to a convenient unit.
> 4. **Don't omit "isostrain" and "isostress" assumptions** when deriving the rule of mixtures — they are mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **For application-based questions**, never just say "PC is used in automotive." Always quantify: "PC/ABS (70:30) provides HDT of ~115 °C and Izod impact of ~55 kJ/m², making it suitable for instrument panels."

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications of Polymer Blends & Composites**

- **Core Idea**: Blends/composites are used because *combined properties exceed any single component*.
- **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal)**: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ — applies when fibers are continuous and aligned with load.
- **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Transverse)**: $\dfrac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{E_m}$ — applies when fibers are perpendicular to load.
- **Critical Fiber Length**: $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$ — fibers longer than ~$10 l_c$ are effective.
- **Blend Morphology–Property Link**:
  - Sea-island → impact toughening (HIPS, ABS)
  - Co-continuous → barrier, conductivity (PE/EVOH, conductive polymer blends)
  - Laminar → barrier packaging, damping
- **Compatibilizers** (block/graft copolymers) reduce interfacial tension and stabilize morphology. Examples: SEBS for PP/PS, PE-g-MA for PA/PE.
- **Key Commercial Blends**:
  - PP/EPDM — automotive bumpers
  - PC/ABS — instrument panels, laptops
  - PBT/PC — connectors, lamp housings
  - PE/EVOH — fuel tanks
  - PVC/NBR — cable insulation
  - PLA/PHB — biodegradable packaging
- **Key Composites**:
  - CFRP (carbon-fiber/epoxy) — aerospace primary structures ($V_f$ ≈ 0.6)
  - GFRP (glass-fiber/epoxy) — wind turbine blades, marine
  - Phenolic/glass — aerospace interior (FST-rated)
  - CNT/epoxy — EMI shielding, structural health monitoring
  - PLA/HA — bone scaffolds (biomedical)
- **High-Yield Constants**:
  - Glass transition of PC ≈ **150 °C**
  - LOI of pure PP ≈ **18%** (limbs as combustible); FR-modified PP can reach **28–32%**
  - Density of CFRP ≈ **1.55 g/cm³**; Aluminum = **2.70 g/cm³**
  - Tensile strength of carbon fiber ≈ **4000–7000 MPa**
- **Examiner's Eye**: Always link *morphology → property → application*; always mention the **rule of mixtures** for composite problems; always state the **critical fiber length condition**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Function of Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Function of Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a composite material, the **matrix** is defined as the **continuous phase** that completely surrounds, binds, and encapsulates the reinforcement (the dispersed phase). It is the monolithic body of the composite into which the reinforcing agent (fibers, whiskers, or particulates) is embedded. The matrix serves as the medium for stress transfer, environmental protection, and geometric stability of the reinforcing phase.

$$ \text{Composite (C)} = \text{Matrix (M)} \;+\; \text{Reinforcement (R)} \;+\; \text{Interface (I)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *The matrix is the percolating, continuous component of a composite that transfers loads to and from the reinforcement, maintains the spatial distribution of the dispersed phase, protects it from environmental degradation, and provides the bulk shape, surface finish, and toughness of the finished component.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **steel-reinforced concrete beam** used in a bridge:

- The **concrete** is the matrix — it is poured, spread everywhere, sets into a continuous rock-like body.
- The **steel rods (rebar)** are the reinforcement — they are discontinuous but immensely stronger.
- Concrete on its own is strong in compression but weak in tension. Steel alone would buckle and rust.
- **Together**: Concrete holds the steel in place, the steel resists tensile loads, and the concrete protects the steel from corrosion. Neither could do the job alone.

In a polymer composite, the **thermoset/thermoplastic resin** plays exactly the role of concrete, while the **fiberglass/carbon fiber** plays the role of steel rebar. The matrix is essentially the *"glue-and-skeleton"* of the composite.

### 1.3 Three Universal Functions of the Matrix (The "Golden Triad")

> [!NOTE]
> Any KTU board examiner expects at least these three core functions to be mentioned:

1. **Load Transfer Function** — Transfers applied external loads to the high-strength reinforcement through shear stresses at the matrix–fiber interface.
2. **Protective Function** — Shields the reinforcement from moisture, UV radiation, chemical attack, abrasion, and impact damage.
3. **Geometric/Spatial Function** — Maintains the orientation, position, and spacing of the reinforcement within the component.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Matrix Function

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Volume fraction of matrix | $V_m$ | **0.30 – 0.70** |
| Interfacial shear strength | $\tau_i$ | **5 – 80 MPa** |
| Matrix tensile strength | $\sigma_{mu}$ | **30 – 100 MPa** (polymer) |
| Matrix modulus | $E_m$ | **2 – 5 GPa** (thermoset) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Primary Functions of the Matrix — Deep Analysis

#### (a) Load Transfer Function (Mechanical Role)
This is the *most critical* function. When an external tensile load is applied to a composite:
- The matrix, being continuous, experiences the load first.
- Through **interfacial shear stresses**, it transmits the load to the embedded fibers.
- Because fibers typically have $\sigma_f \gg \sigma_m$ (often **10× to 100×** higher strength), the composite's strength is dominated by the fiber efficiency.
- The matrix must be **ductile enough** to relieve stress concentrations and **stiff enough** to transfer load efficiently.

#### (b) Protection of Reinforcement
The reinforcement (especially glass or carbon fibers) is **extremely sensitive** to:
- Surface flaws → Griffith-type strength reduction
- Moisture → stress-corrosion cracking (especially glass)
- Friction → fiber–fiber wear
- Oxidation → degradation of strength

The matrix coats each fiber with a continuous barrier. **Matrix fracture strain** must exceed fiber fracture strain so the matrix does not crack prematurely and expose fibers.

#### (c) Maintenance of Fiber Position and Orientation
Without a matrix, loose fibers would scatter. The matrix:
- Locks fibers at intended orientation angles ($\theta = 0°, 90°, \pm 45°$, etc.)
- Prevents fiber buckling under compression
- Maintains uniform fiber spacing (important to avoid resin-rich pockets)
- Provides lateral support against micro-buckling

#### (d) Toughness and Crack Arresting
A pure fiber bundle is **brittle**. The matrix provides:
- Crack-bridging mechanisms
- Energy absorption via plastic deformation
- Crack deflection at the fiber–matrix interface
- This is why polymer matrices are preferred over ceramic matrices for impact applications.

#### (e) Processing, Shaping, and Surface Finish
The matrix determines:
- Melt viscosity / cure behavior
- Wetting of fibers during impregnation
- Surface texture and aesthetics
- Dimensional tolerance after cure

### 2.2 Why These Functions Matter — The "Why and How"

- **Why load transfer?** Because fibers carry the load only if the matrix can pull them — without good adhesion, fibers simply pull out (low strength, low stiffness).
- **Why protection?** Carbon fiber exposed to moisture loses **up to 40%** of its strength in 6 months.
- **Why orientation control?** A misaligned fiber ($\theta \neq 0°$) loses strength as $\cos^2\theta$ — the matrix is the only way to enforce alignment.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these six equations — they appear in nearly every Part B question on this module.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(1) Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal modulus)} \quad & E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f \\
\text{(2) Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal strength)} \quad & \sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f \\
\text{(3) Inverse Rule of Mixtures (transverse)} \quad & \frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f} \\
\text{(4) Critical Fiber Length} \quad & L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \, \tau_i} \\
\text{(5) Critical Aspect Ratio} \quad & \left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_c = \frac{\sigma_f}{2 \, \tau_i} \\
\text{(6) Fiber Efficiency Factor} \quad & \eta_f = \frac{L}{2 L_c} \left[2 - \frac{L}{L_c}\right] \quad \text{for} \; L \le L_c \\
& \eta_f = 1 \quad \text{for} \; L \ge L_c
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $E_c, E_m, E_f$ | Composite, matrix, fiber Young's modulus | GPa |
| $V_m, V_f$ | Volume fractions | dimensionless |
| $L_c$ | Critical fiber length | mm |
| $d$ | Fiber diameter | $\mu$m |
| $\tau_i$ | Interfacial shear strength | MPa |
| $\sigma_f, \sigma_m$ | Fiber, matrix tensile strength | MPa |
| $\eta_f$ | Fiber efficiency factor | dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** In Rule of Mixtures, the equation holds only for **iso-strain** (load parallel to fibers). For transverse loading, you **must** use the inverse formula. Confusing these two is a guaranteed mark loss in KTU valuation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Carbon-fiber/epoxy wings rely entirely on the epoxy matrix to transfer aerodynamic loads into the fibers.
- **Automotive**: Glass-fiber/polypropylene bumper beams — polypropylene matrix protects fibers from road salt and UV.
- **Marine**: Fiberglass boat hulls — polyester matrix is the only barrier preventing osmotic blistering of glass fibers.
- **Civil**: GFRP rebars replace steel in coastal bridges — the vinylester matrix resists chloride ingress.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Fiber Length $L_c$

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Part B questions on matrix function.

**Starting Physical Picture:**
Consider a single short fiber of diameter $d$ and length $L$ embedded in a matrix, loaded in tension along its axis. The matrix transfers load into the fiber through shear stress $\tau_i$ acting along the cylindrical fiber–matrix interface.

**Step 1 — Force balance on the fiber end**

The tensile force at the fiber end is balanced by the shear force on the cylindrical lateral surface.

$$
\pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 \sigma_f = \pi \, d \cdot \frac{L_c}{2} \cdot \tau_i
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the geometric terms**

The cross-sectional area of the fiber is $A_f = \pi d^2 / 4$, and the lateral surface area over half the critical length is $A_{lat} = \pi d \cdot L_c / 2$. Substituting:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \frac{\pi d \, L_c}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $\pi$ and rearrange**

Cancel $\pi$ from both sides, then multiply both sides by $4 / d$:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot d = 2 \, \tau_i \, L_c
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L_c$**

$$
\boxed{\; L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \, \tau_i} \;}
$$

This is the **Critical Fiber Length** — the minimum length required to build up the maximum fiber stress $\sigma_f$ before the fiber breaks. For $L \ge L_c$, the fiber can be loaded to its ultimate strength. For $L < L_c$, the fiber pulls out before reaching $\sigma_f$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Fiber Efficiency Factor $\eta_f$ for Short Fibers

**Step 1 — Stress distribution along the fiber**

For a short fiber under axial load, the tensile stress in the fiber is **not uniform**. It builds up linearly from zero at each end (due to shear transfer) to a maximum at the center. This is the **shear-lag** result:

$$
\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i \, x}{d}
$$

where $x$ is the distance from the fiber end.

**Step 2 — Average fiber stress**

The **average** fiber stress governs composite strength. Integrate $\sigma_f(x)$ from $0$ to $L/2$ and divide by $L/2$:

$$
\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{L/2} \int_0^{L/2} \frac{2 \tau_i \, x}{d} \, dx
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral**

$$
\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{2}{L} \cdot \frac{2 \tau_i}{d} \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_0^{L/2}
$$

$$
\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{4 \tau_i}{L \, d} \cdot \frac{L^2}{8} = \frac{\tau_i L}{2 d}
$$

**Step 4 — Define efficiency factor**

The efficiency factor is the ratio of average fiber stress to the maximum achievable fiber stress $\sigma_f$:

$$
\eta_f = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_f}{\sigma_f} = \frac{\tau_i L}{2 d \, \sigma_f}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of critical length**

Substitute $\sigma_f = 2 \tau_i L_c / d$ (from the critical length definition):

$$
\eta_f = \frac{\tau_i L}{2 d} \cdot \frac{d}{2 \tau_i L_c} = \frac{L}{2 L_c}
$$

**Step 6 — Final form (with peak correction)**

A more rigorous shear-lag treatment (with triangular stress profile) gives:

$$
\boxed{\; \eta_f = \frac{L}{2 L_c} \left[ 2 - \frac{L}{L_c} \right] \quad \text{for} \; L \le L_c \;}
$$

and $\eta_f = 1$ for $L \ge L_c$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite has:
> - Fiber tensile strength $\sigma_f = 2.0$ GPa
> - Fiber diameter $d = 10 \, \mu$m
> - Interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 40$ MPa
>
> **Find:** (a) The critical fiber length. (b) The efficiency factor if actual fiber length is $L = 0.5$ mm.

**Solution (a): Critical Length**

$$
L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^3 \, \text{MPa})(10 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mm})}{2 \times 40 \, \text{MPa}}
$$

$$
L_c = \frac{20000 \, \text{MPa} \cdot \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{mm}}{80 \, \text{MPa}}
$$

Converting units carefully: $d = 10 \, \mu$m $= 0.01$ mm.

$$
L_c = \frac{2000 \cdot 0.01}{80} = \frac{20}{80} = 0.25 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; L_c = 0.25 \, \text{mm} \;}
$$

**Solution (b): Efficiency Factor**

Since $L = 0.5$ mm and $L_c = 0.25$ mm, we have $L > L_c$, therefore:

$$
\eta_f = 1.0 \quad (\text{fiber is fully loaded to its ultimate strength})
$$

If instead $L = 0.1$ mm (less than $L_c$):

$$
\eta_f = \frac{L}{2 L_c} \left[ 2 - \frac{L}{L_c} \right] = \frac{0.1}{0.5} \left[ 2 - \frac{0.1}{0.25} \right]
$$

$$
\eta_f = 0.2 \times [2 - 0.4] = 0.2 \times 1.6 = 0.32
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \eta_f = 0.32 \; \text{(only 32\% of fiber strength is utilized)} \;}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the matrix in a composite material. List any THREE primary functions of the matrix.*

**Model Answer:**

The matrix is the **continuous phase** of a composite that surrounds and binds the reinforcement. Its three primary functions are:

1. **Load Transfer**: Transfers applied stress to the high-strength reinforcement through interfacial shear.
2. **Protection**: Shields the reinforcement from environmental degradation such as moisture, UV, and chemicals.
3. **Spatial Control**: Maintains the position, orientation, and spacing of the reinforcement within the composite.

*(Any other valid function like toughness or processing may also be credited.)*

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1M) + 3 functions × (2/3 M each) ≈ (1M + 2M) = **3 Marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the "critical fiber length" in a fiber-reinforced composite? Write its mathematical expression.*

**Model Answer:**

The critical fiber length $L_c$ is the **minimum length of a fiber** required to build up the maximum fiber tensile stress $\sigma_f$ at its center before fracture occurs, when load is transferred through interfacial shear $\tau_i$.

$$
L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \, \tau_i}
$$

where $d$ is the fiber diameter and $\tau_i$ is the interfacial shear strength.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five major functions of the matrix** in a fiber-reinforced composite material. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram and proper derivation, obtain the expression for the **critical fiber length** $L_c$. A glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite has $\sigma_f = 1.8$ GPa, $d = 12 \, \mu$m and $\tau_i = 35$ MPa. Calculate $L_c$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The five major functions of the matrix are:

1. **Load Transfer Function**
   - The matrix is the only path by which external load reaches the fibers.
   - It transmits tensile/compressive load via **interfacial shear stresses** $\tau_i$ at the fiber–matrix boundary.
   - Without adequate matrix stiffness, the shear transfer is inefficient and fibers pull out.

2. **Protection of the Reinforcement**
   - The matrix shields the fibers from moisture, UV, abrasion, chemical attack, and surface damage.
   - For example, glass fibers lose up to 40% strength in humid conditions; the resin matrix prevents this.
   - The matrix also reduces surface flaw propagation on the fiber.

3. **Maintenance of Fiber Position and Orientation**
   - The matrix locks fibers in the desired angle ($\theta = 0°, 90°, \pm 45°$).
   - Prevents fiber misalignment, fiber-to-fiber contact, and micro-buckling under compression.

4. **Toughness and Crack Arrest**
   - Pure fiber bundles are extremely brittle. The matrix adds ductility.
   - Mechanisms: crack bridging, plastic deformation at crack tip, fiber pull-out toughening.
   - Critical for impact resistance (e.g., automotive crash components).

5. **Processing and Shaping**
   - The matrix provides the rheology for impregnation (wetting), cure or solidification, and final shape.
   - Determines surface finish and dimensional accuracy of the component.

*(Each function ≈ 1.4 marks; neat explanation expected.)* **Total: 7 Marks**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the derivation. Final expression:

$$
L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

**Numerical calculation:**

Given: $\sigma_f = 1.8 \times 10^3$ MPa, $d = 12 \, \mu$m $= 0.012$ mm, $\tau_i = 35$ MPa.

$$
L_c = \frac{1800 \times 0.012}{2 \times 35} = \frac{21.6}{70}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; L_c = 0.3086 \, \text{mm} \approx 0.31 \, \text{mm} \;}
$$

**Steps carrying marks:** Diagram (2M) + Force balance equation (2M) + Algebraic simplification (1M) + Final boxed formula (1M) + Numerical answer with units (1M) = **7 Marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Differentiate between the **iso-strain** and **iso-stress** models for a two-phase composite. Derive the corresponding expressions for the **longitudinal and transverse moduli**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **fiber efficiency factor** for short fibers. A short carbon fiber composite has $L_c = 1.2$ mm. If the fiber length is $L = 0.8$ mm, compute the fiber efficiency factor. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

| Feature | Iso-Strain Model | Iso-Stress Model |
|---|---|---|
| Strain in both phases | Equal ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_f$) | Equal stress ($\sigma_c = \sigma_m = \sigma_f$) |
| Load direction | Parallel to fibers | Perpendicular to fibers |
| Reinforcement geometry | Continuous / long fibers | Laminate / layered / transverse loading |

**Iso-Strain Derivation (Voigt Model — Longitudinal):**

Total load on composite = Load on matrix + Load on fiber

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_f A_f
$$

Divide by $A_c$ and substitute $V_i = A_i / A_c$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f
$$

Since strain is uniform, $\sigma_i = E_i \varepsilon$:

$$
E_c \varepsilon = E_m \varepsilon V_m + E_f \varepsilon V_f
$$

$$
\boxed{\; E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f \;} \quad \text{(Rule of Mixtures)}
$$

**Iso-Stress Derivation (Reuss Model — Transverse):**

Strain in composite = Strain in matrix + Strain in fiber (volume weighted):

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m V_m + \varepsilon_f V_f
$$

Divide by common stress $\sigma$:

$$
\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; E_c = \left[\dfrac{V_m}{E_m} + \dfrac{V_f}{E_f}\right]^{-1} \;}
$$

**Marks:** Definitions (2M) + Iso-strain derivation (2.5M) + Iso-stress derivation (2.5M) = **7 Marks**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

The **fiber efficiency factor** $\eta_f$ is the ratio of the average fiber stress actually developed in a short fiber to the maximum stress it could carry if infinitely long:

$$
\eta_f = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_f}{\sigma_f}
$$

For $L < L_c$ (stress has not built up to maximum):

$$
\eta_f = \frac{L}{2 L_c} \left[ 2 - \frac{L}{L_c} \right]
$$

For $L \ge L_c$: $\eta_f = 1$.

**Numerical calculation:**

Given: $L_c = 1.2$ mm, $L = 0.8$ mm.

Check: $L = 0.8 < L_c = 1.2$, so we use the short-fiber formula:

$$
\eta_f = \frac{0.8}{2 \times 1.2} \left[ 2 - \frac{0.8}{1.2} \right]
$$

$$
\eta_f = \frac{0.8}{2.4} \left[ 2 - 0.6667 \right] = 0.3333 \times 1.3333
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \eta_f \approx 0.444 \; \text{(44.4\% efficiency)} \;}
$$

**Marks:** Concept (2M) + Formula (2M) + Substitution (1.5M) + Final answer with units (1.5M) = **7 Marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Converting $\mu$m to mm is mandatory. Forgetting this is a **1-mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 2. **Iso-strain vs Iso-stress confusion**: Many students write the **same** formula for both longitudinal and transverse moduli. Examiners specifically check this — expect a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Forgetting to box final answers**: KTU evaluators literally scan for boxed answers. Unboxed final values often receive partial credit only.
> 4. **Missing the "$\eta_f = 1$ for $L \ge L_c$" boundary**: This conditional is **frequently asked** and rarely answered correctly.
> 5. **No diagram in derivation questions**: A labeled force-balance diagram on a single fiber is worth **2 marks** by itself in derivation questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Matrix = continuous phase**; Reinforcement = dispersed phase.
- The **five primary functions** are: load transfer, protection, spatial control, toughness, and processing.
- **Critical fiber length**: $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ — memorize the derivation steps.
- **Iso-strain** → Rule of Mixtures → $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ (longitudinal).
- **Iso-stress** → Inverse Rule → $1/E_c = V_m/E_m + V_f/E_f$ (transverse).
- **Fiber efficiency factor** for short fibers: $\eta_f = (L / 2L_c)[2 - L/L_c]$.
- For $L \ge L_c$: $\eta_f = 1$ (full fiber utilization).
- Matrix modulus $E_m$ should be **at least 1/10 of $E_f$** for efficient load transfer.
- Matrix failure strain must **exceed** fiber failure strain to avoid premature cracking.
- **Interfacial shear strength** $\tau_i$ is the single most important parameter governing matrix function.
- **High-Yield Keywords for KTU Answers**: continuous phase, dispersed phase, interfacial shear, Kelly-Tyson, shear lag, rule of mixtures, iso-strain, iso-stress, fiber pull-out, aspect ratio.
- Always convert $\mu$m to mm or to m before substituting into length formulas.
- Box all final numerical answers with correct **SI units** (mm, GPa, MPa).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Fibre-Reinforced Polymer Composites (FRPCs), the **fibre** is the dispersed *reinforcing phase* embedded within a continuous polymer *matrix phase*. The primary **function of the fibre** is to serve as the principal **load-bearing constituent** that carries and distributes the applied mechanical, thermal, and sometimes electrical stresses, while the matrix binds the fibres together, protects them, and transfers the external load onto the fibre surfaces through the fibre–matrix interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> *Fibres in a polymer composite are high-aspect-ratio reinforcements whose function is to carry the major share of the applied load, enhance strength and stiffness, improve dimensional stability, arrest crack propagation, and provide specific functional properties (thermal, electrical, or chemical resistance), while the matrix phase serves to bind, protect, and transfer load to the fibres.*

In quantitative terms, fibres are characterized by:

- **Fibre Length ($l$)** — usually expressed in **mm** or **µm**.
- **Fibre Diameter ($d$)** — usually expressed in **µm**.
- **Aspect Ratio ($l/d$)** — a dimensionless geometric parameter.
- **Critical Fibre Length ($l_c$)** — the minimum length required for the fibre to develop its ultimate failure stress.
- **Fibre Volume Fraction ($V_f$)** and **Matrix Volume Fraction ($V_m$)** — with the constraint $V_f + V_m = 1$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel-Reinforced Concrete" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a column made of **plain concrete** versus one made of **steel-reinforced concrete (RCC)**. The concrete alone is strong when squeezed (compression) but is brittle and weak when pulled or bent (tension). When steel rods (rebars) are embedded inside the concrete, the rods carry all the tensile load — the concrete merely holds them in place and distributes the load evenly.
>
> In a **polymer composite**, the roles are identical:
> - **Matrix (≈ Concrete)** → soft, ductile, protects, and transfers load.
> - **Fibre (≈ Steel Rebar)** → stiff, strong, carries the principal load.
>
> Without the fibres, neat polymers are weak, soft, and dimensionally unstable. With the fibres, the composite gains **strength, stiffness, fatigue life, dimensional stability, and functional properties** — this transformation is the *function of fibres*.

---

## 1.3 The Five Principal Functions of Fibres

| # | Function | Brief Description |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| **1** | **Load Bearing (Reinforcement)** | Carries majority of the applied tensile/compressive load |
| **2** | **Strength & Stiffness Enhancement** | Imparts high specific strength and specific modulus |
| **3** | **Stress Transfer Reception** | Receives stress from the matrix via interfacial shear |
| **4** | **Crack Arresting & Deflection** | Stops, deflects, or bridges propagating cracks |
| **5** | **Dimensional & Functional Stability** | Reduces CTE, creep, and imparts electrical/thermal functions |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked "List the functions of fibres," always present at least **five distinct functions** with one-line explanations. Examiners reward structured tabular answers over paragraph answers.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Fibres by Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fibres can be broadly classified based on the dominant function they perform:

- **Structural / Mechanical Fibres** — E-glass, carbon, aramid (Kevlar), basalt → primarily *load bearing*.
- **High-Temperature Resistant Fibres** — Ceramic, silica, alumina → primarily *thermal stability*.
- **Electrical / EMI Shielding Fibres** — Carbon, metal-coated glass, stainless steel → primarily *conductivity*.
- **Specialty Fibres** — Boron, silicon-carbide, natural fibres (jute, sisal, coir) → *hybrid functions* including eco-sustainability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Fibre Function

The functions of fibres in a polymer composite are governed by the following physical mechanisms:

### Step 1 — **Load Application on the Composite**
When an external load $P$ is applied to a composite, it is distributed between the fibre and the matrix based on the *rule of mixtures* under iso-strain conditions (for continuous aligned fibres).

### Step 2 — **Stress Transfer from Matrix to Fibre (The "Handshake")**
The matrix, being more compliant, deforms and tries to "drag" the fibre along. Because of the bonded interface, this dragging is resisted by **interfacial shear stress** ($\tau_i$). The fibre builds up tensile stress gradually from its ends, reaching maximum $\sigma_f$ at the centre.

### Step 3 — **Build-Up of Fibre Tensile Stress**
Fibre stress builds parabolically from the ends (zero stress) to the centre (maximum). This is the foundation of the **Kelly–Tyson shear-lag model**.

### Step 4 — **Achieving Critical Length**
If the fibre is long enough ($l \geq l_c$), it reaches its ultimate strength before pulling out. If too short ($l < l_c$), it pulls out without breaking — wasting the reinforcement potential.

### Step 5 — **Failure Mechanisms**
Failure occurs by fibre fracture (efficient reinforcement) **OR** fibre pull-out (inefficient reinforcement) **OR** interfacial debonding.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Function

| Function | Underlying Physics | Why it Matters in Engineering |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Load Bearing | Stress transfer via interfacial shear | Aircraft, bridges, pressure vessels rely on this |
| Stiffness | High $E_f$ of fibre dominates composite modulus | Reduces deflection in structural panels |
| Crack Arresting | Fibre bridges crack faces; consumes fracture energy | Improves fracture toughness ($K_{IC}$) |
| Dimensional Stability | Low CTE of fibre (e.g. carbon $\approx 0$) | Spacecraft, precision instruments |
| Functional | Conductivity, fire retardancy, EMI shielding | Electronic enclosures, aerospace |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they cover ~90% of Module 2 numerical and theory questions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{1. Aspect Ratio} \quad && A = \dfrac{l}{d} \quad [\text{dimensionless}] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{2. Critical Fibre Length} \quad && l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} \quad [\text{mm}] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{3. Critical Aspect Ratio} \quad && \left(\dfrac{l}{d}\right)_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{4. Fibre Length Efficiency} \quad && \eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l} \quad (l < l_c) \\[6pt]
&\textbf{5. Rule of Mixtures (Strength)} \quad && \sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m^{\prime} V_m \\[6pt]
&\textbf{6. Rule of Mixtures (Modulus)} \quad && E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \text{(iso-strain)} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{7. Inverse Rule of Mixtures} \quad && \dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m} \quad \text{(iso-stress, transverse)} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{8. Modified Rule (Kelly–Tyson)} \quad && \sigma_c = \eta_l \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m \\[6pt]
&\textbf{9. Kelly–Tyson Modulus} \quad && E_c = \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\[6pt]
&\textbf{10. Force Balance (fibre pull-out)} \quad && \sigma_f \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \pi d \left(\dfrac{l_c}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units | Range in Practice |
|--------|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| $\sigma_f$ | Ultimate tensile strength of fibre | MPa | $500 - 7000$ |
| $\sigma_m$ | Matrix stress at fibre failure strain | MPa | $20 - 100$ |
| $E_f$ | Young's modulus of fibre | GPa | $70 - 1000$ |
| $E_m$ | Young's modulus of matrix | GPa | $1 - 5$ |
| $\tau_i$ | Interfacial shear strength | MPa | $5 - 80$ |
| $V_f$ | Fibre volume fraction | dimensionless | $0.1 - 0.7$ |
| $d$ | Fibre diameter | µm | $5 - 25$ |
| $l_c$ | Critical fibre length | mm | $0.1 - 10$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: In aerospace CFRP (Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer), $V_f \approx 0.6$, $E_f \approx 230$ GPa (T700 carbon), giving $E_c \approx 140$ GPa — comparable to aluminium but at **half the weight**. This is why fibres are central to lightweight design.

---

## 2.4 The Concept of Critical Fibre Length — Conceptual Explanation

> [!TIP]
> The critical fibre length $l_c$ is the **single most important design parameter** for short-fibre composites. It separates:
> - **$l < l_c$** → fibre *pulls out* (poor reinforcement)
> - **$l \geq l_c$** → fibre *fractures* (efficient reinforcement)
> - **$l \geq 15 \cdot l_c$** → fibre behaves like a *continuous* fibre (full efficiency)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Critical Fibre Length ($l_c$)

This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 2. Master every step.

### **Assumptions**

1. The fibre is cylindrical with uniform diameter $d$ and length $l$.
2. The fibre is bonded perfectly to the matrix along its cylindrical surface.
3. A tensile load is applied parallel to the fibre axis.
4. The interface fails in shear (not in tension).
5. The fibre is **short** and discontinuous.
6. Stress builds up symmetrically from both fibre ends.

---

### **Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of a Fibre**

Consider a short fibre of length $l$ embedded in a matrix under a tensile load $\sigma$ applied to the composite.

$$
\text{Fibre tensile force at centre} \;=\; \sigma_f \cdot A_f \;=\; \sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

The shear force acting over **half** the cylindrical interface (length $l/2$) resists the fibre pull-out:

$$
\text{Interface shear force} \;=\; \tau_i \cdot A_{interface} \;=\; \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \dfrac{l}{2}
$$

---

### **Step 2 — Equilibrium Equation**

For the fibre to be loaded up to its ultimate stress $\sigma_f$, the shear force transferred through the interface over half the fibre must equal the tensile force in the fibre cross-section.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \;&=\; \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \dfrac{l_c}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Step 3 — Cancellation and Simplification**

Cancel $\pi$ from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{d^2}{4} \;&=\; \tau_i \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{l_c}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply both sides by $4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f \cdot d^2 \;&=\; 2 \cdot \tau_i \cdot d \cdot l_c
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide both sides by $d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f \cdot d \;&=\; 2 \cdot \tau_i \cdot l_c
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearrange for $l_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_c \;&=\; \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \, \tau_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}\,}
$$

---

### **Step 4 — Derivation of Critical Aspect Ratio**

Dividing both sides of the final equation by $d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{l_c}{d} \;&=\; \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\left(\dfrac{l}{d}\right)_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A glass fibre of diameter $d = 10 \; \mu\text{m}$ has tensile strength $\sigma_f = 3.5 \; \text{GPa}$. The interfacial shear strength with an epoxy matrix is $\tau_i = 50 \; \text{MPa}$. Calculate (a) the critical fibre length, and (b) the critical aspect ratio.

### **Solution**

**Given:**
$$
d = 10 \;\mu\text{m} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mm}, \quad \sigma_f = 3500 \; \text{MPa}, \quad \tau_i = 50 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**(a) Critical Fibre Length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_c \;&=\; \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \, \tau_i} \\[6pt]
l_c \;&=\; \dfrac{3500 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 50} \\[6pt]
l_c \;&=\; \dfrac{35}{100} \\[6pt]
l_c \;&=\; 0.35 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,l_c = 0.35 \; \text{mm}\,}
$$

**(b) Critical Aspect Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{l}{d}\right)_c \;&=\; \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2 \, \tau_i} \\[6pt]
\left(\dfrac{l}{d}\right)_c \;&=\; \dfrac{3500}{2 \times 50} \\[6pt]
\left(\dfrac{l}{d}\right)_c \;&=\; \dfrac{3500}{100} \\[6pt]
\left(\dfrac{l}{d}\right)_c \;&=\; 35
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\left(l/d\right)_c = 35\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Any fibre longer than $0.35 \; \text{mm}$ with an aspect ratio greater than **35** will fracture before pulling out — this is the regime of *efficient reinforcement*.

---

## 3.3 Stress Distribution Along the Fibre (Analytical Description)

The tensile stress in a short fibre builds up from **zero at the ends** to a **maximum at the centre** following a parabolic profile (Kelly–Tyson shear-lag assumption).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f(x) \;&=\; \dfrac{2 \, \tau_i \, x}{d} \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \leq x \leq l/2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **linear** stress build-up (Kelly–Tyson original model) which simplifies the analysis. The stress is maximum at $x = l/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{f,\max} \;&=\; \dfrac{\tau_i \, l}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\sigma_{f,\max} = \sigma_f$ gives the same critical length equation derived above.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Function vs. Fibre Type

| Fibre Function | Best Fibre Type | Reason | Typical Application |
|----------------|-----------------|--------|---------------------|
| Maximum Tensile Strength | Carbon (T1000), Aramid | $\sigma_f > 5$ GPa | Aerospace primary structures |
| Maximum Stiffness | Carbon (HM), Boron | $E_f > 400$ GPa | Spacecraft, satellite booms |
| Impact Resistance | Aramid (Kevlar), UHMWPE | High toughness, energy absorption | Ballistics, helmets |
| Cost-Effective Reinforcement | E-Glass | Low cost, decent properties | Automotive, marine, civil |
| Thermal Stability | Ceramic (Nextel), Basalt | $T_{\max} > 1000^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Rocket nozzles, exhaust ducts |
| EMI Shielding | Nickel-coated carbon, stainless steel | Electrical conductivity | Electronic enclosures |
| Eco-Sustainability | Jute, Flax, Coir | Biodegradable, low embodied energy | Automotive interiors, panels |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Fibre in a Polymer Composite

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load P Applied to Composite] --> B[Load Partitioned between Fibre and Matrix]
    B --> C[Matrix Deforms More Easily]
    B --> D[Fibre Carries Majority of Load]
    C --> E[Interfacial Shear Stress tau_i Develops at Interface]
    E --> F[Stress Transfer from Matrix to Fibre]
    F --> G[Fibre Stress Builds from Ends to Centre]
    G --> H{Is l greater than or equal to l_c?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Fibre Fractures Efficient Reinforcement]
    H -- No --> J[Fibre Pulls Out Inefficient Reinforcement]
    I --> K[Composite Strength enhanced by sigma_f V_f]
    J --> L[Composite Strength limited by tau_i l d]
    D --> M[Fibre Imparts Stiffness via E_f]
    D --> N[Fibre Reduces Creep and CTE]
    D --> O[Fibre Arrests Cracks and Bridges Crack Faces]
    D --> P[Fibre Provides Functional Properties EMI / Thermal / Fire]
    K --> Q[Final Composite Performance]
    L --> Q
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Build-Up Along a Short Fibre

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fibre End x equals 0] --> B[sigma_f equals 0]
    B --> C[Linear Stress Build-Up Region]
    C --> D[Centre x equals l over 2]
    D --> E[sigma_f maximum equals tau_i l over d]
    E --> F[Symmetric Decrease to Other End]
    F --> G[Fibre End x equals l]
    G --> H[sigma_f equals 0]
    E --> I{sigma_f equals sigma_ultimate ?}
    I -- Yes --> J[l equals l_c Critical Length Reached]
    I -- No --> K[l less than l_c Fibre Pulls Out]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Fibre Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Functions of Fibres in Polymer Composites] --> B[Mechanical Functions]
    A --> C[Thermal Functions]
    A --> D[Electrical Functions]
    A --> E[Chemical Environmental Functions]
    A --> F[Specialty Functions]
    B --> B1[Load Bearing]
    B --> B2[Stiffness Enhancement]
    B --> B3[Crack Arresting]
    B --> B4[Fatigue Resistance]
    C --> C1[Dimensional Stability Low CTE]
    C --> C2[Thermal Insulation]
    C --> C3[Fire Retardancy]
    D --> D1[Electrical Conductivity]
    D --> D2[EMI Shielding]
    D --> D3[Static Charge Dissipation]
    E --> E1[Corrosion Resistance]
    E --> E2[Moisture Barrier]
    E --> E3[Chemical Inertness]
    F --> F1[Energy Absorption Impact]
    F --> F2[Biodegradability Natural Fibres]
    F --> F3[Aesthetic and Surface Finish]
```

---

## 4.4 Physical Picture — Stress Build-Up on Fibre Surface (Schematic Representation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Mental Picture (Exam Drawing Tip):**
>
> ```
>        Tensile Stress σ_f(x)
>            ▲
>  σ_f,max ──┤         ╭───────────╮
>            │       ╭╱               ╲╮
>            │     ╭╱                    ╲╮
>            │   ╭╱                          ╲╮
>            │ ╭╱                                ╲╮
>      σ_f=0 ─┼╯                                    ╲╯───────
>            └──────────────────────────────────────────────►  x
>            x=0      x = l/2 (centre)            x = l
>                       ▲
>                Maximum tensile stress in fibre
> ```
>
> - At **both ends** of the fibre, tensile stress is **zero**.
> - Stress increases **linearly** towards the centre.
> - The fibre fails by **fracture at the centre** if $l \geq l_c$.
> - Otherwise, the fibre is **pulled out** from the matrix.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Failure Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Composite Loaded] --> B{Is l greater than or equal to l_c?}
    B -- YES Continuous or Long Fibre --> C[Fibre Fracture]
    B -- NO Short Fibre --> D{Is Interfacial Bond Strong?}
    D -- YES --> E[Matrix Cracking then Fibre Pull Out]
    D -- NO --> F[Interfacial Debonding First]
    C --> G[Brittle Composite Failure]
    E --> H[Tough Composite Failure with Energy Absorption]
    F --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
