## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a continuous fibre composite. How is it different from a discontinuous (short) fibre composite?

**Model Answer:**
A continuous fibre composite is one in which the reinforcing fibres are of effectively infinite length (much greater than the critical fibre length $L_c$), so that both ends of the fibre lie outside the loaded region. Continuous fibres carry load entirely through interfacial shear with no end-effect contribution.

In contrast, a **discontinuous fibre composite** has finite-length fibres ($L \approx L_c$ or less), where the ends of the fibres act as stress concentrators and the load is transferred only over the fibre length $L < \infty$, leading to lower efficiency. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the **rule of mixtures** for the longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional continuous fibre composite. What assumption is it based on?

**Model Answer:**
The rule of mixtures (Voigt / isostrain model) states:

$$E_{c,\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

**Assumption:** The fibre and matrix experience **equal strains** (isostrain condition) under longitudinal tensile loading, i.e., $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$. This requires **perfect bonding** at the fibre–matrix interface and load applied **parallel** to the fibre axis. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:** Each Part B question in PBPOT604 carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of the two alternatives provided.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **isostrain** and **isostress** models for a unidirectional continuous fibre composite. Derive expressions for the longitudinal and transverse Young's modulus in each case. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

- **Isostrain Model** (Longitudinal loading): The fibre and matrix are coupled in parallel, so $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$. (1 mark)
- Force equilibrium gives $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$. Substituting Hooke's law:

$$E_{c,\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \text{...(1, 2 marks)}$$

- **Isostress Model** (Transverse loading): Fibre and matrix coupled in series, so $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$. (1 mark)
- Strain additivity gives $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$, hence:

$$E_{c,\perp} = \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m} \quad \text{...(2, 2 marks)}$$

- Sketches showing parallel (UD) and series (perpendicular) coupling. (1 mark)

**(b)** A unidirectional continuous carbon-fibre/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.6$ GPa. Calculate the longitudinal and transverse moduli. Comment on the anisotropy. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Given: $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.6$ GPa.

**Longitudinal modulus (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{c,\parallel} &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\
&= 230 \times 0.55 + 3.6 \times 0.45 \\
&= 126.5 + 1.62 \\
&= 128.12 \text{ GPa} \quad \text{...(3 marks)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Transverse modulus (Inverse Rule of Mixtures):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{c,\perp} &= \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m} \\
&= \frac{230 \times 3.6}{3.6 \times 0.55 + 230 \times 0.45} \\
&= \frac{828}{1.98 + 103.5} \\
&= \frac{828}{105.48} \\
&\approx 7.85 \text{ GPa} \quad \text{...(3 marks)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Anisotropy Comment:**

$$\frac{E_{c,\parallel}}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{128.12}{7.85} \approx 16.3$$

The composite is **strongly anisotropic**; the longitudinal stiffness is ~16× the transverse stiffness. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **critical fibre volume fraction** in a continuous fibre composite. Derive the expression for $V_{f,\text{crit}}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

- **Definition** (2 marks): The critical fibre volume fraction $V_{f,\text{crit}}$ is the minimum fibre volume fraction above which the composite strength exceeds that of the unreinforced matrix. Below $V_{f,\text{crit}}$, the matrix dictates failure; above it, the fibre dictates failure.
- **Derivation** (4 marks): Setting composite strength = matrix strength:

$$\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_{m,\text{uts}}$$

$$\sigma_f^* V_f + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f) = \sigma_{m,\text{uts}}$$

Solving for $V_f$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f^* V_f + \sigma_m' - \sigma_m' V_f &= \sigma_{m,\text{uts}} \\
V_f (\sigma_f^* - \sigma_m') &= \sigma_{m,\text{uts}} - \sigma_m'
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_{f,\text{crit}} = \frac{\sigma_{m,\text{uts}} - \sigma_m'}{\sigma_f^* - \sigma_m'}}$$

- **Significance** (1 mark): For typical CFC like carbon/epoxy, $V_{f,\text{crit}}$ is very low (~0.003), so any practical $V_f > 0.3$ vastly exceeds it, ensuring fibre-dominated failure.

**(b)** Describe the **pultrusion** process for manufacturing continuous fibre composites with a neat schematic. List any three advantages of pultrusion. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

- **Process Description** (4 marks): Pultrusion is a continuous manufacturing process in which fibre rovings and/or mats are pulled through a resin bath (or resin injection chamber), then through a heated die where the resin cures, and finally through a cutting saw. The word "pultrusion" = "**pull**" + "extr**usion**".
- **Steps:**
  1. Fibre creels feed rovings into a resin bath (impregnation).
  2. Wetted fibres pass through pre-forming guides.
  3. Heated steel die cures the resin (typically 100–180 °C).
  4. Pulling mechanism (hydraulic caterpillar) drags the cured profile.
  5. Cut-off saw cuts to required length.
- **Schematic** (in words — refer to Figure in Section 4.3 above).
- **Advantages** (any 3, 1 mark each):
  1. Continuous, high-volume production with consistent quality.
  2. Very high fibre volume fractions achievable (up to 70 %).
  3. Low labour cost and minimal material wastage.
  4. Excellent for constant-cross-section profiles (rods, beams, channels).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in PBPOT604 Continuous Fibre Composite Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_m$ with $\sigma_m'$ in strength derivations** (–2 marks). Always check: which strain is the matrix at when fibre fails?
> 2. **Applying the wrong model to the wrong loading direction** (–3 marks). Rule of Mixtures (parallel) is ONLY for longitudinal loading. Transverse loading ALWAYS uses the inverse rule (series/Reuss model).
> 3. **Writing the isostrain assumption as $\sigma_f = \sigma_m$** — this is WRONG. The correct statement is $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$ (equal **strain**, not equal stress).
> 4. **Omitting the perfect bonding assumption** (–1 mark). Every rule-of-mixtures derivation MUST mention "perfect fibre–matrix interfacial bonding" as a precondition.
> 5. **Skipping unit checks** in numerical problems (–1 mark). Always write the final answer with **GPa** or **MPa** explicitly.
> 6. **Forgetting $V_f + V_m = 1$** identity when one is missing from the question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Continuous Fibre Composites (PBPOT604 Module 3)**
>
> - ✅ **Definition:** Fibres of length $L \gg L_c$ running continuously through the matrix; no fibre ends in the loaded region.
> - ✅ **Classification:** UD, woven (bidirectional), multidirectional laminate — based on **fibre architecture**.
> - ✅ **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal):** $E_{c,\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — isostrain / Voigt model.
> - ✅ **Inverse Rule (Transverse):** $E_{c,\perp} = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$ — isostress / Reuss model.
> - ✅ **Strength Equation:** $\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$ — note $\sigma_m'$ is matrix stress at **fibre failure strain**, NOT ultimate.
> - ✅ **Critical Fibre Volume Fraction:** $V_{f,\text{crit}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{m,\text{uts}} - \sigma_m'}{\sigma_f^* - \sigma_m'}$ — minimum $V_f$ for reinforcement to be effective.
> - ✅ **Anisotropy:** UD composites have $E_{\parallel} \gg E_{\perp}$ (often 10–20× difference).
> - ✅ **Load Transfer:** Purely through interfacial shear $\tau_i$, uniformly along the fibre (no stress peaks at ends, unlike short fibres).
> - ✅ **Manufacturing Methods:** Pultrusion, filament winding, prepreg/autoclave, RTM, VARTM — all suited to continuous fibre architectures.
> - ✅ **Typical $V_f$ range:** 0.30 – 0.65 in commercial composites; higher $V_f$ requires excellent wetting and processing.
> - ✅ **Typical fibre types:** E-glass, S-glass, carbon (PAN-based & pitch-based), aramid (Kevlar), boron, ceramic (SiC, Al₂O₃).
> - ✅ **Typical matrices:** Epoxy (most common aerospace), polyester (marine), vinyl ester (chemical resistance), PEEK/PPS (thermoplastic, high-temp aerospace).
> - ✅ **Real-world applications:** Aircraft fuselage & wings (CFRP), wind turbine blades (GFRP/CFRP hybrid), pressure vessels (carbon/epoxy filament-wound), bridge cables, racing car monocoques.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Long Fibre Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Long Fibre Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Fibre Composites (LFCs)** are a class of fibre-reinforced polymer composites in which the reinforcing fibres possess a length significantly greater than the **critical fibre length ($l_c$)** — typically $l \gg 15 \cdot l_c$ for "continuous" limits, or $l > l_c$ but finite for "long discontinuous" systems. In this regime, the fibre ends contribute negligibly to load-bearing, and stress transfer from the matrix to the fibre becomes highly efficient.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus context, the analysis of long fibre composites involves:
- Modelling the **stress transfer mechanism** from the polymeric matrix to the embedded fibre via the **interfacial shear stress ($\tau_i$)**.
- Determining the **critical fibre length** below which fibres pull out before fracture.
- Predicting the **tensile strength** and **elastic modulus** of the composite using modified forms of the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Short Fibre Composites:**
> - **Short fibres ($l < l_c$):** Fibres pull out; composite strength is matrix-dominated.
> - **Long fibres ($l \gg l_c$):** Fibres fracture before pulling out; strength is fibre-dominated.
> - **Continuous fibres ($l = \infty$):** End effects vanish; ideal upper-bound case.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to pull a long, smooth steel rod embedded in a block of soft clay. If the rod is **very short** (a stub), it slips out easily — the clay cannot grip it. But if the rod is **very long**, the friction (shear) acting along its entire surface accumulates so strongly that the rod breaks before it can be pulled out.

**This is the essence of long fibre composites:**
- The **fibre** = the steel rod (load-bearing element).
- The **matrix** = the soft clay (load-transfer medium).
- The **interface** = friction/shear grip between them.
- A sufficiently **long fibre** builds up enough interfacial shear stress to reach the fibre's ultimate tensile strength — so the fibre **breaks**, rather than pulls out.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the fibre as a "tug-of-war" rope. A short rope slips through the hands (matrix). A long rope transfers all the tension (load) to the strongest links (fibre strength) before any slip occurs.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Standard Parameters)

- **Critical fibre length ($l_c$):** the minimum length at which the fibre can be loaded to its ultimate strength.
- **Aspect ratio ($l/d$):** ratio of fibre length to diameter.
- **Fibre volume fraction ($V_f$):** the volume of fibres per unit total composite volume.
- **Interfacial shear strength (IFSS), $\tau_i$:** measured in **MPa**, it is the maximum shear stress the fibre–matrix interface can sustain before debonding.
- **Tensile strength of fibre ($\sigma_f$) and matrix ($\sigma_m$):** in **MPa** or **GPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A composite is classified as a **long fibre composite** when $l \geq l_c$, ensuring that the fibre reaches its fracture stress before any pull-out failure can occur.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanics of Stress Transfer — The Shear Lag Model

In a long fibre composite under axial tensile load, the matrix is responsible for transferring the applied load into the fibre through **interfacial shear stresses ($\tau_i$)**. The classical model for this is the **Cox Shear-Lag Theory (1952)**.

#### 2.1.1 Underlying Assumptions of Cox Shear-Lag Theory

1. The **fibre** is elastic, cylindrical, and aligned along the loading axis.
2. The **matrix** is a homogeneous, isotropic elastic medium.
3. The **interfacial bond** is perfect up to $\tau_i$; debonding is not considered.
4. Only the **axial stress in the fibre** and **shear stress at the interface** are significant.
5. The fibre and matrix are bonded at the interface, with no slip under elastic loading.

> [!NOTE]
> The shear-lag model essentially treats the fibre as a "load collector" and the matrix as a "load conveyor." The efficiency of this transfer is captured by the **shear-lag parameter $\beta$**.

#### 2.1.2 The Shear-Lag Parameter $\beta$

The parameter $\beta$ encapsulates the geometric and material properties governing stress transfer:

$$\beta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \pi G_m}{E_f A_f \ln(R/r)}}$$

Or equivalently, expressed via the fibre radius $r$ and cross-sectional area $A_f$:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{r}\sqrt{\frac{G_m}{E_f \ln(R/r)}}$$

where:
- $G_m$ = shear modulus of the matrix
- $E_f$ = Young's modulus of the fibre
- $r$ = fibre radius
- $R$ = effective matrix "reach" radius (typically half the inter-fibre spacing)
- $A_f$ = fibre cross-sectional area

> [!TIP]
> A **higher $\beta$** means stress builds up faster along the fibre — the fibre reaches its peak stress over a shorter distance from the ends.

#### 2.1.3 Fibre Tensile Stress Distribution

The tensile stress in the fibre at a distance $x$ from one end of a fibre of length $l$ (with origin at the centre) is:

$$\sigma_f(x) = E_f \varepsilon \left[1 - \frac{\cosh(\beta(l/2 - x))}{\cosh(\beta l / 2)}\right]$$

- At the **fibre ends** ($x = \pm l/2$): $\sigma_f = 0$ (load is zero where the fibre terminates).
- At the **fibre centre** ($x = 0$): $\sigma_f$ reaches its **maximum value** $\sigma_{f,\max}$.
- As $l \to \infty$ (continuous fibre limit): $\sigma_f(x) \to E_f \varepsilon$ uniformly.

#### 2.1.4 Interfacial Shear Stress Distribution

The shear stress at the interface as a function of $x$ is:

$$\tau(x) = E_f \varepsilon \frac{\beta r}{2} \cdot \frac{\sinh(\beta(l/2 - x))}{\cosh(\beta l / 2)}$$

This **peaks at the fibre ends** and decays exponentially towards the centre.

### 2.2 Critical Fibre Length — Force Balance Derivation Concept

By performing an axial force balance on a small fibre element of length $dx$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_f}{dx} = -\frac{4 \tau_i}{d}$$

Integrating over half the fibre length, and setting the maximum fibre stress equal to its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important equation** in the analysis of long fibre composites. It tells you the minimum fibre length required for **full fibre efficiency**.

### 2.3 Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for Long Fibre Composites

#### 2.3.1 Modulus (Valid for Both Short and Long Fibres)

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \text{(Iso-strain / Voigt Model)}$$

This is valid for **aligned, continuous (or long) fibres** loaded along the fibre axis.

#### 2.3.2 Strength of Long Discontinuous Aligned Fibre Composites

For long but **finite** fibres (with $l > l_c$), the average fibre stress is less than $\sigma_{fu}$ due to end effects. The composite strength becomes:

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f \left(1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\right) + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$$

where:
- $\sigma_m'$ = matrix stress at the strain corresponding to fibre fracture
- The term $(1 - l_c / 2l)$ is the **fibre length efficiency factor**, $\eta_l$

#### 2.3.3 Strength of Continuous Fibre Composites (Upper Bound)

As $l \to \infty$:

$$\sigma_{cu}^{\text{cont}} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | **Critical fibre length** | $\sigma_{fu}$ = fibre strength, $d$ = fibre diameter, $\tau_i$ = IFSS | mm, MPa |
| 2 | $l_c / d = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}$ | **Critical aspect ratio** | Dimensionless ratio | — |
| 3 | $\sigma_f(x) = E_f \varepsilon \left[1 - \dfrac{\cosh(\beta(l/2 - x))}{\cosh(\beta l/2)}\right]$ | **Cox fibre stress distribution** | $\beta$ = shear-lag parameter | MPa |
| 4 | $\tau(x) = E_f \varepsilon \dfrac{\beta r}{2} \dfrac{\sinh(\beta(l/2 - x))}{\cosh(\beta l/2)}$ | **Interfacial shear stress distribution** | Peaked at fibre ends | MPa |
| 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{r}\sqrt{\dfrac{G_m}{E_f \ln(R/r)}}$ | **Shear-lag parameter** | Geometric + material | m$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Rule of mixtures — modulus** | Iso-strain model | GPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f (1 - l_c / 2l) + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$ | **Long discontinuous fibre strength** | Length-corrected ROM | MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_{cu}^{\text{cont}} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$ | **Continuous fibre strength (upper bound)** | As $l \to \infty$ | MPa |
| 9 | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$ | **Fibre length efficiency factor** | $\eta_l \to 1$ as $l \to \infty$ | — |
| 10 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | **Volume conservation** | Always | — |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical bar in expressions like $\lvert x \rvert$ is rendered as `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact. Do NOT use plain `|` in cells.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The analysis of long fibre composites is critical in:

- **Aerospace:** Carbon-fibre/epoxy wings and fuselage skins (Boeing 787, Airbus A350).
- **Automotive:** Glass-fibre/polyamide bumper beams, CFRP drive shafts.
- **Wind Energy:** Glass-fibre/polyester turbine blades (50+ m long).
- **Sports Equipment:** Tennis rackets, bicycle frames, hockey sticks.
- **Civil Engineering:** Basalt/glass fibre rebar replacing steel in corrosion-prone structures.

In each case, designers use the critical length formula to **select fibre–matrix combinations** that ensure fibres break (not pull out) under service loads, maximising toughness *and* strength.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Fibre Length from Force Balance

> **Starting premise:** A cylindrical fibre of diameter $d$ is embedded in a matrix. Tensile load is applied along the fibre axis. The matrix transfers load to the fibre via interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$.

#### Step 1 — Force Equilibrium on a Fibre Element

Consider a fibre element of length $dx$ at position $x$ from the fibre end. The fibre carries axial tensile stress $\sigma_f(x)$ on one face and $\sigma_f(x) + d\sigma_f$ on the opposite face. The lateral surface (circumference $\pi d$) carries shear stress $\tau_i$ acting opposite to the load.

Force balance along the fibre axis:

$$\left(\sigma_f + d\sigma_f\right) \cdot A_f = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot dx$$

> *This equates the force on the right face to the sum of the force on the left face plus the shear traction acting on the cylindrical lateral surface.*

#### Step 2 — Simplify the Equation

$$d\sigma_f \cdot A_f = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot dx$$

Substitute $A_f = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$:

$$d\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot dx$$

Cancel $\pi d$ from both sides:

$$\frac{d\sigma_f \cdot d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot dx$$

Rearrange to get the **gradient of fibre stress**:

$$\frac{d\sigma_f}{dx} = \frac{4 \tau_i}{d}$$

#### Step 3 — Integrate Over Half the Fibre Length

The fibre stress builds up from $0$ at the tip ($x = 0$) to a maximum at the centre ($x = l/2$). Integrating:

$$\int_0^{l/2} d\sigma_f = \int_0^{l/2} \frac{4 \tau_i}{d} \, dx$$

$$\sigma_f(l/2) - \sigma_f(0) = \frac{4 \tau_i}{d} \cdot \frac{l}{2}$$

Applying the boundary conditions $\sigma_f(0) = 0$ and $\sigma_f(l/2) = \sigma_{fu}$ (fibre reaches ultimate strength at the centre):

$$\sigma_{fu} = \frac{2 \tau_i \cdot l}{d}$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for the Critical Length

Rearranging for $l$, and identifying this special length as $l_c$ (the minimum length for full fibre efficiency):

$$\boxed{\,l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}\,}$$

> *This is the **critical fibre length**: the minimum length over which the interfacial shear can build up enough axial stress to equal the fibre's fracture strength.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Fibre Stress in Long Discontinuous Fibre Composite

For a long but finite fibre of length $l > l_c$, the fibre stress varies linearly from zero at each end to $\sigma_{fu}$ at the centre (a good approximation when $\beta l \gg 1$).

#### Step 1 — Express $\sigma_f(x)$ Linearly

For $0 \leq x \leq l/2$:

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \sigma_{fu}}{l} \cdot x$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Average Fibre Stress

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{l} \int_0^l \sigma_f(x) \, dx = \frac{2}{l} \int_0^{l/2} \frac{2 \sigma_{fu}}{l} \cdot x \, dx$$

> *Using symmetry: integrate over half the fibre and multiply by 2.*

#### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{4 \sigma_{fu}}{l^2} \int_0^{l/2} x \, dx = \frac{4 \sigma_{fu}}{l^2} \cdot \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^{l/2}$$

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{4 \sigma_{fu}}{l^2} \cdot \frac{l^2}{8} = \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2}$$

#### Step 4 — Refine Using Critical Length Concept

The above is for the boundary case $l = l_c$. For a general long fibre ($l > l_c$), the **end region** of length $l_c / 2$ at each tip contributes only partial stress. The average fibre stress becomes:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \sigma_{fu} \left(1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\right)$$

> *Logic: At each end, a length $l_c/2$ ramps stress from 0 to $\sigma_{fu}$. The fractional "wasted" length per fibre is $l_c / l$, and it is distributed symmetrically, giving the average correction factor.*

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Tensile Strength of Long Fibre Composite (Rule of Mixtures)

Apply a simple force-balance / iso-strain model to the composite cross-section.

#### Step 1 — Iso-Strain Assumption

When fibres and matrix are well-bonded and aligned, both phases experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ at the instant of fibre fracture:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_{fu}$$

#### Step 2 — Force on the Composite Cross-Section

Total force = Force in fibres + Force in matrix:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

$$\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m$$

#### Step 3 — Divide by Total Area

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

Using $V_f = A_f / A_c$ and $V_m = A_m / A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

#### Step 4 — Insert the Average Fibre Stress for Discontinuous Long Fibres

For long discontinuous fibres ($l > l_c$), substitute $\sigma_f = \bar{\sigma}_f = \sigma_{fu}(1 - l_c/2l)$ and $\sigma_m = \sigma_m'$ (matrix stress at the strain of fibre fracture):

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f \left(1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\right) + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)\,}$$

#### Step 5 — Continuous Fibre Limit

As $l \to \infty$, the efficiency factor $(1 - l_c / 2l) \to 1$:

$$\sigma_{cu}^{\text{cont}} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$$

> *This is the **theoretical maximum** strength achievable from a given fibre–matrix pair.*

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Cox Shear-Lag Stress Distribution (Conceptual Outline)

Cox derived the elastic stress distribution by considering the displacement mismatch between a fibre and its surrounding matrix shell.

#### Step 1 — Set Up the Differential Equation

The axial force in the fibre element $dx$ changes due to interfacial shear:

$$\frac{d\sigma_f}{dx} = -\frac{4 \tau(x)}{d}$$

The shear stress is related to the **displacement difference** $\Delta u = u_m - u_f$ between matrix and fibre:

$$\tau(x) = \frac{G_m}{r} \cdot \frac{\Delta u}{\ln(R/r)}$$

where $R$ is the effective matrix outer radius.

#### Step 2 — Combine with Constitutive Relations

Using $\sigma_f = E_f \cdot du_f/dx$ and the matrix being strained uniformly ($\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$ at far-field), one obtains:

$$\frac{d^2 \sigma_f}{dx^2} - \beta^2 \sigma_f = -\beta^2 E_f \varepsilon$$

with $\beta^2 = \dfrac{4 G_m}{E_f d^2 \ln(R/r)}$.

#### Step 3 — Solve the Second-Order ODE

The general solution is:

$$\sigma_f(x) = A \cosh(\beta x) + B \sinh(\beta x) + E_f \varepsilon$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions

At the fibre ends, the tensile stress must vanish: $\sigma_f(\pm l/2) = 0$.

By symmetry, the $\sinh$ term vanishes ($B = 0$), giving:

$$\sigma_f(x) = E_f \varepsilon \left[1 - \frac{\cosh(\beta(l/2 - x))}{\cosh(\beta l/2)}\right]$$

> *This is the classical Cox result: fibre stress rises from zero at the tips and saturates at $E_f \varepsilon$ towards the centre, with the rise governed by $\beta$.*

#### Step 5 — Long Fibre Limit

When $l \gg 1/\beta$, $\cosh(\beta l/2) \to \infty$, and the entire fibre (except near the tips) carries the full load:

$$\sigma_f(x) \to E_f \varepsilon \quad \text{for most of the fibre length}$$

> *This recovers the **continuous fibre limit** and justifies the use of the simpler ROM for long fibre composites.*

---

### 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A glass-fibre reinforced polyester composite has the following properties:
- Fibre diameter: $d = 10 \, \mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Fibre tensile strength: $\sigma_{fu} = 2.5$ GPa = 2500 MPa
- Interfacial shear strength: $\tau_i = 50$ MPa
- Fibre volume fraction: $V_f = 0.4$
- Matrix strength at fibre fracture strain: $\sigma_m' = 30$ MPa
- Fibre length: $l = 2$ mm

**Find:**
(a) Critical fibre length and critical aspect ratio.
(b) The length efficiency factor.
(c) The tensile strength of the long fibre composite.

#### Solution:

**(a) Critical fibre length:**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{2500 \times 10}{2 \times 50} = \frac{25000}{100} = 250 \, \mu m$$

**Critical aspect ratio:**

$$\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{250}{10} = 25$$

**(b) Length efficiency factor:**

$$\eta_l = 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} = 1 - \frac{250}{2 \times 2000} = 1 - \frac{250}{4000} = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

**(c) Composite tensile strength:**

$$\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f \eta_l + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$$

$$\sigma_{cu} = 2500 \times 0.4 \times 0.9375 + 30 \times 0.6$$

$$\sigma_{cu} = 937.5 + 18 = 955.5 \, \text{MPa}$$

> *The composite has a tensile strength of approximately **956 MPa**, which is far higher than the neat matrix (~30–80 MPa), demonstrating the power of long fibre reinforcement.*

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Long Fibre Composite Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Composite Specs] --> node1[Material Properties Input]
    node1 --> node2[Fibre: Ef, sigma_fu, d, Vf]
    node1 --> node3[Matrix: Em, Gm, sigma_m prime]
    node1 --> node4[Interface: tau_i]
    node2 --> node5[Compute Critical Length lc]
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6{Is l greater than lc?}
    node6 -- Yes --> node7[Classify as LONG Fibre Composite]
    node6 -- No --> node8[Classify as SHORT Fibre Composite]
    node7 --> node9[Compute Shear Lag Parameter beta]
    node9 --> node10[Derive Fibre Stress Profile sigma_f x]
    node9 --> node11[Derive IFSS Profile tau x]
    node10 --> node12[Compute Average Fibre Stress]
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13[Apply Rule of Mixtures]
    node13 --> node14[Compute Composite Modulus Ec]
    node13 --> node15[Compute Composite Strength sigma_cu]
    node15 --> node16[Output: Final Mechanical Properties]
    node14 --> node16
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Build-up Along a Long Fibre (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    tipA[Fibre Tip Left sigma_f equals 0] -->|Increasing x| midA[Fibre Centre sigma_f equals sigma_fu]
    midA -->|Increasing x| tipB[Fibre Tip Right sigma_f equals 0]
    ifssA[IFSS Peak at Left Tip] --> ifssB[IFSS Tapers to Centre]
    ifssB --> ifssC[IFSS Peak at Right Tip]
    shearA[Matrix Transfers Load via tau_i] --> ifssA
    shearA --> ifssC
```

> *Interpretation: The fibre stress profile is "U-shaped" with a plateau at the centre, while the interfacial shear stress profile is "Λ-shaped" (peaked at the ends). Both profiles converge to idealised limits as the fibre becomes effectively continuous.*

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Long vs. Short Fibre Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Short Fibre l less than lc]
        a1[Fibre Pulls Out] --> a2[Strength limited by tau_i and l]
        a2 --> a3[Composite Failure: Matrix dominated]
    end
    subgraph B[Long Fibre l greater than lc]
        b1[Fibre Reaches sigma_fu] --> b2[Strength limited by sigma_fu]
        b2 --> b3[Composite Failure: Fibre dominated]
    end
    A -- Critical Threshold at lc --> B
```

### 4.4 Engineering Application Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    design[Design Requirement: Load X, Stiffness Y] --> selectF[Select Fibre: Carbon / Glass / Aramid]
    design --> selectM[Select Matrix: Epoxy / Polyester / PEEK]
    selectF --> pair[Form Fibre-Matrix Pair]
    selectM --> pair
    pair --> iface[Engineer Interface: Sizing / Coupling Agents]
    iface --> lc[Calculate Critical Length]
    lc --> check{l greater than lc?}
    check -- Yes --> proceed[Proceed to Composite Fabrication]
    check -- No --> redesign[Increase l or Improve Interface]
    redesign --> iface
    proceed --> test[Mechanical Testing: Tensile / Flexural / ILSS]
    test --> validate[Validate against Design Targets]
```

> *This block diagram maps the engineering decision-making process for designing long fibre composite components.*
