
## 4.3 Schematic — Force Distribution on a Single Fibre

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["EXTERNAL TENSILE LOAD ON COMPOSITE"]:::input
    M["MATRIX SURROUNDING FIBRE CARRIES SHEAR LOAD"]:::matrix
    I1["INTERFACIAL SHEAR TAU_i ACTING UPWARD FROM END 1"]:::interface
    F1["FIBRE HALF LENGTH L BY 2"]:::fibre
    CTR2["FIBRE CENTRE: TENSILE STRESS EQUALS SIGMA_f MAX"]:::fibre
    I2["INTERFACIAL SHEAR TAU_i ACTING UPWARD FROM END 2"]:::interface
    EQ["EQUILIBRIUM: TAU_i TIMES 2 pi r TIMES L_c BY 2 EQUALS SIGMA_f TIMES pi r SQUARED"]:::math

    TOP --> M
    M --> I1
    I1 --> F1
    F1 --> CTR2
    CTR2 --> I2
    I2 --> EQ

    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef matrix fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef interface fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    classDef fibre fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef math fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define critical fibre length and explain its significance in fibre-reinforced composites.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Critical fibre length ($L_c$) is the minimum length of a fibre required to develop its ultimate tensile strength when embedded in a matrix. It is given by $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$.
- **Significance (2 Marks):** It serves as the design threshold for fibre selection. Fibres shorter than $L_c$ will pull out of the matrix without achieving their full load-bearing capacity, resulting in inefficient reinforcement. Fibres longer than $L_c$ will fracture under load, ensuring efficient stress transfer. In practice, discontinuous fibre composites are designed with $L/d$ ratios well above the critical aspect ratio to compensate for processing-induced fibre breakage and ensure effective reinforcement.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Distinguish between fibre pull-out and fibre fracture with reference to critical fibre length.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Fibre Pull-Out (1.5 Marks):** Occurs when $L < L_c$. The fibre-matrix interfacial bond fails before the fibre reaches its ultimate strength. The fibre slides out of the matrix, dissipating energy through friction. This is favoured for impact-resistant applications.
- **Fibre Fracture (1.5 Marks):** Occurs when $L > L_c$. The fibre develops its full ultimate tensile strength at the centre and breaks there. This provides maximum strength but with less energy dissipation. This is the desired failure mode for high-strength structural composites.
- **Connection:** $L_c$ is the boundary between these two failure modes; it is calculated by force balance and depends on fibre strength and interfacial shear strength.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Derive the expression for critical fibre length $L_c$ in a short-fibre composite from first principles using force balance.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Cylindrical fibre of radius $r$, length $L$, embedded in a matrix with interfacial shear strength $\tau_i$. Fibre ultimate tensile strength is $\sigma_f$.

**Step 1:** The load is transferred from the matrix to the fibre through interfacial shear stresses acting on the cylindrical fibre surface.

**Step 2:** The tensile stress in the fibre is zero at its ends ($x = 0$ and $x = L$) and builds up to a maximum at the centre ($x = L/2$).

**Step 3:** Consider the half-length of the fibre from one end to the centre. The tensile force at the centre is:

$$F_{\text{tensile}} = \sigma_f \cdot \pi r^2$$

**Step 4:** The shear force transferred from the matrix to the fibre over the half-length $L/2$ is:

$$F_{\text{shear}} = \tau_i \cdot (2\pi r) \cdot (L/2) = \pi r L \tau_i$$

**Step 5:** Applying equilibrium for the critical condition (fibre just reaches ultimate strength):

$$\sigma_f \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau_i \cdot \pi r L_c$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $L_c$:

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot r}{\tau_i} = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2\tau_i}$$

This is the required expression for critical fibre length. **(7 Marks — 1 mark each for the six steps.)**

---

**(b) A glass-fibre reinforced polyester composite has fibre tensile strength $\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa, fibre diameter $d = 14\ \mu m$, and interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 30$ MPa. Calculate the critical fibre length, the critical aspect ratio, and comment on whether a 0.4 mm fibre is suitable for reinforcement.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Critical Fibre Length:**

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2\tau_i} = \frac{2400 \times 14 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mm}}{2 \times 30}$$

$$L_c = \frac{2400 \times 0.014}{60} = \frac{33.6}{60}$$

$$L_c = 0.56\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Critical Aspect Ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_c = \frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{0.56}{0.014} = 40$$

**Step 3 — Comment on Suitability of 0.4 mm Fibre:**

For a 0.4 mm fibre: $L = 0.4$ mm $< L_c = 0.56$ mm. Therefore, the fibre is **sub-critical** and will undergo **fibre pull-out** rather than fibre fracture.

**Conclusion:** The 0.4 mm fibre is **not suitable** for effective reinforcement. The recommended fibre length should be at least $L > L_c = 0.56$ mm (in practice, $L \approx 10 \times L_c = 5.6$ mm is used to account for processing breakage). The composite will show pull-out-dominated failure with reduced strength.

**(7 Marks — 2 marks for $L_c$, 2 marks for aspect ratio, 3 marks for the comment.)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Explain the Kelly–Tyson model for stress distribution in a short fibre. How is critical fibre length derived from this model?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 Marks):** The Kelly–Tyson model (1965) is the foundational analytical model describing stress transfer in short-fibre composites. It assumes:
- Perfect bonding at the fibre-matrix interface (until debonding).
- The matrix shear stress at the interface is constant and equal to $\tau_i$ along the fibre length.
- The fibre ends do not carry load (only the cylindrical surface transfers shear).

**Stress Distribution (3 Marks):** The tensile stress $\sigma_f(x)$ in the fibre builds up linearly with distance $x$ from the fibre end:

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{\tau_i \cdot x}{r}$$

- At $x = 0$ (fibre end): $\sigma_f = 0$
- At $x = L/2$ (fibre centre): $\sigma_f$ reaches its maximum value.

**Derivation of $L_c$ (2 Marks):** When $\sigma_f(L/2) = \sigma_f$ (the ultimate strength), the corresponding length is the critical fibre length:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{\tau_i \cdot (L_c/2)}{r} \implies L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2\tau_i}$$

This shows that $L_c$ depends on the fibre strength and interfacial shear stress.

---

**(b) A carbon-fibre reinforced epoxy composite uses fibres with $\sigma_f = 4000$ MPa, $d = 7\ \mu m$, and $\tau_i = 50$ MPa. After processing, the actual fibre length is found to be $L = 1.0$ mm. Determine whether the fibre will pull out or fracture, and calculate the maximum tensile stress in the fibre at its centre.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Critical Fibre Length:**

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2\tau_i} = \frac{4000 \times 7 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 50} = \frac{4000 \times 0.007}{100} = \frac{28}{100}$$

$$L_c = 0.28\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compare Actual Length to Critical Length:**

$$L = 1.0\ \text{mm} > L_c = 0.28\ \text{mm}$$

Since $L > L_c$, the fibre is **super-critical** and will **fracture** (not pull out). The fibre is suitable for effective reinforcement.

**Step 3 — Calculate Maximum Tensile Stress at Fibre Centre:**

Since $L > L_c$, the fibre reaches its ultimate strength at its centre:

$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \sigma_f = 4000\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Verification Using Linear Buildup:**

The centre is at $x = L/2 = 0.5$ mm. Since $0.5$ mm $\gg$ $L_c/2 = 0.14$ mm, the stress saturates at the maximum value:

$$\sigma_f(0.5\ \text{mm}) = \min\left(\frac{\tau_i \cdot x}{r},\ \sigma_f\right) = \min\left(\frac{50 \times 0.5}{0.0035},\ 4000\right) = \min(7143,\ 4000) = 4000\ \text{MPa}$$

**Conclusion:** The fibre carries its full ultimate strength of **4000 MPa** and will fracture at its centre. The composite is efficiently reinforced. **(7 Marks — 2 marks for $L_c$, 2 marks for comparison, 2 marks for stress calculation, 1 mark for conclusion.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 Mark):** Most students lose marks by mixing µm with mm in the same equation. Always convert all lengths to the **same unit** (preferably mm) before substituting into the formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 (–1 Mark):** A common error is writing $L_c = \sigma_f d / \tau_i$ instead of $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$. The factor of 2 comes from the half-length used in the force balance.
> 3. **Not Stating the Failure Mode (–1 Mark):** After computing $L_c$, you **must** explicitly compare it with the given $L$ and conclude whether pull-out or fracture occurs. Examiners specifically look for this comparison.
> 4. **Confusing $\tau_i$ with $\tau_y$ (–1 Mark):** If both interfacial shear strength and matrix shear yield strength are given, use $\tau_{\text{eff}} = \min(\tau_i, \tau_y)$ as per the Kelly–Tyson model.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Critical Fibre Length**

- **Definition:** $L_c$ = minimum fibre length to develop ultimate tensile strength in a matrix.

- **Canonical Formula:** $L_c = \sigma_f \cdot d / (2\tau_i)$ — must be memorised.

- **Critical Aspect Ratio:** $(L/d)_c = \sigma_f / (2\tau_i)$ — dimensionless design parameter.

- **Linear Stress Buildup:** $\sigma_f(x) = \tau_i \cdot x / r$ — increases from 0 at fibre end to $\sigma_f$ at centre.

- **Three Regimes:**
   - $L < L_c$ → Sub-critical → **Pull-out** → Lower strength, higher toughness.
   - $L = L_c$ → Critical → Transition point.
   - $L > L_c$ → Super-critical → **Fracture** → Higher strength, lower toughness.

- **Derivation Logic:** Force balance: $\pi r^2 \sigma_f = 2\pi r \tau_i (L_c/2)$.

- **Material Property:** Use $\tau_{\text{eff}} = \min(\tau_i, \tau_y)$ if both are provided.

- **Practical Design Rule:** Always select $L \ge 10 \cdot L_c$ to account for fibre breakage during processing (extrusion, injection moulding).

- **Typical Values:**
   - Glass fibre / epoxy: $L_c \approx 0.5$–$1.5$ mm
   - Carbon fibre / epoxy: $L_c \approx 0.2$–$0.5$ mm
   - Natural fibre / polyester: $L_c \approx 1$–$10$ mm

- **Engineering Relevance:** Critical for designing short-fibre composites used in automotive, aerospace, sporting goods, and construction industries.

- **KTU Exam Weightage:** Frequently asked as 3-mark definition, 7-mark derivation, and 14-mark numerical problem (with internal choice).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rule of Mixtures, Preparation and Properties of Glass Fibre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rule of Mixtures, Preparation and Properties of Glass Fibre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rule of Mixtures (ROM) — Formal Definition

The **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** is a fundamental analytical framework in composite materials engineering that estimates the effective properties of a heterogeneous composite material by volume-weighted (or mass-weighted) averaging of the individual constituent (matrix + reinforcement) properties. It provides first-order, boundary predictions of effective physical and mechanical properties such as **density**, **Young's modulus (longitudinal & transverse)**, **tensile strength**, **Poisson's ratio**, **thermal conductivity**, and **electrical conductivity** of fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The Rule of Mixtures is the *foundational predictive tool* for FRP composite design. In KTU university examinations, derivation and numerical application of ROM for **density, modulus (parallel & series), and tensile strength** form the most frequently tested questions in this module.

The two principal ROM variants are:

| Model | Assumption | Load Transfer | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voigt Model (Parallel / Isostrain)** | Strain is uniform in both phases | Load shared proportional to stiffness | Longitudinal fibre direction (UD composite) |
| **Reuss Model (Series / Isostress)** | Stress is uniform in both phases | Load shared proportional to volume | Transverse fibre direction |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Concrete & Steel Rod" Metaphor**
> Imagine you are building a **ladder**. You glue two wooden side rails (soft, cheap) and reinforce them with horizontal metal rungs (hard, strong). The ladder's *along-the-rail strength* (longitudinal) is dominated by the wood plus reinforcement, while the *perpendicular-to-rail* strength depends on the weakest glue joint.
> - The **Rule of Mixtures** is like asking: *"If I have 30% metal and 70% wood, what is the ladder's overall strength?"*
> - The answer depends on **how the load is applied**: along the rungs (parallel) → use the **Voigt model**; across the rungs (series) → use the **Reuss model**.
> - This simple weighting concept is the **Rule of Mixtures**.

For polymer composites, replace wood with **polymer matrix** and metal rungs with **glass fibres**. The same weighting principle gives us the composite's effective property.

## 1.3 Glass Fibre — Formal Definition

**Glass fibre** is an inorganic, non-metallic, silica-based (SiO₂) continuous or discontinuous filament material manufactured by extruding molten glass through fine-bore spinnerets (bushings) at temperatures around **1250 °C to 1350 °C**, then rapidly attenuating (drawing) the molten streams at high speed to form filaments of diameter typically **5 µm to 25 µm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Glass fibres are graded by **filament diameter** (in **µm** or **µin**), **tex (g/km)**, **yards/lb**, and **tensile strength** (in **GPa** or **ksi**).

## 1.4 Types of Glass Fibre (KTU-Relevant Classification)

| Glass Type | Composition Hallmark | Key Properties | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A-Glass (Alkali / Soda-Lime)** | High Na₂O (~14%) | Low cost, low strength | Window glass, containers |
| **E-Glass (Electrical)** | Low alkali, alumina-borosilicate | High strength, high electrical resistivity | **FRP composites (most common)** |
| **S-Glass (Structural / High Strength)** | High MgO, Al₂O₃ | Highest tensile strength | Aerospace, defence |
| **C-Glass (Chemical)** | High CaO, high B₂O₃ | Excellent chemical/acid resistance | Chemical tanks, pipes |
| **D-Glass (Dielectric)** | High B₂O₃ | Low dielectric constant | Electronics, radomes |
| **R-Glass / T-Glass** | High strength variants | High tensile + impact | High-performance FRP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** The most tested glass type is **E-Glass** due to its dominant use in polymer composites. Memorize its typical tensile strength (**~3.5 GPa**) and Young's modulus (**~72 GPa**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic of the Rule of Mixtures

The ROM is grounded in **classical continuum mechanics** and assumes:
1. **Perfect bonding** at the fibre-matrix interface (no slip).
2. **Homogeneous dispersion** of the reinforcement.
3. **Linear elastic** behaviour of both phases.
4. **Macroscopic isotropy/homogeneity** of the composite as a continuum.

For any general property $P_c$ of a two-phase composite, the ROM is expressed as:

$$
P_c = P_m \cdot V_m + P_f \cdot V_f
$$

where:
- $P_c$ = effective property of the **composite**
- $P_m$ = property of the **matrix**
- $P_f$ = property of the **fibre**
- $V_m$ = **volume fraction** of matrix, with $V_m + V_f = 1$
- $V_f$ = **volume fraction** of fibre

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each ROM Variant

### A. Density ROM (Universal — Independent of Geometry)

For density, the composite mass is conserved additively:

$$
\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does density ROM hold universally?** Because **mass** is an **extensive property** that adds linearly with the volume of each phase — geometry of load transfer does not affect mass conservation.

### B. Young's Modulus — Longitudinal (Voigt / Isostrain / Parallel Model)

When load is applied **parallel to continuous fibres**, the strain $\varepsilon$ is identical in both phases:

$$
E_{c,L} = E_m V_m + E_f V_f = E_m (1 - V_f) + E_f V_f
$$

> **Logic Step-by-Step:**
> 1. Iso-strain: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_f$
> 2. Force balance: $\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_f A_f$
> 3. Divide by $A_c$: $\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f$
> 4. Divide by $\varepsilon_c$: $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$

### C. Young's Modulus — Transverse (Reuss / Isostress / Series Model)

When load is applied **perpendicular to fibres**, the stress $\sigma$ is identical in both phases:

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}
$$

> **Logic Step-by-Step:**
> 1. Iso-stress: $\sigma_c = \sigma_m = \sigma_f$
> 2. Strain compatibility: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m V_m + \varepsilon_f V_f$
> 3. Divide by $\sigma_c$: $\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}$

### D. Tensile Strength ROM (Modified Voigt for Brittle Fibre + Ductile Matrix)

$$
\sigma_{c,u} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} (1 - V_f)
$$

A more accurate form accounts for fibre length and orientation efficiency $\eta$:

$$
\sigma_{c,u} = \eta \cdot \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} (1 - V_f)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** The strength ROM uses the **ultimate (failure) stress** of each phase, not the yield stress. For a brittle fibre, use the **fracture strength**; for a ductile matrix, use the **yield or ultimate stress** as specified in the problem.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they are the most heavily tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.**

| # | Property | Rule of Mixtures Equation | Units (SI) | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Density** | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1-V_f)$ | kg/m³ | $0 \le V_f \le 1$ |
| 2 | **Longitudinal Young's Modulus** | $E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m (1-V_f)$ | Pa (GPa) | Upper bound (Voigt) |
| 3 | **Transverse Young's Modulus** | $\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{(1-V_f)}{E_m}$ | Pa (GPa) | Lower bound (Reuss) |
| 4 | **Longitudinal Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u}(1-V_f)$ | Pa (MPa) | For UD aligned fibres |
| 5 | **Longitudinal Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu_{c,L} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m (1-V_f)$ | dimensionless | — |
| 6 | **Thermal Conductivity (Parallel)** | $k_{c,L} = k_f V_f + k_m (1-V_f)$ | W/(m·K) | Heat flow parallel to fibres |
| 7 | **Thermal Conductivity (Series)** | $\frac{1}{k_{c,T}} = \frac{V_f}{k_f} + \frac{(1-V_f)}{k_m}$ | W/(m·K) | Heat flow transverse to fibres |
| 8 | **Electrical Resistivity (Series)** | $\frac{1}{\rho_{e,c}} = \frac{V_f}{\rho_{e,f}} + \frac{(1-V_f)}{\rho_{e,m}}$ | (Ω·m)⁻¹ | — |
| 9 | **Volume Fraction Conversion** | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | dimensionless | Always true |
| 10 | **Mass Fraction → Volume Fraction** | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + w_m / \rho_m}$ | dimensionless | $w_i$ = mass fraction |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Rule of Mixtures

1. **Aerospace Structural Design:** Predicting stiffness of carbon/glass-epoxy wing skins.
2. **Automotive Lightweighting:** Estimating specific strength (strength-to-weight) of glass-fibre/polypropylene bumpers.
3. **Marine Industry:** Designing glass/polyester boat hulls for optimal $V_f$ (typically 30–45%).
4. **Wind Turbine Blades:** Determining optimal glass-fibre volume fraction for fatigue resistance.
5. **Quality Control in FRP Manufacturing:** Using measured $\rho_c$ to back-calculate actual $V_f$ (since $\rho$ ROM is the most reliable).

## 2.5 Preparation of Glass Fibre — Theory & Process Steps

### A. Raw Materials (Batch Composition)

| Raw Material | Chemical Formula | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Silica Sand | SiO₂ | Glass network former (primary) |
| Soda Ash | Na₂CO₃ | Flux (lowers melting point) |
| Limestone | CaCO₃ | Stabilizer |
| Borax / Boric Acid | B₂O₃ / H₃BO₃ | Improves thermal/chemical resistance |
| Feldspar | Al₂O₃·SiO₂ | Source of Al₂O₃ |
| Magnesite | MgCO₃ | Source of MgO (in S-glass) |

### B. Manufacturing Process Flow (Continuous Filament Process)

1. **Batch Mixing & Weighing** — Raw materials proportioned by recipe.
2. **Melting** — Batch fed into a gas-fired or electric furnace; melted at **~1250–1350 °C**.
3. **Refining / Homogenization** — Bubbling, settling to remove bubbles and inhomogeneities.
4. **Bushing (Spinneret) Extrusion** — Molten glass flows through a platinum-rhodium bushing containing **200 to 6000 holes** (each ~1–2 mm dia).
5. **Attenuation (Drawing)** — Streams are drawn down at high speed (≈ 50–100 m/s) to final diameter of **5–25 µm**.
6. **Cooling & Solidification** — Filaments solidify via radiative + convective cooling.
7. **Sizing Application** — Aqueous emulsion (silanes, film-formers, lubricants, antistatic agents) applied to protect filaments and promote matrix adhesion.
8. **Collecting / Winding** — Filaments gathered into a **strand** and wound on a collet at high speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Staple Fibre (Chopped Strand) Process:** Molten streams are attenuated by a **rotating jet of high-velocity air/steam** which fractures the filaments into short lengths (3–25 cm), collected as a sliver/cake for non-woven mats.

### C. Surface Treatment (Sizing) — Detailed Functions

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Silanes (e.g., γ-Methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane)** | Coupling agent — forms chemical bond between glass and resin |
| **Film-Former (PVAc, epoxy emulsion)** | Holds filaments together, prevents abrasion |
| **Lubricants** | Reduce fibre-fibre and fibre-metal friction |
| **Antistatic Agents** | Prevent static buildup during processing |
| **Surfactants / Emulsifiers** | Stabilize the aqueous emulsion |

## 2.6 Properties of Glass Fibre (Engineering Reference Data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the E-Glass property block below — these values are repeatedly used in KTU numerical problems.**

| Property | E-Glass | S-Glass | C-Glass | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density** | 2.54 – 2.60 | 2.48 – 2.49 | 2.45 – 2.55 | g/cm³ |
| **Tensile Strength** | 3.4 – 3.5 | 4.5 – 4.9 | 3.1 – 3.4 | GPa |
| **Young's Modulus** | 72 – 76 | 86 – 90 | 70 – 75 | GPa |
| **Elongation at Break** | 4.5 – 4.8 | 5.0 – 5.4 | 4.5 – 5.0 | % |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | 0.22 | 0.22 | 0.21 | — |
| **Softening Point** | 846 | 1056 | 750 | °C |
| **Annealing Point** | 657 | 816 | 588 | °C |
| **Strain Point** | 614 | 759 | 522 | °C |
| **Thermal Conductivity** | 1.0 – 1.3 | 1.0 – 1.3 | 1.0 | W/(m·K) |
| **Dielectric Strength** | 9 – 12 | 8 – 11 | 8 – 12 | kV/mm |
| **Specific Heat** | 810 | 760 | 800 | J/(kg·K) |
| **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion** | 5.0 × 10⁻⁶ | 2.9 × 10⁻⁶ | 7.5 × 10⁻⁶ | /K |

### Key Engineering Properties Explained:

- **High Tensile Strength-to-Weight Ratio:** Glass fibre has specific strength ~1.4 GPa/(g/cm³), comparable to steel (~0.2 GPa/(g/cm³)).
- **Brittle Behaviour:** Elastic up to fracture — no plastic deformation, no yield point.
- **Thermal Stability:** Softening above 800 °C (E-Glass); usable up to ~300 °C in polymer matrices.
- **Moisture Sensitivity:** Uncoated glass absorbs moisture → reduces strength. Sizing mitigates this.
- **Chemical Inertness:** Resistant to most organics; attacked by HF and strong hot alkalis.
- **Dimensional Stability:** Very low creep under sustained load compared to polymer.
- **Non-Flammable & Non-Conductive:** Excellent for electrical/electronic composites.
- **Transparency to Electromagnetic Radiation:** Used in radome applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rule of Mixtures for Density

**Statement to Prove:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$

### Setup
Consider a representative volume element (RVE) of composite of total volume $V_c$ containing:
- Volume of fibre: $V_f$
- Volume of matrix: $V_m$
- $V_c = V_f + V_m$ ... (i)

### Mass Conservation
Total mass = mass of fibre + mass of matrix:

$$
M_c = M_f + M_m
$$

### Substitute Mass = Density × Volume

$$
\rho_c \cdot V_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m
$$

### Divide by $V_c$

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_c}
$$

### Recognize Volume Fractions

By definition, $V_f^{fraction} = V_f / V_c$ and $V_m^{fraction} = V_m / V_c$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the only ROM that holds **exactly** (not just as a bound) because mass is conserved additively, regardless of fibre geometry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt / Parallel Model)

**Assumptions:**
1. Fibres are continuous, parallel, and aligned with the loading axis.
2. Perfect interfacial bonding — no slip at the fibre-matrix interface.
3. Both matrix and fibre behave as linear elastic.
4. **Iso-strain condition:** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$

### Step 1: Force Equilibrium
The total axial force on the composite cross-section equals the sum of forces carried by fibre and matrix:

$$
F_c = F_f + F_m
$$

### Step 2: Express Force in Terms of Stress
Since $F = \sigma \cdot A$:

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

### Step 3: Divide Throughout by $A_c$

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

If fibre and matrix have uniform cross-sections, the area ratio equals the volume ratio. Therefore:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

### Step 4: Apply Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) and Iso-Strain ($\varepsilon$ identical)

$$
E_c \cdot \varepsilon = E_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_m
$$

### Step 5: Cancel $\varepsilon$ (Non-zero)

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

### Final Result:

$$
\boxed{E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **upper-bound (Voigt)** modulus because load is transferred to the stiffer fibre proportionally to its volume fraction. The composite modulus is always **less than** $E_f$ but **greater than** $E_m$ (since $E_f > E_m$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Transverse Young's Modulus (Reuss / Series Model)

**Assumptions:**
1. Fibres are continuous, parallel, and the load is applied **perpendicular** to the fibre axis.
2. **Iso-stress condition:** $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$

### Step 1: Strain Compatibility (Deformations Add)
The total transverse deformation of the composite is the sum of deformations in each phase (treated as springs in series):

$$
\delta_c = \delta_f + \delta_m
$$

### Step 2: Express Deformation as $\delta = \varepsilon \cdot L$
With each phase having length $L$ in the load direction (iso-stress region):

$$
\varepsilon_c L = \varepsilon_f L_f + \varepsilon_m L_m
$$

For uniform strain regions, the strain over each region is integrated. In the simplest case, $L_f = V_f L$ and $L_m = V_m L$:

$$
\varepsilon_c L = \varepsilon_f V_f L + \varepsilon_m V_m L
$$

### Step 3: Divide by $L$

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m
$$

### Step 4: Apply Hooke's Law: $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$

$$
\frac{\sigma_c}{E_c} = \frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} V_m
$$

### Step 5: Apply Iso-Stress ($\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$)

$$
\frac{\sigma}{E_c} = \frac{\sigma V_f}{E_f} + \frac{\sigma V_m}{E_m}
$$

### Step 6: Cancel $\sigma$ and Invert

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{(1-V_f)}{E_m}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **lower-bound (Reuss)** modulus. The transverse modulus is **significantly less** than the longitudinal modulus — this anisotropy is a hallmark of UD fibre composites.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Tensile Strength ROM (for Brittle Fibre + Ductile Matrix)

**Assumption:** Both phases fail at the same composite failure strain (approximation for brittle-fibre composites).

### Step 1: Stress in Composite at Fibre Failure Strain

At the failure strain of the composite (≈ fibre strain), the stress in matrix and fibre are:

$$
\sigma_{c,u} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m}^{'} (1 - V_f)
$$

where $\sigma_{m}^{'}$ = stress in matrix **at the fibre failure strain** (not the matrix ultimate stress).

### Step 2: For Conservative Design (Lower Bound)

Assuming matrix stress at fibre failure is the matrix yield/ultimate stress:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{c,u} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} (1 - V_f)}
$$

### Step 3: With Efficiency Factor $\eta$ (Orientation / Length Effects)

$$
\sigma_{c,u} = \eta \cdot \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} (1 - V_f)
$$

where $\eta = \eta_l \cdot \eta_o$ (length efficiency × orientation efficiency).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Density ROM

**Problem:** A glass-fibre reinforced epoxy composite contains **40% volume fraction** of E-glass fibre. Given $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$, find the density of the composite.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the equation.

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $V_f = 0.40$, $V_m = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$.

$$
\rho_c = (2.55)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term.

$$
(2.55)(0.40) = 1.020 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

$$
(1.20)(0.60) = 0.720 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Add.

$$
\rho_c = 1.020 + 0.720 = 1.740 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\rho_c = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3}$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt)

**Problem:** For a unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite with $V_f = 0.35$, $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$, calculate the longitudinal Young's modulus.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the Voigt (parallel) ROM.

$$
E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
E_{c,L} = (72)(0.35) + (3.5)(0.65)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
E_{c,L} = 25.20 + 2.275 = 27.475 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{E_{c,L} \approx 27.48 \text{ GPa}}$

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Transverse Modulus (Reuss)

**Problem:** Using the same data as Example 2 ($V_f = 0.35$, $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$), find the transverse modulus.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the Reuss (series) ROM.

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{(1 - V_f)}{E_m}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{0.35}{72} + \frac{0.65}{3.5}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute each fraction.

$$
\frac{0.35}{72} = 0.004861 \text{ GPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{0.65}{3.5} = 0.185714 \text{ GPa}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Add.

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = 0.004861 + 0.185714 = 0.190575 \text{ GPa}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5:** Invert.

$$
E_{c,T} = \frac{1}{0.190575} \approx 5.247 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{E_{c,T} \approx 5.25 \text{ GPa}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The ratio $E_{c,L} / E_{c,T} \approx 27.48 / 5.25 \approx 5.24$. The composite is **highly anisotropic** — strong along the fibres, weak across them. This anisotropy is the **defining feature** of FRP composites.

---

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Tensile Strength ROM

**Problem:** A unidirectional E-glass/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.30$. The fibre tensile strength is $\sigma_{f,u} = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$ and the matrix tensile strength is $\sigma_{m,u} = 80 \text{ MPa}$. Predict the composite tensile strength.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the strength ROM. Convert to consistent units (use GPa for fibre, MPa for matrix, or both in GPa).

$$
\sigma_{c,u} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 2:** Convert matrix strength to GPa: $\sigma_{m,u} = 80 \text{ MPa} = 0.080 \text{ GPa}$.

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$
\sigma_{c,u} = (3.5)(0.30) + (0.080)(0.70)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$
\sigma_{c,u} = 1.050 + 0.056 = 1.106 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert back to MPa: $\sigma_{c,u} = 1106 \text{ MPa}$.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\sigma_{c,u} \approx 1.106 \text{ GPa} = 1106 \text{ MPa}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Tip:** Always state the **unit consistency** step. A common error is mixing GPa and MPa without conversion, leading to a wrong answer despite correct formula usage. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which ROM

| Loading Condition | Fibre Orientation | Applicable ROM | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tension along fibre | $0°$ (parallel) | **Voigt** (parallel) | Iso-strain |
| Tension across fibre | $90°$ (transverse) | **Reuss** (series) | Iso-stress |
| Off-axis loading | $\theta \ne 0°, 90°$ | Use **Halpin–Tsai** or **Classical Laminate Theory** | Shear coupling |
| Random in-plane | Random planar | Modified Voigt with $\eta_o = 3/8$ | Empirical correction |
| Density (any) | Any | **Exact mass average** | Mass conservation |
| Thermal (parallel) | Parallel to fibre | Voigt | Heat flow iso-path |
| Thermal (transverse) | Perpendicular | Reuss | Series resistance |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Rule of Mixtures for composites. State the assumptions of the Voigt (parallel) model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Rule of Mixtures is an analytical method used to predict the effective macroscopic property of a composite material by taking the volume-weighted average of the corresponding properties of the constituent matrix and reinforcement phases. For a two-phase fibre-reinforced composite, the rule is expressed as $P_c = P_f V_f + P_m (1 - V_f)$, where $P_c$, $P_f$, and $P_m$ are the properties of the composite, fibre, and matrix, respectively, and $V_f$ is the volume fraction of fibre.

**Assumptions of the Voigt Model (1.5 marks):**
1. Fibres are continuous, uniform, and perfectly aligned parallel to the loading direction.
2. Perfect bonding exists at the fibre-matrix interface (no slip).
3. Both matrix and fibre behave as linear elastic materials.
4. The **isostrain** condition applies — the strain experienced by the fibre, matrix, and composite is identical ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between E-glass and S-glass fibres. List any two applications of E-glass fibre composites.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | E-Glass (Electrical) | S-Glass (Structural) |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Alumina-borosilicate, low alkali | High MgO, high Al₂O₃ |
| Tensile Strength | 3.4–3.5 GPa | 4.5–4.9 GPa |
| Young's Modulus | 72–76 GPa | 86–90 GPa |
| Softening Point | ~846 °C | ~1056 °C |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (~3–5×) |

**Two Applications of E-Glass Composites (1 mark):**
1. **Automotive body panels and bumpers** (lightweight FRP).
2. **Boat hulls and marine structures** (corrosion resistance + strength).
3. *(Alternate)* Printed circuit board substrates; storage tanks; pipes; wind turbine blades.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — ROM Density & Modulus**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Derive the Rule of Mixtures expression for the **longitudinal Young's modulus** of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite. State the assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A unidirectional E-glass fibre reinforced epoxy composite has a fibre volume fraction of 0.40. Given:
> - $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$
> - $E_m = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$
>
> Calculate: (i) the density of the composite, (ii) the longitudinal Young's modulus, and (iii) the transverse Young's modulus. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution**

### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Continuous, parallel, uniformly distributed fibres aligned with the load axis.
2. Perfect fibre-matrix interfacial bonding (no slip).
3. Both phases are linear elastic.
4. **Isostrain condition**: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Force equilibrium on the composite cross-section:

$$
F_c = F_f + F_m
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $F = \sigma A$:

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 3:** Divide throughout by $A_c$, recognizing that $A_f/A_c = V_f$ and $A_m/A_c = V_m$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Hooke's Law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:

$$
E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \varepsilon \cdot V_m
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel the common strain $\varepsilon$:

$$
\boxed{E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**(i) Composite Density (2 marks):**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)
$$

$$
\rho_c = (2.55)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60) = 1.020 + 0.720 = 1.740 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Modulus (2.5 marks):**

$$
E_{c,L} = (72)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60) = 28.80 + 2.10 = 30.90 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(iii) Transverse Modulus (2.5 marks):**

$$
\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{0.40}{72} + \frac{0.60}{3.5} = 0.005556 + 0.171429 = 0.176985
$$

$$
E_{c,T} = \frac{1}{0.176985} \approx 5.65 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Final Answers:** $\rho_c = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3$; $E_{c,L} = 30.90 \text{ GPa}$; $E_{c,T} \approx 5.65 \text{ GPa}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Glass Fibre Preparation & Properties**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Describe the **continuous filament process** for the manufacture of glass fibre with a neat block diagram. List the raw materials used. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Explain the role of **silanes as coupling agents** in glass-fibre reinforced polymer composites. State any four important properties of E-glass fibre with their typical values. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution**

### Part (a) — Continuous Filament Process [7 marks]

**Raw Materials (1.5 marks):**
1. **Silica sand (SiO₂)** — primary glass network former.
2. **Soda ash (Na₂CO₃)** — flux to lower melting temperature.
3. **Limestone (CaCO₃)** — stabilizer.
4. **Borax / Boric acid (B₂O₃)** — improves chemical and thermal resistance.
5. **Feldspar (Al₂O₃·SiO₂)** — source of alumina for E-glass.

**Process Description (with diagram) (5.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Batch Preparation:** The raw materials are weighed in precise proportions according to the E-glass recipe and mixed thoroughly to form a homogeneous batch.

**Step 2 — Melting:** The batch is fed into a gas-fired or electric furnace and melted at temperatures between **1250 °C and 1350 °C** to form a homogeneous molten glass.

**Step 3 — Refining:** The molten glass is passed through a refining section where bubbles are removed by bubbling, settling, and temperature conditioning to obtain a defect-free, homogeneous melt.

**Step 4 — Bushing (Spinneret) Extrusion:** The melt flows by gravity through a **platinum-rhodium alloy bushing** containing **200 to 6000 precisely sized holes** (each ~1–2 mm diameter).

**Step 5 — Attenuation (Drawing):** The molten streams emerging from the bushing are rapidly drawn down at high speed (typically 50–100 m/s) to final filament diameters of **5–25 µm**.

**Step 6 — Cooling:** The filaments cool and solidify via radiative and convective heat loss as they pass through a conditioning zone.

**Step 7 — Sizing Application:** Aqueous sizing emulsion (containing silane coupling agent, film former, lubricant, antistatic agent) is applied via a roller/applicator to protect the filaments, improve handleability, and enhance matrix adhesion.

**Step 8 — Strand Formation & Winding:** The sized filaments are gathered together into a **strand** (typically 51, 102, or 204 filaments) and wound onto a rotating collet at high speed to form a **roving** package.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Raw Materials] → [Batch Mixing] → [Melting Furnace 1250-1350 °C]
        → [Refining] → [Pt-Rh Bushing] → [Attenuation 5-25 µm]
        → [Sizing Applicator] → [Strand Gathering] → [Winding on Collet]
        → [Continuous E-Glass Roving]
```

### Part (b) — Silane Coupling Agents & E-Glass Properties [7 marks]

**Silanes as Coupling Agents (3.5 marks):**

Silanes are **bifunctional organosilicon compounds** of the general formula $\text{R'–Si(OR)}_3$, where R' is an organofunctional group (e.g., vinyl, amino, methacryl, epoxy) and OR is a hydrolysable alkoxy group (typically methoxy OCH₃ or ethoxy OC₂H₅).

**Mechanism of Action (Two-Step Process):**

**Step 1 — Inorganic Side Bonding to Glass:** The alkoxy groups (–OR) hydrolyse in the presence of moisture to form silanol groups (–Si–OH). These silanols undergo **condensation reactions** with the hydroxyl groups (–OH) on the glass fibre surface, forming stable **covalent Si–O–Si siloxane bonds**.

$$
\text{Si–OH (silane)} + \text{HO–Si (glass)} \longrightarrow \text{Si–O–Si (siloxane)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Organic Side Bonding to Polymer Matrix:** The organofunctional group R' is selected to be chemically reactive with the polymer matrix. For example:
- **Vinyl silane** bonds with unsaturated polyester or vinyl ester matrices.
- **Amino silane** bonds with epoxy matrices.
- **Methacryl silane** bonds with PMMA or unsaturated polyester.

**Functions of Silanes (1 mark):**
1. Improve fibre-matrix interfacial adhesion.
2. Increase composite transverse and interlaminar shear strength.
3. Protect the glass surface from moisture attack (hydrolytic stability).
4. Improve stress transfer from matrix to fibre → higher composite strength.

**Four Important Properties of E-Glass (3.5 marks):**

| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Density** | 2.54 – 2.60 g/cm³ |
| **Tensile Strength** | 3.4 – 3.5 GPa |
| **Young's Modulus** | 72 – 76 GPa |
| **Softening Point** | ~846 °C |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Module 3 Examination:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing GPa with MPa, g/cm³ with kg/m³, or N with kN without explicit conversion. **Always** state the unit and convert at the start of the calculation.
>
> 2. **Wrong ROM Model (–2 to –3 marks):** Applying the **Voigt (parallel) model for transverse direction** or vice versa. **Memorize the trigger word:** "along fibre / longitudinal / axial" → Voigt; "perpendicular / transverse / across" → Reuss.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Assumptions (–1 to –2 marks):** In derivation questions, KTU examiners allocate **1.5–2 marks** for explicitly stating the assumptions (isostrain, perfect bonding, continuous fibres). Omitting these loses easy marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing Mass Fraction with Volume Fraction (–1 mark):** In data, if **mass fraction** is given, the student must convert to volume fraction using $V_f = \dfrac{w_f/\rho_f}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$ before applying the ROM.
>
> 5. **Incorrect Diameter vs Filament Count (–1 mark):** In glass fibre problems, "51" or "204" filament count is asked. Students often confuse **tow count** with **fibre diameter**.
>
> 6. **Missing the "isostrain" keyword in Voigt derivations (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Examiners look for the words "isostrain" or "iso-strain" as a key grading term.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: ROM & Glass Fibre**

### A. Rule of Mixtures Essentials
- ✅ **Density ROM is exact and universal** — mass conservation makes it geometry-independent.
- ✅ **Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt/Parallel):** $E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ — upper bound, applies along fibre direction.
- ✅ **Transverse Modulus (Reuss/Series):** $\dfrac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{(1-V_f)}{E_m}$ — lower bound, applies across fibre direction.
- ✅ **Tensile Strength ROM:** $\sigma_{c,u} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u}(1 - V_f)$; with efficiency factor $\eta$ for short/disoriented fibres.
- ✅ **Volume fractions always sum to 1:** $V_f + V_m = 1$.
- ✅ **Key trigger words:** "parallel / axial / longitudinal / along fibre" → Voigt; "transverse / perpendicular / series / across fibre" → Reuss.
- ✅ Composite is **highly anisotropic**: $E_{c,L} \gg E_{c,T}$ — ratio typically 4–10× for FRP.

### B. Glass Fibre Essentials
- ✅ **E-Glass is the most common FRP reinforcement** — memorize: $\sigma = 3.4 \text{ GPa}$, $E = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$.
- ✅ **S-Glass** = higher strength & modulus, higher cost — for aerospace.
- ✅ **C-Glass** = best chemical/acid resistance.
- ✅ Manufacturing temperature: **1250–1350 °C** in Pt-Rh bushings.
- ✅ Filament diameter: **5–25 µm**; bushings have 200–6000 holes.
- ✅ **Silanes** (e.g., γ-MPS) are the key **coupling agents** — they form **Si–O–Si siloxane** bonds with glass and organic bonds with the matrix.
- ✅ **Sizing** = silane + film former + lubricant + antistatic.
- ✅ Glass fibre is **brittle, non-flammable, non-conductive, low-CTE** material.

### C. Exam-Ready Derivations (Memorize the Flow)
1. **Density ROM** → Start from mass conservation $M_c = M_f + M_m$.
2. **Voigt Modulus** → Force balance + isostrain assumption + Hooke's law.
3. **Reuss Modulus** → Strain compatibility + isostress assumption + Hooke's law.
4. **Strength ROM** → Use ultimate stresses; mention efficiency factor for short fibres.

### D. Quick-Recall Numerical Pattern
- $V_f = 0.30 \text{ to } 0.50$ is **typical** for FRP composites.
- For an E-glass/epoxy at $V_f = 0.40$: $E_{c,L} \approx 30 \text{ GPa}$, $E_{c,T} \approx 5{-}6 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho_c \approx 1.7 \text{ g/cm}^3$.
- Always check: **Voigt > Reuss** for the same constituent values → confirms the model is applied correctly.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — PBPOT604 Polymer Blends & Composites**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon Fibre and Aramid Fibre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carbon Fibre and Aramid Fibre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Carbon Fibre — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Fibre (CF):** A high-performance fibre composed of at least **92% (typically 95–99%) elemental carbon by weight**, in which the carbon atoms are aligned along the long axis of the fibre in a turbostratic/graphitic crystalline arrangement. The fibre is produced by controlled pyrolysis (thermal decomposition in an inert atmosphere) of an organic precursor fibre such as **Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)**, **petroleum/coal-tar pitch**, or **regenerated cellulose (rayon)**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus classification, carbon fibres are categorized based on their **mechanical performance and modulus** into:

| Classification | Tensile Modulus (GPa) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Carbon Content (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ultra-High Modulus (UHM)** | > 500 | ~ 2,000 | > 99 |
| **High Modulus (HM)** | 300 – 500 | ~ 2,500 | > 99 |
| **Intermediate Modulus (IM)** | 200 – 300 | ~ 3,500 | 95 – 99 |
| **High Strength, Standard Modulus (HT)** | 200 – 250 | 3,000 – 7,000 | 92 – 95 |
| **Low Modulus (LM)** | < 200 | < 2,000 | 80 – 95 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The most commonly tested classification in ESE questions is the **HT (High Tensile) vs. HM (High Modulus)** distinction — remember that HT has lower modulus but higher elongation, while HM is stiffer and more brittle.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — Carbon Fibre

Imagine a **bundle of uncooked spaghetti strands** thrown into a furnace. As heat increases, the moisture and organic content burn away, leaving behind **pure, aligned carbon "skeletons"** of those strands. These carbon skeletons are exactly what carbon fibres are — but engineered with molecular perfection so that the carbon atoms are aligned parallel to the fibre axis, giving extraordinary strength along that axis.

In simpler terms:
- A single carbon filament is roughly **5–10 µm in diameter** (thinner than a human hair which is ~ 70 µm).
- Its **density is ~ 1.75–2.10 g/cm³**, which is **lower than steel (7.85 g/cm³)** yet it is **5× stronger** and **2× stiffer** than steel on a weight basis.
- This exceptional **specific strength** (strength-to-weight ratio) and **specific modulus** (modulus-to-weight ratio) make carbon fibre the **"gold standard"** for aerospace, Formula 1, and high-end sporting goods.

---

## 1.2 Aramid Fibre — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aramid Fibre:** A wholly aromatic polyamide fibre in which at least **85% of the amide (–CO–NH–) linkages are directly attached to two aromatic rings**. The aromatic backbone is responsible for exceptional thermal stability, chemical resistance, and mechanical performance. The general formula is:
> $$\left[ \text{–CO–Ar–CO–NH–Ar'–NH–} \right]_n$$
> where **Ar** and **Ar'** are aromatic ring systems. The most commercially significant aramid fibre is **Poly-para-phenylene terephthalamide (PPTA)**, sold by DuPont under the trade name **Kevlar** and by Teijin as **Twaron**.

The IUPAC definition specifies that "aramid" is derived from **"aromatic polyamide."**

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — Aramid Fibre

Think of aramid fibre as a **molecular chainmail**. The aromatic benzene rings are like rigid metal links, and the hydrogen bonds between adjacent chains act like **"spot welds"** holding the links together. When you try to pull on this chainmail, the welds resist — and because the rings are aromatic (rigid planar), the chain cannot easily fold or stretch. The result is a fibre with **very high tensile strength** and **exceptional impact resistance**.

Commercial grades you must know for KTU exams:
- **Kevlar 29** — High toughness, used in **ballistics (bulletproof vests), ropes, cables**.
- **Kevlar 49** — High modulus version, used in **aerospace composites, marine, automotive**.
- **Kevlar 149** — Ultra-high modulus, used in **advanced structural composites**.
- **Nomex** — Meta-aramid (an isomer), used in **flame-resistant clothing, electrical insulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 3 emphasizes the **structure-property correlation** in aramids, particularly the role of **hydrogen bonding** and **rod-like molecular conformation** in producing the anisotropic mechanical properties.

---

## 1.3 Comparison — Why Two Fibres in One Module?

| Property | Carbon Fibre (PAN-HT) | Aramid Fibre (Kevlar 49) |
|---|---|---|
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 1.75 – 1.85 | 1.44 – 1.47 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 3,500 – 7,000 | 2,800 – 3,600 |
| **Tensile Modulus (GPa)** | 230 – 290 | 70 – 130 |
| **Elongation at Break (%)** | 1.5 – 2.0 | 2.5 – 3.5 |
| **Max Service Temperature (°C)** | 400 – 600 (in air) | ~ 250 (decomposes ~ 500) |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | Conductive | Insulator |
| **UV Resistance** | Excellent | Poor (degrades) |
| **Compressive Strength** | Good | Poor (weak in compression) |
| **Cost (relative)** | Very High | High |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Carbon Fibre — Precursor, Structure & Manufacturing

### 2.1.1 The Three Precursor Routes

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** "Compare PAN-based and Pitch-based carbon fibre manufacturing." This is a **14-mark ESE classic**.

**Route 1 — PAN-based (dominant, ~ 90% of global production):**
- The PAN precursor is a copolymer of acrylonitrile (~ 92–95%) with small amounts of methyl acrylate and itaconic acid.
- Steps: Oxidation (Stabilization) → Carbonization → Optional Graphitization → Surface Treatment → Sizing.
- Produces high tensile strength fibres with carbon content ~ 93–97%.

**Route 2 — Pitch-based (mesophase/isotropic):**
- Pitch is a polyaromatic by-product of petroleum refining or coal tar distillation.
- Mesophase (liquid crystal) pitch yields ultra-high-modulus fibres.
- Steps: Pitch purification → Mesophase formation → Melt spinning → Stabilization → Carbonization.
- Carbon content can reach **99%+**.

**Route 3 — Rayon-based (cellulosic, historical):**
- Regenerated cellulose fibre (rayon) is pyrolyzed.
- Difficult to control, low yield, used only for niche ablative applications.

### 2.1.2 The PAN-based Manufacturing — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Spinning the Precursor:**
PAN is dissolved in a solvent (DMF, DMSO, or NaSCN aqueous solution) and wet-spun or dry-jet wet-spun into a coagulation bath. The resulting white PAN fibre is drawn 5–10× to align molecular chains.

**Step 2 — Stabilization (Oxidation) — 200–300 °C in air:**
The linear –CH₂–CH(CN)– chain undergoes **cyclization** to form a **ladder polymer** with a six-membered pyridine ring. This step converts the thermoplastic PAN into a non-melting infusible ladder structure. Stretching is maintained to retain molecular orientation.

**Step 3 — Carbonization — 1,000–1,500 °C in nitrogen:**
The ladder polymer loses H₂, HCN, NH₃, CO, CO₂, and N₂, leaving a turbostratic carbon structure with ~ 92–95% C. This step develops the basic strength and modulus.

**Step 4 — Graphitization (optional) — 2,000–3,000 °C in argon:**
Heating to ultra-high temperatures under tension grows the crystallite size (La, Lc) and perfects the orientation of graphene-like sheets, increasing the modulus dramatically (producing HM or UHM grades).

**Step 5 — Surface Treatment:**
The fibre surface is oxidized (e.g., electrolytic anodization in HNO₃/H₂SO₄) to introduce oxygen-containing functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –C=O). This improves **wettability and chemical bonding with matrix resins** like epoxy.

**Step 6 — Sizing:**
A thin polymer coating (epoxy, polyester, polyurethane, or polyamide) is applied to protect the fibre during handling and to enhance fibre–matrix adhesion.

### 2.1.3 Structure of Carbon Fibre

The structure is best described as a **turbostratic** (turbulent + stratified) arrangement of graphene-like layers.

- **Crystallite dimensions:**
  - Interlayer spacing **d₀₀₂ ≈ 0.34 – 0.40 nm** (vs. 0.335 nm for ideal graphite).
  - Crystallite height **Lc ≈ 1.5 – 50 nm** (increases with HTT).
  - Crystallite width **La ≈ 2 – 50 nm**.
  - The graphene layers are preferentially oriented parallel to the fibre axis, but with waviness (the "shish-kebab" or "ribbon" model).

- **Skin–Core Heterogeneity:** The outer skin has better crystalline orientation than the inner core, contributing to the anisotropic properties.

### 2.1.4 Key Properties & Their Origin

- **High Tensile Strength** → Covalent C–C bonds in aligned graphene sheets, near-absence of defects.
- **High Modulus** → Strong sp² C=C in-plane covalent bonding + alignment.
- **Low Density** → Hollow turbostratic structure; mostly carbon (atomic mass 12).
- **Thermal Conductivity** → Phonon transport along graphene planes; some grades exceed copper.
- **Electrical Conductivity** → Delocalized π electrons in sp² carbon.
- **Chemical Inertness** → No active sites; resistant to most acids (except strong oxidizers), bases, and organic solvents.
- **Negative Axial CTE** → Carbon fibres have a *negative* coefficient of thermal expansion along the fibre axis, which is exploited in **zero-thermal-expansion composites** (used in space telescopes and optical benches).
- **Creep Resistance** → Excellent; near-zero creep at room temperature.

---

## 2.2 Aramid Fibre — Chemistry, Structure & Manufacturing

### 2.2.1 The Chemistry of PPTA (Kevlar)

PPTA is synthesized by a **low-temperature solution polycondensation** of:
- **Para-phenylene diamine (PPD)** + **Terephthaloyl chloride (TDC)** in a solvent system of **N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) with CaCl₂**.

The reaction:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–NH}_2 + \text{Cl–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–Cl} \rightarrow \left[ \text{–NH–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–NH–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–} \right]_n + 2n\,\text{HCl}$$

The polymerization proceeds in a **liquid-crystalline state** because PPTA chains are **rigid rod-like** polymers. Above a critical concentration, the chains align spontaneously into nematic ordered domains.

### 2.2.2 Dry-Jet Wet Spinning — The Key to Aramid's Performance

- The liquid-crystalline PPTA/H₂SO₄ solution (≈ 20 wt% polymer in 100% H₂SO₄) is extruded through a spinneret into a thin air gap (1–2 cm) before entering a cold water coagulation bath.
- During the **air gap**, the rod-like chains align along the flow direction — this is critical for ultimate orientation.
- In the coagulation bath, the solvent (H₂SO₄) is extracted, solidifying the highly oriented fibre.
- The as-spun fibre is then washed, neutralized, dried, and wound.
- **Kevlar 49 and 149** are produced by **additional heat treatment under tension** to increase crystallinity and modulus.

### 2.2.3 Structure of Aramid Fibre

- **Molecular conformation:** PPTA chains adopt an **extended rigid-rod** configuration. The para-linkage of aromatic rings prevents chain folding.
- **Crystallinity:** Highly crystalline (Kevlar 49 ~ 68–80% crystallinity).
- **Hydrogen bonding:** The N–H of one chain hydrogen-bonds to the C=O of an adjacent chain, forming a **2D sheet** of hydrogen-bonded molecules. These sheets stack via weak van der Waals forces.
- **Resulting Anisotropy:** The H-bonded planes are radially arranged in a **pleated-sheet or radial arrangement** in the cross-section, giving **transverse weakness** and **longitudinal strength** — a classic example of structure-property anisotropy.

### 2.2.4 Key Properties & Their Origin

- **High Tensile Strength** → Rigid-rod polymer chains aligned along the fibre axis, with strong covalent bonds in the loading direction.
- **High Modulus** → Aromatic ring rigidity resists chain stretching; high crystallinity.
- **High Impact Resistance / Toughness** → Hydrogen-bonded sheets can dissipate energy through reversible bond breaking; aramid has high work-to-break.
- **Heat & Flame Resistance** → Aromatic backbone is thermally stable; LOI (Limiting Oxygen Index) ≈ 28–30 (self-extinguishing).
- **Chemical Resistance** → Resistant to organic solvents, fuels; degraded by strong acids (H₂SO₄) and bases.
- **Low Density** → 1.44 g/cm³ (lighter than carbon).
- **Electrical Insulator** → No delocalized electrons.
- **UV Sensitivity** → Aromatic amides absorb UV → chain scission → loss of strength. Hence Kevlar composites are protected with UV-blocking coatings.
- **Moisture Regain** ~ 3.5–7% (hygroscopic; affects composite properties).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Carbon Fibre & Aramid Fibre Performance Metrics**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specific Strength** | $\sigma / \rho$ | $\text{N·m/kg}$ or $\text{MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$ | Strength-to-weight ratio |
| 2 | **Specific Modulus** | $E / \rho$ | $\text{N·m/kg}$ or $\text{GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$ | Stiffness-to-weight ratio |
| 3 | **Tensile Strength** | $\sigma = F / A$ | MPa | F = load at break, A = cross-section |
| 4 | **Young's Modulus** | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | GPa | Slope of stress–strain in elastic region |
| 5 | **Volume Fraction of Fibre (Rule of Mixtures for Composite)** | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | V_f + V_m = 1 |
| 6 | **Density of Composite** | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | g/cm³ | For void-free composite |
| 7 | **Bragg's Law (XRD on CF)** | $n\lambda = 2d \sin\theta$ | — | Used to find d₀₀₂ spacing |
| 8 | **Degree of Orientation (cos²ψ)** | $\langle \cos^2\psi \rangle = \dfrac{\int_0^{\pi/2} I(\psi)\cos^2\psi \sin\psi\, d\psi}{\int_0^{\pi/2} I(\psi)\sin\psi\, d\psi}$ | — | ψ = azimuthal angle from fibre axis |
| 9 | **Yield per Precursor** | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{CF}}}{m_{\text{precursor}}} \times 100\%$ | % | PAN: ~ 50–55%, Pitch: ~ 75–80% |
| 10 | **Crystallite Size (Scherrer Eq.)** | $L = K\lambda / (\beta \cos\theta)$ | nm | K ≈ 0.89 for Lc, 1.84 for La |
| 11 | **Thermal Conductivity (Rule of Mixture)** | $k_c = k_f V_f + k_m V_m$ | W/m·K | Parallel to fibre direction |
| 12 | **Kevlar Density (crystalline)** | $\rho_{\text{xtal}} = \dfrac{M \cdot Z}{N_A \cdot V_{\text{cell}}}$ | g/cm³ | Z = formula units per unit cell |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Rule of Mixtures** ($E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$) is the most tested equation in KTU for any fibre-reinforced composite — both CF- and Kevlar-reinforced systems.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Fibres Make an Impact

- **Carbon Fibre:**
  - Boeing 787 Dreamliner, Airbus A350 — primary structural composite (CF/epoxy, ~ 50% by weight).
  - Wind turbine blades (length > 80 m), Formula 1 monocoques, prosthetics, robotics.
  - Civil engineering: CFRP strips for retrofitting bridges and concrete columns.
  - Carbon-carbon (C/C) composites for rocket nozzles and re-entry vehicle nose tips (replacing ablative materials).

- **Aramid Fibre:**
  - Personal ballistic protection (Kevlar 29 in soft body armour — ~ 20–40 layers).
  - Honeycomb cores in sandwich panels (Nomex honeycomb).
  - Brake pads, clutch linings (frictional heat resistance).
  - High-pressure hoses, optical fibre cables (Kevlar as central strength member).
  - Marine ropes and mooring cables (lightweight + corrosion resistance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Why Kevlar is Strong Along the Axis but Weak in Compression

This is a **classic KTU derivable question**. We work out the modulus anisotropy of an aramid fibre from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Given Structural Data for PPTA (Kevlar 49)

- Unit cell: **orthorhombic** with $a = 0.787\,\text{nm}$, $b = 0.518\,\text{nm}$, $c = 1.29\,\text{nm}$ (fibre axis).
- Number of monomer units per cell: $Z = 2$.
- Molecular weight of repeat unit: $M_0 = 238.27\,\text{g/mol}$ (C₁₄H₁₀N₂O₂).
- Bond angles and lengths determine chain stiffness.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Crystalline Density

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{cell}} &= a \times b \times c \\
&= (0.787 \times 10^{-9}) \times (0.518 \times 10^{-9}) \times (1.29 \times 10^{-9}) \,\text{m}^3 \\
&= 5.26 \times 10^{-28} \,\text{m}^3 \\
&= 5.26 \times 10^{-22} \,\text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Convert cm³ to weight using Avogadro's number:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{xtal}} &= \frac{Z \cdot M_0}{N_A \cdot V_{\text{cell}}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 238.27}{(6.022 \times 10^{23}) \times (5.26 \times 10^{-22})} \,\text{g/cm}^3 \\
&= \frac{476.54}{316.76} \,\text{g/cm}^3 \\
&\approx 1.504 \,\text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with measured fibre density:**
The measured density of Kevlar 49 is **1.44–1.47 g/cm³**. The crystalline density is higher because the fibre is not 100% crystalline; the amorphous regions (and the slight chain misalignment) lower the bulk density.

$$\text{Crystallinity} = \frac{\rho_{\text{measured}}}{\rho_{\text{crystalline}}} \times \frac{1}{1 - \text{(void fraction)}} \approx 68 - 80\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This type of problem connects **X-ray crystallography** to **macroscopic fibre properties**. A 14-mark question may ask you to derive density OR to interpret why Kevlar is anisotropic from the unit-cell geometry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Modulus of a Single PPTA Chain (Loose Chain Model)

### 3.2.1 Concept

The theoretical axial modulus of a polymer chain is the product of:
- The covalent bond stiffness, AND
- The geometric factor describing how the load resolves onto each covalent bond.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the load-bearing bonds.**
In PPTA, the chain is approximately a sequence of –CO–C₆H₄–CO–NH–C₆H₄–NH– segments. The stiffest unit is the **para-phenylene ring (C–C bond inside the ring)** plus the **C(=O)–N bond** of the amide.

**Step 2 — For a single C–C bond:**
- Bond length $l_0 = 0.154\,\text{nm}$
- Bond stretching force constant $k_{\text{bond}} \approx 440\,\text{N/m}$ (C–C aromatic)

The "spring constant" of a single bond along the chain axis is:

$$k = k_{\text{bond}} \cdot \sin^2\phi$$

where $\phi$ is the angle the bond makes with the chain axis. For a planar zig-zag with $\phi \approx 30°$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin^2\phi &= \sin^2(30°) = 0.25 \\
\Rightarrow k &= 440 \times 0.25 = 110\,\text{N/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Modulus of a single chain (one-dimensional spring in series).**
For a chain of $N$ bonds in series, the effective chain modulus is:

$$E_{\text{chain}} = \frac{k \cdot l_0}{A_{\text{chain}}}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-sectional area of a single chain:**
Per PPTA chain, $A \approx 0.20\,\text{nm}^2$ (typical literature value).

**Step 5 — Compute the modulus:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{chain}} &= \frac{110 \times 0.154 \times 10^{-9}}{0.20 \times 10^{-18}} \,\text{Pa} \\
&= \frac{16.94 \times 10^{-9}}{0.20 \times 10^{-18}} \,\text{Pa} \\
&= 84.7 \times 10^{9} \,\text{Pa} \\
&\approx 85\,\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **theoretical minimum** for an un-oriented PPTA chain. The experimentally observed modulus of Kevlar 49 (110–130 GPa) is **higher** because the actual chain is **fully extended and aligned** (no amorphous coiling), and the H-bonded network also carries load.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Comparison — Carbon Fibre:** The theoretical modulus of a perfect graphene plane is ~ 1,050 GPa. In practice, PAN-based CF achieves 230–290 GPa (HT) and pitch-based UHM CF can approach 900 GPa.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Longitudinal Modulus

This is the **most frequently tested 14-mark derivation** in any fibre composite module.

### 3.3.1 Assumptions

- Both fibre and matrix are linear elastic.
- Iso-strain condition (perfect bonding, parallel loading): $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$.
- Fibres are continuous, aligned, and uniformly distributed.
- No voids.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Total load on the composite equals sum of loads on constituents:**

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

**Step 2 — Express each load as stress × area:**

$$\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m$$

**Step 3 — Divide through by $A_c$:**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

Define volume fractions $V_f = A_f/A_c$ and $V_m = A_m/A_c$ (valid for constant cross-section along length):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's law in the iso-strain case:**

$$\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_c, \quad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_c, \quad \sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon_c$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the stress equation:**

$$E_c \varepsilon_c = E_f \varepsilon_c \cdot V_f + E_m \varepsilon_c \cdot V_m$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $\varepsilon_c$:**

$$\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Note:** Always explicitly state the **iso-strain assumption**. Examiners award marks for stating the underlying physics, not just the final equation.

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Q: A unidirectional carbon-fibre/epoxy composite contains $V_f = 0.60$ of carbon fibre ($E_f = 230\,\text{GPa}$) and $V_m = 0.40$ of epoxy matrix ($E_m = 3.5\,\text{GPa}$). Find the longitudinal modulus.**

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_c &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\
&= (230 \times 0.60) + (3.5 \times 0.40) \\
&= 138 + 1.4 \\
&= 139.4\,\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{E_c \approx 139.4\,\text{GPa}}$$

**Compare with the transverse modulus (iso-stress):**

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.60}{230} + \frac{0.40}{3.5} = 0.00261 + 0.1143 = 0.1169$$

$$E_T = 8.55\,\text{GPa}$$

The huge ratio $E_c / E_T \approx 16$ quantifies the **anisotropy** — and this is the *engineering problem* designers face with unidirectional composites.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Crystallite Size from XRD (Scherrer Equation)

**Step 1 — Bragg's Law gives the d-spacing:**

$$n\lambda = 2d \sin\theta$$

For the (002) peak of carbon fibre at $2\theta \approx 25°$ with Cu Kα radiation ($\lambda = 0.154\,\text{nm}$):

$$d_{002} = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin\theta} = \frac{0.154}{2 \sin(12.5°)} = \frac{0.154}{0.4330} = 0.356\,\text{nm}$$

**Step 2 — Scherrer equation gives the crystallite height $L_c$:**

$$L_c = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$$

where $\beta$ = full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the (002) peak in radians, $K = 0.89$ for $L_c$.

**Example:** If FWHM $\beta = 0.025\,\text{rad}$ for the (002) peak:

$$L_c = \frac{0.89 \times 0.154}{0.025 \times \cos(12.5°)} = \frac{0.1371}{0.0244} \approx 5.6\,\text{nm}$$

This is a typical value for a high-tensile carbon fibre. Higher $L_c$ (achieved by higher heat treatment temperature) → higher modulus.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PAN-based Carbon Fibre Manufacturing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acrylonitrile Monomer Feed] --> B[Copolymerization with Methyl Acrylate and Itaconic Acid]
    B --> C[PAN Powder in DMF Solvent]
    C --> D[Wet or Dry-Jet Wet Spinning]
    D --> E[Drawing 5-10x Alignment]
    E --> F[Stabilization Oxidation 200-300 C in Air]
    F --> G[Carbonization 1000-1500 C in N2]
    G --> H{Graphitization Needed?}
    H -->|Yes HM or UHM| I[Graphitization 2000-3000 C in Ar under Tension]
    H -->|No HT grade| J[Surface Treatment]
    I --> J[Surface Treatment Electrolytic Oxidation]
    J --> K[Sizing Polymer Coating]
    K --> L[Carbon Fibre Bobbin for Composite Use]
```

## 4.2 Aramid Fibre Manufacturing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Para-Phenylene Diamine PPD] --> M2
    M3[Terephthaloyl Chloride TDC] --> M2
    M2[Low Temp Polycondensation in NMP plus CaCl2] --> M4[PPTA Polymer Liquid Crystal Solution]
    M4 --> M5[Dissolution in Concentrated H2SO4]
    M5 --> M6[Dry-Jet Wet Spinning via Air Gap]
    M6 --> M7[Coagulation Bath Cold Water]
    M7 --> M8[Neutralization and Washing]
    M8 --> M9{Heat Treatment under Tension}
    M9 -->|Kevlar 29| P1[High Toughness Yarn]
    M9 -->|Kevlar 49| P2[High Modulus Yarn]
    M9 -->|Kevlar 149| P3[Ultra High Modulus Yarn]
```

## 4.3 Structure Comparison — CF vs. Aramid (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CARBON
        CF1[Graphene Sheet 2D sp2 Carbon] --> CF2[Stacking with d002 about 0.34 to 0.40 nm]
        CF2 --> CF3[Microfibril aligned along fibre axis]
        CF3 --> CF4[Fibre 5 to 10 micrometer diameter]
    end
    subgraph ARAMID
        AF1[Benzene Ring aromatic] --> AF2[Amide Linkage CONH rigid rod]
        AF2 --> AF3[Hydrogen Bonding lateral sheets]
        AF3 --> AF4[Pleated Sheet Radial Microfibril]
        AF4 --> AF5[Fibre 12 micrometer diameter Kevlar]
    end
```

## 4.4 Composite Property Architecture — How Fibres Build Strength

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Fibre High Strength and Stiffness] --> R2
    R2[Matrix Epoxy or PEEK] --> R3[Interface Coupling Agent Sizing]
    R3 --> R4[Unidirectional Ply Ec about 140 GPa for CF]
    R3 --> R5[Cross Ply Laminate Ec reduced but balanced]
    R5 --> R6[Composite Structure Lightweight High Performance]
```

## 4.5 Property Trade-Off Radar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Carbon Fibre] -- Strongest Modulus --> O1[Aerospace]
    S1 -- Conductive --> O2[EMI Shielding]
    S1 -- Brittle --> O3[Low Impact]
    S2[Aramid Kevlar] -- Toughest --> O4[Ballistics]
    S2 -- Insulator --> O5[Electrical Laminates]
    S2 -- Weak in Compression --> O6[Honeycomb Sandwich Cores]
```
