# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Density of Composite Propellant

**Given (typical PSLV-class formulation):**
- Binder (HTPB): weight fraction $w_{b} = 0.12$, density $\rho_{b} = 920$ kg/m³
- Oxidiser (AP): $w_{o} = 0.70$, $\rho_{o} = 1950$ kg/m³
- Metal fuel (Al): $w_{m} = 0.18$, $\rho_{m} = 2700$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — State the governing equation (reciprocal rule of mixtures):**

For a heterogeneous, volume-additive mixture, the total specific volume is the sum of the component specific volumes:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_{p}} = \frac{w_{b}}{\rho_{b}} + \frac{w_{o}}{\rho_{o}} + \frac{w_{m}}{\rho_{m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in the numerical values:**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_{p}} = \frac{0.12}{920} + \frac{0.70}{1950} + \frac{0.18}{2700}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term with explicit arithmetic:**

$$
\frac{0.12}{920} = 1.3043 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
\frac{0.70}{1950} = 3.5897 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
\frac{0.18}{2700} = 6.6667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions:**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_{p}} = \left( 1.3043 + 3.5897 + 0.6667 \right) \times 10^{-4} = 5.5607 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain the propellant density:**

$$
\rho_{p} = \frac{1}{5.5607 \times 10^{-4}} = 1798.4 \text{ kg/m}^3 \approx 1.80 \text{ g/cc}
$$

**Conclusion:** The theoretical propellant density is **1.80 g/cc**, which matches measured laboratory values of $1.78 \pm 0.02$ g/cc for PSOV/SLS-grade HTPB propellant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solid Loading by Volume

**Definition:** Solid loading by volume is the volume fraction occupied by crystalline fillers (oxidiser + metal fuel) in the propellant.

$$
\phi_{s} = \frac{V_{\text{solids}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\sum \frac{w_{s,i}}{\rho_{s,i}}}{\sum \frac{w_{j}}{\rho_{j}}}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator (solids volume per kg of propellant):**

$$
V_{\text{solids}} = \frac{0.70}{1950} + \frac{0.18}{2700} = 3.5897 \times 10^{-4} + 6.6667 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
V_{\text{solids}} = 4.2564 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — The denominator was already computed in Derivation 1 (Step 4):**

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 5.5607 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
\phi_{s} = \frac{4.2564 \times 10^{-4}}{5.5607 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.7654
$$

**Conclusion:** The propellant is **76.54 % solids by volume** — a typical industry value (range 74–78 % for processable HTPB-AP-Al systems).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Burn Rate under Operating Pressure (Vielle's Law)

**Given:** A HTPB-AP-Al propellant has $a = 5.0$ mm/s/MPa$^{n}$ and $n = 0.40$. Predict the burn rate at the design chamber pressure of $P = 7.0$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Vielle's Law (Saint-Robert's Law):**

$$
r = a \, P^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in $a$, $P$, $n$:**

$$
r = 5.0 \times \left( 7.0 \right)^{0.40}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $7.0^{0.40}$:**

$$
7.0^{0.40} = e^{0.40 \cdot \ln 7.0} = e^{0.40 \times 1.9459} = e^{0.7784} = 2.178
$$

**Step 4 — Final multiplication:**

$$
r = 5.0 \times 2.178 = 10.89 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Conclusion:** At 7 MPa chamber pressure, the propellant regresses at **≈ 10.9 mm/s**, which is well within the safe operating window for tactical and launch-vehicle boosters.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Crosslink Density of Cured HTPB

**Step 1 — Compute the HTPB number-average molecular weight from its hydroxyl value.**

A typical commercial HTPB (e.g., **Poly bd R-45M**) has an OH value of **0.78 mg KOH/g**. Using:

$$
M_{n} = \frac{56.1 \times 1000}{\text{OH value (mg KOH/g)}}
$$

$$
M_{n} = \frac{56.1 \times 1000}{0.78} = \frac{56100}{0.78} = 71923 \text{ g/mol}
$$

*(This is the **equivalent weight per OH function**; the actual $M_{n}$ of the prepolymer is typically 2800–3200 g/mol, the OH value giving the **functionality-weighted** equivalent.)*

**Step 2 — Assume difunctional isocyanate (TDI) cures the prepolymer in a chain-extension mode.** The molecular weight between crosslinks is approximately:

$$
M_{c} \approx \frac{M_{n}}{2} = \frac{3196}{2} = 1598 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Crosslink density:**

$$
\nu = \frac{\rho_{b}}{M_{c}} = \frac{920}{1598} = 0.576 \text{ mol/m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Practical interpretation:** This corresponds to a **moderately cross-linked elastomer** with equilibrium swelling ratio $Q \approx 3$ in toluene, consistent with the strain-capability requirement ($\varepsilon_{\text{break}} > 50$ %).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Curing Reaction Stoichiometry (HTPB + Isocyanate)

**Step 1 — Write the reaction:**

$$
\text{HO–R–OH} + 2\,\text{OCN–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R'–NH–CO–O–R–O–CO–NH–R'}
$$

This produces a **bis-urethane (carbamate) linkage**.

**Step 2 — Define the NCO index (r-value):**

$$
r = \frac{[\text{NCO}]}{[\text{OH}]}
$$

**Step 3 — Select r = 0.85 (typical for sub-stoichiometric cure to retain elasticity):**

If the initial hydroxyl content of the slurry is 0.78 meq/g, then for a 1 kg batch:

$$
[\text{OH}] = 0.78 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 0.78 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
[\text{NCO}]_{\text{required}} = 0.85 \times 0.78 = 0.663 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of TDI required** (TDI molecular weight = 174.2 g/mol, functionality = 2):

$$
m_{\text{TDI}} = \frac{0.663}{2} \times 174.2 = 57.7 \text{ g per kg of HTPB}
$$

**Conclusion:** Adding **57.7 g of TDI per kg of HTPB** at r = 0.85 produces a slightly under-cured, highly elastic network — the engineering sweet-spot for high-strain propellant grains.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Binder Selection for a Given Mission

| Mission Profile | Recommended Binder | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Large launch booster** (long burn, high I_sp) | **HTPB + IPDI** | Superior ageing, low T_g, high solid loading |
| **Tactical missile** (high acceleration, short burn) | **NEPE** or HTPB + catocene | Highest I_sp, fast regression |
| **Smokeless / minimum signature** | **Double-base (NC/NG)** | No AP → no HCl smoke |
| **Cold-storage interceptor** (T < −50 °C) | **HTPB** (T_g ≈ −80 °C) | Stays elastomeric |
| **High-altitude upper stage** (low pressure) | **HTPB with burn-rate catalyst** | Counter low-P extinction |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Cast-Cure Propellant Manufacturing Line

This schematic maps the **industrial cast-cure workflow** used at ISRO's Sriharikota range (SDSC SHAR) and similar facilities. Each block is a unit operation; the arrows represent material flow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Raw Material Receiving and Quarantine] --> B[Pre-weighing of Solid Ingredients]
    A1[HTPB Prepolymer Tank] --> C[Liquid Premix Stage]
    A2[Plasticiser DOA and Curative TDI Storage] --> C
    C --> D[Vertical Planetary Sigma Mixer Stage 1]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Vertical Planetary Sigma Mixer Stage 2 - Al Addition]
    E --> F[Slurry Vacuum Degassing at 5 mbar]
    F --> G[Slurry Quality Control - Viscosity and Density Check]
    G --> H[Pouring into Motor Casing under Vacuum]
    H --> I[Vacuum Casting Assembly]
    I --> J[Oven Curing at 60 deg C for 7 days]
    J --> K[Post-cure Cooling and Unmandrelling]
    K --> L[Non-destructive Testing X-ray and Ultrasonic]
    L --> M[Final Propellant Grain Acceptance]
    M --> N[Motor Integration and Static Test]
```

**How to read this diagram during the exam:**
- The arrows show the **strictly sequential** nature of propellant manufacture — no step can be skipped, and any back-tracking (e.g., re-pouring after failure at NDT) requires the entire motor to be scrapped, since the propellant cannot be re-melted.
- The **degassing step (F)** is the single most important quality control node; residual voids in a cured grain can lead to **uncontrolled pressure spikes** (chuffing) and catastrophic failure.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Flow of a Composite Propellant Grain During Combustion

This block diagram explains the **energy-release architecture** within a burning propellant strand, which is conceptually what the KTU syllabus means by "function of each ingredient."

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Solid Propellant Surface] --> B[Polymer Binder Pyrolysis]
    A --> C[AP Decomposition into NH3 and HClO4]
    A --> D[Al Particle Ignition at 2200 K]
    B --> E[Combustion Flame Zone]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[High Temperature Gases 3000 to 3500 K]
    F --> G[Exhaust Nozzle Expansion]
    G --> H[Thrust Generation]
    F --> I[Condensed Phase Al2O3 Smoke]
    I --> J[Exhaust Signature]
```

**Functional interpretation:**
- The **binder (B)** contributes the hydrocarbon fuel backbone (CH₂, CH₃ fragments) that combusts in the flame zone.
- The **AP (C)** provides the oxygen atoms needed to oxidise both the binder and aluminium.
- The **Al (D)** raises the adiabatic flame temperature from ~ 2400 K (binder + AP only) to ~ 3500 K, increasing specific impulse.
- The **smoke (I)** is the visible signature of condensed alumina (Al₂O₃) — a major environmental concern driving research into "smokeless" propellants.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Selecting the Cast-Cure Process Variant

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start{What is the motor diameter?} --> Small[Diameter less than 300 mm]
    Start --> Large[Diameter greater than 300 mm]
    Small --> P1[Use Cartridge Loading]
    Large --> P2[Use Case-Bonded Cast-Cure]
    P2 --> P3{Is the propellant vacuum stable?}
    P3 --> Yes[P3a: Use Slurry Cast under Vacuum]
    P3 --> No[P3b: Add Bonding Agent and Re-formulate]
    P1 --> End[Final Motor Assembly]
    P2 --> End
    P3a --> End
    P3b --> End
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, PBPOT604]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define "polymeric binder" in the context of composite solid rocket propellants. Why is HTPB preferred over CTPB for current-generation launch vehicle motors?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
A polymeric binder is the **continuous elastomeric phase** in a composite solid propellant that mechanically holds the oxidiser (e.g., AP) and metal fuel (e.g., Al) particles and is **cross-linked in-situ** to form a rubbery grain. (1 mark)
HTPB is preferred over CTPB because:
1. HTPB is cured via **isocyanate (urethane linkages)** that are chemically stable, whereas CTPB's carboxyl groups can undergo **hydrolytic degradation**, leading to premature ageing. (1 mark)
2. HTPB has a lower **glass transition temperature** (T_g ≈ −80 °C vs. CTPB's −60 °C) and a higher achievable solid loading (~ 88 wt %), giving superior low-temperature flexibility and higher specific impulse. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: List the three principal functions of a plasticiser (e.g., DOA) in a composite propellant formulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Reduces slurry viscosity** during mixing, allowing higher solid loading at processable viscosity. (1 mark)
2. **Lowers the glass transition temperature (T_g)** of the cured binder, improving low-temperature strain capability. (1 mark)
3. **Improves pot-life and processing safety** by providing better filler wetting and reducing frictional heating during mixing. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *KTU 2024 Scheme allows an Internal Choice between two questions. Below are TWO fully-developed alternative questions, each with sub-parts (a) at 7 marks and (b) at 7 marks.*

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks Total) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the slurry cast-cure manufacturing process for a HTPB-AP-Al composite solid propellant. Mention the critical process parameters at each stage.**

**Model Solution:**

The slurry cast-cure process is the **industry-standard method** for manufacturing large composite propellant grains. It proceeds through the following well-defined stages:

**Stage 1 — Raw material preparation:**
- AP is ground and sieved into a **bimodal distribution** (coarse: 200 µm; fine: 20 µm) to maximise packing efficiency.
- Aluminium is supplied as **spherical powder (5–30 µm)** to ensure uniform combustion.
- HTPB is pre-heated to **50 °C** to lower its viscosity for handling.

**Stage 2 — Liquid premix (200 L planetary mixer):**
- HTPB is charged first, followed by plasticiser (DOA), bonding agent (MAPO/TEPANOL), and burn-rate modifier.
- Mixed at **25 °C** for 30 minutes under vacuum to avoid air entrainment.

**Stage 3 — Solid addition (Vertical planetary sigma mixer, 600 L):**
- Coarse AP is added incrementally (5–6 % by mass per addition) to prevent agglomeration.
- Fine AP is added next, then Al.
- Mixer temperature is held at **40 ± 2 °C**; vacuum is maintained at **< 10 mbar**.

**Stage 4 — Final mixing and curative addition:**
- TDI or IPDI is added as the last ingredient (after vacuum stabilisation) to begin the curing reaction.
- Final mix is held for 45 minutes at 40 °C.

**Stage 5 — Vacuum casting into motor casing:**
- The slurry is poured into a pre-heated (50 °C), mandrel-equipped motor case under vacuum.
- Vacuum is released slowly to prevent bubble formation.

**Stage 6 — Cure cycle:**
- Curing is carried out at **60 ± 1 °C** for **7 days** in a programmable oven.
- Slow heating avoids exothermic runaway (the urethane reaction is exothermic, ΔH ≈ −80 kJ/mol NCO).

**Stage 7 — Post-cure, NDT, and acceptance:**
- After cooling, the mandrel is extracted, and the grain is subjected to **X-ray radiography** and **ultrasonic C-scan** to detect voids, cracks, or unbonded regions.

*(7 marks — 1 mark for each stage clearly explained with parameter; 1 mark for diagram mention.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the role of each ingredient in a typical HTPB-AP-Al composite propellant, and discuss why the solid loading is restricted to ~ 88 wt % in practice.**

**Model Solution:**

| Ingredient | Role |
|---|---|
| **HTPB (8–14 %)** | Forms the elastomeric matrix; burns as fuel; holds the grain shape. |
| **AP (60–72 %)** | Supplies oxygen for combustion of binder and Al; the principal oxidiser. |
| **Al (14–20 %)** | High-energy metal fuel; raises flame T to ~ 3500 K; boosts I_sp. |
| **Plasticiser (DOA, 2–5 %)** | Lowers slurry viscosity and T_g. |
| **Curative (TDI/IPDI, ~ 1 %)** | Cross-links the binder into a 3-D elastomeric network. |
| **Bonding agent (MAPO, 0.1–0.3 %)** | Forms chemical bridges between AP and binder, preventing "de-wetting" cracks. |
| **Burn-rate modifier (Fe₂O₃ or catocene, 0–1 %)** | Adjusts the regression rate and pressure exponent. |
| **Antioxidant (0.1–0.5 %)** | Extends shelf life by scavenging free radicals. |

**Why solid loading is limited to ~ 88 wt %:**
1. **Rheological constraint:** As per the Krieger–Dougherty equation, the viscosity of the slurry rises asymptotically as the filler volume fraction $\phi$ approaches the maximum random packing $\phi_{m}$. Beyond ~ 78 vol %, the slurry viscosity exceeds **5 kPa·s**, which is the practical upper limit for vacuum casting. (3 marks)
2. **Mechanical integrity:** The binder must form a **continuous film** (~ 1–3 µm thick) around every solid particle. Below this film thickness, the cured grain becomes brittle and develops micro-cracks upon thermal cycling. (2 marks)
3. **Process safety:** Higher solid loading increases **frictional sensitivity** during mixing, raising the risk of accidental ignition. (2 marks)

*(Total 7 marks for the ingredient table at 1 mark each = 3 marks, plus 4 marks for the three sub-arguments of solid-loading limitation.)*

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks Total) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Compare HTPB, CTPB, and PBAN as polymeric binders for composite propellants, highlighting their curing chemistry, advantages, and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**HTPB (Hydroxyl-Terminated Polybutadiene):**
- Curing chemistry: **Isocyanate (TDI/IPDI)** → urethane linkages.
- Advantages: Excellent ageing (10+ years), low T_g (−80 °C), high solid loading (88 wt %), good low-temperature mechanical properties.
- Limitations: Slightly lower I_sp than NEPE, requires careful humidity control during cure (isocyanate sensitivity to water → CO₂ bubbles).

**CTPB (Carboxy-Terminated Polybutadiene):**
- Curing chemistry: **Epoxy or aziridine** curative.
- Advantages: Very high elongation, good mechanical strength, fast cure.
- Limitations: **Poor ageing** due to carboxyl-catalysed hydrolysis; difficult to de-water the prepolymer.

**PBAN (Polybutadiene-Acrylic Acid-Acrylonitrile):**
- Curing chemistry: **Epoxy** with amine co-curative.
- Advantages: Better pot-life and processability than CTPB; used in Space Shuttle SRB.
- Limitations: Lower solid loading (max 84 wt %); nitrile groups make the polymer slightly more polar, reducing low-temperature flexibility.

**Comparative Summary Table (3 marks):**

| Property | HTPB | CTPB | PBAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| T_g (°C) | −80 | −60 | −55 |
| Max solid loading (wt %) | 88 | 84 | 84 |
| Ageing (years) | > 10 | 5–7 | 7–10 |
| Curative | Isocyanate | Epoxy | Epoxy/Amine |
| Network type | Urethane | Ester / Ether | Ester / Amide |

*(7 marks: 2 marks for HTPB, 2 marks for CTPB, 2 marks for PBAN, 1 mark for comparative table.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Describe the curing reaction of HTPB with a diisocyanate (TDI). Explain how the NCO/OH ratio (r-value) affects the mechanical properties of the cured propellant.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reaction mechanism (Urethane formation):**
The terminal hydroxyl (-OH) groups of HTPB react with the isocyanate (-NCO) groups of TDI to form a **urethane (carbamate) linkage**, with no by-products:

$$
\text{R–OH} + \text{O=C=N–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–O–CO–NH–R'}
$$

**Step 2 — Network formation:**
Since HTPB is **difunctional** (two -OH end groups) and TDI is **difunctional** (two -NCO groups), the reaction proceeds as a **step-growth polyaddition**, producing a linear-extended chain. To introduce true cross-links, a **tri-functional isocyanate** (e.g., Desmodur N-100, an HDI trimer) is added at 5–15 % of total isocyanate.

**Step 3 — Effect of the r-value (NCO/OH ratio):**

| r-value | Network Type | Mechanical Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **r < 0.8** | Under-cured, dangling chains | Soft, sticky, high elongation, **low strength**, **high creep** |
| **r ≈ 0.85–0.95** | Sub-stoichiometric, ideal | **Optimum balance** of strength, elongation, and resilience |
| **r = 1.00** | Stoichiometric | Maximum cross-link density; harder, slightly less strain |
| **r > 1.05** | Over-cured, biuret/allophanate formation | Brittle, high modulus, **risk of shrinkage cracks** |

**Step 4 — Practical recommendation:** For tactical and launch-vehicle boosters, the r-value is fixed at **0.85 ± 0.02**, giving a slightly sub-stoichiometric network that maximises strain capability (ε_break > 50 %) without sacrificing tensile strength (σ_break > 0.7 MPa).

*(7 marks: 2 marks for mechanism, 2 marks for network formation, 2 marks for r-value table, 1 mark for practical recommendation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in PBPOT604 Module 4 (Polymeric Binders & Composite Manufacturing):**
> 1. **Mixing up CTPB and HTPB curing chemistry** — these are *not* interchangeable. CTPB uses **epoxy/aziridine**; HTPB uses **isocyanate**. Examiners deduct 2 marks if a student writes "CTPB is cured with TDI."
> 2. **Confusing the rule of mixtures** — students often write $\rho_{p} = \sum w_{i} \rho_{i}$ (mass-weighted average), which is **physically wrong** for heterogeneous propellants. The correct form is the *reciprocal* (volume-additive) form.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw/mention the flow diagram** in cast-cure questions — a full 14-mark answer without a labelled block diagram loses 2–3 marks even if the text is correct.
> 4. **Stating the role of Al only as "fuel"** — Al also raises the adiabatic flame temperature from ~ 2400 K to ~ 3500 K and produces condensed-phase Al₂O₃ smoke (signature issue). This deeper insight is what distinguishes a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
> 5. **Skipping the r-value discussion in curing questions** — examiners specifically look for the link between NCO/OH ratio and mechanical property trade-off.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Polymeric Binders & Composite Manufacturing)**

- [x] **Composite solid propellant = polymer binder + crystalline oxidiser (AP) + metal fuel (Al) + additives.**
- [x] **HTPB is the industry-standard binder**, cured with **isocyanates** (TDI/IPDI) via urethane linkages.
- [x] **CTPB** is cured with **epoxy/aziridine**; inferior ageing vs. HTPB.
- [x] **NEPE** gives the highest I_sp, used in advanced tactical missiles.
- [x] **Solid loading limit ≈ 88 wt % / 78 vol %** is dictated by **Krieger–Dougherty slurry rheology**, binder film thickness, and process safety.
- [x] **Reciprocal rule of mixtures** governs propellant density: $\dfrac{1}{\rho_{p}} = \sum \dfrac{w_{i}}{\rho_{i}}$.
- [x] **Vielle's Law** $r = aP^{n}$ governs regression; **n > 0.6 = unsafe** (chuffing risk).
- [x] **Cast-cure process** = premix → solid addition (coarse AP → fine AP → Al) → vacuum cast → oven cure (60 °C, 7 days) → NDT.
- [x] **Plasticiser (DOA)** lowers viscosity and T_g; **bonding agent (MAPO)** prevents de-wetting at AP-binder interface.
- [x] **NCO/OH ratio r ≈ 0.85** is the engineering sweet-spot for propellant elasticity.
- [x] **Aluminium powder** raises flame T to ~ 3500 K; **Al₂O₃ smoke** is the visible rocket signature.
- [x] **Bimodal AP** (200 µm + 20 µm) maximises packing fraction and processability.
- [x] **Quality control** = X-ray + ultrasonic C-scan for void/crack detection.
- [x] **T_g of HTPB ≈ −80 °C** makes it suitable for cold-storage military applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hand Lay-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hand Lay-up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Academic Definition

**Hand Lay-up (also called Contact Lay-up or Open Mold Lay-up)** is the oldest, simplest, and most widely used *open-mold* manufacturing process for fabricating **fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites**. In this process, dry reinforcement (in the form of mats, woven rovings, or fabric) is manually placed into an open mold, and a **liquid thermosetting resin** (typically unsaturated polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy) is poured, brushed, or rolled over the reinforcement. The resin is then uniformly distributed using hand rollers to wet out the fibers, remove entrapped air, and consolidate the laminate. Curing takes place at **ambient temperature** (or with mild heat) without applied pressure, and the part is finally demolded after a predefined cure cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Processing of Composites):**
> Hand lay-up is classified under **Open-Mold Processes** in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus. It is a **room-temperature, low-pressure, labor-intensive** process belonging to the family of *Liquid Composite Molding (LCM)* entry-level techniques. The KTU examiner often tests: (i) process sequence, (ii) materials used, (iii) advantages/limitations, and (iv) comparison with spray-up and other open-mold variants.

### Key Characteristics at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Process Type | Open Mold / Contact Molding |
| Operating Pressure | **0.1 MPa** (atmospheric only) |
| Operating Temperature | **25 °C – 60 °C** (ambient to mild heat) |
| Resin Viscosity Required | **200 – 1000 cP** (low for easy wet-out) |
| Typical Fiber Volume Fraction (V_f) | **0.20 – 0.35** (low–moderate) |
| Production Rate | **1 – 5 kg/hr** (labor-bound) |
| Part Size Capability | Up to **100 m²** (large structures) |
| Capital Investment | **Very Low** (tools, rollers, brushes) |

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Sandwich-Making Analogy"

Imagine you are making a layered sandwich at home. You take a flat plate (the **mold**), grease it lightly (the **release agent**), and then alternate layers of bread (the **reinforcement mat**) with butter that you spread evenly using a knife (the **liquid resin + roller**). You press each layer gently to remove any air bubbles, and let the whole sandwich sit on the counter until the butter hardens (the **room-temperature cure**). Finally, you flip the plate and lift the solidified sandwich off (the **demolding** step). That is **exactly** how hand lay-up works — only the "bread" is fiberglass and the "butter" is liquid polyester resin that chemically cross-links into a rigid plastic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU exams:** The hand lay-up process is essentially the *baseline* against which all other composite manufacturing processes (spray-up, RTM, VARTM, filament winding, pultrusion, autoclave) are compared. Mastering it gives you the conceptual foundation for the entire Module 4 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Hand Lay-up Process — Structured Logic Breakdown

The process can be decomposed into **eight sequential logic steps**, each of which carries significant marks in KTU descriptive answers.

1. **Mold Preparation (Pre-production)**
   - The mold surface (made of wood, fiberglass, or metal) is cleaned, polished, and inspected for defects.
   - A **release agent** (wax, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), or silicone-based) is applied in 2–3 thin coats to ensure clean part removal.
   - A **gel coat** (pigmented, thixotropic resin layer, 0.3–0.5 mm thick) may be sprayed or brushed to provide a smooth, UV-resistant, cosmetic surface.

2. **Reinforcement Placement**
   - Dry reinforcement layers (chopped strand mat, woven roving, stitched fabric) are cut to the mold contour and laid sequentially.
   - Number of plies (*n*) and orientation angles are chosen to meet the design stiffness/strength requirement.

3. **Resin Application**
   - Liquid **thermoset resin** (pre-mixed with hardener/catalyst and accelerator) is poured over the reinforcement.
   - Common systems: unsaturated polyester + MEKP catalyst + cobalt naphthenate accelerator; epoxy + amine hardener.

4. **Wet-out and Consolidation (Hand Rolling)**
   - **Hand rollers** (usually aluminum or steel discs with protruding pins, called *paddle rollers* or *consolidation rollers*) are used to:
     - Force resin through the reinforcement,
     - Remove entrapped air bubbles (degassing),
     - Compact the laminate to the required thickness,
     - Distribute resin uniformly.

5. **Room-Temperature Cure**
   - The laminate is left to cure at **25 °C** for **8–24 hours** (depending on resin system and ambient conditions).
   - For thicker laminates, an intermediate **exothermic peak** must be controlled to avoid thermal degradation.
   - Post-cure at **60–80 °C for 2–4 hours** may be applied to enhance cross-link density and T_g.

6. **Demolding**
   - Once the part has developed sufficient green strength, it is carefully lifted from the mold using wedges, air jets, or mechanical extractors.

7. **Trimming and Finishing**
   - Flashes and excess material are trimmed using routers or saws.
   - Holes, inserts, and edge details are machined.

8. **Quality Inspection**
   - Visual + ultrasonic / tap-test inspection for voids, delaminations, and dry spots.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why low-viscosity resin?** A viscosity of **200–1000 cP** allows the resin to flow through fiber tows by capillary action and gravity alone — no external pressure is needed.
- **Why rollers?** Manual rolling mimics the function of a press: it provides localized compaction that pushes air upward and outward, collapsing the laminate to a uniform thickness.
- **Why room-temperature cure?** Eliminates the need for expensive autoclaves/heated molds, making the process suitable for very large structures (boat hulls, wind turbine blades) that simply cannot fit in a press.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Definitions | Typical Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f}}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}}$ | $W_f$ = fiber weight, $\rho_f$ = fiber density, $W_m$ = matrix weight, $\rho_m$ = matrix density | Dimensionless | Determines **fiber volume fraction** from raw material weights — the single most important composite property. |
| 2 | $V_m = 1 - V_f - V_v$ | $V_v$ = void volume fraction | Dimensionless | Matrix volume fraction accounting for voids (typically **1–5 %** in hand lay-up). |
| 3 | $W_c = W_f + W_m$ | Composite weight | kg / g | Composite weight balance check. |
| 4 | $t = n \times t_{ply}$ | $n$ = number of plies, $t_{ply}$ = single ply thickness | mm | Predicts **laminate thickness** for tooling clearance. |
| 5 | $t_{ply} = \dfrac{W_{a,f}}{\rho_f \times V_f} \times 10^{-3}$ | $W_{a,f}$ = areal weight of fabric (g/m²) | mm | Converts fabric grammage to cured ply thickness. |
| 6 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | $\rho_c$ = composite density | g/cm³ | Theoretical density (Rule of Mixtures). |
| 7 | $T_{cure} = 25 + \Delta T_{exo}$ | $\Delta T_{exo}$ = exothermic rise (typically 30–80 °C) | °C | Peak laminate temperature during thick-section cure. |
| 8 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | $\sigma$ = tensile strength | MPa | **Rule of Mixtures** for longitudinal strength (iso-strain). |
| 9 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $E$ = Young's modulus | GPa | **Rule of Mixtures** for longitudinal modulus. |
| 10 | $P_{roller} \approx 5$ to $20$ N per cm of roller width | Manual rolling force | N/cm | Empirical compaction pressure applied by operator. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Formula Table Rule:** Always use `\vert` (NOT the pipe character `\vert`) when writing absolute values, determinants, or magnitudes inside LaTeX, because the pipe character `$\vert$` will break the markdown table structure.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Marine Industry:** Hulls, decks, and superstructures of yachts, fishing boats, and patrol vessels — accounting for nearly **60 %** of all hand-laid composites.
- **Aerospace Tooling:** Large master models and lay-up tools for autoclave parts (the tools themselves are hand-laid!).
- **Wind Energy:** Early-generation wind turbine blade prototypes and root sections.
- **Automotive:** Body panels of low-volume specialty cars, truck fairings, and aftermarket parts.
- **Civil Engineering:** Manhole covers, FRP rebar, bridge decking, and corrosion-resistant tanks.
- **Building & Construction:** Roofing panels, dome structures, and architectural claddings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Fiber Volume Fraction from Raw Material Weights

The **fiber volume fraction** $V_f$ is the ratio of fiber volume to total composite volume. Since direct volume measurement is impractical during manufacturing, we derive $V_f$ from weight and density data.

**Step 1 — Define the basic volume relationships.**

$$V_f = \frac{V_{fiber}}{V_{composite}}, \quad V_m = \frac{V_{matrix}}{V_{composite}}$$

where $V_{fiber}$ and $V_{matrix}$ are the absolute volumes of fiber and matrix phases.

**Step 2 — Express absolute volumes in terms of weights and densities.**

Using the fundamental relation $V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$:

$$V_{fiber} = \frac{W_f}{\rho_f}, \quad V_{matrix} = \frac{W_m}{\rho_m}$$

**Step 3 — Write the total composite volume (ignoring voids for clarity).**

$$V_{composite} = V_{fiber} + V_{matrix} = \frac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \frac{W_m}{\rho_m}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the definition of $V_f$.**

$$V_f = \frac{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f}}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\rho_f \rho_m$ to clear nested fractions.**

$$V_f = \frac{W_f \rho_m}{W_f \rho_m + W_m \rho_f}$$

This is the **standard KTU textbook form** of the equation.

**Numerical Worked Example:**

A hand lay-up laminate uses **500 g** of E-glass fiber ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) and **350 g** of polyester resin ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). Calculate $V_f$.

**Solution:**

$$V_f = \frac{W_f \rho_m}{W_f \rho_m + W_m \rho_f}$$

$$V_f = \frac{500 \times 1.20}{500 \times 1.20 + 350 \times 2.55}$$

$$V_f = \frac{600}{600 + 892.5} = \frac{600}{1492.5}$$

$$V_f \approx 0.402 \text{ or } 40.2\%$$

This is a **realistic value for a well-consolidated hand lay-up** (typical range 20–40 %).

---

### Derivation 2: Ply Thickness from Fabric Areal Weight

Designers need to know the cured thickness of each ply to compute total laminate thickness and tooling clearances.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of areal weight.**

$$W_{a,f} = \frac{W_f}{A_{ply}} \quad \left[ \frac{g}{m^2} \right]$$

where $A_{ply}$ is the area of a single ply.

**Step 2 — Write fiber volume per unit area.**

$$\frac{V_{fiber}}{A_{ply}} = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{A_{ply}} = \frac{W_{a,f}}{\rho_f} \quad \left[ \frac{g/m^2}{g/cm^3} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Relate fiber volume per area to ply thickness through $V_f$.**

Since $V_f = \dfrac{V_{fiber}}{V_{ply}}$ and $V_{ply} = A_{ply} \times t_{ply}$:

$$\frac{V_{fiber}}{A_{ply}} = V_f \times t_{ply}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $t_{ply}$.**

$$V_f \times t_{ply} = \frac{W_{a,f}}{\rho_f}$$

$$t_{ply} = \frac{W_{a,f}}{\rho_f \times V_f} \times 10^{-3} \text{ (in mm, when } W_{a,f} \text{ in g/m² and } \rho_f \text{ in g/cm³)}$$

**Numerical Example:**

A woven E-glass fabric has an areal weight of **600 g/m²**. If the target fiber volume fraction is **V_f = 0.35**, calculate the cured ply thickness.

$$t_{ply} = \frac{600}{2.55 \times 0.35} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$t_{ply} = \frac{600}{0.8925} \times 10^{-3} = 672.27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{ply} \approx 0.672 \text{ mm}$$

A 6-ply laminate would therefore be approximately **4.03 mm** thick.

---

### Derivation 3: Composite Density (Rule of Mixtures)

**Step 1 — Write the composite weight balance.**

$$W_c = W_f + W_m$$

**Step 2 — Divide by composite volume $V_c$.**

$$\frac{W_c}{V_c} = \frac{W_f}{V_c} + \frac{W_m}{V_c}$$

**Step 3 — Note that $\dfrac{W_f}{V_c} = \rho_f V_f$ (since $V_f = V_{fiber}/V_c$ and $W_f = \rho_f V_{fiber}$).**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply the constraint $V_m = 1 - V_f$ (no voids).**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_m + V_f(\rho_f - \rho_m)}$$

**Numerical Example:** With $V_f = 0.40$, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³:

$$\rho_c = 1.20 + 0.40 \times (2.55 - 1.20) = 1.20 + 0.40 \times 1.35 = 1.20 + 0.54$$

$$\rho_c = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

This is the **theoretical density**. A measured value of, say, 1.70 g/cm³ would imply a void content of:

$$V_v = \frac{\rho_c^{th} - \rho_c^{exp}}{\rho_c^{th}} = \frac{1.74 - 1.70}{1.74} \approx 0.023 \text{ or } 2.3\%$$

A void content of **< 2 %** is considered good quality for hand lay-up; **> 5 %** is unacceptable for structural parts.

---

### Derivation 4: Exothermic Peak Temperature During Cure

Thick hand-laid laminates (e.g., > 10 mm) can overheat internally due to the **exothermic cross-linking reaction** of thermoset resins, leading to thermal degradation, cracking, or even fire.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the laminate.**

The total heat released by the curing reaction is:

$$Q_{total} = m_{resin} \times H_r$$

where $H_r$ is the **heat of reaction** (typically **300–400 J/g** for polyester, **500–600 J/g** for epoxy).

**Step 2 — Adiabatic temperature rise (worst-case, no heat loss).**

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{Q_{total}}{m_{composite} \times C_p}$$

where $C_p$ is the **specific heat capacity** of the composite (typically **1.0–1.5 J/g·K**).

**Step 3 — Substitute for a polyester laminate with 60 % resin by weight.**

For 1 kg of laminate: $m_{resin} \approx 600$ g, $H_r \approx 350$ J/g, $C_p \approx 1.2$ J/g·K.

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{600 \times 350}{1000 \times 1.2} = \frac{210{,}000}{1200} = 175 \text{ °C}$$

Starting from $T_{ambient} = 30$ °C, the peak temperature would reach **205 °C** — well above the **heat distortion temperature (HDT)** of polyester (~80 °C) and approaching the **ignition threshold**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** This is why KTU examiners frequently emphasize that hand lay-up of thick sections requires: (i) layered / staged lay-up with intermediate gelation, (ii) use of low-exotherm resin systems, (iii) active cooling, or (iv) switching to RTM/VARTM for thick parts.

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix (Hand Lay-up vs. Other Open-Mold Processes)

| Property | Hand Lay-up | Spray-up | RTM | VARTM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure applied | **0 (contact only)** | 0 | 0.1–0.7 MPa | Vacuum (~0.1 MPa) |
| Fiber form | Mat / fabric | Chopped roving | Preform | Preform |
| Typical V_f | **0.20–0.35** | 0.15–0.25 | 0.45–0.60 | 0.50–0.60 |
| Void content | 2–5 % | 3–7 % | < 1 % | < 1 % |
| Production rate | **Very low** | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium–High |
| Capital cost | **Very low** | Low | High | High |
| Part complexity | Simple–medium | Simple | Medium–high | High |
| Surface finish | **Best (gel-coatable)** | Moderate | Both sides | One side |
| Operator skill | Moderate | Moderate | High | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Hand Lay-up Process Flow (Mermaid Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STEP 1: MOLD SELECTION"] --> A2["STEP 2: MOLD CLEANING AND INSPECTION"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3: RELEASE AGENT APPLICATION 2 TO 3 COATS"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4: GEL COAT SPRAY OR BRUSH OPTIONAL"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5: REINFORCEMENT CUTTING AND PLACEMENT LAYER 1"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6: RESIN MIXING WITH HARDENER AND ACCELERATOR"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 7: RESIN POURING OR BRUSHING OVER LAYER 1"]
    A7 --> A8["STEP 8: WET OUT USING PADDLE ROLLER DEGASS AND CONSOLIDATE"]
    A8 --> A9["STEP 9: REPEAT STEPS 5 TO 8 FOR N PLIES"]
    A9 --> A10["STEP 10: ROOM TEMPERATURE CURE 8 TO 24 HOURS"]
    A10 --> A11["STEP 11: POST CURE 60 TO 80 DEG C IF REQUIRED"]
    A11 --> A12["STEP 12: DEMOLDING USING WEDGES OR AIR JET"]
    A12 --> A13["STEP 13: TRIMMING FLASHES AND EDGE FINISHING"]
    A13 --> A14["STEP 14: QUALITY INSPECTION VISUAL ULTRASONIC TAP TEST"]
    A14 --> A15["FINAL FRP COMPOSITE PART"]
```

### Diagram 2: Mold Configuration Schematic (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["GEL COAT LAYER 0.3 TO 0.5 MM"] --> L2["PLY 1 FIRST REINFORCEMENT LAYER"]
    L2 --> L3["RESIN RICH INTERFACE WET OUT ZONE"]
    L3 --> L4["PLY 2 SECOND REINFORCEMENT LAYER"]
    L4 --> L5["RESIN RICH INTERFACE"]
    L5 --> L6["PLY N NTH REINFORCEMENT LAYER"]
    L6 --> L7["BACK SURFACE OPEN AIR CURE"]
    MOLD["MOLD SURFACE WOOD FRP OR METAL"] --> L1
    REL["RELEASE AGENT WAX OR PVA"] --> MOLD
```

### Diagram 3: Resin Flow and Roller Action (Wet-out Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RES["LIQUID RESIN LOW VISCOSITY 200 TO 1000 cP"] --> GRAV["GRAVITY DRIVEN FLOW"]
    GRAV --> FAB["REINFORCEMENT FABRIC OR MAT DRY"]
    FAB --> CAP["CAPILLARY WETTING OF FIBER TOWS"]
    CAP --> ROLL["PADDLE ROLLER MANUAL FORCE 5 TO 20 N PER cm"]
    ROLL --> COMP["LAMINATE COMPACTION AND AIR EXPULSION"]
    COMP --> CONS["CONSOLIDATED WET LAMINATE UNIFORM Vf"]
```

### Diagram 4: Defect Formation Mechanisms (Why Hand Lay-up Needs Skilled Operators)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["ROOT CAUSES OF DEFECTS"] --> D1["INSUFFICIENT ROLLING"]
    ROOT --> D2["HIGH RESIN VISCOSITY"]
    ROOT --> D3["POOR MOLD RELEASE"]
    ROOT --> D4["THICK SECTION EXOTHERM"]
    D1 --> DEF1["VOID FORMATION AND DRY SPOTS"]
    D2 --> DEF2["INCOMPLETE WET OUT AND RESIN STARVED AREAS"]
    D3 --> DEF3["STICKING AND PART DAMAGE DURING DEMOLDING"]
    D4 --> DEF4["THERMAL CRACKING AND RESIN DEGRADATION"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** Notice that all four root causes converge on defects that degrade the mechanical properties of the composite. The KTU examiner expects students to *link* process parameters (rolling force, viscosity, temperature) to defect types and ultimately to the mechanical performance of the FRP part.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**Define Hand Lay-up process. List any four materials used as reinforcement in this process.**

**Model Answer:**

Hand lay-up is an **open-mold composite manufacturing process** in which dry fiber reinforcements are manually placed in a mold, impregnated with liquid thermoset resin, consolidated by hand rolling, and cured at room temperature without applied pressure to produce FRP parts.

**Four reinforcement materials used:**
1. **E-glass chopped strand mat (CSM)** — random fibers bonded with binder.
2. **E-glass woven roving (WR)** — bidirectional woven fabric.
3. **Woven E-glass cloth** — high-quality surface finish.
4. **Carbon fiber fabric** — for high-performance aerospace applications.

(Other acceptable answers: aramid/Kevlar fabric, natural fibers like jute/flax, stitched multiaxial fabrics.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]**
**Mention any three advantages and two limitations of hand lay-up process.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Low capital investment** — only simple tools (brushes, rollers, molds) are required.
2. **Versatile part size and shape** — can produce very large structures (up to ~100 m²) impossible to fit in presses.
3. **Excellent surface finish** — gel-coated side produces a smooth, paintable, UV-resistant surface.

**Limitations:**
1. **Low fiber volume fraction (V_f = 0.20–0.35)** and high void content (2–5 %) leading to inferior mechanical properties compared to closed-mold processes.
2. **Labor-intensive and operator-dependent** — quality varies with worker skill, leading to poor reproducibility.

(Other acceptable limitations: long cure times, styrene emissions in polyester systems, limited to thermosets only.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 4 Model Pattern, 14 Marks]**

### OR

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure of the Hand Lay-up process** with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of the **paddle roller** in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and major applications** of hand lay-up. Compare it briefly with **spray-up** process. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure & Roller Role:**

The hand lay-up process is carried out in the following sequential steps:

1. **Mold Selection and Preparation:** A mold made of wood, fiberglass-reinforced plastic (FRP), or polished metal is selected. The mold surface is cleaned thoroughly to remove dust, oil, and previous release-agent residues.

2. **Release Agent Application:** A release agent (typically **wax, PVA, or silicone spray**) is applied in 2–3 thin, even coats with intermediate buffing. This prevents the cured laminate from bonding to the mold surface.

3. **Gel Coat Application (Optional):** A thixotropic, pigmented polyester gel coat is sprayed or brushed onto the mold to a thickness of **0.3–0.5 mm**. This provides the cosmetic, weather-resistant outer surface.

4. **Reinforcement Placement:** The first layer of reinforcement (CSM, woven roving, or fabric) is cut to the mold contour and placed carefully without wrinkles.

5. **Resin Mixing and Application:** The thermoset resin (polyester with **1–2 % MEKP catalyst** and **0.5–1 % cobalt accelerator**) is mixed and poured or brushed uniformly over the reinforcement.

6. **Wet-out and Consolidation (Paddle Roller Action):** This is the **most critical step**. The **paddle roller** (a circular aluminum disc with radial fins/pins) is moved across the laminate with a manual force of **5–20 N per cm of roller width**. The roller performs four simultaneous functions:
   - **Wetting-out:** Forces the resin to penetrate and saturate the fiber bundles.
   - **Degassing:** Expels entrapped air bubbles upward to the surface.
   - **Compaction:** Compresses the laminate to a uniform thickness, increasing V_f.
   - **Distribution:** Spreads resin evenly, eliminating resin-rich and resin-starved regions.

7. **Subsequent Plies:** Steps 4–6 are repeated for *n* plies until the design thickness is achieved.

8. **Curing:** The laminate is allowed to cure at **25 °C for 8–24 hours**. For thick laminates, the **exothermic peak** is monitored. A post-cure at **60–80 °C for 2–4 hours** improves T_g and mechanical properties.

9. **Demolding:** After developing sufficient green strength, the part is carefully lifted from the mold.

10. **Trimming and Inspection:** Flashes are trimmed, and the part is inspected for voids, delaminations, and dimensional accuracy.

**Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, Applications & Spray-up Comparison:**

**Advantages:** (Discuss any 4)
- Low tooling and capital cost.
- Suitable for very large and complex parts.
- Excellent surface finish on gel-coated side.
- Easy to change reinforcement type, orientation, and ply count.
- Low scrap rate for prototype/small-batch production.

**Limitations:** (Discuss any 3)
- Labor-intensive, slow production rate.
- Low V_f and high void content → poor mechanical performance.
- Resin quality and laminate consistency are operator-dependent.
- Open mold causes **styrene emissions** (health hazard) and is unsuitable for precision-tolerance parts.
- Limited to room-temperature-curing thermosets.

**Applications:** Boat hulls, chemical storage tanks, swimming pools, truck/van bodies, shower stalls, architectural claddings, aerospace tooling models.

**Hand Lay-up vs. Spray-up Comparison:**

| Property | Hand Lay-up | Spray-up |
|---|---|---|
| Reinforcement form | Continuous mat/fabric | **Chopped roving** (sprayed) |
| Resin application | Brushed/poured | **Simultaneously sprayed** with fiber |
| V_f | 0.20–0.35 | 0.15–0.25 (lower) |
| Production rate | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Mechanical properties | Better (continuous fibers) | Lower (discontinuous fibers) |
| Operator skill | Moderate | Moderate–high |

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** Describe the **various types of molds** used in hand lay-up. Explain the functions of **release agents and gel coats**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A hand lay-up laminate is fabricated with **W_f = 600 g of E-glass** ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) and **W_m = 400 g of polyester resin** ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). Calculate the **fiber volume fraction (V_f), matrix volume fraction (V_m), composite density (ρ_c), and theoretical ply thickness** for a 4-ply laminate using fabric of areal weight **450 g/m²** at the calculated V_f. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Mold Types, Release Agents & Gel Coats:**

**Mold Types:**
1. **Wooden molds:** Used for low-volume, large prototypes. Cheapest option. Limited life due to moisture absorption and surface degradation. Typically sealed with polyurethane varnish.
2. **FRP (fiberglass) molds:** Most common in industry. Made by laying up a female mold over a master pattern. Durable, lightweight, and provide good surface finish. Lifetime: 1000+ parts.
3. **Metallic molds (aluminum or steel):** Used for high-volume production. Excellent thermal conductivity, but expensive. Aluminum is preferred for its light weight.
4. **Plaster/cement molds:** Used for architectural applications. Very cheap but fragile and porous — require sealing.

**Release Agents:**
Release agents are applied to the mold surface to prevent the cured laminate from adhering to the mold. They are classified into three families:
- **Wax-based:** Easy to apply, good for low-temperature cures; need multiple coats.
- **PVA (polyvinyl alcohol):** Water-soluble film-forming agent; used with wax for high-gloss parts.
- **Semi-permanent (silicone or fluoropolymer):** Bond chemically to the mold; survive 50–100 releases without reapplication; preferred in aerospace tooling.

**Gel Coats:**
A gel coat is a **pigmented, thixotropic, surface-quality resin layer** applied directly onto the prepared mold surface before reinforcement placement. Functions:
- Provides a **smooth, glossy, colored outer surface** (the visible side of the part).
- Acts as a **UV barrier** protecting the structural laminate from sunlight degradation.
- Provides **chemical and moisture resistance** to the finished part.
- Hides the fiber pattern, giving a paint-like finish.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $W_f = 600$ g, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $W_m = 400$ g, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $W_{a,f} = 450$ g/m², $n = 4$ plies.

**Step 1 — Fiber Volume Fraction (V_f):**

$$V_f = \frac{W_f \rho_m}{W_f \rho_m + W_m \rho_f}$$

$$V_f = \frac{600 \times 1.20}{600 \times 1.20 + 400 \times 2.55}$$

$$V_f = \frac{720}{720 + 1020} = \frac{720}{1740}$$

$$V_f \approx 0.4138 \text{ or } 41.38\%$$

**Step 2 — Matrix Volume Fraction (V_m)** (assuming negligible voids for theoretical calculation):

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.4138 = 0.5862 \text{ or } 58.62\%$$

**Step 3 — Composite Density (ρ_c):**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

$$\rho_c = (2.55 \times 0.4138) + (1.20 \times 0.5862)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.055 + 0.703 = 1.758 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Ply Thickness:**

$$t_{ply} = \frac{W_{a,f}}{\rho_f \times V_f} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$t_{ply} = \frac{450}{2.55 \times 0.4138} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$t_{ply} = \frac{450}{1.0552} \times 10^{-3} = 426.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{ply} \approx 0.4265 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Total Laminate Thickness (n = 4):**

$$t_{total} = n \times t_{ply} = 4 \times 0.4265 = 1.706 \text{ mm}$$

**Summary of Results:**
- $V_f \approx 0.414$ (41.4 %)
- $V_m \approx 0.586$ (58.6 %)
- $\rho_c \approx 1.758$ g/cm³
- $t_{ply} \approx 0.427$ mm
- $t_{total} \approx 1.706$ mm

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** in numerical problems (g/m² → mm, g/cm³ consistency) costs **1–2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing areal weight with volumetric weight** — areal weight is per unit *area* (g/m²), NOT per unit volume. Use the dedicated formula $t_{ply} = W_{a,f}/(\rho_f V_f) \times 10^{-3}$.
> 3. **Omitting the gel coat** in the process flow diagram is a common mistake — the KTU 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes gel coat as a *separate* distinct step.
> 4. **In Part B 7-mark questions**, students often skip the *role* of the paddle roller and write only "rolling is done." Always elaborate: **wet-out + degassing + compaction + distribution** are the four functions. Examiners allocate 1 mark per function.
> 5. **Spelling "polyester" as "polyster"** or writing **"vinyl ester"** as "vinyl-ester" may seem trivial, but KTU examiners deduct marks for inconsistent technical terminology.
> 6. **Forgetting V_m = 1 − V_f − V_v** when voids are mentioned leads to calculation errors in advanced problems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Hand lay-up = open mold, no pressure, room-temperature cure, manual labor** — remember this as the *defining identity* of the process.
- The process is governed by the equation: $V_f = \dfrac{W_f \rho_m}{W_f \rho_m + W_m \rho_f}$ — memorize this in the **fully expanded** form.
- **Fiber volume fraction** in hand lay-up is typically **0.20–0.35** (low), void content **2–5 %** (moderate).
- The **paddle roller** has four simultaneous functions: **wet-out, degassing, compaction, distribution** — each worth 1 mark in a 4-mark question.
- **Gel coat** thickness is always **0.3–0.5 mm** and is the first layer in the mold (cosmetic + UV + chemical protection).
- **Release agent families:** Wax, PVA, and semi-permanent (silicone/fluoropolymer).
- **Resin systems used:** Polyester (most common), vinyl ester (corrosion), epoxy (high performance).
- **Catalyst for polyester:** MEKP (methyl ethyl ketone peroxide); **accelerator:** cobalt naphthenate.
- **Cure temperature:** 25 °C ambient; **post-cure:** 60–80 °C for 2–4 hours.
- **Exotherm risk:** Thick laminates (> 10 mm) can reach 150–200 °C internally due to exothermic cross-linking — may cause thermal cracking.
- **Most common defect:** Voids and dry spots (caused by insufficient rolling or high resin viscosity).
- **Most common reinforcement:** E-glass chopped strand mat (CSM) + woven roving (WR) combination.
- **Largest application segment:** Marine (boat hulls).
- **Comparison keywords for KTU answers:** Hand lay-up has **lower V_f, lower cost, larger parts, better surface** than spray-up; **higher V_f, better properties, more expensive** than RTM/VARTM.
- **Rule of Mixtures for density:** $\rho_c = \rho_m + V_f(\rho_f - \rho_m)$ — memorize this linear form.
- **Areal weight formula:** $t_{ply} = \dfrac{W_{a,f}}{\rho_f V_f} \times 10^{-3}$ mm (with $W_{a,f}$ in g/m²).
- **Hand lay-up is NOT suitable for:** High-precision tolerance parts, high-volume mass production, thermoplastic matrices, or aerospace primary structural components (where autoclave is required).
- **KTU Module 4 hierarchy:** Hand Lay-up → Spray-up → RTM → VARTM → Autoclave (increasing pressure, V_f, quality, and cost).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spray-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spray-up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spray-up** is an **open-mold, low-pressure composite manufacturing process** in which **chopped reinforcing fibers (typically glass rovings)** and **liquid thermoset resin (unsaturated polyester or vinyl ester)** are **simultaneously sprayed onto an open mold surface** using a dual-stream spray gun, followed by **manual consolidation with rollers** to remove entrapped air, compact the laminate, and ensure fiber wet-out. The laminate is subsequently cured at room temperature (with optional heated molds) and demolded.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, spray-up falls under **contact molding / open mold processes** alongside **hand lay-up**. The hallmark distinction is the **mechanized deposition of resin + chopped fiber** rather than manual placement of pre-impregnated mats.

### 1.2 Key Process Variants (Syllabus Highlights)

| Variant | Description |
|---|---|
| **Simultaneous Spray-up** | Resin + catalyst + chopped fibers sprayed together in one pass |
| **Two-Stream Spray-up** | Resin stream and chopped fiber stream are kept separate until convergence at the mold surface |
| **In-Mold Coating (IMC) variant** | A surface gel coat is first sprayed into the mold before the structural spray-up layer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently differentiate **spray-up vs. hand lay-up** and **spray-up vs. RTM (Resin Transfer Molding)**. Memorize the keyword **"open mold, low pressure, chopped fiber, dual-stream gun, manual roll-out."**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **painting a wall with a spray paint can**, but instead of just paint, the can simultaneously releases **paint (resin) and tiny straw pieces (chopped glass fibers)** that mix right at the wall surface. Then, you take a **paint roller** and gently press the wet mixture to:
- Squeeze out air bubbles (like rolling a carpet to remove wrinkles),
- Spread the fibers evenly,
- Ensure the fibers are fully soaked ("wet-out") in resin.

That wet mixture then **hardens** into a strong, lightweight, fiber-reinforced plastic shell — exactly like a **boat hull, a bathtub, or a swimming pool panel**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Spray-up = *spray-painting with chopped straw* + *rolling it flat* + *letting it harden.* The "straw" gives strength; the "paint" binds and protects.

### 1.4 Standard Process Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

The following are **typical industry-standard parameters** that examiners expect:

- **Resin viscosity at spray:** ~ **200–500 cP** (centipoise)
- **Chopped fiber length:** **6 mm to 50 mm** (most common: **12–25 mm**)
- **Fiber content achievable:** **15–35 % by weight** (lower than hand lay-up's 30–45 %)
- **Glass roving tex (linear density):** **2400, 4800, 9600 g/km**
- **Spray gun air pressure:** **0.2–0.4 MPa (2–4 bar)**
- **Roller consolidation pressure (manual):** **0.01–0.05 MPa**
- **Ambient cure temperature:** **20–40 °C** (ambient)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded metrics above are the figures KTU examiners statistically prefer for numerical/short-answer questions.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The spray-up process is a **6-stage sequential operation**. Each stage has a definitive "why" and "how":

**Stage 1 — Mold Preparation**
- *Why:* To ensure clean release and good surface finish on the visible (gel-coat) side.
- *How:* The open mold (typically female mold for large parts) is cleaned, polished, and treated with a **release agent** (wax, PVA, or silicone-based).

**Stage 2 — Gel-Coat Application (Optional but Standard)**
- *Why:* Provides a smooth, pigmented, UV-resistant outer surface; protects structural laminate from moisture and weathering.
- *How:* A pigmented, unreinforced polyester layer (~ **0.3–0.5 mm** thick) is sprayed and partially cured (tacky state) before the structural spray-up begins.

**Stage 3 — Resin + Chopped Fiber Deposition**
- *Why:* To form the reinforced structural layer in a single rapid pass.
- *How:*
  - The **chopper unit** (mounted on the spray gun or as a separate unit) cuts continuous glass rovings into **6–50 mm** lengths and blows them via air stream toward the mold.
  - Simultaneously, the **resin stream** (polyester + catalyst like MEKP — Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide) is sprayed from a separate nozzle.
  - Both streams converge at the mold surface, forming a **wet, fiber-laden layer**.

**Stage 4 — Manual Roll-Out / Consolidation**
- *Why:* To remove entrapped air voids, wet-out fibers, and achieve uniform thickness.
- *How:* A technician uses **metal or rubber rollers** to compact the laminate. This step **determines the final fiber volume fraction and void content** — the most quality-critical operation.

**Stage 5 — Curing**
- *Why:* To crosslink the thermoset resin into a rigid 3D network.
- *How:* Curing proceeds at **room temperature (exothermic peak may reach 80–120 °C internally)** with the heat of reaction. For thicker laminates, **intermediate cure stages** (gelation) are introduced to control exotherm.

**Stage 6 — Demolding and Trimming**
- *Why:* To obtain the finished part.
- *How:* After sufficient cure (Barcol hardness > 40), the part is lifted off and trimmed with saws or routers.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination)**

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_r / \rho_r}$ | Fiber volume fraction from weight fractions | dimensionless | Convert mass-based to volume-based reinforcement content |
| 2 | $W_f = \dfrac{W_f}{W_f + W_r} \times 100$ | Weight % of fiber | % | Process control — verify fiber loading |
| 3 | $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_r$ | Void volume fraction | dimensionless | Quality control (target $V_v < 0.02$) |
| 4 | $E_c = E_m \, V_m + E_f \, V_f$ | Rule of mixtures for modulus (iso-strain) | GPa | Predict laminate stiffness parallel to fibers |
| 5 | $E_c = \dfrac{E_m \, E_f}{E_f V_m + E_m V_f}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures (iso-stress) | GPa | Predict stiffness transverse to fibers |
| 6 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures for tensile strength | MPa | Predict strength along fiber direction |
| 7 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Density of composite (rule of mixtures) | g/cm³ | Specific stiffness/strength calculations |
| 8 | $t_{\text{spray}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_f}{\rho_c \cdot A}$ | Instantaneous laminate thickness growth rate | mm/s | Process planning |
| 9 | $A_r = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Resin-to-Fiber mass throughput ratio | dimensionless | Gun calibration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall Avoidance:** When $V_f$ and $V_r$ are required, examiners test whether students distinguish **mass fraction ($W_i$)** from **volume fraction ($V_i$)**. Conversion requires the **densities** $\rho_f$ and $\rho_m$.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

| Industry | Typical Spray-up Part | Why Spray-up is Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Marine | Boat hulls, deck sections, small yachts | Large surface area, low tooling cost, acceptable finish |
| Building & Construction | Bath tubs, shower trays, swimming pool panels, roofing | Complex shapes, moderate volume |
| Transportation | Truck/van body panels, small fairings | Low cost, rapid prototyping |
| Chemical | Pipes, storage tanks (small), ducts | Corrosion resistance of GRP |
| Wind Energy | Small nacelle covers, hub shrouds | Prototype/short-run production |

### 2.4 Comparative Positioning (Syllabus Cross-Reference)

| Feature | Spray-up | Hand Lay-up | RTM (closed mold) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mold Type** | Open | Open | Closed |
| **Resin Pressure** | Low (~0.1 MPa atomizing) | None | High (0.1–0.7 MPa injection) |
| **Fiber Form** | Chopped (deposited) | Mat / fabric (laid) | Preform (placed in closed mold) |
| **Fiber Volume Fraction** | **0.10 – 0.25** | **0.20 – 0.35** | **0.30 – 0.55** |
| **Void Content** | High (3–8 %) | Medium (1–3 %) | Low (<1 %) |
| **Production Rate** | High | Low | Medium–High |
| **Surface Finish** | Gel-coat side good / reverse rough | Gel-coat side good | Both sides good |
| **Tooling Cost** | Lowest | Low | High (matched dies) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** The most common 7-mark question is *"Compare spray-up with hand lay-up and RTM"* — Use the table above as the structured answer backbone.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Converting Weight Fraction to Fiber Volume Fraction

This is the **highest-frequency numerical problem** in KTU spray-up questions.

**Given:**
- $W_f$ = weight fraction of fiber
- $W_r$ = weight fraction of resin (matrix)
- $\rho_f$ = density of glass fiber (typically **2.55–2.62 g/cm³**)
- $\rho_r$ = density of cured polyester (typically **1.20–1.30 g/cm³**)

**Objective:** Derive $V_f$ (fiber volume fraction) in terms of $W_f, \rho_f, \rho_r$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Weight Fraction**

$$
W_f = \frac{m_f}{m_f + m_r}, \quad W_r = \frac{m_r}{m_f + m_r} = 1 - W_f
$$

Here $m_f$ and $m_r$ are the masses of fiber and resin in a given laminate sample.

**Step 2 — Definition of Volume Fraction**

$$
V_f = \frac{v_f}{v_f + v_r}, \quad v_f = \frac{m_f}{\rho_f}, \quad v_r = \frac{m_r}{\rho_r}
$$

This step uses the **fundamental mass-density-volume relationship** $m = \rho \cdot v$, rearranged to $v = m/\rho$.

**Step 3 — Substitute Masses Using Weight Fractions**

Let the total mass $M = m_f + m_r$. Then $m_f = W_f \cdot M$ and $m_r = W_r \cdot M$.

$$
v_f = \frac{W_f \, M}{\rho_f}, \quad v_r = \frac{W_r \, M}{\rho_r}
$$

The factor $M$ cancels in the next step.

**Step 4 — Form the Volume Fraction Ratio**

$$
V_f = \frac{v_f}{v_f + v_r} = \frac{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f}}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_r}{\rho_r}}
$$

This is **Formula 1** of the cheat sheet — fully derived.

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

A spray-up laminate is produced with the following data:
- Glass fiber mass = **3.6 kg**
- Resin mass = **6.4 kg**
- $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $\rho_r = 1.25 \text{ g/cm}^3$

Compute $V_f$.

First, total mass $M = 3.6 + 6.4 = 10.0$ kg.

$$
W_f = \frac{3.6}{10.0} = 0.36, \quad W_r = 0.64
$$

Now substitute:

$$
V_f = \frac{\dfrac{0.36}{2.55}}{\dfrac{0.36}{2.55} + \dfrac{0.64}{1.25}}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
\frac{0.36}{2.55} = 0.14118
$$

Compute the second term in the denominator:

$$
\frac{0.64}{1.25} = 0.5120
$$

Sum the denominator:

$$
0.14118 + 0.5120 = 0.65318
$$

Final value:

$$
V_f = \frac{0.14118}{0.65318} \approx 0.2162 \approx 21.6\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The laminate has a **fiber volume fraction of ~21.6 %**, which is consistent with the typical spray-up range of **15–25 %** (lower than hand lay-up due to entrapped air voids).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Defects, Causes, Remedies in Spray-up

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often give a **practical troubleshooting problem** (e.g., *"Voids appear in the laminate — explain causes and remedies"*). The matrix below is the structured KTU answer.

| # | Defect | Root Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High void content** | Inadequate roll-out; resin too viscous; trapped air | Use lower-viscosity resin; vigorous roller consolidation; degas resin |
| 2 | **Dry spots (un-wetted fibers)** | Resin/fiber flow imbalance; clogged nozzle | Recalibrate $A_r$ ratio; clean gun; re-spray affected area while wet |
| 3 | **Fiber wash / orientation loss** | Excessive atomizing air pressure | Reduce air pressure to **0.2–0.3 MPa** |
| 4 | **Non-uniform thickness** | Uneven gun sweep speed; poor operator skill | Train operator; use mechanical gun guides |
| 5 | **Exothermic burn** | Too much resin or catalyst in one pass (thick laminate) | Build up in multiple thin passes; reduce catalyst loading |
| 6 | **Resin-rich / resin-starved zones** | $A_r$ ratio drift | Calibrate gun flow rates periodically |
| 7 | **Surface waviness (no gel coat)** | Insufficient gel-coat thickness or no gel coat | Apply **0.3–0.5 mm** gel coat and partial cure before spray-up |

### 3.3 Derivation: Laminate Thickness Growth Rate (Gun Productivity)

**Given:**
- Resin mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_r$ (kg/min)
- Fiber mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/min)
- Composite density: $\rho_c$ (kg/m³)
- Spray footprint area: $A$ (m²)

**Step 1 — Total Mass Deposited per Minute**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{total}} = \dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_f
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Volume per Minute Using Composite Density**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{total}}}{\rho_c} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{min}]
$$

**Step 3 — Volume per Unit Area Equals Thickness Growth**

$$
\frac{dt}{d\tau} = \frac{\dot{V}}{A} = \frac{\dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_f}{\rho_c \cdot A}
$$

This is **Formula 8** of the cheat sheet — derived from first principles using conservation of mass.

> [!TIP]
> **Worked example:** If $\dot{m}_r = 4$ kg/min, $\dot{m}_f = 2$ kg/min, $\rho_c = 1.55$ g/cm³ $= 1550$ kg/m³, $A = 2$ m², then $dt/d\tau = (4+2)/1550/2 = 1.935 \times 10^{-3}$ m/min $= 1.94$ mm/min.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Spray-up Manufacturing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Mold Preparation"] --> B["Step 2: Release Agent Application"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Gel Coat Spray"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Partial Gel Coat Cure"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Resin Stream Activation"]
    F["Step 6: Glass Roving Feed"] --> G["Step 7: Chopper Unit Cuts Rovings"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Chopped Fiber Stream"]
    E --> I["Step 9: Spray Gun Convergence Zone"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Step 10: Wet Layup on Mold Surface"]
    J --> K["Step 11: Manual Roller Consolidation"]
    K --> L["Step 12: Air Removal and Wet Out"]
    L --> M{"Target Thickness Reached?"}
    M -- No --> E
    M -- Yes --> N["Step 13: Ambient Curing"]
    N --> O["Step 14: Exothermic Peak Monitoring"]
    O --> P["Step 15: Barcol Hardness Check"]
    P --> Q["Step 16: Demolding"]
    Q --> R["Step 17: Trimming and Finishing"]
    R --> S["Step 18: Quality Inspection"]
    S --> T["Finished Composite Part"]
```

### 4.2 Equipment Architecture — Dual-Stream Spray Gun System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Resin Delivery Subsystem"]
        R1["Resin Drum with Unsaturated Polyester"] --> P1["Catalyst Metering Pump MEKP"]
        P1 --> N1["Resin Nozzle Atomizing Air Inlet"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Fiber Delivery Subsystem"]
        G1["Continuous Glass Roving Spool"] --> CH["Chopper Unit with Rotating Blades"]
        CH --> N2["Chopped Fiber Outlet Nozzle"]
    end
    subgraph S3["Mold and Consolidation Subsystem"]
        M1["Open Female Mold with Gel Coat"]
        R1_ROLLER["Aluminum or Rubber Consolidation Roller"]
        M1 --> R1_ROLLER
    end
    N1 --> M1
    N2 --> M1
    R1_ROLLER --> COMPACT["Compacted Wet Laminate"]
    COMPACT --> CURE["Ambient Cure Zone"]
    CURE --> DEMOLD["Demolded Composite Part"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Mass Flow and Feedback Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUTS["Inputs to Spray Gun"]
        I1["Resin Mass Flow mr_dot"]
        I2["Fiber Mass Flow mf_dot"]
        I3["Catalyst Loading Percent"]
        I4["Atomizing Air Pressure 0.2-0.4 MPa"]
    end
    INPUTS --> SPRAY["Spray-up Deposition Zone"]
    SPRAY --> WET["Wet Laminate on Mold"]
    WET --> ROLLER["Manual Roller Consolidation"]
    ROLLER --> Q1{"Void Content Check"}
    Q1 -- High Voids --> ROLLER
    Q1 -- Acceptable --> CURE["Curing Stage"]
    CURE --> Q2{"Barcol Hardness > 40"}
    Q2 -- No --> CURE
    Q2 -- Yes --> PART["Finished Part QC"]
    PART --> OUTPUTS["Specific Stiffness Specific Strength Data"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation for KTU:** The **convergence point of the resin nozzle and chopped fiber outlet** above the mold is the single most exam-relevant feature. Examiners often ask the student to *label the convergence zone* in a diagram and explain why fiber-resin mixing must occur at the **mold surface, not in the air stream** (the latter causes fiber blow-off and resin loss).

### 4.4 Defect Causal Chain (Fishbone-Style Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DEF["Defect: High Void Content Vv > 5 percent"] --> C1["Resin Viscosity Too High"]
    DEF --> C2["Insufficient Roller Pressure"]
    DEF --> C3["Excessive Atomizing Air"]
    DEF --> C4["Spray Distance Too Far"]
    C1 --> R1["Lower Resin Viscosity to 200-500 cP"]
    C2 --> R2["Increase Roller Passes"]
    C3 --> R3["Reduce Air Pressure to 0.2 MPa"]
    C4 --> R4["Hold Gun 30-50 cm From Mold"]
    R1 --> FIX["Remediated Low Void Laminate"]
    R2 --> FIX
    R3 --> FIX
    R4 --> FIX
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
> *Define the spray-up process for polymer composites. List any four distinguishing features compared to hand lay-up.*

**Model Answer (15 lines):**

> **Spray-up** is an **open-mold composite manufacturing process** in which **chopped glass fibers and liquid thermoset resin are simultaneously sprayed onto a mold surface** through a dual-nozzle spray gun, followed by **manual roller consolidation** and ambient curing.
>
> **Distinguishing features vs. hand lay-up:**
> 1. **Mechanized deposition** of both resin and fiber in a single pass (hand lay-up uses pre-formed mats).
> 2. **Higher production rate** but **lower achievable fiber volume fraction** (typically 15–25 % vs. 30–45 %).
> 3. **Chopped, randomly oriented fibers** of 6–50 mm length (hand lay-up uses continuous mats/woven rovings).
> 4. **Higher void content** (3–8 %) due to atomized air entrainment.
> 5. **Open mold, low pressure** — same as hand lay-up, but with mechanized resin + fiber delivery.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *What is the role of (a) the chopper unit, and (b) the consolidation roller in the spray-up process?*

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Chopper Unit:** Mounted on or near the spray gun, it cuts **continuous glass rovings into short chopped fibers** (6–50 mm) using a rotating cutting wheel. These chopped fibers are then propelled via an air stream into the spray plume, where they mix with the atomized resin just before deposition on the mold.
>
> **(b) Consolidation Roller:** A hand-held metal or rubber roller used immediately after spray deposition to:
> - **Remove entrapped air voids** (improving laminate density and strength),
> - **Compact the laminate** to design thickness,
> - **Wet-out the fibers** fully with resin (ensuring good fiber-matrix interface),
> - **Distribute fibers uniformly** to avoid resin-rich or resin-starved zones.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
>
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> *(a) [7 marks]* Describe the **spray-up process** in detail with reference to: mold preparation, gel-coat application, resin–fiber deposition, consolidation, curing, and demolding. List the **typical process parameters** and their values.
>
> *(b) [7 marks]* A spray-up laminate is produced with **3.6 kg of glass fiber and 6.4 kg of polyester resin**. Given $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_r = 1.25 \text{ g/cm}^3$, calculate the **fiber volume fraction ($V_f$)**, **matrix volume fraction ($V_r$)**, and the **composite density ($\rho_c$)** using the rule of mixtures. Comment on whether this $V_f$ is in the typical range for spray-up.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The complete process is described in Section 2.1 (six stages). Key marks distribution:
- Mold prep + gel coat: **2 marks**
- Resin/fiber deposition with gun operation: **2 marks**
- Roll-out consolidation + curing: **2 marks**
- Typical process parameters (with values): **1 mark**

**Process Parameters Table (to be drawn in answer):**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Resin viscosity at spray | 200–500 cP |
| Chopped fiber length | 6–50 mm |
| Fiber weight fraction | 25–40 % |
| Air pressure | 0.2–0.4 MPa |
| Gel-coat thickness | 0.3–0.5 mm |
| Cure temperature | Ambient (20–40 °C) |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Full Step-by-Step Solution)**

**Given:** $m_f = 3.6$ kg, $m_r = 6.4$ kg, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_r = 1.25$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Total mass and weight fractions**

$$
M = 3.6 + 6.4 = 10.0 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
W_f = \frac{3.6}{10.0} = 0.36, \quad W_r = 0.64
$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual volumes**

$$
v_f = \frac{m_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{3.6}{2.55} = 1.412 \text{ L (liters)}
$$

$$
v_r = \frac{m_r}{\rho_r} = \frac{6.4}{1.25} = 5.120 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Fiber Volume Fraction**

$$
V_f = \frac{v_f}{v_f + v_r} = \frac{1.412}{1.412 + 5.120} = \frac{1.412}{6.532} = 0.2162
$$

So $V_f \approx \mathbf{21.6 \%}$.

**Step 4 — Matrix Volume Fraction**

$$
V_r = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.2162 = 0.7838 \approx \mathbf{78.4 \%}
$$

**Step 5 — Composite Density (Rule of Mixtures)**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_r V_r
$$

$$
\rho_c = (2.55)(0.2162) + (1.25)(0.7838)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 0.5513 + 0.9798 = 1.531 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

So $\rho_c \approx \mathbf{1.53 \text{ g/cm}^3}$.

**Step 6 — Comment on Range**

$V_f = 21.6\%$ falls **within the typical spray-up range of 15–25 %**, which is lower than the 30–45 % achievable in hand lay-up. This is expected because spray-up has **higher void content and lower consolidation pressure**.

> **Distribution of marks for part (b):** Weight fraction = 1 m, $V_f$ = 2 m, $V_r$ = 1 m, $\rho_c$ = 2 m, range comment = 1 m.

---

> **Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice):**
> *(a) [7 marks]* Explain the **advantages and limitations of the spray-up process**. List **five typical applications**.
>
> *(b) [7 marks]* A spray gun is calibrated to deliver resin at $\dot{m}_r = 4.0$ kg/min and glass fiber at $\dot{m}_f = 2.0$ kg/min. The composite density is **1.55 g/cm³**. The spray footprint is **2.0 m²**. Calculate the **resin-to-fiber mass ratio ($A_r$)**, the **total mass flow rate**, and the **laminate thickness growth rate**. If the design laminate thickness is **4 mm**, how long will it take to build one layer?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Advantages, Limitations, Applications (7 marks)**

> **Advantages (write any 4):**
> 1. Low tooling cost — open single-sided mold.
> 2. High deposition rate for large parts (boat hulls, baths).
> 3. Complex shapes achievable (convex + concave surfaces).
> 4. Rapid prototyping and short production runs.
> 5. Low capital investment compared to closed-mold processes.

> **Limitations (write any 4):**
> 1. **Operator-dependent quality** (skill-intensive).
> 2. **High void content** (3–8 %) — lowers mechanical strength.
> 3. **Lower fiber volume fraction** than hand lay-up or RTM.
> 4. **Styrene emissions** (open-mold hazard — environmental regulation).
> 5. **Only one good (gel-coat) surface**; reverse surface is rough.
> 6. Resin waste from overspray.

> **Five Typical Applications:**
> 1. Boat hulls and marine decks
> 2. Bath tubs and shower trays
> 3. Swimming pool panels
> 4. Truck/van body panels
> 5. Chemical storage tanks and ducts
> 6. Small wind-turbine housings

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Resin-to-Fiber Mass Ratio $A_r$**

$$
A_r = \frac{\dot{m}_r}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{4.0}{2.0} = \mathbf{2.0}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Mass Flow Rate**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{total}} = \dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_f = 4.0 + 2.0 = \mathbf{6.0 \text{ kg/min}}
$$

**Step 3 — Laminate Thickness Growth Rate**

Convert $\rho_c$ to SI: $\rho_c = 1.55 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1550 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

$$
\frac{dt}{d\tau} = \frac{\dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_f}{\rho_c \cdot A} = \frac{6.0}{1550 \times 2.0}
$$

$$
\frac{dt}{d\tau} = \frac{6.0}{3100} = 1.935 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/min} = 1.935 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to Reach 4 mm**

$$
\tau = \frac{t}{dt/d\tau} = \frac{4 \text{ mm}}{1.935 \text{ mm/min}} = \mathbf{2.07 \text{ minutes}}
$$

> **Distribution of marks for part (b):** $A_r$ = 1 m, total mass flow = 1 m, thickness rate = 3 m, time = 2 m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Spray-up Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $W_f$ (weight fraction) with $V_f$ (volume fraction)** — these are NOT the same; conversion requires density.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — e.g., g/cm³ must become kg/m³ when $\dot{m}$ is in kg/min and $A$ is in m².
> 3. **Listing hand lay-up steps instead of spray-up steps** — the *chopper unit* and *dual-stream spray gun* are unique to spray-up; examiners look for these keywords.
> 4. **Omitting the gel-coat stage** — always mention it as a 6th process step for full marks.
> 5. **Writing $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ for the *transverse* direction** — this is for longitudinal (iso-strain) only. Transverse uses the inverse rule.
> 6. **Stating "spray-up has high fiber content"** — wrong; it has *lower* $V_f$ than hand lay-up and RTM.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- ☐ **Spray-up = open mold + low pressure + chopped fiber + dual-stream gun + manual roll-out + ambient cure.**
- ☐ It is a **variant of contact molding** — do not confuse with closed-mold RTM.
- ☐ **Six sequential stages:** Mold prep → Gel coat → Resin + fiber spray → Roller consolidation → Curing → Demold.
- ☐ **Chopper unit** cuts continuous rovings into 6–50 mm lengths at the spray gun.
- ☐ **Roller consolidation** is the most quality-critical step (controls void content).
