- ☐ **Typical parameters to memorize:** Viscosity 200–500 cP, fiber length 12–25 mm, $V_f$ 15–25 %, air pressure 0.2–0.4 MPa, gel coat 0.3–0.5 mm.
- ☐ **Volume fraction conversion formula** (master this): $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_r/\rho_r}$.
- ☐ **Rule of mixtures for density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ (iso-strain case).
- ☐ **Rule of mixtures for modulus (longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
- ☐ **Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse):** $E_c = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_f V_m + E_m V_f}$.
- ☐ **Thickness growth rate formula:** $dt/d\tau = (\dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_f)/(\rho_c \cdot A)$.
- ☐ **Advantages:** Low cost, complex shapes, high production rate.
- ☐ **Limitations:** High voids (3–8 %), operator-dependent, styrene emissions, low $V_f$, one good surface.
- ☐ **Applications:** Boats, baths, pools, truck panels, tanks.
- ☐ **Comparison hooks:** Spray-up = open mold; RTM = closed mold. Spray-up < Hand lay-up < RTM in $V_f$. Hand lay-up < Spray-up in production rate.
- ☐ **Styrene emission regulation** is a common KTU question on environmental compliance.
- ☐ Always mention **MEKP (Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide)** as the typical room-temperature catalyst for polyester spray-up.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compression Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compression Moulding** is a closed-mould, high-pressure thermoset composite manufacturing process in which a pre-measured charge of polymer matrix (typically a thermosetting resin such as unsaturated polyester, epoxy, phenolic, or vinyl ester) — either neat, pre-impregnated with reinforcement (SMC), or in bulk dough form (BMC/DMC) — is placed directly into the open cavity of a heated matched-metal mould. The mould halves are then closed under substantial hydraulic pressure (typically **5–25 MPa**), forcing the material to flow, fill the cavity, and cure in situ at elevated temperatures (**120–180 °C**) into a consolidated, net-shape composite part.

In the specific context of **PBPOT604 (Polymer Blends & Composites)**, compression moulding is highlighted as the *primary industrial route* for processing fibre-reinforced thermoset composites — including **Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC)**, **Bulk Moulding Compound (BMC)**, **Glass Mat Thermoplastic (GMT)**, and pre-preg lay-ups — because it offers excellent control over fibre orientation, low void content, and the ability to mould large, complex structural parts in a single operation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Waffle Iron / Sandwich Press:**
> Think of compression moulding as the industrial cousin of a kitchen waffle iron or a sandwich press. You place a measured dollop of batter (the polymer charge) onto the bottom hot plate, close the lid, and the heat cooks the batter while the pressure squeezes it outward to fill the patterned cavity. Just as the batter cannot escape sideways (the mould is closed), it is forced to take the exact shape of the iron. The difference is that the "batter" here is a reactive polymer/glass-fibre dough that chemically cross-links (cures) under the heat and pressure to become a rigid, load-bearing composite — say, the bonnet of a car or an electrical switchgear housing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Highlighted:**
> - **Clamping Force:** 500 – 8,000 tonnes (depending on part size)
> - **Moulding Pressure:** 5 – 25 MPa
> - **Mould Temperature:** 120 – 180 °C (thermoset cure window)
> - **Cycle Time:** 1 – 10 minutes
> - **Part Size Capability:** Up to ~ 6 m² in automotive class-A panels

---

## 1.3 Classification within Polymer Processing

Compression moulding sits within the broader family of **closed-mould processes** for composites, and is *not* to be confused with the related (but distinct) processes below:

| Process | Reinforcement Form | Resin State | Pressure Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compression Moulding (CM)** | SMC sheets / BMC dough / GMT | Partially cured (B-stage) | Press platens |
| Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) | Dry fibre preform | Low-viscosity liquid injection | Pump + clamp |
| Injection Moulding (IM) | Short fibres compounded into pellets | Molten thermoplastic | Screw injection |
| Hand Lay-up | Woven mat, manually placed | Liquid resin brushed on | None (or vacuum bag) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this distinction matters for KTU:**
> In a 14-mark exam question, you are often asked to *compare* compression moulding with RTM or injection moulding. The crucial difference is the **resin state at the moment of mould closure** — in CM the charge is already a viscous, fibre-laden putty/sheet; in RTM it is a low-viscosity liquid injected *after* mould closure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Stages of a Compression Moulding Cycle

The process unfolds in a strictly sequential, time-resolved manner. Each stage governs a specific physical/chemical phenomenon, and the *quality* of the final part is determined by how cleanly these stages are separated (or, in some advanced variants, overlapped).

### Stage 1 — **Charge Preparation & Loading**
- A *pre-weighed* charge (the "preform") is placed on the lower, open mould half.
- The charge's **mass and footprint area** are critical: too small → incomplete fill (short-shot); too large → excessive flash, fibre orientation disruption, and press overload.
- For SMC, the charge is typically a stack of 2–6 plies cut to ~ 40–80 % of the projected mould area; for BMC, it is an extruded or ladled "log" of dough.

### Stage 2 — **Mould Closure & Flow (Plastic Flow Stage)**
- The upper platen descends at *high velocity* (closing speed ≈ 100 – 500 mm/s) until the charge contacts the mould.
- Then it switches to a *low, controlled velocity* (1 – 25 mm/s) — this is the **flow phase**, during which the resin–fibre matrix squeezes radially outward.
- The flowing material must: (a) reach all cavity extremities before gelation, (b) wet out the fibres, (c) entrap minimal air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Squeeze Flow Theory:** The flow during compression moulding is *not* a simple shear or Poiseuille flow. It is dominated by **squeeze flow** between two approaching parallel plates — a slow, extensional, lubrication-theory-governed motion where the resin acts as a lubricant for the fibres. This is why viscosity is the single most important resin property for processability.

### Stage 3 — **Breathing / Degassing (Optional but Common)**
- The press is momentarily paused or the mould is "breathed" (opened 1–3 mm) to release trapped air and volatiles.
- Without breathing, void content can exceed 5 %, severely degrading mechanical properties and surface finish.

### Stage 4 — **Cure under Pressure (Hold Stage)**
- The press holds the mould closed at the *clamping tonnage* while the resin cross-links.
- Heat transfers from the hot steel mould (≈ **150 °C**) into the charge by conduction; exothermic cure raises the core temperature, sometimes to **> 200 °C**.
- Hold time is governed by the **thickest section's cure kinetics** — typically 30 s/mm of wall thickness.

### Stage 5 — **Demoulding & Trimming**
- The press opens; the part is ejected (often with rubber ejector pins or air poppets).
- Flash (excess material squeezed into the land area) is trimmed by a secondary operation — die cutting, routing, or sanding.

---

## 2.2 Key Process Parameters — The "Process Triangle"

A handy mental model for any compression moulding problem in the KTU exam is the **Process Triangle**: every part quality metric is governed by exactly three knobs, and you must always be able to identify *which knob* controls *which quality*.

```
            TEMPERATURE
               /\
              /  \
             /    \
            /      \
   PRESSURE /________\  TIME
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in the Real World:**
> In an automotive SMC floor pan line producing ~ 100 000 parts/year, an incorrectly set temperature triangle can either (a) under-cure the part (lower crosslink density → poor solvent resistance, low HDT), or (b) over-cure/exotherm (cracking, sink marks, fibre print-through). Mastering this triangle is a **6–8 LPA** skill in the Indian composites industry (Tata AutoComp, Mahindra Composites, SABIC Innovative Plastics).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains the high-yield equations you should memorise for any CM numerical or short-answer question. *Note:* the table uses `\vert` and `\mid` in place of `|` to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Equation | Meaning / Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{ch} = \rho_{c} \cdot A_{p} \cdot t$ | Pre-charge volume = charge density × projected area × thickness | Charge weight calculation |
| 2 | $F_{clamp} = P_{mould} \cdot A_{p}$ | Required clamp tonnage (kN) | Press selection |
| 3 | $t_{cure} = k \cdot s$ | Cure time (s) = 30–60 s/mm × wall thickness | Holding time selection |
| 4 | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} \approx \dfrac{F \cdot h^{3}}{2 \pi R^{5}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\dot{h}}$ | Apparent viscosity in squeeze flow (Stefan equation) | Resin flow simulation |
| 5 | $\dot{h} = \dfrac{2 \pi F \cdot h^{3}}{3 \eta \cdot R^{4}}$ (inverted from 4) | Platen closing velocity under force F | Press speed profile design |
| 6 | $\eta_{0} = A \cdot e^{\dfrac{E_{a}}{RT}}$ | Arrhenius viscosity–temperature relation | Cure profile design |
| 7 | $\% V_{f} = \dfrac{\rho_{m} \cdot w_{f}}{\rho_{f} \cdot w_{m} + \rho_{m} \cdot w_{f}}$ | Theoretical fibre volume fraction | Composite property prediction |
| 8 | $t_{fill} \le t_{gel} - 5 \, s$ | Fill-time gelation safety margin | Process window setting |
| 9 | $P_{specific} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{p}}$ | Specific moulding pressure (MPa) | Standardisation between presses |
| 10 | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{x}$ | Fourier heat conduction across mould wall | Cure uniformity analysis |

> Where: $\rho_{c}$ = charge bulk density, $A_{p}$ = projected part area, $t$ = part thickness, $P_{mould}$ = moulding pressure, $F$ = applied force, $h$ = instantaneous gap, $R$ = radial spread, $\dot{h}$ = closing velocity, $E_{a}$ = activation energy, $w_{f}, w_{m}$ = weight fractions of fibre/matrix, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $x$ = mould wall thickness.

---

## 2.4 Material Forms Used in CM

| Form | Acronym | Composition | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sheet Moulding Compound | **SMC** | Chopped glass (25–30 mm) + unsaturated polyester + CaCO₃ + thickener, in sheet form | Car body panels, bath tubs |
| Bulk Moulding Compound | **BMC** | Shorter fibres (3–12 mm) + dough-like resin | Electrical housings, headlamp reflectors |
| Thick Moulding Compound | **TMC** | Longer fibres (~ 50 mm) than SMC | Structural auto parts |
| Glass Mat Thermoplastic | **GMT** | Random glass mat + PP / PA | Bumpers, front-end carriers |
| Pre-preg (B-stage) | — | Unidirectional/woven fibres pre-impregnated with partially cured epoxy | Aerospace primary structure |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Squeeze-Flow (Stefan) Equation for Compression Moulding

This is the most frequently examined derivation in Module 4 because it directly links **resin viscosity, closing velocity, and part size** — the three quantities an engineer must balance.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions
1. The resin behaves as a **Newtonian, incompressible** fluid during flow.
2. The fibre bed is treated as a **permeable porous medium**, and the resin acts as the lubricant.
3. The flow is **axisymmetric**, with the charge spreading radially from radius $R_{0}$ to $R(t)$.
4. **Lubrication theory** applies: $h \ll R$ (the cavity gap is much smaller than the lateral extent), so inertia and edge effects are negligible.
5. **No-slip** at both mould surfaces; **no through-thickness pressure gradient** (the resin flows radially only).

### 3.1.2 Governing Equations

The continuity equation for an incompressible fluid in cylindrical coordinates, with only radial velocity $v_{r}(r, z)$:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_{r})}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_{z}}{\partial z} = 0
$$

The z-component of the Navier–Stokes equation, with the lubrication assumption (neglecting inertia and gravity):

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = 0
$$

The r-component of the Navier–Stokes equation:

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \eta \frac{\partial^{2} v_{r}}{\partial z^{2}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Integrate the z-momentum equation.**

From the z-momentum, we conclude that pressure does not vary across the gap:

$$
p = p(r, t) \quad \text{(function of } r \text{ and } t \text{ only)}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate the r-momentum twice with boundary conditions.**

Boundary conditions (no-slip at both plates, symmetry about the midplane if needed):

$$
v_{r}(z = 0) = 0 \qquad v_{r}(z = h) = 0
$$

Integrating twice with respect to $z$:

$$
v_{r}(r, z) = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}(z^{2} - h\,z)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the kinematic condition at the upper plate.**

The upper plate descends at velocity $\dot{h} = dh/dt$. The vertical velocity at the surface must equal $\dot{h}$:

$$
v_{z}(z = h) = \dot{h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_{0}^{h} \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_{r})}{\partial r} dz = -\dot{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v_{r}$ and integrate in $z$.**

$$
\int_{0}^{h} \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left[ r \cdot \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}(z^{2} - h z) \right] dz = -\dot{h}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{dp}{dr} \right) \int_{0}^{h}(z^{2} - h z)\,dz = -\dot{h}
$$

The integral evaluates to:

$$
\int_{0}^{h}(z^{2} - h z)\,dz = \left[\frac{z^{3}}{3} - \frac{h z^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{h} = -\frac{h^{3}}{6}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
-\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\cdot \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{dp}{dr} \right) = -\dot{h}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{dp}{dr} \right) = \frac{12 \eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate twice in $r$ with boundary conditions.**

Integrate once:

$$
r \frac{dp}{dr} = \frac{6\eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}} r^{2} + C_{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dp}{dr} = \frac{6\eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}} r + \frac{C_{1}}{r}
$$

For finiteness at $r = 0$, we must have $C_{1} = 0$. Then:

$$
\frac{dp}{dr} = \frac{6\eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}} r
$$

Integrate again:

$$
p(r) = \frac{3\eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}} r^{2} + C_{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the boundary condition at the free edge $r = R(t)$.**

At the free surface, the pressure equals atmospheric $p_{atm}$, so gauge pressure vanishes there:

$$
p(R) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{2} = -\frac{3\eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}} R^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;p(r) = \frac{3\eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)\;}
$$

This is the **Stefan pressure distribution** — a parabolic pressure field, maximum at the centre, zero at the periphery.

**Step 7 — Compute the total squeezing force $F$.**

$$
F = \int_{0}^{R} p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{3\eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
F = \frac{6\pi \eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}} \int_{0}^{R}\left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr = \frac{6\pi \eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}}\left[\frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R}
$$

$$
F = \frac{6\pi \eta \dot{h}}{h^{3}} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{3\pi \eta \dot{h} R^{4}}{2 h^{3}}
$$

**Step 8 — Invert to obtain the closing-velocity relationship (Formula #5 in the cheat sheet).**

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{h} = \frac{2 F h^{3}}{3\pi \eta R^{4}}\;}
$$

### 3.1.4 Engineering Interpretation

| Observation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\dot{h} \propto h^{3}$ | As the gap shrinks, closing velocity collapses rapidly → press slows itself down naturally |
| $\dot{h} \propto F$ | Higher clamp tonnage = faster fill (but risk of fibre wash) |
| $\dot{h} \propto R^{-4}$ | Larger parts are *much* harder to fill — 4th-power dependence! |
| $\dot{h} \propto 1/\eta$ | Lower resin viscosity = easier fill; this is why SMC thickener chemistry is so critical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common 7-mark derivation question asks you to *start* from the Navier–Stokes equations and arrive at $\dot{h} = \dfrac{2Fh^{3}}{3\pi\eta R^{4}}$. Practice writing out the **boundary conditions** explicitly — many students lose 2 marks by simply omitting the no-slip condition and the pressure-equals-atmospheric free-edge condition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Required Clamp Tonnage from Projected Area

Starting from Formula #2 in the cheat sheet:

$$
F_{clamp} = P_{mould} \cdot A_{p}
$$

Where $A_{p}$ is the projected (shadow) area of the part on the parting plane. If the part is rectangular of dimensions $L \times W$ and the moulding pressure required is $P_{mould}$:

$$
F_{clamp} = P_{mould} \cdot L \cdot W
$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical Example

A Class-A automotive SMC door panel has dimensions **1.2 m × 0.9 m** and the recommended moulding pressure is **7 MPa**. Compute the clamp tonnage.

**Step 1 — Compute projected area.**

$$
A_{p} = 1.2 \, \text{m} \times 0.9 \, \text{m} = 1.08 \, \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by moulding pressure.**

$$
F_{clamp} = 7 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} \times 1.08 \, \text{m}^{2} = 7.56 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to tonnes-force (1 tonne-force ≈ 9.81 kN).**

$$
F_{clamp} = \frac{7.56 \times 10^{6}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \approx 770.6 \, \text{tonnes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A real automotive press for such a part would be selected with a **safety factor of 1.2–1.5**, so a **1000-tonne press** would be specified. This is exactly the kind of number you will encounter in KTU 14-mark design problems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parametric Effects on Part Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **high-yield** for a 14-mark question. Memorise the qualitative direction of each arrow — the "why" is more important than memorising the numbers.

| Process Parameter ↑ | Tensile Strength | Surface Finish | Cycle Time | Void Content | Fibre Orientation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** ↑ | ↓ (over-cure, degradation) | ↑ (better flow, fewer knit-lines) | ↓ (faster cure) | ↓ (lower viscosity, easier bleed) | Slightly ↓ (less fibre wash) |
| **Pressure** ↑ | ↑ (fewer voids, better wet-out) | ↑ (full cavity fill) | ↑ (slower fill possible) | ↓ (voids squeezed out) | ↓ (fibre wash, re-orientation) |
| **Hold Time** ↑ | ↑ then plateau | Neutral | ↑ (cycle ↑) | ↓ (more time for volatiles to escape) | Neutral |
| **Charge Size** ↑ | Neutral to slight ↑ | ↓ (flash, fibre print-through) | Neutral | ↓ (more material to purge air) | ↓ (excess flow disrupts orientation) |
| **Closing Speed** ↑ | ↓ (fibre wash, knit-lines) | ↓ (trapped air) | ↓ (shorter fill) | ↑ (air entrapment) | ↓ severely (fibre re-orientation along flow) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fibre Wash:** The most-cited defect in compression moulding. When the closing speed is too high or the resin viscosity is too low, the reinforcement fibres are *washed* from their original SMC orientation into a flow-aligned orientation, causing anisotropic shrinkage, warpage, and loss of stiffness in the original fibre direction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Compression Moulding Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture** mapping the energy, material, and information flows during a single compression moulding cycle. It adheres to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords as node names, and no markdown formatting inside labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Charge Prep] --> B[Stage 2: Mould Loading]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Platen Descent - Fast]
    C --> D{Stage 4: Charge Contact Detected}
    D -- Yes --> E[Stage 5: Squeeze Flow Phase]
    D -- No --> C
    E --> F[Stage 6: Breathing or Pause]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Full Clamp Tonnage]
    G --> H[Stage 8: Heat Transfer into Charge]
    H --> I[Stage 9: Exothermic Cure Reaction]
    I --> J[Stage 10: Hold Timer Complete]
    J --> K[Stage 11: Platen Retraction]
    K --> L[Stage 12: Ejection]
    L --> M[Stage 13: Flash Trimming and Inspection]
    M --> N{Stage 14: Part Within Spec}
    N -- Yes --> O[Stage 15: Approved Part]
    N -- No --> P[Stage 16: Reject or Rework]

    subgraph Inputs
        Q1[Thermoset Charge - SMC or BMC or GMT]
        Q2[Heat Energy from Mould Platens]
        Q3[Hydraulic Clamp Force]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        R1[Consolidated Composite Part]
        R2[Flash Material - Recyclable]
        R3[Volatiles and Trapped Air - Vented]
    end

    Q1 --> B
    Q2 --> H
    Q3 --> G
    L --> R1
    M --> R2
    F --> R3
```

## 4.2 Functional Sub-Architecture — The Mould Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Upper Platen] --> A2[Force Distribution Plate]
    A2 --> A3[Upper Mould Half - Cavity]
    A3 --> A4[Shear Edge - Land]
    A4 --> A5[Lower Mould Half - Core]
    A5 --> A6[Bolster Plate]
    A6 --> A7[Lower Platen]

    A1 --> A8[Heating Channels - Oil or Electric]
    A5 --> A9[Ejector Pins]
    A5 --> A10[Parting Line Sensor]
    A3 --> A11[Vacuum Vent - For Breathing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The force path travels top-down from the hydraulic ram through the upper platen, force distribution plate, upper mould, then compresses the charge against the lower mould. Heat flows *from* both platens *into* the charge — that is why moulds are heated on both halves. Ejector pins push the cured part out of the lower mould after the press opens.

## 4.3 Sequence-of-Operations (Timing Diagram)

| Time (s) | Event | Platen Position | Pressure (MPa) | Charge State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Mould open, charge loaded | 100 % open | 0 | B-stage solid |
| 1 – 3 | High-speed close | 80 % → 10 % open | 0 → 0.5 | Solid, no flow |
| 3 – 4 | Slow close (flow) | 10 % → 2 % open | 0.5 → 5 | Squeeze flow begins |
| 4 – 5 | Breathing pause | 2 % open | 0.5 | Venting |
| 5 – 6 | Full clamp | 0 % (closed) | 7 → 15 | Cavity filled |
| 6 – 60 | Cure hold | 0 % | 15 | Crosslinking |
| 60 – 62 | Open | 0 → 100 % | 15 → 0 | Cured part |
| 62 – 65 | Eject | — | 0 | Part removed |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between SMC, BMC, and GMT as compression moulding charge materials. Mention one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> - **SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound):** Chopped glass fibres (25–30 mm length) pre-impregnated with unsaturated polyester resin, MgO thickener, CaCO₃ filler, and release agent, formed into handleable sheets of B-stage viscosity. *Application:* Car body panels (bonnet, roof, door outer).
> - **BMC (Bulk Moulding Compound):** Shorter fibres (3–12 mm) compounded with the same resin system into a dough-like putty. *Application:* Electrical switchgear housings, headlamp reflectors.
> - **GMT (Glass Mat Thermoplastic):** Random continuous glass mat pre-impregnated with a thermoplastic (PP, PA). *Application:* Bumper beams, front-end carriers, battery trays in EVs.

> [!NOTE]
> The key differentiator in a 3-mark answer is *length* and *orientation* of the fibre, and whether the matrix is **thermoset** (SMC, BMC) or **thermoplastic** (GMT).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is "fibre wash" in compression moulding? State two process parameters that influence it.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fibre wash** is the unwanted re-orientation of reinforcement fibres away from their as-placed (e.g., random-in-plane SMC) orientation into the direction of resin flow, caused when the resin velocity gradient exceeds the fibre-fibre friction threshold. It produces anisotropic shrinkage, warpage, and reduced in-plane stiffness.
>
> The two dominant process parameters are:
> 1. **Platen closing speed** — higher speed = more fibre wash.
> 2. **Resin viscosity** — lower viscosity = more fibre wash.
>
> A third valid answer: **charge placement location** (off-centre charges induce asymmetric flow).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE format: *Answer any ONE full question from this module.* Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — *Theory + Process Window Design*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the compression moulding process for Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC). List the sequence of operations involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1200 mm × 900 mm × 3 mm SMC car body panel is to be moulded at 7 MPa pressure. Compute the **clamp tonnage** and the **weight of SMC charge required** (assume SMC bulk density 1.85 g/cm³, fibre volume fraction 0.30, fibre density 2.55 g/cm³, matrix density 1.20 g/cm³). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Definition (2 marks):** Compression moulding of SMC is a closed-mould process where a pre-weighed stack of B-stage SMC sheets is placed in a heated matched-metal mould, the mould is closed under hydraulic pressure, and the resin cures to yield a finished composite part.

**Sequence of Operations (3 marks):**
1. Charge preparation: SMC sheets cut to ~ 40–80 % of projected area.
2. Loading onto lower mould.
3. High-speed mould closure until charge contact.
4. Slow, controlled closure → squeeze flow fills cavity.
5. Breathing (optional) to vent air.
6. Hold under full clamp tonnage at 140–160 °C for cure (1–3 min).
7. Mould opening and part ejection.
8. Flash trimming and inspection.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Use the upper-mould / lower-mould / charge / hydraulic press block diagram (as in Section 4.2).

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the projected area.**

$$
A_{p} = 1.2 \, \text{m} \times 0.9 \, \text{m} = 1.08 \, \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the part volume and SMC charge weight.**

$$
V_{part} = A_{p} \times t = 1.08 \times 0.003 = 3.24 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{3} = 3240 \, \text{cm}^{3}
$$

$$
m_{SMC} = \rho_{c} \times V_{part} = 1.85 \, \text{g/cm}^{3} \times 3240 \, \text{cm}^{3} = 5994 \, \text{g} \approx 6.0 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the clamp tonnage.**

$$
F_{clamp} = P_{mould} \times A_{p} = 7 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} \times 1.08 \, \text{m}^{2} = 7.56 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N}
$$

$$
F_{clamp} = \frac{7.56 \times 10^{6}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \approx 770.6 \, \text{tonnes}
$$

> Apply a **1.25× safety factor** → **964 tonnes** → round up to a **1000-tonne press**.

**Step 4 (optional) — Verify fibre volume fraction is consistent.**

$$
\% V_{f} = \frac{\rho_{m} w_{f}}{\rho_{f} w_{m} + \rho_{m} w_{f}}
$$

If $w_{f} = 0.45$ (typical for SMC): $\% V_{f} \approx 0.30$ ✓ (matches the problem statement)

**Final Answer (boxed):** Clamp tonnage ≈ **1000 t** (with safety factor); SMC charge ≈ **6.0 kg**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common 2-mark Loss:**
> Students often *forget the safety factor* on clamp tonnage. In industry, an undersized press is a **catastrophic** safety hazard (mould explosion at high tonnage). Examiners expect to see at least a sentence like *"with a 1.25× safety factor, a 1000-tonne press is selected."* Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — *Comparative + Numerical*

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare compression moulding with resin transfer moulding (RTM) for thermoset composites under the following heads: (i) resin state at mould closure, (ii) reinforcement form, (iii) cycle time, (iv) capital cost, (v) typical fibre volume fraction achievable, (vi) void content. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular SMC disc of final diameter 400 mm and thickness 2.5 mm is being compression moulded from a charge of initial diameter 120 mm. The resin viscosity at process temperature is 800 Pa·s. The press is delivering a constant squeeze force of 80 kN. Estimate the **instantaneous closing velocity** when the cavity gap is 5 mm. Use the Stefan squeeze-flow equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

| # | Head | Compression Moulding (SMC) | Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Resin state at closure | B-stage viscous putty/sheet (already contains resin) | Dry fibre preform; low-viscosity resin injected *after* closure |
| (ii) | Reinforcement form | Pre-impregnated chopped glass sheet (SMC) | Dry mat / woven fabric / stitched preform placed manually |
| (iii) | Cycle time | 1 – 5 min | 10 – 60 min (longer injection + cure) |
| (iv) | Capital cost | Moderate (hydraulic press + heated mould) | High (press + injection equipment + resin pot + vacuum) |
| (v) | Achievable $V_{f}$ | 20 – 30 % | 40 – 55 % (higher due to dry preform compaction) |
| (vi) | Void content | 1 – 3 % (with breathing) | < 1 % (vacuum-assisted) |

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** CM is faster and cheaper but lower in $V_{f}$; RTM yields higher performance and lower voids but is capital-intensive. CM dominates automotive, RTM dominates wind-turbine blades and aerospace.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Given:**
- $F = 80 \times 10^{3} \, \text{N}$
- $\eta = 800 \, \text{Pa \cdot s}$
- $R = 0.200 \, \text{m}$ (final radius — assume steady-state at the moment of measurement; for a disc of final diameter 400 mm, $R = 200 \, \text{mm} = 0.2 \, \text{m}$)
- $h = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 0.005 \, \text{m}$

**Stefan Squeeze-Flow Equation:**

$$
\dot{h} = \frac{2 F h^{3}}{3\pi \eta R^{4}}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $h^{3}$.**

$$
h^{3} = (0.005)^{3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R^{4}$.**

$$
R^{4} = (0.2)^{4} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
\dot{h} = \frac{2 \times 80 \times 10^{3} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7}}{3 \pi \times 800 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Numerator:
$$
2 \times 80{,}000 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7} = 0.02
$$

Denominator:
$$
3 \pi \times 800 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} = 12.06
$$

$$
\dot{h} = \frac{0.02}{12.06} = 1.66 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s} = 1.66 \, \text{mm/s}
$$

> **Physical interpretation (1 mark):** At a 5 mm gap with 80 kN, the press closes at ~ 1.7 mm/s — comfortably within the typical 1–25 mm/s flow-phase window. As the gap shrinks further, $\dot{h} \propto h^{3}$ → closing speed falls off naturally, preventing fibre wash at the final stage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Pitfall #1:**
> A very common 1-mark loss is forgetting to **convert mm to m** consistently. If you use $h = 5$ and $R = 200$ (in mm) in the formula, your answer will be off by a factor of $(1000)^{7}$ — and you will not get any credit. Always write the substitution with units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — Compression Moulding (Module 4, PBPOT604):**

- **Process identity:** Closed-mould, high-pressure, *thermoset-curing* composite manufacturing.
- **Charge forms (memorise acronym → composition):** SMC, BMC, TMC, GMT, pre-preg.
- **Typical operating window:** Pressure 5–25 MPa · Temperature 120–180 °C · Cure time 30–60 s/mm · Clamp tonnage 500–8000 t.
- **Five-stage cycle:** Charge prep → Mould closure & flow → Breathing → Cure under pressure → Demould & trim.
- **Stefan squeeze-flow equation (derivable, 7-mark):** $\dot{h} = \dfrac{2Fh^{3}}{3\pi \eta R^{4}}$ and its inverted force form.
- **Clamp tonnage formula (numerical, 7-mark):** $F_{clamp} = P_{mould} \cdot A_{p}$, with **1.2–1.5× safety factor**.
- **Fibre volume fraction check:** $V_{f} = \dfrac{\rho_{m} w_{f}}{\rho_{f} w_{m} + \rho_{m} w_{f}}$.
- **Process triangle:** Temperature ↔ Time ↔ Pressure — every quality attribute is a function of these three.
- **Fibre wash** is the most-cited CM defect; controlled by closing speed and resin viscosity.
- **Comparison vs RTM:** CM has the resin *in* the charge; RTM has the resin *injected* after closure.
- **Comparison vs IM:** CM is for thermoset / long-fibre composites; IM is for thermoplastics / short-fibre pellets.
- **Industrial applications:** Class-A car panels (SMC), electrical housings (BMC), bumper beams (GMT).
- **Common valuation traps:** forgetting safety factors on clamp tonnage, mixing mm/m units in the Stefan equation, and confusing CM with RTM in comparison questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vacuum Bag Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Vacuum Bag Moulding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM)** is a closed-mould composite manufacturing process in which a fibre-reinforced polymeric lay-up is consolidated against a single-sided rigid mould by evacuating the air trapped between a flexible polymeric bag and the laminate. The resulting **transverse consolidation pressure equals atmospheric pressure (~101.325 kPa or 14.7 psi)**, which compacts the laminate, expels trapped air voids, and squeezes out excess resin through bleeder plies before and during cure. The process is formally classified under **Low-Pressure Liquid Composite Moulding (LPCM)** family of processes and is considered an enhancement of the **Hand Lay-Up (HLU)** technique. It is governed by Darcy's Law for resin flow through porous fibre beds and by the compaction mechanics of fibre tows. **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Vacuum Bag Moulding is examined as a primary manufacturing route for thermosetting polymer composites (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester matrices) reinforced with glass, carbon, or aramid fibres, particularly in aerospace secondary structures, marine hulls, and wind turbine blades. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Suction-Seal Sandwich"** > Imagine placing a slice of bread, tomato, and cheese on a plate, then covering the stack with a transparent plastic wrap and sucking the air out through a straw. The atmospheric pressure (the weight of the air column above us, ~10 tonnes per square metre) pushes the wrap firmly down, compressing the sandwich into a tight, uniform stack, expelling excess moisture/air gaps. > > **The composite laminate behaves identically:** > - **Plate** = Rigid mould tool > - **Bread/Tomato/Cheese** = Fibre plies + resin > - **Plastic wrap** = Vacuum bag film > - **Straw** = Vacuum port connected to pump > - **Suction force** = Atmospheric pressure consolidation This simple intuition immediately clarifies why VBM produces laminates with **higher fibre volume fraction (Vf ≈ 50–60 %)** and **lower void content (≤ 2 %)** compared to HLU (Vf ≈ 30–45 %, voids ≈ 3–5 %). ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm) | **101.325** | kPa | | Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm) | **14.696** | psi | | Typical Cure Temperature (Epoxy) | **RT to 80** | °C | | Typical Cure Temperature (Polyester) | **RT to 60** | °C | | Vacuum Level Achievable | **≥ 95 %** of atm | – | | Max Consolidation Pressure | **~ 0.101** | MPa | | Achievable Fibre Volume Fraction | **50 – 60** | % | | Achievable Void Content | **1 – 3** | % | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The Six Governing Principles ### Principle 1: **Atmospheric Pressure Consolidation** When a vacuum pump evacuates the air beneath the bag to an absolute pressure $P_{vac}$, the *gauge pressure differential* across the bag drives compaction: $$\Delta P_{compaction} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$ **Why it matters:** $\Delta P$ is the *only* source of consolidation in VBM (no external press). At a typical industrial vacuum of **95 % of atm**, $\Delta P \approx 96.26$ kPa — equivalent to placing **a 9.8-tonne weight on every square metre** of laminate. ### Principle 2: **Fibre Bed Compressibility** The fibre stack behaves as a nonlinear spring. As pressure increases, fibre volume fraction rises logarithmically: $$V_{f} = V_{f0} + m \cdot \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_{0}}\right)$$ where $m$ is the compressibility index (depends on fibre architecture: woven roving ≈ 0.08, unidirectional ≈ 0.12). ### Principle 3: **Resin Bleed Kinetics (Darcy's Law)** Resin flows from high-pressure (laminate interior) to low-pressure (bleeder ply) regions through the porous fibre bed: $$Q = -\frac{K \cdot A}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$ where $K$ is the preform permeability, $\mu$ is resin viscosity, and $\frac{dP}{dx}$ is the pressure gradient. ### Principle 4: **Void Removal Mechanism** Three mechanisms operate simultaneously: 1. **Mechanical squeezing** — vacuum pressure physically collapses entrapped air bubbles. 2. **Volumetric absorption** — dissolved gases diffuse out under reduced partial pressure. 3. **Boiling/volatilisation** — at low $P_{vac}$, residual volatiles and moisture vapourise and are pumped away. ### Principle 5: **Cure Kinetics Overlay** Resin viscosity first drops (as temperature ramps up), reaches a minimum (gelation onset), then rises sharply. Vacuum must be held **throughout** the gelation window to prevent re-inflation of voids. ### Principle 6: **Bag Material Selection Logic** The bag film must withstand (a) cure temperature (typically **Nylon 6 at ≤ 120 °C**, **Polyimide Kapton at ≤ 230 °C**), (b) tensile load without necking, and (c) chemical attack from styrene in polyester resins. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every equation you must memorise for Module 4 examination. **Memorise the symbols, not just the formula.** | # | Formula | Meaning of Terms | Engineering Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $P_{comp} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ | $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure; $P_{vac}$ = absolute bag pressure | Maximum achievable pressure | | 2 | $V_{f} = \dfrac{\rho_{c} \cdot w_{f}}{\rho_{f}}$ | $V_{f}$ = fibre volume fraction; $\rho_{c}$ = composite density; $w_{f}$ = fibre weight fraction; $\rho_{f}$ = fibre density | Quality control check | | 3 | $Q = \dfrac{K \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | $Q$ = volumetric resin flow rate; $K$ = permeability; $A$ = flow area; $\Delta P$ = pressure drop; $\mu$ = viscosity; $L$ = flow length | Bleeder design | | 4 | $V_{v} \approx \dfrac{h_{b}}{h_{t}}$ | $V_{v}$ = void fraction; $h_{b}$ = total bubble height; $h_{t}$ = laminate thickness | Void estimation (ultrasonic) | | 5 | $\sigma_{comp} = V_{f} \cdot \sigma_{f} \cdot \eta_{l} + V_{m} \cdot \sigma_{m}$ | $\sigma_{comp}$ = composite strength; $\eta_{l}$ = fibre length efficiency factor; $V_{m}$ = matrix volume fraction | Property prediction | | 6 | $t_{cure} = \dfrac{t_{gel}}{\alpha_{c}}$ | $t_{gel}$ = gel time; $\alpha_{c}$ = cure acceleration factor | Process scheduling | | 7 | $P_{hydrostatic,eq} = \rho_{atm} \cdot g \cdot h$ | $h$ = equivalent air column height ($\approx 10{,}332$ m for sea level) | Intuitive pressure sense | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Why VBM is Preferred | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Aerospace** | Ailerons, rudders, fairings | Lower void content than HLU; cheaper than autoclave | | **Marine** | Yacht hulls, deck panels | Large part capability (up to 30 m); good surface finish | | **Wind Energy** | Wind turbine blade shells | Cost-effective for large laminates | | **Automotive** | Body panels, leaf springs | Medium-volume production with acceptable cycle time | | **Sports Goods** | Surfboards, kayaks, tennis racquets | Excellent strength-to-weight ratio | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Consolidation Pressure in VBM **Statement to Prove:** The theoretical maximum consolidation pressure achievable in vacuum bag moulding equals standard atmospheric pressure, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa. **Step 1: Define the pressure system.** The vacuum bag is a sealed enclosure that contains the laminate. Two pressure zones exist: $$P_{above-bag} = P_{atm} \quad \text{(ambient atmospheric pressure on outer bag surface)}$$ $$P_{below-bag} = P_{vac} \quad \text{(reduced absolute pressure inside bag)}$$ **Step 2: Apply the ideal-gas/dead-end pump assumption.** When a vacuum pump evacuates the bag, it removes gas molecules until the absolute pressure equals the *partial-pressure limit* of the pump. For an ideal mechanical pump: $$\lim_{t \to \infty} P_{vac}(t) \to 0 \quad \text{(absolute zero pressure is theoretical)}$$ In practice, due to leakage and pump performance: $$P_{vac,min} \approx 0.05 \cdot P_{atm} \approx 5.07 \text{ kPa (absolute)}$$ **Step 3: Compute the pressure differential across the bag membrane.** By Newton's third law, the net force on the laminate is the pressure differential times the laminate area: $$\Delta P_{comp} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$ **Step 4: Substitute the values.** Taking the industrial benchmark of **95 % vacuum**: $$P_{vac} = 0.05 \cdot P_{atm} = 0.05 \cdot 101.325 = 5.066 \text{ kPa (absolute)}$$ Therefore: $$\Delta P_{comp} = 101.325 - 5.066 = 96.259 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 5: Establish the upper bound.** In the absolute theoretical limit where $P_{vac} \to 0$: $$\Delta P_{comp,max} = P_{atm} - 0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P_{comp,max} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 0.1013 \text{ MPa} = 14.696 \text{ psi}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the *fundamental ceiling* of VBM. To exceed this, one must switch to **autoclave moulding** (which uses external pressurised gas at 0.3–1.4 MPa). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fibre Volume Fraction from Resin Bleed Control **Statement to Prove:** Given the initial laminate thickness $h_0$ and the desired final fibre volume fraction $V_{f,target}$, derive the required bleeder ply thickness $h_{bleeder}$. **Step 1: State the rule of mixtures for the unbleed laminate (assuming zero voids initially):** $$\frac{w_{f}}{\rho_{f}} + \frac{w_{m}}{\rho_{m}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{f,initial} = \frac{w_{f}/\rho_{f}}{w_{f}/\rho_{f} + w_{m}/\rho_{m}}$$ **Step 2: Express the initial laminate thickness.** For an $n$-ply lay-up with areal fibre weight $A_w$ (g/m²) and matrix content $w_m$: $$h_0 = \frac{n \cdot A_{w}}{\rho_{f} \cdot V_{f,initial}}$$ **Step 3: Define the target thickness under vacuum consolidation.** When vacuum is applied, resin is bled out into the bleeder ply until: $$V_{f,target} = \frac{n \cdot A_{w} / \rho_{f}}{h_{final} \cdot 1}$$ Rearranging: $$h_{final} = \frac{n \cdot A_{w}}{\rho_{f} \cdot V_{f,target}}$$ **Step 4: Compute the volume of resin bled out (per unit area):** $$\Delta h_{resin} = h_0 - h_{final}$$ $$\Delta h_{resin} = \frac{n \cdot A_{w}}{\rho_{f}} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{V_{f,initial}} - \frac{1}{V_{f,target}} \right)$$ **Step 5: Compute the bleeder ply capacity.** A bleeder (typically non-woven polyester) absorbs resin up to its saturation mass per unit area, $\beta$ (g/m²). The mass of resin bled out per unit area is: $$m_{resin,bleed} = \Delta h_{resin} \cdot \rho_{m} = \beta_{bleeder} \cdot N_{bleeder}$$ Solving for the number of bleeder plies: $$N_{bleeder} = \frac{\Delta h_{resin} \cdot \rho_{m}}{\beta_{bleeder}}$$ $$\boxed{N_{bleeder} = \frac{n \cdot A_{w} \cdot \rho_{m}}{\rho_{f} \cdot \beta_{bleeder}} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{V_{f,initial}} - \frac{1}{V_{f,target}} \right)}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** An 8-ply glass-fibre/epoxy laminate uses plies of $A_w = 600$ g/m² each. Glass density $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³; epoxy density $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³; initial $V_{f,initial} = 0.40$. Target $V_{f,target} = 0.55$. Bleeder absorbs $\beta = 800$ g/m² per ply. Calculate the number of bleeder plies required. **Solution — Step 1: Calculate initial laminate thickness.** $$h_0 = \frac{8 \cdot 600}{2.55 \cdot 0.40} = \frac{4800}{1.02} = 4705.88 \text{ microns} = 4.706 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Calculate target final thickness.** $$h_{final} = \frac{8 \cdot 600}{2.55 \cdot 0.55} = \frac{4800}{1.4025} = 3422.62 \text{ microns} = 3.423 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3: Calculate resin thickness to be bled.** $$\Delta h_{resin} = 4.706 - 3.423 = 1.283 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4: Calculate mass of resin bled per unit area.** $$m_{resin} = 1.283 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \cdot 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1.540 \text{ kg/m}^2 = 1540 \text{ g/m}^2$$ **Step 5: Calculate bleeder plies required.** $$N_{bleeder} = \frac{1540}{800} = 1.925 \approx \boxed{2 \text{ bleeder plies (round up)}}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Vacuum Bag Moulding Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Mould Tool
Cleaning & Polish] --> A2[Apply Release Agent
Wax or PVA] A2 --> A3[Optional Gel Coat
Spray Application] A3 --> B1[Cut Fibre Plies
Per Nesting Plan] B1 --> B2[Lay-Up First Ply
on Mould] B2 --> B3[Wet Resin Application
Hand Brush or Roller] B3 --> B4[Repeat Lay-Up
Until n Plies] B4 --> C1[Place Peel Ply
Nylon 6 Mesh] C1 --> C2[Place Bleeder Ply
Non-Woven Polyester] C2 --> C3[Place Breather Ply
Glass or Polyester Mat] C3 --> D1[Position Vacuum Bag Film
Nylon or Polyimide] D1 --> D2[Seal Periphery
Tacky Sealant Tape] D2 --> D3[Insert Vacuum Port
Through Bag] D3 --> E1[Connect to Vacuum Pump
via Hose] E1 --> E2[Apply Vacuum
95 percent of atm] E2 --> E3[Check Leak Rate
Less than 25 mmHg per min] E3 --> F1[Cure Cycle Initiated
RT to 80 degree C] F1 --> F2[Hold Vacuum Through Gel
Until Full Cure] F2 --> F3[Release Vacuum
After Cool-Down] F3 --> G1[Remove Bag and
Consumables] G1 --> G2[Demould Part
Using Ejector Pins] G2 --> G3[Trim and Inspect
NDI Methods] G3 --> H1[Final Composite Part
Vf 50 to 60 percent] style A1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style H1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style E2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Layer Stack (Top-Down View of Bag Assembly) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LAYER_STACK[" "] direction TB TOP[Atmospheric Air
P equals 101.325 kPa] L1[Vacuum Bag Film
Nylon 6 Thickness 50 to 75 microns] L2[Breather Ply
Provides Air Path and Pressure Transmission] L3[Bleeder Ply
Absorbs Excess Resin] L4[Peel Ply
Allows Clean Surface Release] L5[Fibre Reinforcement Stack
n Plies of Woven or UD Fabric] L6[Resin Rich Layer
Matrix Pool] L7[Mould Tool Surface
Rigid Composite or Aluminium] BOT[Support Structure
Table or Platen] end TOP --- L1 L1 --- L2 L2 --- L3 L3 --- L4 L4 --- L5 L5 --- L6 L6 --- L7 L7 --- BOT ``` ## 4.3 Pressure & Material Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR PUMP[Vacuum Pump
Evacuates Air] -->|Hose| PORT[Vacuum Port
Through Bag] PORT -->|Reduces P| BAG[Bag Interior
P vac approx 5 kPa] BAG -->|Transmits Force| COMPACT[Laminate Compaction
Delta P approx 96 kPa] COMPACT -->|Drives Out| RESIN[Resin Flow
Into Bleeder] COMPACT -->|Expels| VOIDS[Air Voids
Out Through Breather] LAMINATE[Fibre Resin Lay-Up] --> COMPACT RESIN --> BLEEDER[Bleeder Ply
Saturates with Resin] VOIDS --> PUMP style PUMP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style COMPACT fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825 style BLEEDER fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **"Define vacuum bag moulding. Mention any two advantages it offers over hand lay-up technique." [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** Vacuum bag moulding (VBM) is a closed-mould composite fabrication process in which a flexible polymeric bag is sealed over a wet fibre–resin lay-up placed on a rigid mould, and the air beneath the bag is evacuated by a vacuum pump. The resulting atmospheric pressure differential (~101 kPa) consolidates the laminate, removes entrapped air, and expels excess resin, yielding a higher-quality composite than hand lay-up. **Two advantages over hand lay-up:** 1. **Higher fibre volume fraction** (50–60 % vs 30–45 %), because the consolidation pressure squeezes out excess resin. 2. **Lower void content** (1–3 % vs 3–5 %), because entrapped air is actively evacuated rather than trapped. **[Mark Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 Marks]** --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **"List the functions of (i) breather ply and (ii) bleeder ply in vacuum bag moulding." [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **(i) Breather Ply:** A porous fabric (typically glass or polyester non-woven mat) placed between the bleeder and the vacuum bag. Its functions are: 1. To provide a continuous air path from the laminate to the vacuum port, ensuring uniform pressure distribution. 2. To act as a pressure-transmitting medium that distributes compaction force evenly across the laminate surface. **(ii) Bleeder Ply:** An absorbent fabric (typically non-woven polyester) placed directly on the peel ply. Its functions are: 1. To absorb excess resin bled out from the laminate under vacuum compaction, thereby controlling fibre volume fraction. 2. To serve as a reservoir for trapped air and volatiles, helping reduce void content. **[Mark Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 Marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Convention:** Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. --- ### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **"With the help of a neat sketch, describe the vacuum bag moulding process for fibre-reinforced polymer composites. List the consumables used in the process with their specific functions."** **Model Solution:** **Process Description (5 Marks):** 1. **Mould Preparation:** The rigid mould (typically composite, aluminium, or steel) is cleaned, polished, and coated with release agent (wax or PVA). An optional gel coat may be sprayed for superior surface finish. 2. **Lay-Up:** Pre-cut fibre plies (woven roving, mat, or unidirectional fabric) are laid on the mould one ply at a time, with liquid thermoset resin (epoxy/polyester/vinyl ester) impregnated by hand using brushes or rollers. Each ply is rolled to remove entrapped air. 3. **Consumable Stack Assembly:** After the desired number of plies are placed, the consumables are stacked in order — peel ply (bottom-most on laminate), bleeder ply, and breather ply (top-most under bag). 4. **Bagging:** A flexible vacuum bag film (Nylon 6 for RT cure; Polyimide for elevated temp) is placed over the stack and sealed around the periphery using tacky sealant tape. A vacuum port is inserted through the bag. 5. **Vacuum Application:** The vacuum pump is connected and the system is evacuated to ≥ 95 % of atmospheric pressure (~5 kPa absolute). Leak rate is checked (must be < 25 mmHg/min). 6. **Cure:** The laminate is allowed to cure at room temperature or in an oven. Vacuum is held throughout gelation to prevent void re-inflation. 7. **Demoulding:** After full cure and cool-down, vacuum is released, bag and consumables are stripped, and the part is demoulded using ejector pins. **Consumables and Functions (2 Marks):** | Consumable | Function | | :--- | :--- | | Release Agent | Prevents laminate sticking to mould | | Peel Ply | Provides clean, bondable surface after stripping | | Bleeder Ply | Absorbs excess resin to control Vf | | Breather Ply | Distributes vacuum and transmits pressure | | Vacuum Bag Film | Sealed membrane that transmits atm pressure | | Sealant Tape | Airtight seal at bag periphery | | Vacuum Port | Connects bag interior to pump | #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **"Discuss the effect of vacuum level on (i) fibre volume fraction, (ii) void content, and (iii) laminate thickness in vacuum bag moulding."** **Model Solution:** The vacuum level, expressed as the percentage of atmospheric pressure evacuated, is the single most critical process variable in VBM. Its effects are: **(i) Effect on Fibre Volume Fraction (V_f) (2.5 Marks):** As vacuum level increases, the consolidation pressure $\Delta P_{comp} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ rises, compressing the fibre bed to higher packing density. Fibre volume fraction follows a logarithmic relationship: $$V_{f} \propto \ln(\Delta P_{comp})$$ - At 50 % vacuum ($\Delta P \approx 50.7$ kPa): V_f ≈ 0.35–0.42 - At 90 % vacuum ($\Delta P \approx 91.2$ kPa): V_f ≈ 0.48–0.55 - At 99 % vacuum ($\Delta P \approx 100.3$ kPa): V_f ≈ 0.55–0.62 **(ii) Effect on Void Content (V_v) (2.5 Marks):** Higher vacuum level directly reduces entrapped air. The Henry's law relationship shows that dissolved gas concentration in the resin decreases proportionally with absolute pressure: $$C_{dissolved} = k_{H} \cdot P_{vac}$$ - At 80 % vacuum: V_v ≈ 3–5 % - At 95 % vacuum: V_v ≈ 1–2 % - At 99 % vacuum: V_v ≈ 0.5–1 % **(iii) Effect on Laminate Thickness (2 Marks):** Thickness decreases monotonically with vacuum level as resin is squeezed out. The final thickness is: $$h_{final} = \frac{n \cdot A_{w}}{\rho_{f} \cdot V_{f}}$$ As V_f rises with vacuum, h_final decreases. For example, an 8-ply laminate with $A_w = 600$ g/m² compresses from ~4.7 mm (no vacuum, V_f = 0.40) to ~3.4 mm (high vacuum, V_f = 0.55). --- ### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **"Compare hand lay-up, vacuum bag moulding, and autoclave moulding processes based on (i) applied pressure, (ii) achievable fibre volume fraction, (iii) void content, (iv) equipment cost, and (v) typical applications."** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Hand Lay-Up (HLU) | Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM) | Autoclave Moulding | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Applied Pressure** | Roller pressure only (~contact pressure) | Atmospheric (~101 kPa max) | 300 – 1400 kPa (external gas) | | **Fibre Volume Fraction (V_f)** | 0.30 – 0.45 | 0.50 – 0.60 | 0.55 – 0.70 | | **Void Content (V_v)** | 3 – 5 % | 1 – 3 % | < 0.5 – 1 % | | **Equipment Cost** | Very Low (₹) | Moderate (₹₹) | Very High (₹₹₹₹) | | **Part Size Limit** | Unlimited | Large (~ 30 m) | Limited (~ 5–10 m typical) | | **Cycle Time** | 8 – 24 hrs | 4 – 12 hrs | 6 – 24 hrs (incl. ramp/dwell) | | **Surface Quality** | Tool side: Good; Bag side: Poor | Tool side: Good; Bag side: Fair | Both sides: Excellent | | **Typical Applications** | Boat hulls, truck panels, building components | Yacht decks, wind blade shells, ailerons | Aerospace primary structures (wing skins, fuselages) | | **Labour Intensity** | High | Medium-High | Medium | **Analytical Commentary (2 Marks for the comparison interpretation):** - The progression HLU → VBM → Autoclave represents an increase in pressure, quality, and cost. - VBM is the *sweet spot* for non-aerospace structural composites where autoclave cost is unjustified but HLU quality is insufficient. - Autoclave remains mandatory for *primary aerospace structures* where void content < 1 % is mandated by FAA/EASA regulations. #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **"A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy laminate is fabricated using vacuum bag moulding. The laminate has 10 plies, each with an areal weight of 450 g/m². Glass fibre density is 2.55 g/cm³ and cured epoxy density is 1.18 g/cm³. After vacuum bagging, the laminate shows a fibre volume fraction of 0.55. Calculate: (i) the final laminate thickness, (ii) the resin weight fraction, and (iii) the thickness of resin bled out per square metre, assuming initial V_f was 0.40."** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Number of plies: n = 10 - Areal weight per ply: A_w = 450 g/m² - $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³ = 2550 kg/m³ - $\rho_m = 1.18$ g/cm³ = 1180 kg/m³ - V_f,target = 0.55 - V_f,initial = 0.40 **(i) Final Laminate Thickness (3 Marks):** $$h_{final} = \frac{n \cdot A_w}{\rho_f \cdot V_{f,target}} = \frac{10 \cdot 450 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^2}{2550 \text{ kg/m}^3 \cdot 0.55}$$ $$h_{final} = \frac{4.5}{1402.5} = 3.21 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = \boxed{3.21 \text{ mm}}$$ **(ii) Resin Weight Fraction (2 Marks):** Volume fraction of matrix: $V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$ Using the rule of mixtures for weight fraction: $$w_f = \frac{V_f \cdot \rho_f}{V_f \cdot \rho_f + V_m \cdot \rho_m} = \frac{0.55 \cdot 2.55}{0.55 \cdot 2.55 + 0.45 \cdot 1.18}$$ $$w_f = \frac{1.4025}{1.4025 + 0.531} = \frac{1.4025}{1.9335} = 0.7253$$ Therefore: $$w_m = 1 - 0.7253 = \boxed{0.2747 \text{ or } 27.47\%}$$ **(iii) Thickness of Resin Bled Out (2 Marks):** Initial thickness with V_f,initial = 0.40: $$h_{initial} = \frac{10 \cdot 450 \times 10^{-3}}{2550 \cdot 0.40} = \frac{4.5}{1020} = 4.412 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.412 \text{ mm}$$ Resin bled out thickness: $$\Delta h_{resin} = h_{initial} - h_{final} = 4.412 - 3.210 = \boxed{1.202 \text{ mm}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Vacuum Bag Moulding Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Students often mix g/cm³ with kg/m³ in Rule of Mixtures calculations, losing 1–2 marks instantly. **Always convert to SI (kg/m³) before arithmetic.** > > 2. **Confusing V_f and w_f:** Volume fraction and weight fraction are NOT equal for fibre-matrix systems (because $\rho_f \neq \rho_m$). Use the proper conversion formula; do NOT equate them. > > 3. **Stating "atmospheric pressure" without numerical value:** Examiners in KTU often award a partial mark for the number. Always write **"101.325 kPa"** alongside "atmospheric pressure." > > 4. **Skipping the neat sketch in process questions:** Part (a) of a 14-mark question ALWAYS requires a labelled diagram. Drawing the *layer stack* (mould → laminate → peel ply → bleeder → breather → bag) earns at least 2 marks. > > 5. **Ignoring the breather ply's role:** Many students wrongly state that the breather absorbs resin. **Breather absorbs AIR/volatile vapours; Bleeder absorbs RESIN.** Mixing these is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark deduction. > > 6. **Not justifying the choice of bag material:** Nylon vs. Polyimide vs. Silicone bag selection based on cure temperature is a frequently tested point. Always mention the **temperature rating**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vacuum Bag Moulding (Module 4)** ### A. Must-Know Facts - VBM applies a maximum consolidation pressure of **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**. - Achievable fibre volume fraction: **50 – 60 %**; void content: **1 – 3 %**. - VBM is an *enhancement* of hand lay-up using atmospheric pressure as the consolidation source. - Resin flow follows **Darcy's Law** through porous fibre preforms. ### B. Must-Know Layer Stack (Top to Bottom) 1. Vacuum Bag Film (Nylon 6 / Polyimide) 2. **Breather Ply** (transmits pressure, vents air) 3. **Bleeder Ply** (absorbs excess resin) 4. **Peel Ply** (gives clean surface) 5. Fibre–Resin Laminate 6. Rigid Mould Tool ### B'. Function Quick Reference (Mnemonic — "**B**reather = **B**ubbles, **B**leeder = **R**esin") - **B**reather handles **B**ubbles (air) - **B**leeder handles **R**esin (liquid) ### C. Must-Know Process Steps (Sequence) Mould prep → Release agent → Gel coat (optional) → Ply lay-up with resin → Consumable stack → Bag sealing → Vacuum application → Cure (with vacuum held) → De-bagging → Demoulding. ### D. Must-Know Comparative Statements - **HLU vs VBM:** VBM gives higher V_f, lower voids, at the cost of more consumables and labour. - **VBM vs Autoclave:** Autoclave gives higher pressure (300–1400 kPa) but is far more expensive and size-limited. - **VBM vs RTM:** RTM uses matched metal dies and injected resin; VBM uses vacuum-driven compaction with pre-placed resin. ### E. Must-Know Equations - $\Delta P_{comp} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ - $V_f = \dfrac{w_f \cdot \rho_m}{\rho_f + w_f(\rho_m - \rho_f)}$ - $h_{final} = \dfrac{n \cdot A_w}{\rho_f \cdot V_f}$ ### F. Must-Know Material Properties - E-glass fibre density: **2.55 g/cm³** - Carbon fibre density: **1.75–1.95 g/cm³** - Aramid (Kevlar) density: **1.44 g/cm³** - Epoxy resin density: **1.15–1.20 g/cm³** - Polyester resin density: **1.20–1.30 g/cm³** ### G. Must-Know Engineering Limits - Maximum laminate area: **~ 30 m** (limited by vacuum pump capacity and bag integrity). - Maximum cure temperature: **~ 230 °C** (limited by bag material — Kapton polyimide). - Typical cycle time: **4 – 12 hours**. - Minimum void content achievable: **~ 0.5 %** (theoretical limit).

topics.title: Pressure Bag Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Bag Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Pressure Bag Moulding** is a closed-mould composite manufacturing technique belonging to the family of *bag moulding* processes, in which a flexible, airtight elastomeric bag (the *pressure bag*) is placed over a wet laminate laid inside a rigid matched mould, sealed at the periphery, and then inflated with compressed air or fluid (typically **200 kPa to 700 kPa**) to apply a uniform hydrostatic forming pressure on the laminate. This pressure consolidates the plies, expels trapped air and excess resin, reduces void content, and drives the cure of the thermosetting matrix, producing a high-quality, low-porosity composite component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pressure Bag Moulding is grouped under *Module 4 – Processing of Composites* alongside Vacuum Bag Moulding, Autoclave Moulding, and Resin Transfer Moulding. Examiners expect students to clearly distinguish it from Vacuum Bag Moulding (which uses suction ~ 1 atm differential) by emphasizing the **positive, externally-applied pressure** delivered by a pressurised bag.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine making a **sandwich that you want perfectly flat and tightly packed**. If you simply press the sandwich with your hand, you get uneven pressure and air pockets remain between the bread layers. Now imagine placing the sandwich inside a sealed plastic pouch and then **blowing air into the pouch until it inflates and squeezes the sandwich uniformly from all sides** — this is exactly what a pressure bag does to a composite laminate.

- **The Bread =** Prepreg plies / reinforcement layers
- **The Filling =** Liquid thermoset resin
- **The Sealed Pouch =** The flexible elastomeric pressure bag
- **The Air You Blow =** Compressed air at 3–7 bar
- **The Counter-Surface =** The rigid mould tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Car Tyre on a Wet Puddle:** When you roll a car tyre over a wet patch, the inflated inner bladder of the tyre pushes outward against the road, sweeping away water and air pockets — leaving a uniform, compacted surface. The pressure bag behaves identically with a fibre–resin stack.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Operating Pressure Range:** **200 kPa – 700 kPa** (typically ~ 400 kPa)
- **Cure Temperature Range:** **25 °C – 180 °C** (depending on resin system)
- **Bag Material:** Polyurethane (PU) rubber, silicone rubber, or natural rubber (latex)
- **Maximum Achievable Fibre Volume Fraction (V_f):** **0.55 – 0.65**
- **Typical Void Content Achieved:** **< 1 %** (compared to 3–5 % in hand lay-up)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The pressure bag moulding process relies on the **Pascal's Law of fluid pressure transmission** through the flexible elastomeric membrane. Each operational step is a critical control point:

1. **Mould Preparation:** A rigid, polished mould (typically female for convex parts) is cleaned and release-agent coated.
2. **Lay-up:** Dry reinforcement (fibreglass / carbon / Kevlar fabric) is laid on the mould along with catalysed liquid thermoset resin (polyester, vinylester, or epoxy).
3. **Bag Placement:** A flexible, airtight elastomeric bag (PU or silicone) is draped over the laminate, covering it completely.
4. **Peripheral Sealing:** The open mouth of the bag is clamped against the mould flange using mechanical clamps or a sealing gasket, creating a closed pressure chamber.
5. **Pressurisation:** Compressed air is introduced between the mould and the bag through a port. The bag inflates like a balloon, pressing uniformly against the laminate.
6. **Consolidation & Cure:** The applied pressure:
   - Squeezes out excess resin (resin-rich regions are eliminated).
   - Drives out trapped air and volatiles (void reduction).
   - Compacts the fibres into a closer packing arrangement.
   - Provides the hydrostatic pressure needed for the resin to cure into a dense, low-porosity matrix.
7. **Depressurisation & Demoulding:** After cure, pressure is released, the bag is deflated and removed, and the finished composite part is lifted from the mould.

### 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Works |
|------|----------------|--------------|
| Rigid mould | Provides finished surface contour & dimensional accuracy | Acts as the male/female die surface |
| Flexible bag | Transmits uniform pressure to irregular contours | Pascal's Law: pressure equal in all directions |
| Sealed flange | Contains pressurised air without leakage | Mechanical clamps + rubber gasket |
| Compressed air | Generates consolidation pressure 4–7× atmospheric | External compressor feeds regulated air |
| Elevated temp (optional) | Accelerates resin cure kinetics | Oven, heated mould, or heated platen |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Bank for Pressure Bag Moulding & Composite Consolidation**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Area | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c - \rho_r}{\rho_f - \rho_r}$ | Fibre volume fraction by densities | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f \cdot N}{A_c \cdot t}$ | Fibre volume fraction geometrically | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_r$ | Void volume fraction | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_r \rho_r$ | Composite density (rule of mixtures) | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $E_c = V_f E_f + V_r E_r$ | Longitudinal modulus (rule of mixtures) | GPa |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{n \cdot h_{ply}}{\text{Consolidation ratio}}$ | Final laminate thickness | mm |
| 8 | $V_f \propto \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | Toll's compaction law (fibre bed) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 t_{bag}}$ | Hoop stress in the inflated bag | MPa |
| 10 | $T_{cure} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}$ (approx.) | Cure onset relation | K |

**Key Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Problems:**
- Atmospheric pressure reference: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- Typical bag operating pressure: $P_{bag} = 300 \text{ kPa to 500 kPa}$
- Typical fibre density (E-glass): $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Typical cured epoxy density: $\rho_r = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pressure bag moulding is the workhorse process for manufacturing **large, hollow, or aerodynamically-curved composite structures** where the part is too large for an autoclave. Major industry applications include:

- **Aerospace:** Radomes, leading edges of wings, helicopter rotor blades, missile nose cones.
- **Marine:** Hulls of small boats, kayaks, canoes, buoyancy tanks.
- **Automotive:** Prototype body panels, spoiler prototypes, F1 chassis components.
- **Wind Energy:** Rotor blade root sections and spar caps.
- **Sports Goods:** Surfboards, snowboards, hockey sticks, helmets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Consolidated Laminate Thickness under Bag Pressure

For a laminate of **n** plies, each ply having an areal weight $w_p$ (g/m²) of fibre plus resin, the final cured thickness after pressure-bag consolidation can be derived from conservation of fibre and resin mass.

**Starting Point — Mass balance per unit area:**

$$
m_{total} = m_f + m_r = n \cdot (w_f + w_r) \quad \left[\frac{\text{g}}{\text{m}^2}\right]
$$

**Volume per unit area equals thickness:**

$$
t = \frac{m_f}{\rho_f} + \frac{m_r}{\rho_r} = \frac{m_{total}}{\rho_c}
$$

**Where composite density from rule of mixtures:**

$$
\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + (1 - V_f) \rho_r
$$

**Combining and solving for thickness:**

$$
t = \frac{n \cdot w_{ply}}{\rho_c} = \frac{n \cdot w_{ply}}{V_f \rho_f + (1 - V_f)\rho_r}
$$

**Stepwise Example:**
Given 10 plies of 600 g/m² glass fabric prepreg, V_f target = 0.50, ρ_f = 2.55 g/cm³, ρ_r = 1.20 g/cm³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= (0.50)(2.55) + (0.50)(1.20) \\
       &= 1.275 + 0.600 = 1.875 \ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{10 \times 600}{1.875 \times 10^6} \quad \text{(converting g/m² to kg/m³ basis)} \\
  &= \frac{6000}{1.875 \times 10^6} \\
  &= 3.20 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} = 3.20 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Void Content Reduction with Applied Pressure

The **void content** in a cured laminate arises from entrapped air bubbles between plies. The pressure-bag process reduces voids according to Henry's-Law-inspired gas dissolution:

$$
V_v \approx V_{v,0} \cdot \frac{P_{atm}}{P_{atm} + P_{bag}}
$$

Where:
- $V_{v,0}$ = initial void content (before bag pressure), typically 0.03 – 0.05
- $P_{bag}$ = applied bag pressure (gauge)

**Numerical Demonstration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_v &= 0.04 \times \frac{101.3}{101.3 + 400} \\
    &= 0.04 \times \frac{101.3}{501.3} \\
    &= 0.04 \times 0.2021 \\
    &= 0.00808 \quad (\text{i.e., } 0.81\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Starting with 4 % voids (typical of hand lay-up), a 400 kPa pressure bag reduces it to **less than 1 %** — a 5-fold improvement in laminate densification.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Hoop Stress in the Inflated Bag

To select the right bag material, we calculate the tensile (hoop) stress developed in the inflated elastomeric membrane using a thin-walled pressure-vessel model:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot t_{bag}}
$$

**Where:**
- $P$ = inflation pressure
- $r$ = bag radius (assumed spherical segment)
- $t_{bag}$ = bag wall thickness

**Worked Example:**
Bag radius $r = 0.5$ m, $P = 500$ kPa, bag thickness $t_{bag} = 2$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{(500 \times 10^3) \times 0.5}{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})} \\
       &= \frac{250{,}000}{0.004} \\
       &= 6.25 \times 10^7 \ \text{Pa} = 62.5 \ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The bag material must have a tensile strength greater than 62.5 MPa (with safety factor 4× → > 250 MPa). Silicone and PU rubbers typically offer 7–15 MPa tensile strength at 2 mm — so a **safety factor of 4–8×** is mandatory, often requiring the bag to be embedded in fabric reinforcement.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Process Parameters

| Parameter | Low Value Effect | High Value Effect | KTU Typical |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Bag pressure | High void content, low V_f | Excellent compaction, risk of fibre washout | **300–500 kPa** |
| Cure temperature | Slow cure, long cycle | Fast cure, exotherm risk | **80–120 °C (epoxy)** |
| Resin viscosity | Resin-starved regions | Poor wet-out of fibres | **300–500 cP at 25 °C** |
| Bag thickness | Risk of burst | Slow pressure transmission | **1.5–3 mm** |
| Hold time | Incomplete cure | Full cure, longer cycle | **1–4 hours** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
