<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define Pressure Bag Moulding. Mention any two advantages over hand lay-up.**

**Model Answer:**

Pressure Bag Moulding is a closed-mould composite fabrication process in which a flexible elastomeric bag is placed over a wet laminate inside a rigid mould, sealed at the edges, and inflated with compressed air (typically 200–700 kPa) to apply uniform hydrostatic pressure during cure.

**Two advantages over hand lay-up:**

1. **Lower void content (< 1 % vs 3–5 %)** — applied pressure expels entrapped air and volatiles, producing denser laminates.
2. **Higher fibre volume fraction (V_f up to 0.55–0.65)** — pressure compacts the reinforcement, increasing fibre loading and therefore mechanical properties.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse Pressure Bag with Vacuum Bag. Both use flexible membranes — but Pressure Bag uses **positive compressed air** pushing inward, whereas Vacuum Bag uses **suction** pulling outward. Mixing the two costs full 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**List the materials used for the elastomeric pressure bag and state the reason for its selection.**

**Model Answer:**

Common pressure bag materials:

1. **Polyurethane (PU) rubber** — high tensile strength, good flexibility, resistant to oils and solvents.
2. **Silicone rubber** — excellent thermal stability (up to 200 °C), used for high-temperature epoxy cures.
3. **Natural rubber (latex)** — inexpensive, good elasticity, used for room-temperature cures.

**Reason for selection:** The bag must be **flexible, airtight, chemically inert to the resin, and able to withstand inflation pressures of 300–700 kPa** without bursting or leaking. Elastomers offer the necessary combination of elasticity, tensile strength, and chemical resistance.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Exam]`

**(a)** Explain the Pressure Bag Moulding process with a neat labelled schematic. Describe the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A composite laminate is made from 8 plies of E-glass fabric, each of areal weight 450 g/m², using epoxy resin (ρ_r = 1.20 g/cm³, ρ_f = 2.55 g/cm³). The cured fibre volume fraction is V_f = 0.48. Calculate:
(i) The cured laminate thickness.
(ii) The cured laminate density.
(iii) The void volume fraction. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Process Explanation (7 marks):**

The Pressure Bag Moulding process consists of the following sequential steps:

1. **Mould preparation:** A rigid, polished mould (typically steel or aluminium, female for concave parts) is cleaned and coated with a release agent (wax or PVA).
2. **Lay-up:** Reinforcement plies (woven fabric / mat) are laid on the mould in the required stacking sequence, and catalysed liquid resin is poured and rolled in for wet-out.
3. **Bag placement:** A flexible elastomeric bag (PU or silicone) is draped over the wet laminate, covering it completely.
4. **Sealing:** The bag opening is clamped against the mould flange with a rubber gasket and mechanical clamps, creating a hermetically sealed pressure chamber.
5. **Pressurisation:** Compressed air is introduced through an inlet valve. Pressure rises to **300–500 kPa**, inflating the bag.
6. **Consolidation & cure:** The bag pressure uniformly squeezes the laminate, expelling excess resin, air voids, and volatiles. Heat may be applied to accelerate cure.
7. **Depressurisation & demoulding:** After full cure, pressure is released, the bag is removed, and the finished part is demoulded.

**Role of Each Component:**

- **Rigid mould:** Defines the geometry and surface finish of the part.
- **Release agent:** Prevents adhesion of cured laminate to mould surface.
- **Reinforcement & resin:** Provide the structural fibre–matrix system.
- **Elastomeric bag:** Transmits uniform hydrostatic pressure to the laminate.
- **Sealing gasket & clamps:** Maintain airtight chamber.
- **Compressed air inlet:** Source of consolidation pressure.
- **Pressure regulator & gauge:** Control and monitor bag pressure.
- **Heat source (optional):** Accelerates resin cure kinetics.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Cured laminate thickness:**

Using $t = \dfrac{n \cdot w_{ply}}{\rho_c}$ and $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + (1 - V_f) \rho_r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= (0.48)(2.55) + (1 - 0.48)(1.20) \\
       &= 1.224 + 0.624 \\
       &= 1.848 \ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{8 \times 450}{1.848 \times 10^6} \ \text{m} \\
  &= \frac{3600}{1.848 \times 10^6} \\
  &= 1.948 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} \\
  &= \boxed{1.95 \ \text{mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Cured laminate density:** $\boxed{\rho_c = 1.848 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$

**(iii) Void volume fraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_v &= 1 - V_f - V_r \\
    &= 1 - 0.48 - (1 - 0.48) \\
    &= 1 - 0.48 - 0.52 \\
    &= 0.00 \ \text{(i.e., negligible voids in ideal case)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a more realistic scenario where density measurement gives $\rho_{measured} = 1.82$ g/cm³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_v &= 1 - \frac{\rho_{measured}}{\rho_c} \\
    &= 1 - \frac{1.82}{1.848} \\
    &= 1 - 0.985 \\
    &= \boxed{0.015 \ \text{i.e., } 1.5\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare Pressure Bag Moulding with Vacuum Bag Moulding and Autoclave Moulding in terms of pressure applied, equipment cost, part quality, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During pressure bag moulding, a hemispherical silicone bag of radius 0.4 m and wall thickness 2.5 mm is inflated to a gauge pressure of 450 kPa. Calculate the hoop stress developed in the bag wall and comment on whether the bag can safely withstand the load. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Vacuum Bag | Pressure Bag | Autoclave |
|-----------|------------|--------------|-----------|
| Pressure applied | ~ 100 kPa (1 atm differential) | 300–700 kPa | 500–1500 kPa |
| Pressure source | Vacuum pump | Compressed air | High-pressure gas in pressure vessel |
| Equipment cost | Low | Medium | Very high |
| Void content achieved | 1–3 % | < 1 % | < 0.5 % |
| Achievable V_f | 0.45–0.55 | 0.55–0.65 | 0.60–0.70 |
| Temperature capability | Limited (resin hot-melt) | Up to ~ 180 °C | Up to ~ 250 °C |
| Part size limit | Medium | Large | Limited by autoclave chamber |
| Typical applications | Small marine parts, prototypes | Aerospace radomes, rotor blades, large boat hulls | Aircraft primary structures (wing skins, fuselage) |

---

**(b) Hoop Stress Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $P = 450$ kPa, $r = 0.4$ m, $t_{bag} = 2.5$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{hoop} &= \frac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot t_{bag}} \\
              &= \frac{(450 \times 10^3) \times 0.4}{2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-3})} \\
              &= \frac{180{,}000}{0.005} \\
              &= 3.6 \times 10^7 \ \text{Pa} \\
              &= \boxed{36 \ \text{MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Safety Assessment:**
Silicone rubber typically has an ultimate tensile strength of 7–10 MPa.

$$
\text{Safety Factor} = \frac{\sigma_{ult}}{\sigma_{hoop}} = \frac{8}{36} = 0.22
$$

**Conclusion:** A plain silicone bag at 2.5 mm thickness **cannot** safely withstand 450 kPa. The bag must be:
- Increased in thickness to ~ 12 mm (giving SF ≈ 1.0), OR
- Reinforced with fabric plies (e.g., nylon or polyester scrim embedded in the elastomer), OR
- Operated at lower pressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Forgetting to convert kPa to Pa (×10³) and mm to m (×10⁻³) — this single error costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Bag Formula:** Some students use the cylindrical vessel formula $P r / t$ instead of $P r / (2 t)$ for spherical bag — examiner deducts full mark.
> 3. **Safety Factor Reporting:** KTU expects the conclusion statement ("Bag cannot safely operate / requires reinforcement") — omitting the comment loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Pressure Bag Moulding**

- **Process family:** Bag moulding — uses a flexible membrane to apply pressure.
- **Driving force:** Compressed air at **300–500 kPa** (gauge).
- **Bag material:** Polyurethane, silicone, or natural rubber (latex).
- **Pressure principle:** Pascal's Law — uniform hydrostatic transmission via elastomer.
- **Key outcomes:** Voids < 1 %, V_f up to 0.55–0.65, better fibre–matrix bonding than hand lay-up.
- **Cure cycle:** 1–4 hours at 80–120 °C (epoxy), 25 °C (room-temp polyester).
- **Difference from vacuum bag:** Vacuum uses suction (ΔP ≈ 100 kPa); Pressure Bag uses positive air pressure (ΔP ≈ 400 kPa).
- **Difference from autoclave:** Autoclave uses an external pressure vessel (up to 1.5 MPa); Pressure Bag pressure is limited by bag burst strength.
- **Key formula:** $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c - \rho_r}{\rho_f - \rho_r}$
- **Key formula:** $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_r$
- **Key formula:** $t = \dfrac{n \cdot w_{ply}}{V_f \rho_f + (1 - V_f)\rho_r}$
- **Key formula:** $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot t_{bag}}$
- **Applications:** Aerospace radomes, marine hulls, wind-turbine blades, automotive panels, sports goods.
- **Advantages:** Low void, high V_f, uniform pressure, suitable for large parts, low-to-medium tooling cost.
- **Limitations:** Bag burst risk, pressure limited (~ 7 bar), not for very thick laminates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filament Winding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Filament Winding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Filament Winding** is an automated, open-mold composite fabrication process in which continuous reinforcing fibers (rovings or tows) — pre-impregnated with a thermosetting resin matrix or passed through a resin bath during winding — are wound under controlled tension onto a rotating mandrel of predetermined geometry in a precise, programmable geometric pattern. The process is governed by the relative motion between the fiber delivery (pay-out) eye and the rotating mandrel, yielding parts with exceptionally **high fiber volume fraction (typically 55–65%)** and superior specific strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Filament winding is a process in which continuous rovings of reinforcement are drawn through a resin bath and wound onto a rotating mandrel at a controlled angle and tension to manufacture hollow, axisymmetric composite structures.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine you are wrapping a **balloon with a long, sticky thread of honey-coated string** while someone else slowly turns the balloon. As the balloon spins, you move your hand back and forth at a steady pace. The thread automatically lays itself down in beautiful criss-crossing patterns. After several layers, the string hardens (curing), the balloon is deflated, and you are left with a strong, lightweight shell.

**Mapping the Analogy to the Engineering Process:**

| Analogy Element | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Balloon | Rotating mandrel (mold) |
| Sticky honey-thread | Resin-impregnated fiber roving |
| Your moving hand | Fiber delivery carriage/pay-out eye |
| Criss-cross pattern | Winding angle ($\pm\theta$) |
| Hardened shell | Cured composite laminate |

> [!NOTE]
> Filament winding is essentially a **"controlled yarn-winding process"** — the angle, speed, and tension are mathematically calculated, not random.

## 1.3 Classification of Filament Winding

There are **two principal classifications** based on the resin application method:

1. **Wet Winding:** Fibers pass through a **resin bath** just before being wound. The resin viscosity, bath temperature, and residence time are critical.
2. **Dry Winding (Prepreg):** Pre-impregnated, partially cured fiber tows (B-stage prepregs) are wound onto a heated mandrel. Provides superior quality control.

Based on **winding pattern**, the process is divided into:
- **Hoop (Circumferential) Winding** — fibers at $\theta \approx 90°$
- **Helical Winding** — fibers at $0° < \theta < 90°$
- **Polar (Dome) Winding** — used for closed-end pressure vessels

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU Exams:** Always mention the fiber material (E-glass, carbon, aramid) and resin system (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester) in process descriptions to fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Logic

### Stage 1: Mandrel Preparation
- A **mandrel** (steel, aluminum, sand-filled, or soluble plaster) is mounted on the winding machine's lathe.
- Surface is coated with a **release agent** (wax or PVA) to allow part removal.
- For removable mandrels, collapsible or sacrificial designs are used.

### Stage 2: Fiber Pay-out & Resin Impregnation
- Multiple roving spools are mounted on a **creel rack**.
- Fibers are guided through tensioning devices, a **resin bath** (in wet winding), and a **pay-out eye** (delivery carriage).
- Tension is maintained between **2–10 N per tow** (for E-glass) depending on tow count.

### Stage 3: Winding Operation
- The mandrel rotates at a programmed speed (N rpm).
- The carriage traverses axially at a synchronized rate.
- The **winding angle $\theta$** is governed by the ratio of traverse speed to rotational speed.

### Stage 4: Curing
- The wound part is cured either at room temperature or in an oven.
- For thermosets like epoxy, curing is done at **80–180°C** for 1–6 hours.
- Post-cure may be required for high-Tg systems.

### Stage 5: Mandrel Extraction
- Mandrel is removed (collapsing, dissolving, or pulling out axially).

## 2.2 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

| Engineering Question | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Why rotate the mandrel and not the fiber source?** | Rotation provides uniform fiber tension and consistent lay-down; the heavy mandrel acts as a stable inertial mass. |
| **Why control winding angle precisely?** | The angle determines whether the part is optimized for **hoop stress** ($\theta = 90°$) or **axial stress** ($\theta$ near $0°$); for **bi-axial stress** (e.g., pressure vessels), geodesic/isoclinic patterns are used. |
| **Why pre-tension the fibers?** | Pre-tension ensures **compaction**, removes slack, and provides the compressive stress needed to consolidate layers and reduce voids. |
| **Why use a release agent?** | The cured composite must be separable from the mandrel without damage. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Equations for KTU 2024 Examinations** — Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Winding Angle** | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{v_c}{\pi D N}$ | radians/° | $v_c$ = carriage speed, $D$ = mandrel diameter, $N$ = rotational speed |
| 2 | **Band Width ($b$)** | $b = \dfrac{\pi D}{\cos\theta}$ | mm/inch | Width of fiber band laid in one revolution |
| 3 | **Band Advance per Rev** | $a = \dfrac{v_c}{N}$ | mm | Linear axial advancement per mandrel rotation |
| 4 | **Carriage Speed** | $v_c = N \cdot a$ | mm/s | Synchronized with mandrel rotation |
| 5 | **Layer Thickness ($t$)** | $t = \dfrac{\rho_a}{V_f \cdot \rho_f} \cdot N_{plies}$ | mm | $N_{plies}$ = number of plies |
| 6 | **Fiber Volume Fraction** | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_r / \rho_r}$ | dimensionless | $W_f, W_r$ = weight fractions; $\rho_f, \rho_r$ = densities |
| 7 | **Helical Winding Equation** | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{w}{\pi D \cdot f_{rot}}$ | radians | $\alpha$ = helix angle, $w$ = winding width, $f_{rot}$ = rotational frequency |
| 8 | **Hoop Stress (Thin Wall)** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | MPa | Internal pressure $p$, diameter $D$, thickness $t$ |
| 9 | **Axial Stress (Thin Wall)** | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | MPa | Half of hoop stress for closed-end vessels |
| 10 | **Net Winding Tension** | $T_{net} = \mu \cdot T_{prev}$ | N | Friction-based tension decay through resin bath ($\mu < 1$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Notation Reminder:** Always use `\vert x \vert` instead of $\vert x \vert$ in your answer scripts to avoid markdown table breaking.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Filament winding is the **workhorse process** for manufacturing:
- **Rocket motor casings** (solid propellant) — carbon/epoxy, high burst-pressure ratings
- **CNG & LPG storage tanks** (Type IV composite tanks)
- **Chemical storage vessels & pipes** — corrosion-resistant FRP
- **Wind turbine blade root sections**
- **Aerospace pressure vessels** for satellite propulsion
- **Drive shafts & roll tubes** for automotive and paper industries

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection (KTU Expectation):** Whenever you describe filament winding, link it to a real product — examiners reward "applied thinking."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Winding Angle ($\theta$) as a Function of Process Parameters

**Problem Setup:** A mandrel of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ revolutions per second. The fiber delivery carriage translates axially at velocity $v_c$. Determine the relationship between the winding angle and the process variables.

### Step 1 — Define the Winding Angle

The winding angle $\theta$ is the angle that the fiber makes with the **generatrix (longitudinal axis)** of the mandrel at the point of contact.

### Step 2 — Relate Axial Displacement to Angular Displacement

In one full rotation of the mandrel, the carriage advances an axial distance equal to the **band advance**, $a$:

$$a = \frac{v_c}{N}$$

### Step 3 — Unroll the Cylindrical Surface

When the cylindrical surface of the mandrel is "unrolled" into a flat plane, the fiber trajectory becomes a straight line forming angle $\theta$ with the horizontal axis.

The **horizontal distance** traversed in one revolution equals the **circumference** of the mandrel:

$$\text{Circumference} = \pi D$$

The **vertical distance** traversed in one revolution equals the band advance $a$.

### Step 4 — Apply Trigonometry

In the unrolled geometry, the tangent of the winding angle is the ratio of vertical to horizontal displacement:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{a}{\pi D}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Band Advance Expression

Substituting $a = v_c / N$:

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \frac{v_c}{\pi D N}}$$

### Step 6 — Verification of Boundary Conditions

- **Hoop Winding Limit:** When $v_c \to 0$, $\tan\theta \to 0$, so $\theta \to 90°$ ✓ (purely circumferential)
- **Helical Winding:** For $0 < v_c < \infty$, $0° < \theta < 90°$ ✓
- **Units Check:** $[v_c] = $ mm/s, $[D] = $ mm, $[N] = $ 1/s → $\tan\theta$ is dimensionless ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Band Width ($b$) on a Cylindrical Mandrel

**Problem Setup:** Determine the width of the fiber band laid down on a cylindrical mandrel of diameter $D$ when the winding angle is $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Visualize the Geometry

In the unrolled (developed) surface, the fiber band forms a parallelogram. The band width $b$ is the **perpendicular distance** between two adjacent fiber paths.

### Step 2 — Establish the Geometric Relationship

In the unrolled view, one full revolution of the mandrel corresponds to a horizontal distance of $\pi D$ (the circumference). The fiber path, lying at angle $\theta$ to the axis, covers a length $L$ per revolution. The projection of this length onto the horizontal axis gives:

$$\pi D = L \cos\theta$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Path Length $L$

$$L = \frac{\pi D}{\cos\theta}$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Band Width

When a single tow (or band of tows) of width $w_0$ is laid on the cylinder, the apparent width $b$ on the cylindrical surface is related to $w_0$ through the same angle:

$$\boxed{b = \frac{\pi D}{\cos\theta}}$$

> This is the **width of one complete helical pass** on the mandrel surface.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hoop and Axial Stress in a Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel

**Problem Setup:** A filament-wound cylindrical pressure vessel of diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$ is subjected to internal pressure $p$. Determine the hoop and axial stresses.

### Step 1 — Equilibrium of a Half-Cylinder (Hoop Stress)

Consider a longitudinal section of the cylinder. The internal pressure acts on the projected area $D \cdot L$ with force $pDL$. This is balanced by the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting on the two cut sections of total area $2tL$:

$$p D L = 2 \sigma_h t L$$

Solving for $\sigma_h$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}}$$

### Step 2 — Equilibrium of an End Cap (Axial Stress)

The internal pressure acting on the end cap area $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ is balanced by the axial stress $\sigma_a$ acting on the cylindrical cross-section $\pi D t$:

$$p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_a \cdot \pi D t$$

Solving for $\sigma_a$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{\sigma_h}{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Engineering Implication for Winding Design

Because $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_a$, the winding pattern must be **biased toward hoop direction** in a pressure vessel. This is achieved by:
- Including **multiple hoop ($\theta = 90°$) plies** for hoop strength.
- Adding **helical plies at $\pm 55°$** to balance axial strength (the classical "55° geodesic pattern" used in rocket motors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** For an isotropic filament pattern at angle $\theta$, the optimum for a closed-end pressure vessel (minimum weight) is given by the netting analysis result: $\tan^2\theta = 2$, i.e., $\theta = 54.7°$. This is a **very common KTU 2-mark or 7-mark question**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A filament-wound pipe has a mandrel diameter of **300 mm**, a winding angle of **55°**, and a carriage speed of **15 mm/s**. Determine:
(i) The rotational speed of the mandrel.
(ii) The band width per revolution.

**Solution:**

**Part (i): Rotational Speed**

From the winding angle equation:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v_c}{\pi D N}$$

Solving for $N$:

$$N = \frac{v_c}{\pi D \tan\theta} = \frac{15}{\pi \times 300 \times \tan 55°}$$

$$\tan 55° \approx 1.4281$$

$$N = \frac{15}{\pi \times 300 \times 1.4281} = \frac{15}{1345.6} \approx 0.01115 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 0.669 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Part (ii): Band Width**

$$b = \frac{\pi D}{\cos\theta} = \frac{\pi \times 300}{\cos 55°} = \frac{942.48}{0.5736}$$

$$\boxed{b \approx 1643 \text{ mm}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Process Selection

| Process Variant | Best Suited For | Limitations |
|---|---|---|
| **Wet Winding** | Large structures, low cost, on-site fabrication | Resin viscosity sensitivity, VOC emissions |
| **Dry Winding (Prepreg)** | Aerospace-grade parts, tight tolerance | Higher cost, refrigerated storage |
| **Helical Winding** | Pressure vessels, pipes | Complex programming, slow |
| **Hoop Winding** | High hoop stress, large diameter tanks | Low axial strength |
| **Polar Winding** | Closed-end spherical/dome shapes | Specialized equipment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define filament winding. List any four advantages of the process over hand lay-up.**

**Model Answer:**
Filament winding is an automated composite manufacturing process in which continuous fiber rovings, impregnated with a thermosetting resin, are wound under controlled tension onto a rotating mandrel in a predetermined pattern.

**Advantages over hand lay-up:**
1. **Higher fiber volume fraction (up to 65%)** — superior strength-to-weight ratio.
2. **Better uniformity** — automated, repeatable fiber placement.
3. **Faster production** for axisymmetric parts.
4. **Lower labor cost** and reduced human error.
5. Better control over winding angle and tension.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between wet winding and dry (prepreg) winding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Wet Winding | Dry (Prepreg) Winding |
|---|---|---|
| Resin application | At the time of winding (resin bath) | Pre-applied; fibers are B-stage pre-impregnated |
| Resin content control | Operator-dependent | Factory-controlled, uniform |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (cold storage needed) |
| Quality | Variable | High, consistent |
| Applications | Pipes, tanks, large structures | Aerospace, high-performance parts |
| Storage | Roving & resin stored separately | Requires refrigeration |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Helical Winding Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the relation between **winding angle ($\theta$)** and the process parameters: carriage speed ($v_c$), mandrel diameter ($D$), and rotational speed ($N$). State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A filament-wound pressure vessel of inner diameter **500 mm** and wall thickness **8 mm** is subjected to an internal pressure of **5 MPa**. Calculate the **hoop stress** and **axial stress** in the wall. If the vessel uses an optimum winding pattern for a closed-end pressure vessel, what is the theoretical winding angle? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Winding Angle Equation**

*Assumptions:*
1. The mandrel rotates at a constant speed $N$ (rev/s).
2. The carriage moves at a constant axial speed $v_c$ (mm/s).
3. Fiber slippage on the mandrel is negligible.
4. The fiber tension is uniform throughout the winding.

*Derivation:*

In one full revolution of the mandrel, the carriage moves an axial distance $a$ (band advance):

$$a = \frac{v_c}{N} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Unroll the cylindrical surface into a flat plane. The fiber path forms a straight line at angle $\theta$ to the longitudinal (axial) direction. In this unrolled view:
- Horizontal projection (circumference) $= \pi D$
- Vertical projection (band advance) $= a$

Applying trigonometry:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{a}{\pi D} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Substituting (i) into (ii):

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \frac{v_c}{\pi D N}}$$

---

**(b) Stress Calculation and Optimum Winding Angle**

Given: $D = 500$ mm $= 0.5$ m; $t = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m; $p = 5$ MPa.

**Hoop Stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{5 \times 0.5}{2 \times 0.008} = \frac{2.5}{0.016}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = 156.25 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Axial Stress:**

$$\sigma_a = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{156.25}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = 78.125 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Optimum Winding Angle (Netting Analysis for Closed-End Vessel):**

For balanced strength where the fiber stress from both hoop and axial loads is equal:

$$\tan^2\theta = 2$$

$$\theta = \arctan(\sqrt{2}) = \arctan(1.4142)$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 54.74°}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Lose 2 Marks):** Students often confuse **inside diameter** with **mean diameter** in pressure vessel stress calculations. For thin-walled vessels ($D/t > 20$), the inner, mean, and outer diameters are nearly equal, but always **state which one you are using** in the answer. Also, units must be consistent (convert mm to m before substitution when using MPa).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Winding Pattern & Process Design**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional sketch, explain the **stacking sequence** used in a typical filament-wound rocket motor casing. Justify the choice of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **wet filament winding** machine winds a **200 mm diameter** mandrel with a fiber band width of **5 mm** per tow. The carriage speed is **12 mm/s** and the desired winding angle is **45°**. Calculate: (i) The required mandrel rotational speed. (ii) The number of tows needed per band for full coverage. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stacking Sequence of a Rocket Motor Casing**

A typical rocket motor casing (closed-end pressure vessel) uses a **balanced, symmetric lay-up** combining helical and hoop plies:

| Layer (from inside) | Angle | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Gelcoat / inner liner | — | Sealing, chemical resistance |
| Hoop ply | +90° | Resists dominant **hoop stress** ($\sigma_h = 2\sigma_a$) |
| Hoop ply | +90° | Additional hoop reinforcement |
| Helical ply | +55° | Carries combined load at geodesic angle |
| Helical ply | -55° | Symmetric counter-helix (no twisting moment) |
| Helical ply | +55° | Continues balanced pattern |
| Helical ply | -55° | Mirror layer |
| Hoop ply | +90° | Outer hoop reinforcement |
| Hoop ply | +90° | Damage tolerance, outer protection |

**Justification:**
- **Hoop plies ($\theta = 90°$):** Since $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_a$, hoop-direction stress is critical and requires maximum reinforcement.
- **Helical plies at $\pm 55°$:** Optimum for balanced biaxial loading (close to the theoretical 54.74°). The + and - angles cancel each other's in-plane shear, preventing the part from unscrewing under internal pressure.
- **Symmetric stacking:** Avoids coupling between bending and extension (B-matrix = 0).

---

**(b) Process Parameter Calculations**

Given: $D = 200$ mm; $v_c = 12$ mm/s; $\theta = 45°$; tow width $w_0 = 5$ mm.

**(i) Rotational Speed:**

From the winding angle equation:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v_c}{\pi D N}$$

$$N = \frac{v_c}{\pi D \tan\theta} = \frac{12}{\pi \times 200 \times \tan 45°}$$

Since $\tan 45° = 1$:

$$N = \frac{12}{\pi \times 200 \times 1} = \frac{12}{628.32}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 0.0191 \text{ rev/s} \approx 1.146 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Number of Tows per Band:**

The total band width on the mandrel surface for a single helical pass at angle $\theta$ is:

$$b = \frac{\pi D}{\cos\theta} = \frac{\pi \times 200}{\cos 45°} = \frac{628.32}{0.7071} \approx 888.6 \text{ mm}$$

Each tow covers a width $w_0 = 5$ mm. Number of tows required:

$$n = \frac{b}{w_0} = \frac{888.6}{5} \approx 177.7$$

Rounding up:

$$\boxed{n = 178 \text{ tows per helical pass}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark Losses):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Mixing mm with m, or rpm with rev/s, is a 1-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 2. **Misidentifying the winding angle** — Some textbooks use $\alpha$ measured from the **axis**, others from the **circumference**. Always define your angle at the start of the answer.
> 3. **Skipping assumptions** in derivations — Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if assumptions (e.g., no slip, constant tension) are not stated.
> 4. **Forgetting symmetric stacking** in lay-up questions — An asymmetric lay-up introduces bending-twisting coupling, which is a major design flaw.
> 5. **Ignoring the friction-based tension decay** in wet winding through the resin bath — real tensions are always less than the input tension.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024**

- ✅ Filament winding = **automated, open-mold, continuous-fiber process** for axisymmetric parts.
- ✅ Two main types: **Wet (resin bath)** and **Dry (prepreg)**.
- ✅ Three main patterns: **Hoop, Helical, Polar.**
- ✅ Master formula: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{v_c}{\pi D N}$
- ✅ Band width: $b = \dfrac{\pi D}{\cos\theta}$
- ✅ Band advance per revolution: $a = \dfrac{v_c}{N}$
- ✅ Hoop stress (thin wall): $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$
- ✅ Axial stress (thin wall): $\sigma_a = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$
- ✅ Optimum winding angle for closed-end pressure vessel: $\theta = 54.74°$ (from $\tan^2\theta = 2$).
- ✅ Typical $V_f$ = **55–65%**, much higher than hand lay-up.
- ✅ Common fibers: **E-glass, carbon, aramid**; Common resins: **polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy**.
- ✅ Key advantages: **High strength, low weight, repeatable, automation-friendly**.
- ✅ Key limitations: **Limited to convex axisymmetric shapes; capital-intensive equipment**.
- ✅ Real products: **rocket motor casings, CNG tanks, chemical pipes, drive shafts**.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions, units, and angle definitions** in derivations.
- ✅ Winding tension typically **2–10 N/tow**; too high → fiber damage, too low → poor compaction.

> **End of Module 4 — Filament Winding Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — PBPOT604: Polymer Blends & Composites*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resin Transfer Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Resin Transfer Moulding # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM)** is a closed-mould, low-pressure composite manufacturing process in which a dry, pre-shaped fibre reinforcement (preform) is placed inside a matched metallic mould cavity, the mould is closed and clamped, and a catalysed, low-viscosity thermosetting resin is then injected under pressure (typically **0.1 – 0.4 MPa** for standard RTM and up to **1 atm pressure differential** in VARTM) until the preform is fully impregnated. The resin is subsequently cured *in-situ* under controlled temperature, producing a near-net-shape, high-fibre-volume-fraction composite part with excellent surface finish on both sides. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"RTM is a closed-mould liquid composite moulding (LCM) process where a preform of dry fibres is impregnated by a reactive liquid resin under a positive pressure gradient, governed by Darcy's law of flow through a porous medium."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Through a Sponge" Model Imagine a tightly packed kitchen sponge sitting inside a sealed plastic box: | RTM Element | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | Closed matched mould | The sealed plastic box | | Dry fibre preform | The kitchen sponge | | Low-viscosity resin | Warm coffee (free-flowing liquid) | | Injection pump | The kettle pouring coffee | | Bleeder / vent | The small hole on the lid | | Cure stage | The sponge + coffee setting into a solid cake | You **pour** liquid resin in one side, and because the mould is **sealed**, the liquid has only one way to go — *through* the fibre network — driving out the air through the vents. Once full, heat triggers polymerisation, locking the fibre architecture permanently. This is essentially what RTM does, but with **engineering precision** in fibre alignment, void control, and dimensional repeatability. > [!NOTE] > **Why RTM matters to an engineer:** It bridges the gap between hand lay-up (cheap, low quality) and autoclave processing (expensive, aerospace-grade). It produces aerospace-quality laminates at automotive cost. ## 1.3 Salient Features of RTM (High-Yield Board Points) - **Mould Type:** Closed, matched, rigid (steel/aluminium/composite tooling). - **Pressure Regime:** Low pressure (typically **< 1 MPa** gauge). - **Resin Viscosity Requirement:** **< 500 mPa·s** (preferably **< 200 mPa·s**). - **Fibre Volume Fraction ($V_f$):** Achievable up to **55 – 60 %** (vs. ~30 % in hand lay-up). - **Surface Finish:** Both moulded surfaces are smooth — Class-A finish possible. - **Emissions:** Closed mould → **< 5 % styrene emission** (BENZENE-free compliance). - **Production Rate:** Moderate (cycle time **30 – 90 min** depending on part thickness). - **Part Size Capability:** Small (aero brackets) to large (bus body panels, wind-turbine blade root joints). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Process Flow Logic — The "Why & How" Behind Each Stage The RTM process is governed by a strict sequence of seven physical stages. Each stage is a potential source of defect (voids, dry spots, race-tracking) — therefore, **KTU examiners frequently ask stage-wise defect analysis**. ### Stage 1 — Preform Preparation - **Why:** Dry fibres cannot be injected through; they must already be in the final 3D shape. - **How:** Fibres are tailored, stitched, bindered, or thermoformed into a near-net-shape preform. - **KTU Trap Question:** *"Why is a binder used in preforms?"* → To hold fibre shape during mould closure and prevent fibre washout. ### Stage 2 — Preform Loading - Preform is placed in the lower (female) mould half. - **Critical:** Preform must be dry (no moisture); moisture causes **voids** during cure. ### Stage 3 — Mould Closure & Clamping - The mould is closed using hydraulic or mechanical clamps. - **Tonnage Required:** typically **0.7 – 1.4 MPa** of projected area. - **Why:** Prevents resin flash (leakage) and maintains cavity thickness. ### Stage 4 — Resin Injection (Impregnation Phase) - Low-viscosity, catalysed resin is injected through a **gate** (inlet port). - Pressure drives resin through the porous preform. - Air is displaced toward the **vent(s)**, exiting through a vent line. - **Flow-front tracking** is critical — non-uniform flow creates dry spots. ### Stage 5 — Fill Completion & Saturation - Once resin reaches the vent, the mould is **filled to a calculated percentage (typically 95 – 100 %)**. - The vent is closed to build back-pressure. ### Stage 6 — Cure (In-Situ Polymerisation) - Mould is heated (typically **80 – 180 °C** depending on resin system). - Heat triggers the crosslinking reaction (exothermic). - Cure time is dictated by resin chemistry, part thickness, and exotherm management. ### Stage 7 — De-moulding & Trimming - Part is de-moulded after sufficient green strength develops. - Sprues, gates, and vents are trimmed (post-processing). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variants of RTM — Must Memorise for KTU 2024 Board Exam:** > 1. **VARTM (Vacuum-Assisted RTM):** Uses vacuum instead of positive pressure; one-sided mould; used for very large parts. > 2. **SCRIMP (Seemann Composites Resin Infusion Molding Process):** Uses a flow media to accelerate resin flow. > 3. **HP-RTM (High-Pressure RTM):** Injection pressure > 10 MPa; cycle times < 5 min; used in automotive (BMW 7-series CFRP roof). ## 2.2 The Governing Physics — Darcy's Law of Flow Through a Porous Medium The injection flow in RTM is **NOT** a simple pipe flow. It is flow through a **porous medium** (the fibre preform), and the governing equation is **Darcy's Law (1856)**. > [!NOTE] > **Darcy's Law (1-D, Compressible-Incompressible Forms):** > Darcy's law states that the superficial velocity $\vec{v}$ of a fluid through a porous medium is proportional to the pressure gradient and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity. For a Newtonian resin in an incompressible preform (1-D flow along x-axis): $$\vec{v} = -\frac{K}{\mu} \nabla P$$ Where: - $\vec{v}$ = Superficial (Darcy) velocity of the resin front (m/s) - $K$ = **Permeability** of the preform (m²) — function of fibre architecture and $V_f$ - $\mu$ = **Dynamic viscosity** of the resin (Pa·s) - $\nabla P$ = Pressure gradient (Pa/m) > The **negative sign** indicates flow in the direction of decreasing pressure (high pressure at inlet → low pressure at vent). ## 2.3 Kozeny-Carman Equation — Permeability vs. Fibre Architecture The permeability $K$ is not a constant; it depends strongly on the fibre volume fraction $V_f$ and the preform architecture (woven, unidirectional, stitched, etc.). The most widely used correlation is the **Kozeny-Carman equation**: $$K = \frac{r_f^2}{4 \kappa} \cdot \frac{(1 - V_f)^3}{V_f^2}$$ Where: - $r_f$ = Fibre radius (m) - $\kappa$ = Kozeny-Carman constant (≈ **4.5 – 5.0** for unidirectional, ≈ **11** for random mats) - $V_f$ = Fibre volume fraction (dimensionless) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** As $V_f$ increases (denser preform), $K$ decreases **drastically** (cubic dependence on $(1-V_f)$). This is why highly loaded preforms are very hard to impregnate and require either higher injection pressure or very low-viscosity resin. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Equation / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables & Units** | **Application** | |:-:|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Darcy's Law (1-D) | $v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | $v$ [m/s], $K$ [m²], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $P$ [Pa] | Governing resin flow equation | | 2 | Darcy's Law (3-D) | $\vec{v} = -\dfrac{\mathbf{K}}{\mu} \nabla P$ | $\mathbf{K}$ = permeability tensor | Anisotropic preforms | | 3 | Kozeny-Carman | $K = \dfrac{r_f^2}{4\kappa}\cdot\dfrac{(1-V_f)^3}{V_f^2}$ | $r_f$ [m], $V_f$ [–], $\kappa$ [–] | Permeability prediction | | 4 | Linear Flow-Front Position | $x_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K \Delta P \, t}{\mu \phi}}$ | $x_f$ [m], $\Delta P$ [Pa], $t$ [s], $\phi$ = porosity [–] | Time to fill a rectangular mould | | 5 | Radial Flow-Front Position | $r_f^2 = \dfrac{2 K \Delta P \, t}{\mu \phi \ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | $r_i, r_o$ = inner, outer radii [m] | Disc-shaped part fill time | | 6 | Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m \, w_f}{\rho_f \, h}$ | $w_f$ = areal weight, $h$ = thickness, $\rho_f$ [kg/m³] | Preform design | | 7 | Capillary Number | $Ca = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}$ | $\gamma_{LV}$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle | Wetting vs. viscous regime | | 8 | Permeability Ratio | $\alpha = K_1 / K_2$ | $K_1, K_2$ = principal permeabilities | Race-tracking severity | > [!WARNING] > **Absolute Value Notation in Exam:** In KTU answer sheets, write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value (use `\vert` in LaTeX). When writing inline fractions in a sentence, NEVER use the vertical pipe symbol as it breaks markdown tables. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Wing ribs, fuselage skins, helicopter rotor blades (Airbus A350, Boeing 787 use RTM/VARTM extensively). - **Automotive:** BMW i3, BMW 7-series CFRP roof panel (HP-RTM, cycle time **< 5 min**). - **Wind Energy:** Root joints, spar caps, hub components. - **Marine:** Boat hulls, decks, bulkheads. - **Civil Engineering:** Bridge decks, retrofitting wraps. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Flow-Front Position Equation (1-D Rectangular Cavity) This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Polymer Processing papers. The following is a complete, line-by-line derivation that a student can reproduce verbatim in an exam. ### Step 1: Set Up the Control Volume Consider a 1-D rectangular RTM cavity of length $L$, width $w$, and constant cavity thickness $h$. The fibre preform occupies the entire cavity, with porosity $\phi$ (i.e., the volume fraction of the cavity that can be filled with resin). The volume of the cavity up to the flow front position $x_f$ is: $$V_{cavity}(x_f) = w \cdot h \cdot x_f$$ ### Step 2: Volume of Resin Injected The volume of resin that has entered the mould is the porosity-multiplied cavity volume up to $x_f$: $$V_{resin} = \phi \cdot w \cdot h \cdot x_f$$ ### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Mass (Continuity) The mass flow rate of resin entering the cavity at the inlet must equal the rate of cavity filling: $$Q_{in} = \frac{dV_{resin}}{dt}$$ Where $Q_{in} = A_{in} \cdot v$ is the volumetric flow rate, and $A_{in} = w \cdot h$ is the inlet cross-section. Therefore: $$w \cdot h \cdot v = \phi \cdot w \cdot h \cdot \frac{dx_f}{dt}$$ Simplify (cancelling $w \cdot h$): $$v = \phi \cdot \frac{dx_f}{dt} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$ > **Logic Note:** The factor $\phi$ appears because the resin is only filling the *void* space, not the fibres themselves. ### Step 4: Substitute Darcy's Law From Darcy's law, the superficial velocity is: $$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$ For a 1-D linear flow from inlet ($P = P_{in}$) to flow front ($P = P_{vent}$), the pressure gradient is uniform along the preform length $x_f$: $$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{\Delta P}{x_f} = -\frac{P_{in} - P_{vent}}{x_f}$$ Substituting into Darcy's law: $$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \left( -\frac{\Delta P}{x_f} \right) = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot x_f}$$ ### Step 5: Equate and Rearrange Equating the two expressions for $v$ from Equation 1 and Step 4: $$\phi \cdot \frac{dx_f}{dt} = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot x_f}$$ Rearrange to separate variables: $$x_f \cdot dx_f = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \phi} \cdot dt$$ ### Step 6: Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t$ (Initial Condition: $x_f = 0$ at $t = 0$) $$\int_{0}^{x_f} x_f \, dx_f = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \phi} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ Evaluate the integrals: $$\frac{x_f^2}{2} = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \phi} \cdot t$$ ### Step 7: Solve for the Flow-Front Position $$x_f^2 = \frac{2 K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \phi} \cdot t$$ $$\boxed{\,x_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}{\mu \cdot \phi}}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** The flow front moves as the **square root of time** ($x_f \propto \sqrt{t}$). This is a classic signature of Darcy flow — the same $t^{1/2}$ law seen in heat conduction (Fourier) and diffusion (Fick). The fill time to reach mould length $L$ is: > $$t_{fill} = \frac{\mu \phi L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fill Time for a Disc-Shaped Cavity (Radial Flow) For a circular cavity with central inlet (radius $r_i$) and peripheral vent (radius $r_o$), the derivation is analogous but uses cylindrical coordinates. ### Step 1: Apply Continuity in a Cylindrical Shell The volumetric flow rate through a cylindrical surface at radius $r$ with thickness $h$ is: $$Q(r) = 2\pi r h \cdot v(r)$$ ### Step 2: Substitute Darcy's Law in Radial Form $$v(r) = \frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dr}$$ The pressure drop occurs from $r_i$ to $r_o$: $$\frac{dP}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{r \ln(r_o/r_i)} \quad \text{(from log-pressure distribution in radial flow)}$$ ### Step 3: Equate with Volume Filling Rate Setting $\frac{d}{dt}[\phi \cdot \pi h (r_f^2 - r_i^2)] = Q(r_f)$ and integrating, we obtain: $$\boxed{\,r_f^2 - r_i^2 = \frac{2 K \Delta P}{\mu \phi \ln(r_o/r_i)} \cdot t\,}$$ For the case $r_i \ll r_o$ (small inlet), $r_i$ is negligible: $$r_f^2 \approx \frac{2 K \Delta P \cdot t}{\mu \phi \ln(r_o/r_i)}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Standard) **Problem:** A rectangular RTM cavity of length **$L = 0.5$ m** and porosity **$\phi = 0.45$** is to be filled with an epoxy resin of viscosity **$\mu = 0.3$ Pa·s**. The preform permeability is **$K = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²**. The applied pressure differential is **$\Delta P = 200$ kPa = $2 \times 10^5$ Pa**. Calculate: (a) The fill time. (b) The flow-front position at $t = 300$ s. ### Solution (a) — Fill Time Using the linear fill time equation: $$t_{fill} = \frac{\mu \phi L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$$ Substitute values: $$t_{fill} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.45 \times (0.5)^2}{2 \times (5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (2 \times 10^5)}$$ $$t_{fill} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.45 \times 0.25}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$t_{fill} = \frac{0.03375}{0.002} = 16.875 \text{ s}$$ ### Solution (b) — Flow-Front Position at $t = 300$ s Since $t = 300$ s $\gg t_{fill} = 16.875$ s, the mould is fully filled before 300 s. The flow-front has reached the vent at $x_f = 0.5$ m. If the question had used $t = 5$ s (which is **< fill time**), then: $$x_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 5}{0.3 \times 0.45}}$$ $$x_f = \sqrt{\frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{0.135}} = \sqrt{0.0741} \approx 0.272 \text{ m}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Defect Type This matrix is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU board exams (asked as "Discuss the defects in RTM and the process parameters that influence them"). | **Defect** | **Root Cause** | **Process Parameter to Control** | **Mitigation** | |---|---|---|---| | **Voids (dry spots)** | Air entrapment; incomplete impregnation | Injection pressure; resin viscosity; preform permeability | Increase $\Delta P$; preheat resin; use vacuum (VARTM) | | **Race-tracking** | Resin flows along mould walls (low-resistance path) | Mould closure force; seal design | Improved mould seals; edge bleeders | | **Fibre Wash-out** | High resin velocity displaces fibres | Inlet velocity; viscosity | Lower inlet flow rate; use binder in preform | | **Warpage** | Non-uniform cure; residual stress | Cure ramp rate; cooling uniformity | Controlled cure cycle; balanced heating | | **Dry Preform Regions** | Premature gelation | Pot life; mould temperature | Pre-chilled resin; lower mould temp | | **Voids from Volatiles** | Moisture in preform | Drying of preform | Pre-dry fibres at **100 °C / 2 h** | | **Sink Marks** | Localised shrinkage | Cure uniformity; filler loading | Uniform heating; lower exotherm resin | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: RTM Process Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fibre Material Roll
Glass / Carbon / Aramid] --> B[Preform Preparation
Cutting and Stitching] B --> C[Preform with Binder
Vf 50 to 55 percent] C --> D[Lower Mould Half
Female Cavity] D --> E[Preform Loading
Manual or Robotic] E --> F[Upper Mould Half
Male Cavity] F --> G[Mould Closure
Hydraulic Clamping 0.7 to 1.4 MPa] G --> H{Injection Phase} H --> I[Resin Mixing Unit
Resin plus Hardener] I --> J[Injection Pump
0.1 to 0.4 MPa] J --> K[Resin Inlet Port
Gate] K --> L[Preform Impregnation
Darcy Flow Through Porous Media] L --> M[Flow Front Tracking
Sensor Array] M --> N[Vent and Vacuum Line
Air and Excess Resin Exit] N --> O[Fill Complete Signal
95 to 100 percent Saturation] O --> P[Cure Phase
Heat 80 to 180 degree C] P --> Q[Exotherm Management
Thermocouple Monitoring] Q --> R[De moulding
Part Extraction] R --> S[Trimming and Post Cure
Deflashing] S --> T[Final Composite Part
Class A Surface Finish] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Pressure-Velocity Distribution in RTM Cavity ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inlet_High_Pressure A[Inlet Pressure Pin equals 0.3 MPa] --> B[High Velocity Region] end subgraph Mid_Cavity_Flow_Front B --> C[Flow Front Position xf] C --> D[Velocity Decreases with sqrt t] end subgraph Vent_Low_Pressure D --> E[Vent Pressure Pvent equals 0.1 MPa] end A --- F[Delta P equals 0.2 MPa
Driving Force] F --- E ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: RTM vs. VARTM vs. HP-RTM ```mermaid flowchart TD RTM[Standard RTM
Pressure 0.1 to 0.4 MPa
Cycle 30 to 90 min
Part Size Small to Medium] --> A VARTM[VARTM Vacuum Assisted
Pressure equals 1 atm vacuum
Cycle 60 to 180 min
Part Size Very Large Wind Blades] --> B HPRTM[HP RTM High Pressure
Pressure greater than 10 MPa
Cycle less than 5 min
Automotive Mass Production] --> C A[Matched Two Sided Rigid Mould] --> D[Common Feature Closed Mould Low VOC Emission] B --> D C --> D ``` ## 4.4 Process Parameter Window (Engineering Schematic) | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Standard RTM Range** | **VARTM Range** | **HP-RTM Range** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Injection Pressure | $\Delta P$ | **0.1 – 0.4 MPa** | ~**0.1 MPa (vacuum)** | **> 10 MPa** | | Resin Viscosity | $\mu$ | **100 – 500 mPa·s** | **100 – 300 mPa·s** | **< 100 mPa·s** | | Mould Temperature | $T_{mould}$ | **Room – 60 °C** | **Room – 50 °C** | **100 – 140 °C** | | Cure Temperature | $T_{cure}$ | **80 – 180 °C** | **Ambient / Post-cure** | **120 – 160 °C** | | Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_f$ | **40 – 55 %** | **45 – 60 %** | **50 – 60 %** | | Permeability | $K$ | $10^{-9}$ – $10^{-11}$ m² | $10^{-9}$ – $10^{-11}$ m² | $10^{-10}$ – $10^{-12}$ m² | | Cycle Time | $t_{cycle}$ | **30 – 90 min** | **60 – 180 min** | **< 5 min** | > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping:** Since RTM involves physical mould geometries, the Mermaid diagrams above represent the **process flow** and **pressure distribution** rather than literal hardware schematics. The full hardware schematic includes: resin reservoir, metering pump, mixing head, heated resin lines, mould with integrated heaters, vacuum pump (VARTM), thermocouples, pressure transducers, and flow-front sensors. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM). List any four advantages of RTM over hand lay-up.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Resin Transfer Moulding is a closed-mould liquid composite moulding process in which a dry fibre preform is placed in a matched mould and impregnated with a low-viscosity thermoset resin under pressure, followed by in-situ curing. **Advantages over hand lay-up (2 Marks — any four):** 1. Higher fibre volume fraction achievable (**up to 55 – 60 %** vs. ~30 % in hand lay-up). 2. Better surface finish on both sides (Class-A finish possible). 3. Lower volatile emissions (closed mould, **< 5 % styrene**). 4. Better dimensional accuracy and repeatability. 5. Higher mechanical properties and lower void content. 6. Lower labour cost at production volumes. --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State Darcy's Law as applied to RTM and explain the significance of the permeability term $K$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Darcy's Law (1 Mark):** $$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}$$ Where $v$ = superficial velocity, $K$ = permeability, $\mu$ = resin viscosity, $dP/dx$ = pressure gradient. **Significance of $K$ (2 Marks):** - $K$ is a **geometric property of the preform** that quantifies the ease with which resin can flow through the fibre bed. - It depends on the **fibre architecture** (woven, unidirectional, random mat, stitched) and the **fibre volume fraction** $V_f$. - Higher $K$ → faster fill, lower injection pressure required; lower $K$ → difficult impregnation, higher void risk. - Typical values: $K \sim 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-11}$ m² for glass-fibre reinforcements. --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always draw the mould schematic** — students lose 2 marks for skipping diagrams in RTM questions. > 2. **Always state assumptions** (1-D flow, incompressible resin, rigid mould, isothermal) before any derivation. > 3. **Units MUST be SI throughout** — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per unit mismatch. > 4. **Mention $V_f$ and $\phi$ separately** — $\phi$ is porosity (void fraction available for resin), $V_f$ is fibre volume fraction. Confusing them is a common 1-mark penalty. > 5. **In numerical problems, show the substitution step clearly** — even a 1-line "Substituting values" gets partial credit. --- ### Question A: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Describe the Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) process with the help of a neat schematic. Explain the functions of (i) the injection gate, (ii) the vent, and (iii) the bleeder. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the linear flow-front position equation for an RTM cavity. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — RTM Process Description **Process Steps (5 Marks):** 1. **Preform Preparation:** Dry fibres (woven roving, mat, unidirectional cloth) are cut, layered, and stitched or bindered into a 3D preform matching the final part geometry. 2. **Mould Loading:** The preform is placed in the lower (female) half of a matched metallic mould. Mould release agent is applied. 3. **Mould Closure:** The upper (male) mould half is closed and clamped hydraulically at **0.7 – 1.4 MPa** projected area pressure to prevent flash. 4. **Resin Injection:** A catalysed, low-viscosity thermoset resin (epoxy, vinyl ester, polyester) is injected through the **inlet gate** under pressure (**0.1 – 0.4 MPa**). The resin flows through the porous preform, displacing air. 5. **Fill & Vent:** Resin reaches the **vent** at the mould periphery; air and excess resin exit through the vent line. A **bleeder** (porous medium, e.g., peel-ply + breather fabric) absorbs excess resin and ensures air removal. 6. **Cure:** The mould is heated (**80 – 180 °C**) to trigger crosslinking. Cure is monitored via thermocouples to control exotherm. 7. **De-moulding:** After sufficient green strength, the part is extracted and post-cured if needed. **Function of Components (2 Marks):** - **(i) Injection Gate:** Controlled entry point of resin; position determines flow pattern and affects void formation. - **(ii) Vent:** Low-pressure exit for air and excess resin; its location should be at the last point to fill. - **(iii) Bleeder:** Porous absorbent material placed near the vent to capture excess resin and ensure complete air evacuation (reduces voids). #### Model Solution (b) — Derivation of Linear Flow-Front Equation **Assumptions (2 Marks):** 1. One-dimensional flow (linear, $x$-direction). 2. Incompressible, Newtonian resin. 3. Rigid, isothermal mould. 4. Homogeneous, isotropic preform (constant $K$). 5. Constant cavity thickness $h$; perfect seal (no race-tracking). 6. Constant viscosity (no significant cure during fill). **Derivation (5 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.)* Final Result: $$x_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 K \Delta P t}{\mu \phi}}$$ Fill time: $$t_{fill} = \frac{\mu \phi L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$$ --- ### Question B: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Discuss the major defects encountered in Resin Transfer Moulding. How does injection pressure and mould temperature affect the quality of the part? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A rectangular RTM mould of length **1.0 m**, width **0.5 m**, and cavity thickness **5 mm** contains a glass-fibre preform with fibre volume fraction **$V_f = 0.50$** and permeability **$K = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ m²**. The resin viscosity is **0.25 Pa·s** and the applied pressure differential is **$3 \times 10^5$ Pa**. (i) Calculate the porosity $\phi$. (ii) Calculate the time required to completely fill the mould. (iii) What is the flow-front position at **$t = 100$ s**? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — Defects and Process Parameters **Major Defects (4 Marks — any four, 1 mark each):** 1. **Voids (porosity):** Air entrapment due to incomplete impregnation. Cause: low $\Delta P$, high $\mu$, low $K$. 2. **Race-tracking:** Resin flows preferentially along mould edges due to lower resistance. Cause: poor mould seal, high closure force variation. 3. **Fibre wash-out:** Fibre displacement by resin flow. Cause: high inlet velocity, low binder content. 4. **Dry spots:** Localised regions of unimpregnated preform. Cause: premature gelation, gate/vent misplacement. 5. **Warpage / Distortion:** Non-uniform cure shrinkage. Cause: uneven heating, high exotherm. 6. **Sink marks:** Surface depressions from internal shrinkage. Cause: localised over-packing or thermal gradients. **Effect of Injection Pressure (1.5 Marks):** - Higher $\Delta P$ → faster fill → reduced void content. - Too high → fibre wash-out, mould deflection, flash. - Optimal range: **0.1 – 0.4 MPa** for standard RTM. **Effect of Mould Temperature (1.5 Marks):** - Higher $T_{mould}$ → lower resin viscosity → faster fill, better wet-out. - Too high → premature gelation, short pot life, exotherm runaway. - Typical: **Room T to 60 °C** for injection; **80 – 180 °C** for cure. #### Model Solution (b) — Numerical **Given:** $L = 1.0$ m, $w = 0.5$ m, $h = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $V_f = 0.50$, $K = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ m², $\mu = 0.25$ Pa·s, $\Delta P = 3 \times 10^5$ Pa. **(i) Porosity (1.5 Marks):** $$\phi = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.50 = 0.50$$ **(ii) Fill Time (3 Marks):** $$t_{fill} = \frac{\mu \phi L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$$ $$t_{fill} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.50 \times (1.0)^2}{2 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-9}) \times (3 \times 10^5)}$$ $$t_{fill} = \frac{0.125}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = 208.33 \text{ s}$$ **(iii) Flow-Front Position at $t = 100$ s (2.5 Marks):** Since $t = 100$ s $< t_{fill} = 208.33$ s, the mould is **not yet fully filled**. $$x_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 K \Delta P t}{\mu \phi}}$$ $$x_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-9}) \times (3 \times 10^5) \times 100}{0.25 \times 0.50}}$$ $$x_f = \sqrt{\frac{0.06}{0.125}} = \sqrt{0.48} \approx 0.693 \text{ m}$$ **Result:** At $t = 100$ s, the flow front has progressed to **~0.693 m** (i.e., 69.3 % of the mould length is impregnated). --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) ### ✅ Core Definition - [x] RTM = **Closed-mould, low-pressure** injection of **low-viscosity** thermoset resin into a **dry fibre preform**, cured **in-situ**. ### ✅ Governing Equation - [x] **Darcy's Law:** $v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ - [x] **Linear fill front:** $x_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K \Delta P t}{\mu \phi}}$ - [x] **Fill time:** $t_{fill} = \dfrac{\mu \phi L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$ - [x] **Permeability (Kozeny-Carman):** $K = \dfrac{r_f^2}{4\kappa} \cdot \dfrac{(1-V_f)^3}{V_f^2}$ ### ✅ Key Process Parameters (Numerical Ranges to Memorise) - [x] Injection pressure: **0.1 – 0.4 MPa** (RTM); **< 0.1 MPa vacuum** (VARTM); **> 10 MPa** (HP-RTM). - [x] Resin viscosity: **< 500 mPa·s** (preferably **< 200 mPa·s**). - [x] Mould temperature: **RT – 60 °C**; Cure temperature: **80 – 180 °C**. - [x] Fibre volume fraction: **40 – 60 %**. - [x] Permeability: **$10^{-9}$ to $10^{-11}$ m²** for glass preforms. ### ✅ Variants - [x] **VARTM** — vacuum-driven, one-sided mould, very large parts (wind, marine). - [x] **HP-RTM** — high pressure, automotive mass production (BMW 7-series roof). - [x] **SCRIMP** — flow media for fast impregnation. ### ✅ Major Defects - [x] Voids (air entrapment), race-tracking, fibre wash-out, dry spots, warpage, sink marks. ### ✅ Critical Assumptions for Derivations - [x] 1-D flow, incompressible Newtonian resin, rigid isothermal mould, homogeneous preform, no race-tracking, constant $\mu$. ### ✅ Real-World Applications - [x] Aerospace (A350, 787), automotive (BMW i3, 7-series), wind energy (spar caps), marine (hulls), civil (bridge decks). ### ✅ Common Examiner Traps - [x] Confusing $V_f$ (fibre volume fraction) with $\phi$ (porosity) — they are **complementary** ($\phi = 1 - V_f$). - [x] Forgetting to mention **assumptions** in derivations (loses ~2 marks). - [x] Using **inconsistent units** (mixing cP with Pa·s without conversion: 1 cP = $10^{-3}$ Pa·s). - [x] Omitting **schematic diagram** in descriptive questions (loses ~2 marks).

topics.title: Pultrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pultrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pultrusion** is a continuous, automated, closed-mold composite manufacturing process in which **reinforcing fibers impregnated with a liquid thermosetting resin** are **pulled** (not pushed) through a heated, stationary die of constant cross-section, where the resin undergoes curing (cross-linking), producing a finished composite profile of theoretically infinite length that is subsequently cut to desired sizes.

The very word **"pultrusion"** is a portmanteau of **"pull" + "extrusion"**. While conventional polymer **extrusion** pushes (forces) molten thermoplastic through a die using a rotating screw, pultrusion **pulls** the fiber-resin bundle through the die using hydraulically or caterpillar-driven grippers. This distinction is crucial because:

- **Extrusion** → Thermoplastics (Pumps molten polymer)
- **Pultrusion** → Fiber-Reinforced Thermosets (Pulls impregnated fibers)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Pultrusion is classified under *Continuous Fiber-Reinforced Composite Manufacturing Processes* and is paired alongside Filament Winding and Continuous Lamination as the three principal techniques for producing unidirectional, constant-section FRP (Fiber-Reinforced Polymer) products.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of pultrusion like **dipping a wick into melted wax and pulling it slowly through a hollow candle mold** 🔥:

| Analogy Element | Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Cotton wick | Continuous fiberglass/carbon rovings |
| Melted wax pool | Liquid thermoset resin bath (impregnation tank) |
| Hollow candle mold | Heated steel die (curing zone) |
| Hand pulling the wick | Hydraulic caterpillar pullers |
| Solidified candle | Cured composite profile |
| Cutting the candle | Flying cut-off saw |

Just as the cooled wax solidifies into the candle's shape as the wick is pulled through, the heated die cures the thermoset resin around the fibers as the assembly is pulled through.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (KTU-Relevant)

The following industry-standard figures are typically quoted in KTU reference materials:

- **Pull speed (line speed)**: **0.3 to 3.0 m/min** (typical for polyester/glass)
- **Die temperatures**: **100 °C to 200 °C** (multi-zone, ramped)
- **Resin systems**: Polyester (60 %), Vinyl Ester (25 %), Epoxy (10 %), Phenolic (5 %)
- **Fiber loadings**: Up to **65–70 % by weight** of glass
- **Profile cross-sectional area**: 12 mm² to 1 m²

> [!TIP]
> **Why pull instead of push?** Fibers cannot tolerate the compressive (push) forces of an extruder screw without buckling, breaking, or losing alignment. Pulling puts the fibers in **tension**, where they are strongest, while the resin matrix is in mild compression — a mechanically efficient state for a unidirectional composite.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Sequential Stages of Pultrusion

The pultrusion line is divided into **six functional zones**. Each stage has distinct thermal, chemical, and mechanical roles:

**Stage 1 — Fiber Creels (Roving Dispenser)**
- Spools of continuous rovings (glass, carbon, aramid) are mounted on **creels**.
- Rovings are paid out under controlled tension to maintain alignment and prevent tangling.
- For complex profiles, **continuous strand mats, woven rovings, or stitched fabrics** may be added in addition to unidirectional rovings.

**Stage 2 — Resin Impregnation (Wet-Out Bath)**
- Rovings pass through a **resin bath** containing:
  - Liquid thermoset resin (polyester / vinyl ester / epoxy)
  - Curing agent (initiator / hardener)
  - Accelerator
  - Fillers, pigments, release agents, UV stabilizers
- Wet-out is aided by **guide bars, squeeze rollers, and ultrasonic vibration**.
- Goal: complete fiber wet-out and removal of entrapped air voids.

**Stage 3 — Pre-Forming Station**
- The wet, resin-saturated bundle is shaped (ovalized or flattened) and excess resin is squeezed out.
- Compacts the fiber architecture before entry into the heated die.

**Stage 4 — Heated Die (Curing Zone)**
- Steel die with **electric resistance or induction heaters**, often 0.6 to 1.5 m long.
- Typically has **two or three temperature zones** (entrance warm-up → middle gel/cure → exit post-cure).
- Resin viscosity drops initially (heat plasticization) then rises sharply as cure progresses (gel point → vitrification).

**Stage 5 — Pulling Mechanism**
- **Reciprocating hydraulic clamps** (caterpillar tracks with rubber pads) grip the cured profile and pull it forward.
- Modern systems use **two clamps alternating** — one pulls while the other returns — to ensure *continuous* motion (no jerk).

**Stage 6 — Cut-Off Saw**
- A **diamond or carbide-tipped flying saw** cuts the continuously emerging profile to specified lengths.
- Modern CNC saws synchronize cut position with line speed to avoid jerking.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_m = v \cdot A_c \cdot \rho_c$ | Mass throughput rate | $\text{kg/h}$ |
| 2 | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{(w_f / \rho_f) + (w_r / \rho_r)}$ | Fiber volume fraction (from weights) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $W_r \% = \dfrac{m_r}{m_r + m_f} \times 100$ | Resin weight percentage | % |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot f(\alpha)$ | General cure kinetics rate equation | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius cure rate constant | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\tau_{res} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{v_{pull}}$ | Residence time in die | $\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $F_{pull} = F_{visc} + F_{fric} + F_{aero}$ | Total pulling force balance | $\text{N}$ |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{gen} = \rho_r \cdot V_r \cdot H_R \cdot \dfrac{d\alpha}{dt}$ | Volumetric heat generation by cure | $\text{W/m}^3$ |
| 9 | $\alpha_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{V}\int_V \alpha(x,y,z)\,dV$ | Average degree of cure in profile | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{comp} = \dfrac{m_{profile}}{m_{fiber}+m_{resin}}$ | Composite material yield efficiency | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Notation Notice**: In the table above, vertical bars in equations such as $w_f \vert$ are rendered using $\vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Always copy equations carefully into your answer booklet.

### 2.3 Physical Interpretation of Key Equations

**Equation 1 (Throughput):** $Q_m = v \cdot A_c \cdot \rho_c$
- Doubling the pull speed doubles production — but only if cure is complete at that speed. **Pull speed is cure-limited**, not machine-limited.
- $A_c$ is the actual finished cross-section (post-cure, after resin shrinkage).

**Equation 5 (Arrhenius):** $k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$
- Higher die temperature $\uparrow$ → much faster cure (exponential dependence).
- $E_a$ for polyester ≈ **70–85 kJ/mol**; for epoxy ≈ **55–70 kJ/mol**.

**Equation 6 (Residence Time):** $\tau_{res} = L_{die} / v_{pull}$
- Cure must reach **α ≥ 0.95** within $\tau_{res}$ — this sets the upper limit on $v_{pull}$.

**Equation 7 (Pull Force):** $F_{pull} = F_{visc} + F_{fric} + F_{aero}$
- $F_{visc}$: resistance from viscous resin flow along die wall.
- $F_{fric}$: friction between cured profile and die exit wall.
- $F_{aero}$: any aerodynamic drag (negligible in enclosed die).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pultrusion is the **backbone of the global FRP structural market**, producing:
- **Electrical**: Third-rail covers, cable trays, ladder rails, insulator rods (FRP I-beams for subways).
- **Civil**: Rebar (BFRP/GFRP), bridge decks, handrails, structural I-sections.
- **Marine**: Fishing rod blanks, mast sections, deck beams.
- **Automotive**: Leaf springs (glass/polyester pultrusions), drive shafts, bumper reinforcements.
- **Wind Energy**: Wind turbine blade spar caps (carbon/epoxy pultrusion — *key enabler of modern megawatt-class blades*).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Permissible Pull Speed from Cure Kinetics

The pull speed is dictated by the requirement that **the centerline of the profile must reach ≥ 95 % cure** before exiting the die. The derivation proceeds in five steps:

**Step 1 — Convert cure rate into spatial form.**
The resin cures as it moves through the die. Convert the time derivative into a position derivative using $dt = dz / v$:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dz} = \frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Arrhenius kinetics.**
Substitute the general rate law $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot f(\alpha)$:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dz} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT(z)}}{v} \cdot f(\alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate along die length.**
Cure builds from inlet $\alpha = 0$ at $z = 0$ to exit cure $\alpha_{exit}$ at $z = L_{die}$:

$$\int_{0}^{\alpha_{exit}} \frac{d\alpha}{f(\alpha)} = \frac{A}{v} \int_{0}^{L_{die}} e^{-E_a / RT(z)}\, dz$$

**Step 4 — Define a thermal-averaged rate constant.**
For an isothermal die approximation (or piecewise isothermal zones):

$$\overline{k} = \frac{1}{L_{die}} \int_{0}^{L_{die}} A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT(z)}\, dz$$

So the equation simplifies to:

$$\int_{0}^{\alpha_{exit}} \frac{d\alpha}{f(\alpha)} = \overline{k} \cdot \frac{L_{die}}{v}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v_{max}$.**
For an nth-order kinetic model $f(\alpha) = (1 - \alpha)^n$, the integral gives the closed form:

$$v_{max} = \frac{\overline{k} \cdot L_{die}}{\dfrac{(1-\alpha_{exit})^{1-n} - 1}{n - 1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: Doubling the die temperature does **not** simply double the permissible pull speed — because $\overline{k}$ appears inside an exponential ($A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$), small temperature increases produce disproportionately large gains in $v_{max}$. This is why die temperature optimization is the **single most powerful productivity lever** in pultrusion.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A pultrusion line uses a vinyl ester resin with $E_a = 75{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$, $A = 1.2 \times 10^{10} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $n = 1.4$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$. Die length $L_{die} = 1.2 \text{ m}$, isothermal die at $T = 423 \text{ K}$ ($150 \text{ °C}$). Required exit cure $\alpha_{exit} = 0.95$. Compute the maximum pull speed.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $k$ at the die temperature:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} = 1.2 \times 10^{10} \cdot e^{-75000 / (8.314 \times 423)}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$-E_a / RT = -\dfrac{75{,}000}{3517.7} = -21.32$$

$$e^{-21.32} = 5.65 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$k = 1.2 \times 10^{10} \times 5.65 \times 10^{-10} = 6.78 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Step 2 — Evaluate the kinetic integral for $n = 1.4$:

$$\int_{0}^{0.95} \frac{d\alpha}{(1-\alpha)^{1.4}} = \left[ \frac{(1-\alpha)^{-0.4}}{-0.4} \cdot (-1) \right]_{0}^{0.95}$$

$$= \left[ \frac{(1-\alpha)^{-0.4}}{0.4} \right]_{0}^{0.95} = \frac{(0.05)^{-0.4} - (1)^{-0.4}}{0.4}$$

Compute $(0.05)^{-0.4}$:

$$0.05^{-0.4} = e^{-0.4 \ln(0.05)} = e^{-0.4 \times (-2.996)} = e^{1.198} = 3.31$$

So the integral becomes:

$$\frac{3.31 - 1.0}{0.4} = \frac{2.31}{0.4} = 5.78$$

Step 3 — Solve for $v_{max}$:

$$v_{max} = \frac{k \cdot L_{die}}{\text{Integral}} = \frac{6.78 \times 1.2}{5.78} = \frac{8.136}{5.78} = 1.408 \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to standard units (KTU convention):

$$v_{max} = 1.408 \text{ m/s} \approx 84.5 \text{ m/min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Reality Check**: This theoretical value is **too high** because the calculation assumed **isothermal die and no thermal lag in the profile interior**. In practice, the center of a thick profile lags behind the surface by tens of seconds — real pull speeds are 0.3–3 m/min, two orders of magnitude lower. This is why thick profiles (> 25 mm wall) are challenging to pultrude.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Pultrusion Variants

| Variant | Reinforcement Architecture | Resin System | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard Pultrusion** | Unidirectional rovings | Polyester / Vinyl Ester | Rebar, ladders, structural shapes |
| **Carbon Pultrusion** | Carbon tow (12K–50K) | Epoxy | Wind blade spar caps |
| **Pulwinding** | Roving + hoop winding | Vinyl Ester | High-pressure pipe, CNG tanks |
| **Pull-Braiding** | Braided overbraid | Epoxy | Drive shafts, antenna masts |
| **Co-extrusion Pultrusion** | Dual-resin skin/core | Polyester + PU foam core | Insulation panels, door panels |
| **Resin-Injection Pultrusion** | Dry fibers injected in die | Epoxy / Phenolic | High-fiber, low-void aerospace parts |

### 3.4 Resin Content Calculation (Batch Audit Example)

A pultruded GFRP I-beam weighs **8.4 kg/m**. Glass fiber input is **5.7 kg/m**, density $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Resin density $\rho_r = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Find the fiber volume fraction $V_f$.

Step 1 — Resin weight per meter:

$$m_r = m_{total} - m_f = 8.4 - 5.7 = 2.7 \text{ kg/m}$$

Step 2 — Apply $V_f$ formula:

$$V_f = \frac{w_f / \rho_f}{(w_f / \rho_f) + (w_r / \rho_r)}$$

$$V_f = \frac{5.7 / 2.55}{(5.7 / 2.55) + (2.7 / 1.20)}$$

$$V_f = \frac{2.235}{2.235 + 2.250} = \frac{2.235}{4.485} = 0.498$$

So **$V_f \approx 49.8 \text{ \%}$** — typical for unidirectional GFRP pultrusions (50–55 % is the design sweet spot).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pultrusion Process Line — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Roving Spool A] --> B1[Creel Rack]
    A2[Roving Spool B] --> B1
    A3[Continuous Mat Roll] --> B1
    A4[Woven Fabric Roll] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Resin Wet-Out Bath]
    C1 --> C2[Squeeze Roller Station]
    C2 --> D1[Pre-Former Guide]
    D1 --> E1[Die Zone 1 - Pre-Heat 100C]
    E1 --> E2[Die Zone 2 - Gel Cure 140C]
    E2 --> E3[Die Zone 3 - Post Cure 170C]
    E3 --> F1[Pulling Clamp 1 - Active]
    F1 --> F2[Pulling Clamp 2 - Return Stroke]
    F2 --> G1[Flying Cut-Off Saw]
    G1 --> H1[Finished Composite Profile]
    H1 --> I1[Quality Control Station]
    I1 --> J1[Packaging and Stacking]
    C1 --> X1[Resin Mixer Tank]
    X1 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide**: Each block represents a station in the pultrusion line. Material flow proceeds **left → right**. The die zone is intentionally split into three sub-blocks to model the **multi-zone thermal profile** typical of production pultrusion dies. The dual pulling clamps model the **reciprocating clamp mechanism** essential for jerk-free continuous motion.

### 4.2 Thermal and Cure State Evolution Through the Die

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Inlet State: alpha = 0, T = 25C] --> S1[Die Entry: Heating Begins]
    S1 --> S2[Viscosity Drops - Resin Flows]
    S2 --> S3[Viscosity Minimum - Gel Point Approaching]
    S3 --> S4[alpha crosses 0.5 - Gelation]
    S4 --> S5[alpha crosses 0.9 - Vitrification Onset]
    S5 --> S6[Die Exit: alpha = 0.95, Profile Solid]
    S6 --> S7[Post Cure Cooling: T drops to 25C]
```

### 4.3 Pull Force Composition (Forces Acting on the Profile)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Total Pull Force F_pull] --> P1[F_viscous - Resin Shear Drag]
    P0 --> P2[F_friction - Die Wall Sliding Contact]
    P0 --> P3[F_aerodynamic - Air Drag in Die Vent]
    P1 --> Q1[Depends on: resin viscosity, die wall area]
    P2 --> Q2[Depends on: surface roughness, normal pressure]
    P3 --> Q3[Negligible for enclosed die]
```

### 4.4 Process Parameter Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Increase Die Temp] --> O1[Higher cure rate]
    O1 --> O2[Higher pull speed possible]
    O2 --> O3[Higher throughput]
    R2[Increase Pull Speed] --> O4[Lower residence time]
    O4 --> O5[Risk of under-cure]
    O5 --> O6[Lower mechanical properties]
    R3[Increase Fiber Volume] --> O7[More tooling wear]
    O7 --> O8[Higher mechanical performance]
    R4[Add Filler CaCO3] --> O9[Lower cost, lower exotherm]
    O9 --> O10[Easier to pultrude thick sections]
```
