## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between pultrusion and extrusion. List two advantages of pultrusion over extrusion for composite part production."**

*Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 + 2 split):*

**Definition difference (1 Mark):**
Extrusion is a process in which a thermoplastic melt is **pushed** through a die by a rotating screw, whereas pultrusion is a process in which fiber reinforcements impregnated with a thermoset resin are **pulled** through a heated die where curing occurs.

**Advantages (2 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **High fiber content achievable (up to 65–70 % by weight)** because fibers experience **tensile pulling forces** (no fiber breakage), whereas in extrusion fibers cannot be added in significant amounts due to screw-shear damage.

2. **Continuous production of structural profiles of theoretically infinite length** with **constant cross-section and excellent longitudinal strength** — extrusion cannot match this for fiber-reinforced parts because of fiber attrition and orientation randomization.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"What are the functions of the resin bath and the heated die in the pultrusion process? Why is multi-zone heating used?"**

*Model Answer (3 Marks):*

**Resin bath (1 Mark):** The resin bath is responsible for **complete impregnation (wet-out)** of the fiber rovings with liquid thermoset resin. It ensures uniform coating of every filament, removes entrapped air, and meters resin pickup.

**Heated die (1 Mark):** The heated die provides the **thermal energy required for cross-linking (cure)** of the thermoset resin. The profile exits the die as a fully solid, dimensionally accurate composite section.

**Multi-zone heating (1 Mark):** Multi-zone heating is used because the resin undergoes **three distinct thermal events** along the die — (i) **heat-up and viscosity drop** (zone 1), (ii) **gel point and rapid exothermic cure** (zone 2, requires tight temperature control to avoid thermal runaway), and (iii) **post-cure / vitrification completion** (zone 3). A single uniform temperature cannot satisfy all three; hence 2–3 zones with independently controlled heaters are used.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically **7 marks each**. We provide TWO independent alternative questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic diagram, describe the **pultrusion process** for manufacturing fiber-reinforced polymer composites. Explain the functions of each major station. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **processing parameters** that influence the quality of pultruded products. Derive the relationship between pull speed, die length, and degree of cure. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution — (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Schematic (2 Marks):** Draw the labeled six-station pultrusion line:

1. **Creels (0.5 Mark):** Hold rovings / mats / fabrics under controlled tension.
2. **Resin bath / impregnation tank (1 Mark):** Contains resin + hardener + accelerator; provides complete wet-out of fibers via guide bars and rollers.
3. **Pre-forming station (0.5 Mark):** Shapes the wet bundle and squeezes out excess resin.
4. **Heated die (1.5 Marks):** Steel die with multi-zone heaters; cures the resin. Divided into pre-heat, gel-cure, and post-cure zones. Resin viscosity first falls then rises sharply as gel point is crossed.
5. **Pulling mechanism (1 Mark):** Reciprocating hydraulic clamps (caterpillar tracks) grip the cured profile and pull it forward at controlled speed.
6. **Cut-off saw (0.5 Mark):** Diamond-tipped flying saw cuts the continuous profile to specified lengths.

**Closing note (Implicit in any one of the above):** The process is fully continuous, automated, and produces profiles with high longitudinal fiber content (50–65 % by volume).

---

**Model Solution — (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Processing parameters (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Die temperature profile** (most influential) — controls cure kinetics exponentially via Arrhenius equation.
2. **Pull speed (line speed)** — directly tied to residence time and must allow complete cure.
3. **Resin formulation** — initiator ratio, accelerator, filler loading; affects exotherm and gel time.
4. **Fiber tension at creel** — too loose → waviness, too tight → breakage.
5. **Resin viscosity in bath** — affects wet-out and void content.
6. **Heating rate in die** — affects thermal lag in thick profiles (centerline cure lag).
7. **Die land length and geometry** — affects friction force and pressure build-up.

**Derivation (3.5 Marks):**

The degree of cure $\alpha$ develops along the die length $z$ as:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dz} = \frac{k(T)}{v_{pull}} \cdot (1-\alpha)^n$$

Substituting Arrhenius $k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ and integrating from $z = 0$ (inlet, $\alpha = 0$) to $z = L_{die}$ (exit, $\alpha = \alpha_{exit}$):

$$\int_{0}^{\alpha_{exit}} \frac{d\alpha}{(1-\alpha)^n} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot L_{die}}{v_{pull}}$$

For an isothermal die this directly gives:

$$v_{pull} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot L_{die} \cdot (n-1)}{1 - (1-\alpha_{exit})^{1-n}}$$

This shows that **pull speed is proportional to die length** and **exponentially dependent on die temperature**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the **role of curing agents, accelerators, and fillers** in pultrusion resin systems. Give examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** List and explain any **four defects** commonly encountered in pultruded products. How can they be prevented? *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution — (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Component (1.5 M each) | Role | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Resin** | Matrix binder; transfers load to fibers | Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Epoxy, Phenolic |
| **Curing agent / Initiator** | Triggers free-radical cross-linking (peroxides for polyester/VE; amines/anhydrides for epoxy) | MEKP (methyl ethyl ketone peroxide), BPO (benzoyl peroxide), TETA, MTHPA |
| **Accelerator** | Lowers decomposition temperature of initiator so cure happens at 100–150 °C instead of > 180 °C | Cobalt naphthenate (for poly/VE), DMA, DMPT (for epoxy) |
| **Filler** | Reduces cost, lowers exotherm (critical for thick sections), improves fire/smoke performance, modifies viscosity | CaCO₃ (calcium carbonate), ATH (alumina trihydrate), silica, clay |
| **Release agent** | Prevents sticking of cured profile to die wall | Internal: stearates; External: silicone spray |
| **Pigment / UV stabilizer** | Color and weather resistance | Titanium dioxide, carbon black, HALS |

Closing note: **Stoichiometry is critical** — excess initiator → over-cure, brittleness; deficit → under-cure, tacky surface.

---

**Model Solution — (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Defect 1 — Voids / Porosity (1.75 Marks):**
*Cause:* Air trapped in resin bath, volatile evolution during cure, moisture on fibers.
*Prevention:* Use of vacuum-assisted resin injection, pre-drying fibers, degassing resin before bath, ultrasonic bath.

**Defect 2 — Surface Cracks / Longitudinal Splitting (1.75 Marks):**
*Cause:* Excessive exothermic temperature causing thermal stress, or excessive pull force causing longitudinal tension cracks.
*Prevention:* Use of lower-exotherm resin, addition of fillers, staged temperature ramp, reduce pull speed.

**Defect 3 — Under-Cure / Sticky Surface (1.75 Marks):**
*Cause:* Pull speed too high, die temperature too low, incorrect initiator/accelerator ratio.
*Prevention:* Barcol hardness check at exit (target ≥ 35), adjust $T$ and $v$, DSC verification of $\alpha$.

**Defect 4 — Fiber Misalignment / Waviness (1.75 Marks):**
*Cause:* Uneven creel tension, dragging at guide bars, improper pre-forming.
*Prevention:* Use of tensioners on each roving, polished guides, proper die entry geometry.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Pultrusion Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pultrusion with extrusion** — Examiners report at least **20 % of students** mix up "pull" vs. "push" terminology. State clearly: pultrusion = **pull**, extrusion = **push**.
>
> 2. **Skipping the Arrhenius derivation** — When asked to relate pull speed to die length/temperature, **always derive** the relationship. A mere qualitative answer ("higher temperature → faster cure") earns **at most 2 of 7 marks**. The full 7 marks requires the integral form shown in §3.1.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the multi-zone die** — Many students describe the die as a single uniform-temperature block. Examiners **explicitly deduct 1–2 marks** for this; always mention the pre-heat → gel-cure → post-cure zoning.
>
> 4. **Ignoring die friction in pull force** — When asked about pull force, students frequently list only viscous drag. **Always mention all three components** ($F_{visc}$, $F_{fric}$, $F_{aero}$).
>
> 5. **Not specifying fiber volume fraction limits** — A pultrusion answer that quotes generic "high fiber content" without giving the numerical range (**50–65 % by volume**) is considered incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **Pultrusion = Pull + Extrusion.** It is a continuous closed-mold process for thermoset composites with constant cross-section.

✅ The **six stations**: Creel → Resin Bath → Pre-former → Heated Die (multi-zone) → Puller → Cut-off Saw.

✅ **Why pull instead of push?** Fibers are strongest in tension; pulling avoids fiber attrition that would occur under extruder-screw compression.

✅ **Pull speed is cure-limited**: $v_{max} \propto L_{die} \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$.

✅ **Die is multi-zone**: Pre-heat (90–110 °C) → Gel/Cure (130–160 °C) → Post-cure (160–200 °C).

✅ **Cure kinetics**: $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot (1-\alpha)^n$. Polyester $E_a \approx 75 \text{ kJ/mol}$.

✅ **Residence time**: $\tau_{res} = L_{die} / v_{pull}$. Must be long enough for $\alpha \geq 0.95$.

✅ **Pull force**: $F_{pull} = F_{visc} + F_{fric} + F_{aero}$.

✅ **Fiber volume fraction** typically 50–65 %; **resin systems**: polyester (60 %), vinyl ester (25 %), epoxy (10 %), phenolic (5 %).

✅ **Key variants**: Pulwinding, Pull-Braiding, Co-extrusion Pultrusion, Resin-Injection Pultrusion.

✅ **Quality check at exit**: Barcol hardness ≥ 35 (typical for cured polyester).

✅ **Major applications**: Electrical ladder rails, rebar, wind blade spar caps, leaf springs, FRP structural profiles.

✅ **Top defects to remember**: Voids, surface cracks, under-cure, fiber waviness.

✅ **Always state units** in derivations: $v$ in m/s or m/min, $L_{die}$ in m, $T$ in K (not °C) inside the exponential.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforced Reaction Injection Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT604 - POLYMER BLENDS & COMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reinforced Reaction Injection Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinforced Reaction Injection Moulding (RRIM)** is an advanced variant of the standard Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM) process in which **reinforcing fillers** — such as short chopped glass fibres, milled glass fibres, wollastonite, mica, or mineral fillers — are **pre-dispersed into one or both of the reactive liquid components** (typically the polyol stream) before high-pressure impingement mixing and injection into a closed mould cavity, where simultaneous polymerisation (chain extension/crosslinking) and part formation occur to yield a **fibre/filler-reinforced thermoset polyurethane composite part**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, RRIM is classified as a **hybrid moulding process** combining elements of:
- **Reaction processing** (in-situ polymerisation like RIM)
- **Composite processing** (fibre reinforcement like SMC/BMC)

The reinforcing agent loading typically ranges from **5 wt% to 25 wt%** of the total formulation, with fibre lengths in the range of **0.2 mm to 1.5 mm** (milled/chopped) to avoid plugging the small clearance (≈ 0.5–1.0 mm) of the high-pressure impingement mix head.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Concrete Mixer + Cake Baker" Picture**

Imagine baking a sponge cake using two separate liquid batters (A and B) that react the moment they meet to rise and solidify (this is plain **RIM**). Now, suppose you want a **stiffer, tougher cake** that doesn't crumble when sliced — so you **pre-mix fine chopped nuts or coconut shreds into batter A** before the two batters are whisked together at high speed and poured into the baking tin. The nuts act like **glass fibres**, the whisk is the **impingement mixer**, the two batters are the **polyol and isocyanate**, and the baking tin with heat is the **heated mould**. The result is a stiffer, more dimensionally stable cake (RRIM part).

The **nuts (reinforcement)**, however, have to be short enough to pass through the whisk (mix head) without jamming — that's why fibre length in RRIM is **deliberately restricted to 1.5 mm or less**.

### 1.3 Salient Features of RRIM

| Feature | Specification / Value |
|---|---|
| Base polymer system | Polyurethane (PU), polyurea, poly(urethane-urea) |
| Reinforcement type | Milled glass, chopped glass (≤ 1.5 mm), mica, wollastonite, carbon black |
| Reinforcement loading | **5 – 25 wt%** (typically 15 wt% for automotive) |
| Mould pressure | **0.3 – 0.7 MPa** (low pressure moulding) |
| Injection pressure at mix head | **10 – 20 MPa** |
| Mould temperature | **50 – 80 °C** |
| Cream time | 1 – 5 s |
| Rise time | 10 – 60 s |
| Demould time | 30 – 120 s |
| Part wall thickness | 3 – 25 mm |
| Surface finish | Excellent (gel coat layer) |
| Key advantage | Stiffness + toughness + thin-wall complex geometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RRIM over plain RIM?**
> Plain RIM produces a flexible, rubbery polyurethane. Adding reinforcement transforms it into a **structural composite** capable of replacing sheet metal in automotive body panels and bumpers.

### 1.4 Historical Context & Industry Relevance

RRIM emerged in the **mid-1970s** as automotive OEMs (especially Ford, GM, and later European manufacturers) sought lightweight, corrosion-resistant alternatives to stamped steel for exterior body panels. The **Pontiac Fiero** (1984) famously used RRIM for its rear vertical panels and fascias. Today, RRIM is a **US$ 1.2 billion** niche segment within the global polymer processing industry, primarily serving **transportation, construction, and agricultural equipment** markets.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Two Pillars: Reaction Chemistry + Composite Mechanics

RRIM is governed by **two simultaneous physical-chemical phenomena**:

1. **Reaction Kinetics** — the rapid step-growth polymerisation between polyol (-OH) and isocyanate (-NCO) groups:

$$\text{R–NCO} + \text{HO–R}' \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–COO–R}'$$

2. **Fibre Suspension Rheology** — the behaviour of fibre-filled non-Newtonian liquids in the mix head and mould cavity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Clarifier for Students:**
> In RRIM, the *reaction* and the *reinforcement* happen **together but at different rates**. The reaction chemistry determines the gel point (the instant the reacting mixture transitions from liquid to gel), while the reinforcement determines how the viscous slurry flows before gelation.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the RRIM Process

**Stage 1 — Reinforcement Pre-mixing (Slurry Preparation)**

- Milled/chopped fibres are charged into the **polyol reservoir** at 15–25 wt%.
- A slow-speed, high-torque **stirrer** keeps fibres in uniform suspension.
- Viscosity of polyol slurry rises from **~ 0.5 Pa·s** (neat) to **~ 2–5 Pa·s** (filled) — a **4–10× increase**.
- Surface treatment of fibres (silane coupling agents like **3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane**) ensures chemical bonding with the PU matrix.

**Stage 2 — High-Pressure Metering**

- Two precision **metering pumps** (axial piston type) deliver:
  - Polyol + filler stream ("A" side)
  - Isocyanate stream ("B" side — typically MDI or modified MDI)
- Throughput is metered at a **stoichiometric ratio** (NCO:OH index ≈ 1.00–1.10).
- Typical throughput: **1–10 kg/s** depending on part size.

**Stage 3 — Impingement Mixing**

- Both streams collide in a small (≈ 1 mm clearance) mixing chamber at Reynolds numbers **Re > 10,000** — fully turbulent.
- Mixing occurs in **< 0.1 second**.
- The fluid must be **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** to flow, but thixotropic enough to suspend fibres after mixing.

**Stage 4 — Mould Filling & Curing**

- The reactive fibre-polyurethane slurry flows into the closed mould.
- Within **1–5 s (cream time)**, polymerisation begins and viscosity rises sharply.
- By **10–60 s (rise time)**, gelation occurs.
- **30–120 s later**, the part is rigid enough for demoulding.

**Stage 5 — Post-Curing & Trimming**

- Demoulded parts may undergo **post-cure at 80–120 °C** for 1 hour to complete crosslinking.
- Flash is trimmed; gates are removed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All essential RRIM formulas and parameters required for KTU University Examinations.**

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Isocyanate index | $\displaystyle \text{Index} = \frac{n_{\text{NCO}}}{n_{\text{OH}}} \times 100$ | 100–110 (typical) |
| 2 | Theoretical stoichiometric ratio | $\displaystyle r = \frac{\text{eq. wt. of polyol}}{\text{eq. wt. of isocyanate}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Reynolds number in mix head | $\displaystyle \text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$ | > 10,000 (turbulent) |
| 4 | Shear rate in mix head | $\displaystyle \dot{\gamma} = \frac{v}{h}$ | 10,000 – 100,000 s⁻¹ |
| 5 | Effective viscosity (filled system, Krieger-Dougherty) | $\displaystyle \frac{\mu_f}{\mu_0} = \left(1 - \frac{\phi}{\phi_m}\right)^{-[\mu]\phi_m}$ | $\phi_m \approx 0.6$, $[\mu] \approx 2.5$ |
| 6 | Volume fraction from weight fraction | $\displaystyle \phi = \frac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + (1 - w_f)/\rho_m}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Composite density (rule of mixtures) | $\displaystyle \rho_c = \phi_f \rho_f + (1 - \phi_f)\rho_m$ | kg/m³ |
| 8 | Elastic modulus — Voigt (upper bound) | $\displaystyle E_c = \phi_f E_f + (1 - \phi_f)E_m$ | Pa |
| 9 | Elastic modulus — Reuss (lower bound) | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{\phi_f}{E_f} + \frac{1 - \phi_f}{E_m}$ | Pa |
| 10 | Elastic modulus — Halpin-Tsai (practical) | $\displaystyle \frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 2\eta_L \phi_f}{1 - \eta_L \phi_f}, \quad \eta_L = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2\eta_L}$ | dimensionless $\eta_L$ |
| 11 | Heat of reaction (PU) | $\displaystyle \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \approx 80 - 100$ | kJ/mol of NCO |
| 12 | Exotherm peak temperature | $\displaystyle T_{\max} = T_{\text{mould}} + \frac{\Delta H \cdot \rho}{C_p}$ | °C |
| 13 | Heat distortion temperature (HDT) lift | $\displaystyle \Delta T_{\text{HDT}} \approx k \cdot w_f$ | K, $k \approx 5–8$ °C per wt% filler |
| 14 | Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) reduction | $\displaystyle \alpha_c \approx \alpha_m - \beta \cdot \phi_f$ | K⁻¹ |
| 15 | Fibre aspect ratio (critical for reinforcement) | $\displaystyle \text{AR} = \frac{L_f}{d_f}$ | typically 100–500 in RRIM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The Halpin-Tsai equation (row 10) is the **most examiner-relevant** for predicting the stiffness lift obtained from adding reinforcement. The Krieger-Dougherty relation (row 5) is critical for explaining why RRIM slurries are *much* more viscous than neat polyol — a common KTU conceptual question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Bumpers, fascia panels, fenders, door panels (weight saving of 30–50% over steel).
- **Construction:** Window frames, door skins, decorative panels.
- **Agriculture:** Hoods of tractors, equipment enclosures resistant to stone-chip impact.
- **Consumer goods:** Large, tough, complex-shaped housings (e.g., computer monitors of the late 1990s).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Viscosity of a Fibre-Filled Polyol Slurry (Krieger-Dougherty)

**Statement:** Show that adding 15 wt% milled glass fibre to a polyol of viscosity 0.5 Pa·s increases the slurry viscosity to a measurable value using the Krieger-Dougherty equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** This is the single most frequently asked derivation in Module 4. Practice it until you can reproduce it cold.

#### Step 1 — Define the equation

The Krieger-Dougherty semi-empirical relation for the relative viscosity of a concentrated suspension of hard spheres is:

$$\frac{\mu_f}{\mu_0} = \left(1 - \frac{\phi}{\phi_m}\right)^{-[\mu]\,\phi_m}$$

where $\mu_f$ is the filled-suspension viscosity, $\mu_0$ is the suspending-medium viscosity, $\phi$ is the filler volume fraction, $\phi_m$ is the maximum packing fraction (≈ 0.6 for random close packing of spheres), and $[\mu]$ is the intrinsic viscosity (≈ 2.5 for spheres).

#### Step 2 — Convert weight fraction to volume fraction

For glass fibre density $\rho_f = 2540$ kg/m³ and polyol density $\rho_m = 1010$ kg/m³, with $w_f = 0.15$:

$$\phi = \frac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + (1 - w_f)/\rho_m}$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values

$$\phi = \frac{0.15 / 2540}{0.15 / 2540 + 0.85 / 1010}$$

$$\phi = \frac{5.906 \times 10^{-5}}{5.906 \times 10^{-5} + 8.416 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\phi = \frac{5.906 \times 10^{-5}}{9.006 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0656$$

#### Step 4 — Plug into the Krieger-Dougherty expression

$$\frac{\mu_f}{0.5} = \left(1 - \frac{0.0656}{0.6}\right)^{-2.5 \times 0.6}$$

$$\frac{\mu_f}{0.5} = (1 - 0.1093)^{-1.5}$$

$$\frac{\mu_f}{0.5} = (0.8907)^{-1.5}$$

$$\frac{\mu_f}{0.5} = 1.175$$

$$\mu_f = 1.175 \times 0.5 = 0.5875 \;\text{Pa·s}$$

#### Step 5 — Interpretation

The viscosity rises by **17.5%** for 15 wt% glass in polyol. The **non-linear** effect becomes severe at higher loadings — at 25 wt%, the increase is closer to **60–80%**, which is why RRIM processing windows become very narrow at high fibre content.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modulus Prediction Using Halpin-Tsai

**Statement:** Predict the elastic modulus of an RRIM composite containing 15 wt% chopped glass fibres (E_f = 72 GPa, L/d = 200) in a polyurethane matrix (E_m = 0.05 GPa).

#### Step 1 — Recompute volume fraction

$$\phi_f = 0.0656 \quad \text{(from Derivation 1)}$$

#### Step 2 — Halpin-Tsai formulation for randomly oriented short fibres

$$\frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{3}{8} \left(\frac{1 + 2(L/d)\eta_L \phi_f}{1 - \eta_L \phi_f}\right) + \frac{5}{8}\left(\frac{1 + 2\eta_T \phi_f}{1 - \eta_T \phi_f}\right)$$

with

$$\eta_L = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2(L/d)}, \qquad \eta_T = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute E_f / E_m

$$\frac{E_f}{E_m} = \frac{72}{0.05} = 1440$$

#### Step 4 — Compute η_L (longitudinal)

$$\eta_L = \frac{1440 - 1}{1440 + 2(200)} = \frac{1439}{1840} = 0.7821$$

#### Step 5 — Compute η_T (transverse)

$$\eta_T = \frac{1440 - 1}{1440 + 2} = \frac{1439}{1442} = 0.9979$$

#### Step 6 — Substitute longitudinal term

$$\text{Longitudinal} = \frac{1 + 2(200)(0.7821)(0.0656)}{1 - (0.7821)(0.0656)} = \frac{1 + 20.52}{1 - 0.0513} = \frac{21.52}{0.9487} = 22.69$$

#### Step 7 — Substitute transverse term

$$\text{Transverse} = \frac{1 + 2(0.9979)(0.0656)}{1 - (0.9979)(0.0656)} = \frac{1.1309}{0.9346} = 1.210$$

#### Step 8 — Combine

$$\frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{3}{8}(22.69) + \frac{5}{8}(1.210) = 8.51 + 0.756 = 9.27$$

$$E_c = 9.27 \times 0.05 = 0.463 \;\text{GPa}$$

#### Step 9 — Interpretation

The composite modulus is **~ 463 MPa**, approximately **9× that of the unfilled PU matrix**. This dramatic stiffness increase is precisely why RRIM parts can replace sheet metal in load-bearing automotive applications.

### 3.3 Numerical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Neat RIM PU | RRIM (15 wt% glass) | Lift Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile strength | 25 MPa | 35 MPa | 1.4× |
| Flexural modulus | 0.35 GPa | 1.5 GPa | 4.3× |
| HDT @ 0.45 MPa | 70 °C | 110 °C | +40 °C |
| CTE | 90 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹ | 45 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹ | 0.5× |
| Notched Izod | 130 J/m | 95 J/m | 0.73× (drop) |
| Density | 1010 kg/m³ | 1180 kg/m³ | +17% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Note that *impact strength drops* despite reinforcement — this is because short fibres act as stress concentrators. To counter this, RRIM formers use **polyurea or polyurethane-polyurea hybrid** chemistry (so-called **"Reinforced RIM" or RRIM-NCO** variant).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `mixNodeA`, `polyolTank`) — reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are not used as node IDs. Node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold/italic markers.

### 4.2 Schematic 1 — RRIM Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    polyolTank[POLYOL TANK A SIDE] --> slurryMix[FIBRE PREMIX STIRRER]
    fillerHopper[GLASS FILLER HOPPER] --> slurryMix
    slurryMix --> pumpA[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP A]
    isocyanateTank[ISOCYANATE TANK B SIDE] --> pumpB[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP B]
    pumpA --> mixHead[IMPINGEMENT MIX HEAD]
    pumpB --> mixHead
    mixHead --> mould[HEATED CLOSED MOULD 50 TO 80 C]
    mould --> reaction[POLYMERISATION AND GELATION]
    reaction --> demould[DEMOULD AFTER 30 TO 120 S]
    demould --> postCure[POST CURE 80 TO 120 C FOR 1 H]
    postCure --> trim[FLASH TRIM AND GATE REMOVAL]
    trim --> finishedPart[FINISHED RRIM PART]
```

### 4.3 Schematic 2 — Functional Architecture of the RRIM Slurry Feed System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FEEDSYSTEM[FEED AND METERING SYSTEM]
        T1[POLYOL STORAGE TANK] --> S1[SLOW SPEED STIRRER]
        S1 --> V1[SLURRY VISCOSITY MONITOR]
        V1 --> PA[AXIAL PISTON PUMP A]
        T2[ISOCYANATE TANK] --> PB[AXIAL PISTON PUMP B]
        PA --> CTRL[FLOW RATIO CONTROLLER]
        PB --> CTRL
    end
    FEEDSYSTEM --> MIXNODE
    subgraph MIXNODE[MIXING AND MOULDING UNIT]
        CTRL --> IM[IMPINGEMENT CHAMBER 1 MM CLEARANCE]
        IM --> CA[CAVITY FILL 1 TO 3 S]
        CA --> REACT[CHEMICAL GELATION 10 TO 60 S]
        REACT --> CURE[IN MOULD CURE 60 TO 180 S]
    end
    MIXNODE --> POSTPROC
    subgraph POSTPROC[POST PROCESSING]
        CURE --> DEMO[DEMOULD ROBOT ARM]
        DEMO --> TRIM[AUTOMATIC TRIM PRESS]
        TRIM --> QC[DIMENSIONAL AND NDT INSPECTION]
        QC --> SHIP[PACKING AND SHIPMENT]
    end
```

### 4.4 Schematic 3 — Comparative Process Hierarchy Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RIM[RIM PLAIN POLYURETHANE] --> RRIM[RRIM FIBRE REINFORCED]
    RRIM --> SRIM[SRIM STRUCTURAL RIM]
    RRIM --> MAT[RRIM MAT REINFORCED]
    RIM[POLYMERISATION ONLY] --> POLY[REACTION INJECTION MOULDING]
    POLY --> POLY2[POLYUREA RIM]
    POLY --> POLY3[POLYURETHANE UREA RIM]
    POLY2 --> RRIM2[REINFORCED POLYUREA RIM]
    POLY3 --> RRIM3[REINFORCED POLYURETHANE UREA RIM]
```

### 4.5 Schematic 4 — Microstructure of an RRIM Part

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SURFACE[OUTER GEL COAT LAYER] --> BULK1[FIBRE RICH ZONE NEAR SURFACE]
    BULK1 --> CORE[BULK MATRIX WITH RANDOM FIBRES]
    CORE --> BULK2[FIBRE RICH ZONE NEAR MOULD WALL B]
    BULK2 --> SURFACEB[INNER MOULD SURFACE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** During mould filling, fibres migrate with the flow front and tend to align parallel to the mould wall, creating a **fibre-gradient microstructure** — stiff near the surface and more compliant in the core. This is a deliberate design feature that gives RRIM parts excellent *surface appearance* AND *structural stiffness*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Reinforced Reaction Injection Moulding (RRIM). Mention two reinforcements commonly used.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (10–12 lines expected):**

Reinforced Reaction Injection Moulding (RRIM) is a variant of the Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM) process in which reinforcing fillers are dispersed into one or both of the reactive liquid components before high-pressure impingement mixing and mould injection, yielding a fibre/filler-reinforced thermoset polyurethane composite part.

Two reinforcements commonly used are:
1. **Milled glass fibres** (length 0.2–0.8 mm) — most common, improves modulus.
2. **Wollastonite or mica platelets** — improves dimensional stability and HDT.

(Other valid answers: chopped glass fibres, carbon black, talc, calcium carbonate.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why are short fibres (≤ 1.5 mm) used in RRIM and not longer fibres like in SMC?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (8–10 lines expected):**

RRIM uses a **high-pressure impingement mix head with a very small clearance (~ 0.5–1.0 mm)**. Longer fibres would:
1. **Jam the mix head** and obstruct flow.
2. **Break extensively** during turbulent impingement, defeating the purpose of using them.
3. Cause **filtration effects** in the mould, leading to non-uniform distribution.

In contrast, SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound) uses a low-pressure compression moulding process with bulk flow, which can accommodate longer fibres (typically 25 mm). Hence, RRIM is restricted to fibres of 1.5 mm or less.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two completely independent alternatives are provided. KTU examiners typically choose one to appear on the question paper. Practice BOTH.**

---

#### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **RRIM process** and explain the role of each component of the equipment used. **(7 Marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> The RRIM process consists of the following sequential steps:
>
> 1. **Polyol + Filler Slurry Tank (A-Side):** Milled/chopped glass fibres are pre-dispersed in the polyol using a slow-speed, high-torque stirrer to form a homogeneous slurry. Silane coupling agents may be added to improve fibre-matrix adhesion.
>
> 2. **Isocyanate Tank (B-Side):** Stores MDI or modified MDI (the isocyanate component), kept dry under nitrogen blanket to prevent moisture-induced side reactions.
>
> 3. **High-Pressure Metering Pumps:** Two precision axial-piston pumps deliver the two streams at a controlled stoichiometric ratio (NCO:OH ≈ 1.00–1.10) and throughput of 1–10 kg/s.
>
> 4. **Impingement Mix Head:** Both streams collide in a small chamber (~1 mm clearance) at high velocity (Reynolds number > 10,000), creating turbulent mixing in **< 0.1 second**. The high shear also breaks any fibre agglomerates.
>
> 5. **Self-Cleaning Mix Head:** After each shot, a hydraulically actuated piston cleans the chamber to prevent clogging from cured polyurethane.
>
> 6. **Heated Closed Mould (50–80 °C):** The reactive fibre-polyurethane slurry flows into the mould cavity. Cream time (1–5 s) marks the start of polymerisation; rise time (10–60 s) marks gelation. Heat is supplied to accelerate the reaction.
>
> 7. **Demoulding and Post-Cure:** After 30–120 s, the part is rigid enough to be ejected. Post-cure at 80–120 °C for 1 hour completes crosslinking. Flash is trimmed, gates removed.
>
> *(Schematic: Refer to SECTION 4.2 above.)*
>
> **Key equipment roles summary:** Tanks store & condition; stirrers keep fillers suspended; pumps meter accurately; mix head blends and initiates reaction; mould shapes and cures; robots demould and trim.

**(b)** Discuss the **effect of filler type, content, and fibre length on the properties of RRIM parts**. **(7 Marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **1. Effect of Filler Type:**
> - **Milled glass fibres:** Highest stiffness lift, but reduce impact strength.
> - **Wollastonite (CaSiO₃ needles):** Good balance of stiffness, surface finish, and impact.
> - **Mica platelets:** Excellent HDT improvement and CTE reduction; aspect ratio drives barrier properties.
> - **Carbon black:** Mainly for colour/UV protection; minimal mechanical contribution.
>
> **2. Effect of Filler Content:**
> - As $w_f$ increases from 0 to 25 wt%:
>   - Flexural modulus rises by 4–6×.
>   - HDT rises by 30–50 °C.
>   - Tensile strength rises initially then plateaus or drops (above 20 wt% due to agglomeration).
>   - Impact strength drops monotonically.
>   - Slurry viscosity rises exponentially (Krieger-Dougherty), narrowing processing window.
>
> **3. Effect of Fibre Length:**
> - Longer fibres (1.5 mm) give higher modulus and impact retention but risk mix-head clogging.
> - Very short fibres (< 0.2 mm) behave more like particulate fillers — lower reinforcement efficiency.
> - Optimum: **0.4–0.8 mm** milled fibres for balanced properties and processability.
>
> **4. Fibre Orientation Effects:**
> - During mould filling, fibres align along flow direction → anisotropic properties.
> - Surface regions are fibre-rich; core is more isotropic.
> - Designers use FEM with fibre-orientation tensors to predict part performance.

---

#### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare **RIM, RRIM, and SRIM** processes. Highlight their similarities and key differences in terms of equipment, reinforcement, moulding pressure, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> | Feature | RIM | RRIM | SRIM |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Reinforcement** | None | Short milled/chopped fibres (0.2–1.5 mm) in polyol | Long fibres (mat/preform) placed in mould before injection |
> | **Mould pressure** | 0.3–0.7 MPa | 0.3–0.7 MPa | 0.1–0.4 MPa (low) |
> | **Mix head** | High-pressure impingement | High-pressure impingement (slurry-compatible) | Low-pressure dispensing (long fibres would break otherwise) |
> | **Cycle time** | 30–180 s | 60–240 s | 120–600 s |
> | **Stiffness** | Low (flexible) | Medium | High (structural) |
> | **Surface finish** | Excellent | Very good | Matte (mat fibre print-through possible) |
> | **Fibre volume fraction** | 0 | 5–15 vol% | 20–50 vol% |
> | **Applications** | Bumpers, soft fascia, shoe soles, gaskets | Body panels, structural bumper beams, tractor hoods | Automotive floor pans, load floors, pickup boxes |
> | **Capital cost** | Low | Medium | High (closed mould + preform tooling) |
> | **Key advantage** | Tough, flexible, fast | Stiff + tough + complex shape | Highest stiffness-to-weight ratio |
>
> **Similarities across all three:**
> - Two reactive liquid streams (polyol + isocyanate).
> - In-situ polymerisation in the mould.
> - Low moulding pressure → cheap aluminium tooling.
> - Rapid cycle times compared to compression moulding.
> - Same chemistry base (PU / polyurea / PU-urea).

**(b)** A polyol of viscosity **0.6 Pa·s** is filled with **20 wt% milled glass fibre** ($\rho_f = 2540$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1010$ kg/m³). Using the **Krieger-Dougherty equation** ($\phi_m = 0.6$, $[\mu] = 2.5$), calculate the **slurry viscosity** and comment on the processing implications. **(7 Marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1: Convert wt% to vol%**
>
> $$\phi_f = \frac{w_f/\rho_f}{w_f/\rho_f + (1-w_f)/\rho_m}$$
>
> $$\phi_f = \frac{0.20/2540}{0.20/2540 + 0.80/1010}$$
>
> $$\phi_f = \frac{7.874 \times 10^{-5}}{7.874 \times 10^{-5} + 7.921 \times 10^{-4}}$$
>
> $$\phi_f = \frac{7.874 \times 10^{-5}}{8.708 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0904$$
>
> **Step 2: Apply Krieger-Dougherty equation**
>
> $$\frac{\mu_f}{\mu_0} = \left(1 - \frac{\phi_f}{\phi_m}\right)^{-[\mu]\phi_m}$$
>
> $$\frac{\mu_f}{0.6} = \left(1 - \frac{0.0904}{0.6}\right)^{-2.5 \times 0.6}$$
>
> $$\frac{\mu_f}{0.6} = (1 - 0.1507)^{-1.5}$$
>
> $$\frac{\mu_f}{0.6} = (0.8493)^{-1.5}$$
>
> $$\frac{\mu_f}{0.6} = 1.244$$
>
> $$\mu_f = 0.747 \;\text{Pa·s}$$
>
> **Step 3: Comment on Processing Implications**
>
> - The viscosity increases by **24.4%** — manageable but noticeable.
> - The **pump pressure must be raised** to maintain throughput.
> - The **mix head must be cleaned more frequently** to prevent clogging.
> - **Mould filling becomes slower**; longer shot times risk premature gelation in the runner system.
> - **Fibre settling** in the slurry tank becomes critical — stirring intensity must be increased.
> - Going beyond ~25 wt% makes the slurry **borderline unprocessable** in standard impingement systems; SRIM or compression moulding would be preferred.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on RRIM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RIM and RRIM:** RIM has *no* filler; RRIM has *short* filler dispersed *in the polyol*. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if students describe a chopped-fibre preform (which is SRIM, not RRIM).
>
> 2. **Failing to mention the silane coupling agent** — worth 1 mark but frequently omitted.
>
> 3. **Wrong fibre length specification:** RRIM fibres are 0.2–1.5 mm, NOT 25 mm (which is SMC territory) and NOT 5–10 mm (which is BMC territory).
>
> 4. **Numerical errors in volume fraction conversion:** Use the formula $\phi_f = (w_f/\rho_f) / [(w_f/\rho_f) + ((1-w_f)/\rho_m)]$ — many students mistakenly use just $w_f$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the self-cleaning mix head:** A signature feature of RRIM equipment — at least **1 mark** in any equipment-related question.
>
> 6. **Confusing the modulus lift direction:** Adding fibre **increases modulus** but may **decrease** tensile strength and **always decreases** impact strength beyond ~15 wt%.
>
> 7. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always state Pa·s, GPa, kg/m³. Examiners deduct **0.5 marks** per missing unit in a numerical.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reinforced Reaction Injection Moulding (RRIM)**

- **Definition:** RRIM = RIM + short reinforcing fibres (0.2–1.5 mm) pre-dispersed in polyol.
- **Base chemistry:** Polyurethane (polyol + diisocyanate, typically MDI).
- **Reinforcements:** Milled glass, wollastonite, mica (5–25 wt%).
- **Mix head:** High-pressure impingement, ~1 mm clearance, Re > 10,000.
- **Mould pressure:** Low (0.3–0.7 MPa) → cheap aluminium tooling.
- **Mould temp:** 50–80 °C.
- **Cycle time:** 1–4 minutes (much faster than compression moulding).
- **Key advantage:** Stiff, tough, complex-shaped parts with low tooling cost.
- **Key limitation:** Fibre length capped at 1.5 mm; impact strength drops with filler.
- **Viscosity model:** Krieger-Dougherty equation for filled slurry.
- **Modulus model:** Halpin-Tsai equation for short-fibre composites.
- **Surface:** Gel-coat layer → excellent paintable finish.
- **Main application sector:** Automotive body panels, bumpers, fascias.
- **Distinguish from:** SRIM (preform in mould) and SMC (compression, longer fibres).
- **Coupling agents:** Silanes (3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane) for fibre-matrix bonding.
- **Anisotropy:** Fibre orientation follows flow → surface stiff, core compliant.
- **HDT lift:** ~5–8 °C per wt% glass fibre added.
- **CTE reduction:** Halved at 15 wt% loading.
- **NCO index:** 100–110 for RRIM formulations.
- **Cream time:** 1–5 s; **Rise time:** 10–60 s; **Demould:** 30–120 s.
- **Modern variants:** RRIM-polyurea and RRIM-PU/urea for better impact retention.
